Changyi was by the riverbank when he encountered a black dragon carrying a dark jade map. At the time, an old man told Changyi, "Your child will follow the water element and become a king." Ten years later, Zhuanxu was born, and his hand had markings resembling a dragon, as well as a jade map. That night, Changyi looked up at the sky, and the North Star transformed into an old man. When Zhuanxu ascended the throne, all unusual signs of good fortune and blessings gathered, and even those who did not follow the correct calendar came from distant lands.

    The emperor bowed to the spirits of the four directions, and the feudal lords held jade disks as a sign of respect. All the feudal lords had their proper ranks and order. Those who received cultural virtues were given bells and chimes, while those who received martial virtues were given spears and halberds. There was a golden bell that floated and a clear jade chime that sank. When brushed with feathers, the sound could be heard for a hundred miles. A stone floated on water as light as duckweed or algae, and it could be used as a chime without further processing.

    When the emperor held court with the world's nations, and when the music of the refined arts was played, the sound was clear and harmonious, like the feathers falling from the clouds, and the whales and sharks surged through the calm sea. There was a sword that dragged its shadow and extended into the sky. If there was war in any direction, the sword would fly up and point to that direction, indicating victory. When not in use, the sword made sounds like a dragon or tiger in the scabbard. [12]

    溟海之北，有勃鞮之国。人皆衣羽毛，无翼而飞，日中无影，寿千岁。食以黑河水藻，饮以阴山桂脂。凭风而翔，乘波而至。中国气暄，羽毛之衣，稍稍自落。帝乃更以文豹为饰。献黑玉之环，色如淳漆。贡玄驹千匹。帝以驾铁轮，骋劳殊乡绝域。其人依风泛黑河以旋其国也。
    　　闇河之北，有紫桂成林，其实如枣，群仙饵焉。韩终采药四言诗曰：“闇河之桂，实大如枣。得而食之，后天而老。”

    高辛

    　　帝喾之妃，邹屠氏之女也。轩辕去蚩尤之凶，迁其民善者于邹屠之地，迁恶者于有北之乡。其先以地命族，后分为邹氏、屠氏。女行不践地，常履风云，游于伊、洛。帝乃期焉，纳以为妃。妃常梦吞日，则生一子，凡经八梦，则生八子。世谓为“八神”，亦谓“八翌”，翌，明也，亦谓“八英”，亦谓“八力”，言其神力英明，翌成万象，亿兆流其神睿焉。 [13]

    To the north of the Sea of Mist, there is the country of Bodi. Its people are clothed in feathers, able to fly without wings, casting no shadow at noon, and living for a thousand years. They eat black river water algae and drink the fat of the yin mountain laurel. They ride the wind to soar and ride the waves to arrive. The warmth of the central land causes their feathered clothing to gradually fall off. The emperor then used patterned leopardskin as decoration. They presented black jade rings, colored like pure lacquer, and offered a thousand black horses. The emperor used them to drive iron wheels, exerting effort in distant and remote places. They relied on the wind and rode the black river to return to their country.

    To the north of the Dark River, there is a forest of purple laurel trees, whose fruit is like dates, eaten by the gods. Han Zhong, in his four-character poem on gathering medicine, said: "The laurel of the dark river, its fruit is large like dates. If one eats it, they will age after the heavens."

    Emperor Ku

    The consort of Emperor Ku was a daughter of the Zou and Tu clans. The Yellow Emperor drove away the evil of Chiyou, moving the good people to the land of Zou and Tu and the bad people to the northern place. Their ancestors named their clan after the land, and later divided into the Zou and Tu clans. She walked without touching the ground, always treading on wind and clouds, traveling in the rivers Yi and Luo. The emperor then invited her, taking her as his consort. The consort often dreamed of swallowing the sun, and then gave birth to a son. After eight dreams, she gave birth to eight sons. The world calls them "Eight Gods," also "Eight Bright Ones," "Eight Heroes," and "Eight Powers," describing their divine power and brilliance, they illuminate all things, and countless beings are influenced by their divine wisdom. [13]

    有丹丘之国，献码瑙瓮，以盛甘露。帝德所洽，被于殊方，以露充于厨也。码瑙，石类也，南方者为之胜。今善别马者，死则破其脑视之。其色如血者，则日行万里，能腾空飞；脑色黄者，日行千里；脑色青者，嘶闻数百里；脑色黑者，入水毛鬣不濡，日行五百里；脑色白者，多力而怒。今为器多用赤色，若是人工所制者，多不成器，亦殊朴拙。其国人听马鸣则别其脑色。丹丘之地，有夜叉驹跋之鬼，能以赤马脑为瓶。盂及乐器，皆精妙轻丽。中国人有用者，则魑魅不能逢之。一说云，马脑者，言是恶鬼之血，凝成此物。昔黄帝除蚩尤及四方群凶，并诸妖魅，填川满谷，积血成渊，聚骨如岳。数年中，血凝如石，骨白如灰，膏流成泉。故南方有肥泉之水，有白垩之山，望之峨峨，如霜雪矣。又有丹丘，千年一烧，黄河千年一清，至圣之君，以为大瑞。丹丘之野多鬼血，化为丹石，则码瑙也。 [14]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [14]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 有丹丘之国 - Classical phrase meaning 'there is a country called Danqiu,' introducing the setting of the story.
    • 献码瑙瓮 - Means 'presented a rhinoceros horn jar,' where '码瑙' is a transliteration of 'rhinoceros horn' (犀角), not 'agate' or 'agate-like stone.'
    • 帝德所洽，被于殊方 - Classical expression meaning 'the emperor's virtue permeates distant lands,' emphasizing the spread of imperial influence.
    • 以露充于厨也 - Means 'to fill the kitchen with dew,' suggesting the use of the jar to store dew or other substances.
    • 码瑙，石类也 - Means 'rhinoceros horn is a type of stone,' clarifying the material.
    • 南方者为之胜 - Means 'those from the south are superior,' indicating a preference for southern rhinoceros horns.
    • 听马鸣则别其脑色 - Means 'by listening to the horse's neigh, one can discern the color of its brain,' referring to a method of identifying horse brains.
    • 脑色如血者，则日行万里，能腾空飞 - Means 'if the brain color is like blood, it can travel ten thousand miles a day and fly through the air,' describing a mythical horse.
    • 脑色黄者，日行千里 - Means 'if the brain color is yellow, it can travel a thousand miles a day.'
    • 脑色青者，嘶闻数百里 - Means 'if the brain color is green, its neigh can be heard for hundreds of miles.'
    • 脑色黑者，入水毛鬣不濡，日行五百里 - Means 'if the brain color is black, it can enter water without getting wet and travel five hundred miles a day.'
    • 脑色白者，多力而怒 - Means 'if the brain color is white, it has great strength and is fierce.'
    • 今为器多用赤色 - Means 'now, most vessels are made in red,' indicating a preference for red-colored vessels.
    • 若是人工所制者，多不成器，亦殊朴拙 - Means 'if made by humans, they often fail to be functional and are quite crude,' criticizing human-made vessels.
    • 中国人有用者，则魑魅不能逢之 - Means 'if Chinese people use them, they cannot be harmed by demons,' suggesting a protective quality.
    • 一说云，马脑者，言是恶鬼之血，凝成此物 - Means 'another saying goes that rhinoceros horn is the blood of evil spirits, coagulated into this substance,' providing an alternative explanation for the origin of rhinoceros horn.
    • 昔黄帝除蚩尤及四方群凶，并诸妖魅，填川满谷，积血成渊，聚骨如岳 - Means 'in ancient times, Emperor Huangdi defeated Chiyou and the various evil spirits, filling rivers and valleys with blood and bones,' describing a mythological event.
    • 数年中，血凝如石，骨白如灰，膏流成泉 - Means 'after several years, the blood coagulated into stone, the bones turned white like ash, and the fat flowed into springs,' describing the transformation of the battlefield.
    • 故南方有肥泉之水，有白垩之山，望之峨峨，如霜雪矣 - Means 'thus, the south has the Fatty Spring and the White Chalk Mountain, which look majestic and white like frost and snow,' describing the geographical features.
    • 又有丹丘，千年一烧，黄河千年一清，至圣之君，以为大瑞 - Means 'there is also Danqiu, which burns once every thousand years, and the Yellow River, which clears once every thousand years, considered great omens by the most virtuous rulers,' describing the significance of these natural phenomena.
    • 丹丘之地，多鬼血，化为丹石，则码瑙也 - Means 'the land of Danqiu is rich in ghost blood, which transforms into red stone, which is rhinoceros horn,' explaining the origin of rhinoceros horn.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 丹丘 - A mythical place in Chinese mythology, often associated with immortality and longevity.
    • 黄帝 - Emperor Huangdi, the legendary first emperor of China, known for his wisdom and achievements.
    • 蚩尤 - A mythological figure often depicted as a powerful and malevolent leader of a group of tribes.
    • 四方群凶 - Refers to various evil spirits and demons.
    • 诸妖魅 - Refers to various supernatural beings.
    • 肥泉之水 - A mythical spring in the south.
    • 白垩之山 - A mythical mountain in the south.
    • 黄河 - The Yellow River, one of the most important rivers in China.
    • 至圣之君 - Refers to the most virtuous rulers, often associated with the concept of the Mandate of Heaven.

    Proper Nouns

    • 丹丘 - A mythical place in Chinese mythology.
    • 黄帝 - Emperor Huangdi, the legendary first emperor of China.
    • 蚩尤 - A mythological figure.
    • 四方群凶 - Various evil spirits and demons.
    • 诸妖魅 - Various supernatural beings.
    • 肥泉之水 - A mythical spring in the south.
    • 白垩之山 - A mythical mountain in the south.
    • 黄河 - The Yellow River.
    • 至圣之君 - The most virtuous rulers.

    There is a country called Danqiu, which presented a rhinoceros horn jar to be used for storing dew. The emperor's virtue permeates distant lands, and the jar is used to fill the kitchen with dew. Rhinoceros horn is a type of stone, and those from the south are considered superior. Nowadays, skilled horsemen can identify the color of a horse's brain by breaking it open after the horse's death. If the brain color is like blood, the horse can travel ten thousand miles a day and fly through the air; if the brain color is yellow, it can travel a thousand miles a day; if the brain color is green, its neigh can be heard for hundreds of miles; if the brain color is black, it can enter water without getting wet and travel five hundred miles a day; if the brain color is white, it has great strength and is fierce. Now, most vessels are made in red, and if made by humans, they often fail to be functional and are quite crude. The people of Danqiu can identify the brain color of a horse by listening to its neigh. The land of Danqiu is home to nightmarish creatures known as Yecha Juba, who can make bottles, bowls, and musical instruments from red rhinoceros horn. These items are all exquisite and delicate. If Chinese people use them, they cannot be harmed by demons. Another saying goes that rhinoceros horn is the blood of evil spirits, coagulated into this substance. In ancient times, Emperor Huangdi defeated Chiyou and the various evil spirits, filling rivers and valleys with blood and bones. After several years, the blood coagulated into stone, the bones turned white like ash, and the fat flowed into springs. Thus, the south has the Fatty Spring and the White Chalk Mountain, which look majestic and white like frost and snow. There is also Danqiu, which burns once every thousand years, and the Yellow River, which clears once every thousand years, considered great omens by the most virtuous rulers. The land of Danqiu is rich in ghost blood, which transforms into red stone, which is rhinoceros horn. [14]

    不可斫削雕琢，乃可铸以为器也。当黄帝时，码瑙瓮至，尧时犹存，甘露在其中，盈而不竭，谓之宝露，以班赐群臣。至舜时，露已渐减。随帝世之污隆，时淳则露满，时浇则露竭，及乎三代，减于陶唐之庭。舜迁宝瓮于衡山之上，故衡山之岳有宝露坛。舜于坛下起月馆，以望夕月。舜南巡至衡山，百辟群后皆得露泉之赐。时有云气生于露坛，又迁宝瓮于零陵之上。舜崩，瓮沦于地下。至秦始皇通汨?之流为小溪，径从长沙至零陵，掘地得赤玉瓮，可容八斗，以应八方之数，在舜庙之堂前。后人得之，不知年月。至后汉东方朔识之，朔乃作《宝瓮铭》曰“宝云生于露坛，祥风起于月馆，望三壶如盈尺，视八鸿如萦带。”三壶，则海中三山也。一曰方壶，则方丈也；二曰蓬壶，则蓬莱也；三曰瀛壶，则瀛洲也。形如壶器。此三山上广、中狭、下方，皆如工制，犹华山之似削成。八鸿者，八方之名；鸿，大也。登月馆以望四海三山，皆如聚米萦带者矣。 [15]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [15]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 不可斫削雕琢，乃可铸以为器也 - Metaphorical expression meaning that something cannot be shaped or refined by cutting or carving; it must be cast into a form.
    • 甘露在其中，盈而不竭 - Metaphorical expression describing a substance (here, 'sweet dew') that is abundant and inexhaustible.
    • 谓之宝露 - Term used to describe the sweet dew as a precious treasure.
    • 露已渐减 - Phrase indicating that the sweet dew is gradually diminishing.
    • 随帝世之污隆 - Metaphorical expression meaning that the state of the sweet dew corresponds to the moral and political condition of the emperor's reign.
    • 时淳则露满，时浇则露竭 - Metaphorical expression indicating that the sweetness of the dew corresponds to the purity of the times.
    • 及乎三代，减于陶唐之庭 - Phrase indicating that by the time of the Three Dynasties, the amount of sweet dew had decreased compared to the reign of Emperor Yao.
    • 舜迁宝瓮于衡山之上 - Phrase indicating that Emperor Shun moved the precious vessel to Mount Heng.
    • 故衡山之岳有宝露坛 - Phrase indicating that Mount Heng has a sacred altar for the sweet dew.
    • 舜于坛下起月馆，以望夕月 - Phrase indicating that Emperor Shun built a moon pavilion at the base of the altar to observe the evening moon.
    • 舜南巡至衡山，百辟群后皆得露泉之赐 - Phrase indicating that during his southern tour, Emperor Shun bestowed the sweet dew upon all the feudal lords.
    • 时有云气生于露坛 - Phrase indicating that clouds sometimes appeared over the altar of the sweet dew.
    • 又迁宝瓮于零陵之上 - Phrase indicating that the precious vessel was moved again to Mount Lingling.
    • 舜崩，瓮沦于地下 - Phrase indicating that after Emperor Shun's death, the vessel sank into the earth.
    • 至秦始皇通汨?之流为小溪，径从长沙至零陵，掘地得赤玉瓮，可容八斗，以应八方之数 - Phrase indicating that Emperor Qin Shi Huang constructed a small stream from Changsha to Mount Lingling, where a red jade vessel capable of holding eight dou was found, corresponding to the eight directions.
    • 至后汉东方朔识之 - Phrase indicating that during the Later Han Dynasty, Dongfang Shuo recognized the vessel.
    • 朔乃作《宝瓮铭》曰“宝云生于露坛，祥风起于月馆，望三壶如盈尺，视八鸿如萦带” - Phrase indicating that Dongfang Shuo wrote an inscription for the precious vessel, describing the clouds and winds associated with it and the distant appearance of the Three Mountains and the Eight Great Oceans.
    • 三壶，则海中三山也 - Phrase indicating that the Three Mountains are the three mythical islands in the sea.
    • 一曰方壶，则方丈也；二曰蓬壶，则蓬莱也；三曰瀛壶，则瀛洲也 - Phrase indicating the names of the Three Mountains.
    • 形如壶器 - Phrase indicating that the shape of the mountains resembles that of a vessel.
    • 此三山上广、中狭、下方，皆如工制，犹华山之似削成 - Phrase indicating that the Three Mountains are broad at the top, narrow in the middle, and flat at the bottom, like artificial constructions, similar to Mount Hua.
    • 八鸿者，八方之名；鸿，大也 - Phrase indicating that the Eight Great Oceans refer to the eight cardinal directions, with '鸿' meaning 'great.'
    • 登月馆以望四海三山，皆如聚米萦带者矣 - Phrase indicating that from the moon pavilion, one can see the Four Seas and the Three Mountains as if they were small clusters of rice surrounded by ribbons.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 黄帝时 - Refers to the reign of the Yellow Emperor, a legendary ruler in Chinese mythology.
    • 尧时 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Yao, a legendary sage ruler.
    • 舜时 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Shun, another legendary sage ruler.
    • 三代 - Refers to the Xia, Shang, and Zhou dynasties.
    • 陶唐之庭 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Yao.
    • 衡山 - Refers to Mount Heng, one of the Five Great Mountains in China.
    • 零陵 - Refers to Mount Lingling, a mountain in Hunan Province.
    • 秦始皇 - Refers to Emperor Qin Shi Huang, the first emperor of a unified China.
    • 汨?之流 - Refers to a river or stream.
    • 长沙 - Refers to Changsha, a city in Hunan Province.
    • 后汉 - Refers to the Later Han Dynasty.
    • 东方朔 - Refers to Dongfang Shuo, a famous scholar and court jester during the Han Dynasty.
    • 宝云 - Refers to a precious cloud associated with the sweet dew.
    • 祥风 - Refers to auspicious winds.
    • 三壶 - Refers to the Three Mountains in the sea.
    • 方壶 - Refers to Fangzhang, one of the Three Mountains.
    • 蓬壶 - Refers to Pengle, another of the Three Mountains.
    • 瀛壶 - Refers to Yingzhou, the third of the Three Mountains.
    • 八鸿 - Refers to the Eight Great Oceans, symbolizing the eight cardinal directions.
    • 四海三山 - Refers to the Four Seas and the Three Mountains, mythical locations in Chinese mythology.

    In all writing, it is like riding a swift steed — though it may possess a wild and unrestrained spirit, one must control it with bit and bridle, and must not let it run wild, straying from the proper path or indulging in aimless wanderings. [15]

    唐尧

    　　帝尧在位，圣德光洽。河洛之滨，得玉版方尺，图天地之形。又获金璧之瑞，文字炳列，记天地造化之始。四凶既除，善人来服，分职设官，彝伦攸叙。乃命大禹，疏川潴泽。有吴之乡，有北之地，无有妖灾。沉翔之类，自相驯扰。幽州之墟，羽山之北，有善鸣之禽，人面鸟喙，八翼一足，毛色如雉，行不践地，名曰青鸐，其声似钟磬笙竽也。《世语》曰：“青鸐鸣，时太平。”故盛明之世，翔鸣薮泽，音中律吕，飞而不行。至禹平水土，栖于川岳，所集之地，必有圣人出焉。自上古铸诸鼎器，皆图像其形，铭赞至今不绝。尧登位三十年，有巨查浮于西海，查上有光，夜明昼灭。海人望其光，乍大乍小，若星月之出入矣。查常浮绕四海，十二年一周天，周而复始，名曰贯月查，亦谓挂星查，羽人栖息其上。 [16]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [16]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 光洽 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'illuminated the realm,' emphasizing the widespread influence of virtue.
    • 善鸣 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'good at singing,' describing the bird's ability to sing well.
    • 行不践地 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'does not touch the ground when walking,' describing the bird's supernatural movement.
    • 太平 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'peace and prosperity,' often associated with a time of harmony.
    • 平水土 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'tamed the waters and earth,' describing Emperor Yu's efforts to control floods.
    • 栖于川岳 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'settled in rivers and mountains,' describing the bird's habitat.
    • 铭赞 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'inscribed praises,' describing the practice of engraving commendations on bronze vessels.
    • 巨查 - Idiomatic expression referring to a large raft or log, often associated with mythological events.
    • 乍大乍小 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'fluctuated in size,' describing the changing appearance of the raft's light.
    • 周而复始 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'completed a cycle and began again,' describing the raft's cyclical movement.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 帝尧 - Emperor Yao, the second of the Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors in Chinese mythology, known for his virtuous rule.
    • 河洛 - The Yellow and Luo rivers, important rivers in Chinese history and mythology.
    • 玉版方尺 - A sacred jade tablet, often associated with divination and spiritual significance.
    • 吴之乡，有北之地 - Refers to the regions of Wu (a historical state in the Yangtze River Delta) and the northern regions of China.
    • 幽州之墟，羽山之北 - Youzhou (a historical region in northern China) and Yushan (a mythical mountain).
    • 青鸐 - A mythical bird, often associated with peace and prosperity.
    • 《世语》 - A historical or mythological text, likely referring to a classical Chinese work.
    • 禹 - Emperor Yu, the third of the Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors, known for taming the floods.
    • 鼎器 - Bronze vessels, often used in rituals and as symbols of power.
    • 上古 - Ancient times, often referring to the mythical or semi-mythical past.
    • 西海 - The Western Sea, often referring to the Caspian Sea or the Mediterranean Sea in Chinese mythology.

    Proper Nouns

    • 帝尧 - Emperor Yao.
    • 河洛 - The Yellow and Luo rivers.
    • 玉版方尺 - A jade tablet.
    • 吴之乡，有北之地 - The regions of Wu and the northern regions.
    • 幽州之墟，羽山之北 - Youzhou and Yushan.
    • 青鸐 - A mythical bird.
    • 《世语》 - A classical text.
    • 禹 - Emperor Yu.
    • 鼎器 - Bronze vessels.
    • 上古 - Ancient times.
    • 西海 - The Western Sea.

    Emperor Yao

    When Emperor Yao was on the throne, his virtuous rule illuminated the realm. By the banks of the Yellow and Luo rivers, a jade tablet a foot square was found, depicting the forms of heaven and earth. He also received the auspicious sign of a golden jade, with inscriptions clearly arranged, recording the beginning of the creation of heaven and earth. After the four malevolent beings were eradicated, good people came to serve, and officials were appointed to their positions, with the proper order established. Emperor Yao then appointed Yu the Great to clear the rivers and lakes. In the lands of Wu and the northern regions, there were no calamities or disasters. Among the creatures that fly and swim, they naturally became tame and harmonious. In the ruins of Youzhou and north of Yushan, there was a bird that could sing well, with a human face and a bird's beak, eight wings and one foot, feathers like a pheasant, and it did not touch the ground when walking. It is called the Qingli, and its sound is like bells, chimes, flutes, and pipes. The Shi Yu says, "When the Qingli sings, it is a time of peace." Therefore, in times of great prosperity and clarity, the Qingli would soar and sing in the marshes and lakes, its voice harmonizing with the music of the universe, flying but not moving. When Yu tamed the waters and earth, it settled in rivers and mountains. Wherever it gathered, there would surely be a sage born. Since ancient times, when casting various bronze vessels, they all depicted its form and inscribed praises, which have continued to the present day. After Emperor Yao had been on the throne for thirty years, a large raft floated in the Western Sea, and on it there was light, which shone at night and disappeared during the day. The people by the sea watched its light, which fluctuated in size, like the rising and setting of stars and the moon. The raft often floated around the four seas, completing a cycle every twelve years, and then began again. It is called the 'Guan Yue Cha' or 'Guaxing Cha,' and immortals lived on it. [16]

    群仙含露以漱，日月之光则如冥矣。虞、夏之季，不复记其出没。游海之人，犹传其神伟也。西海之西，有浮玉山。山下有巨穴，穴中有水，其色若火，昼则通昽不明，夜则照耀穴外，虽波涛灌荡，其光不灭，是谓“阴火”。当尧世，其光烂起，化为赤云，丹辉炳映，百川恬澈。游海者铭曰“沉燃”，以应火德之运也。尧在位七十年，有鸾雏岁岁来集，麒麟游于薮泽，枭鸱逃于绝漠。有秖支之国献重明之鸟，一名“双睛”，言双睛在目。状如鸡，鸣似凤。时解落毛羽，肉翮而飞。能搏逐猛兽虎狼，使妖灾群恶不能为害。饴以琼膏。或一岁数来，或数岁不至。国人莫不扫洒门户，以望重明之集。其未至之时，国人或刻木，或铸金，为此鸟之状，置于门户之间，则魑魅丑类自然退伏。今人每岁元日，或刻木铸金，或图画为鸡于牖上，此之遗像也。 [17]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [17]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 群仙含露以漱 - Poetic expression meaning 'the immortals wash their mouths with dew,' symbolizing purity and transcendence.
    • 日月之光则如冥矣 - Means 'the light of the sun and moon is as if it were dark,' suggesting an unusual or supernatural phenomenon.
    • 虞、夏之季 - Refers to the late periods of the Yu and Xia dynasties, indicating a historical context.
    • 浮玉山 - A mythical mountain, possibly named for its jade-like appearance or association with jade.
    • 阴火 - Literally 'yin fire,' a term for a mysterious or supernatural fire that burns without visible fuel.
    • 火德之运 - Refers to the 'fire virtue' or 'fire era' in the Five Elements theory, symbolizing a particular historical or cosmic phase.
    • 沉燃 - A term used by those who travel the sea to describe the 'sinking flame,' possibly referring to the '阴火' phenomenon.
    • 重明之鸟 - A mythical bird, also known as '双睛' (double-eyed), symbolizing purity and protection against evil.
    • 饴以琼膏 - Means 'fed with jade-like fat,' a metaphor for nourishment or reward.
    • 今人每岁元日 - Refers to people today on the first day of the year, indicating a tradition that continues into modern times.
    • 遗像 - Means 'leftover image' or 'legacy,' referring to the enduring influence of ancient practices.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 群仙 - Refers to immortals or deities in Chinese mythology, often associated with longevity and supernatural powers.
    • 虞、夏 - Refers to the Yu and Xia dynasties, early Chinese dynasties in ancient history.
    • 西海 - Refers to the Western Sea, a mythical or distant sea in Chinese geography.
    • 尧世 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Yao, a legendary sage ruler in Chinese history.
    • 鸾雏 - Refers to young phoenixes, symbolizing good fortune and harmony.
    • 麒麟 - Refers to the qilin, a mythical creature symbolizing peace and prosperity.
    • 枭鸱 - Refers to owls, often associated with ill omens or darkness.
    • 秖支之国 - Refers to a mythical or distant land, possibly a foreign or exotic place.
    • 琼膏 - Refers to a mythical or divine substance, often associated with immortality or nourishment.
    • 元日 - Refers to the first day of the year, a traditional Chinese festival.

    Proper Nouns

    • 群仙 - Immortals or deities in Chinese mythology.
    • 虞、夏 - The Yu and Xia dynasties, early Chinese dynasties in ancient history.
    • 西海 - The Western Sea, a mythical or distant sea in Chinese geography.
    • 浮玉山 - A mythical mountain, possibly named for its jade-like appearance.
    • 阴火 - A mysterious or supernatural fire that burns without visible fuel.
    • 尧世 - The reign of Emperor Yao, a legendary sage ruler in Chinese history.
    • 鸾雏 - Young phoenixes, symbolizing good fortune and harmony.
    • 麒麟 - The qilin, a mythical creature symbolizing peace and prosperity.
    • 枭鸱 - Owls, often associated with ill omens or darkness.
    • 秖支之国 - A mythical or distant land, possibly a foreign or exotic place.
    • 重明之鸟 - A mythical bird, also known as '双睛' (double-eyed), symbolizing purity and protection against evil.
    • 琼膏 - A mythical or divine substance, often associated with immortality or nourishment.
    • 元日 - The first day of the year, a traditional Chinese festival.
    • 遗像 - The enduring influence of ancient practices, such as the tradition of hanging images of chickens on windows to ward off evil.

    The Immortals wash their mouths with dew, and the light of the sun and moon is as if it were dark. During the late periods of the Yu and Xia dynasties, this phenomenon was no longer recorded. Those who travel the sea still speak of it as a marvel. To the west of the Western Sea, there is a Floating Jade Mountain. At the foot of the mountain lies a great cave, within which is water that appears like fire. By day, it is dim and unclear, but at night it illuminates the outside of the cave. Even when waves and tides surge, its light does not fade; this is called "Yin Fire." During the reign of Emperor Yao, the light of the Yin Fire blazed forth, transforming into a red cloud that illuminated the rivers with its brilliant red glow. Those who travel the sea named it "Chenran," corresponding to the era of Fire Virtue. Emperor Yao ruled for seventy years, during which young phoenixes came annually, qilins roamed the marshes, and owls fled to the desolate wilderness. A country from the distant land of Zhi contributed the Double-Eyed Bird, also known as the "Chongming Bird," which is said to have two eyes. It resembles a chicken but sings like a phoenix. Sometimes it sheds its feathers and flies with its flesh, capable of chasing away fierce beasts and tigers, preventing evil disasters and malevolent forces from causing harm. It was fed with divine fat. Sometimes it comes once a year, and sometimes it does not appear for several years. The people of the country would sweep and clean their doors and windows, hoping for the arrival of the Chongming Bird. When it did not come, they would carve wood or cast metal to create images of the bird, placing them in their doors and windows, which would naturally cause the demons and evil spirits to retreat. Today, on the first day of the year, people either carve wood, cast metal, or paint images of chickens on their windows, as a legacy of this ancient practice. [17]

    虞舜

    　　虞舜在位十年，有五老游于国都，舜以师道尊之，言则及造化之始。舜禅于禹，五老去，不知所从。舜乃置五星之祠以祭之。其夜有五长星出，熏风四起，连珠合璧，祥应备焉。万国重译而至。有大频之国，其民来朝，乃问其灾祥之数。对曰：“昔北极之外，有潼海之水，渤潏高隐于日中。有巨鱼大蛟，莫测其形也，吐气则八极皆暗，振鬐则五岳波荡。当尧时，怀山为害，大蛟萦天，萦天则三河俱溢，海渎同流。”三河者，天河、地河、中河是也。此三水有时通壅，至圣之治，水色俱澄，无有流沫。 [18]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [18]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 虞舜 - Refers to Shun, the legendary sage-king of the Xia dynasty, often depicted as a paragon of virtue and governance.
    • 五老 - A term for five elderly sages or wise men, often associated with Shun's reign.
    • 言则及造化之始 - Means 'their words reached the beginning of creation,' implying deep wisdom and insight into the origins of the universe.
    • 虞舜在位十年 - Indicates that Shun ruled for ten years, a specific period of his reign.
    • 置五星之祠以祭之 - Means 'established a temple to the Five Planets to worship them,' where the Five Planets are traditionally Mercury, Venus, Mars, Jupiter, and Saturn.
    • 有五长星出 - Refers to the appearance of five long stars in the sky, often interpreted as a celestial sign.
    • 熏风四起 - Describes a gentle, warm breeze, often associated with the arrival of summer.
    • 连珠合璧 - A metaphor for a series of auspicious signs or events, often used in ancient Chinese texts to describe harmonious and favorable conditions.
    • 万国重译而至 - Means 'from all over the world, people came through multiple translations,' indicating the widespread influence and recognition of Shun's rule.
    • 大频之国 - Refers to a distant or far-off country, possibly a metaphor for a remote or exotic land.
    • 怀山为害 - Means 'to cause harm by holding onto mountains,' suggesting a metaphorical threat or danger.
    • 萦天 - Describes a situation where something is entangled or wrapped around the sky, often used to describe a large, threatening presence.
    • 三河 - Refers to the three major rivers: the Milky Way (天河), the Yellow River (地河), and the Central River (中河).
    • 有时通壅 - Means 'sometimes flowing and sometimes blocked,' indicating the cyclical nature of water flow.
    • 至圣之治 - Refers to the governance of a sage-king, emphasizing the ideal state of order and harmony.
    • 水色俱澄 - Means 'the water is clear and pure,' describing a state of natural beauty and purity.
    • 无有流沫 - Means 'there is no foam or turbulence,' indicating calm and stability.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 虞舜 - Shun is a legendary figure in Chinese history, known for his virtuous rule and the establishment of the Xia dynasty.
    • 五老 - The Five Elders are often associated with Shun's reign and are seen as symbols of wisdom and longevity.
    • 五星之祠 - The Five Planets (Mercury, Venus, Mars, Jupiter, and Saturn) were considered important celestial bodies in ancient Chinese astronomy and were often worshipped.
    • 熏风 - A gentle, warm breeze, often associated with the arrival of summer and the reign of a sage-king.
    • 连珠合璧 - A metaphor for a series of auspicious signs, often used in ancient Chinese texts to describe harmonious and favorable conditions.
    • 万国重译而至 - This phrase emphasizes the widespread influence and recognition of Shun's rule, suggesting that his virtue and governance were known and respected far and wide.
    • 大频之国 - A distant or far-off country, possibly a metaphor for a remote or exotic land.
    • 怀山为害 - A metaphorical threat or danger, often used to describe a situation where something is causing harm or disruption.
    • 三河 - The three major rivers in Chinese mythology: the Milky Way, the Yellow River, and the Central River.
    • 至圣之治 - The ideal state of governance under a sage-king, characterized by order, harmony, and natural beauty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 虞舜 - Shun, the legendary sage-king of the Xia dynasty.
    • 五老 - The Five Elders, wise men associated with Shun's reign.
    • 禹 - Yu, the successor to Shun and the founder of the Xia dynasty.
    • 五星 - The Five Planets: Mercury, Venus, Mars, Jupiter, and Saturn.
    • 三河 - The three major rivers: the Milky Way, the Yellow River, and the Central River.
    • 尧 - Yao, the legendary sage-king who preceded Shun and Yu.

    Shun the Sage

    Shun the Sage reigned for ten years, during which five elderly sages roamed the capital. Shun honored them as teachers, and their words reached the beginning of creation. When Shun abdicated in favor of Yu, the five sages departed, and no one knew where they went. Shun then established a temple to the Five Planets to worship them. That night, five long stars appeared, a gentle breeze blew, and auspicious signs manifested in the form of connected pearls and a united jade disk. From all over the world, people came through multiple translations to pay homage. A distant country sent its people to court Shun, asking about signs of good and bad fortune. They replied: "In the past, beyond the North Pole, there was the vast sea of Tong, hidden high above the sun. There were a great fish and a large dragon, whose shapes were unknown. When they breathed, the eight directions were darkened, and when they flapped their fins, the five great mountains trembled. During the time of Yao, the great dragon caused harm by entangling itself in the sky, and when it did so, the three rivers overflowed, and the seas and rivers flowed together." The three rivers are the Milky Way, the Yellow River, and the Central River. These three rivers sometimes flow and sometimes block, but under the governance of a sage-king, the water is clear and pure, with no foam or turbulence. [18]

    及帝之商均，暴乱天下，则巨鱼吸日，蛟绕于天，故诬妄也。此言吸日而星雨皆坠，抑亦似是而非也。故使后来为之回惑，托以无稽之言，特取其爱博德奇之间，录其广异宏丽之靡矣。舜葬苍梧之野，有鸟如雀，自丹州而来，吐五色之气，氤氲如云，名曰凭霄雀，能群飞衔土成丘坟。此鸟能反形变色，集于峻林之上。在木则为禽，行地则为兽，变化无常。常游丹海之际，时来苍梧之野。衔青砂珠，积成垄阜，名曰“珠丘”。其珠轻细，风吹如尘起，名曰“珠尘”。今苍梧之外，山人采药，时有得青石，圆洁如珠，服之不死，带者身轻。故仙人方回《游南岳七言赞》曰：“珠尘圆洁轻且明，有道服者得长生。” [19]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [19]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 巨鱼吸日，蛟绕于天 - Metaphor for celestial anomalies, symbolizing great disturbances or omens.
    • 此言吸日而星雨皆坠 - Statement describing the sun being swallowed and stars raining down, somewhat true but not entirely accurate.
    • 故使后来为之回惑 - Clause indicating the impact of such stories on future generations, causing confusion.
    • 托以无稽之言 - Phrase attributing the story to baseless rumors.
    • 特取其爱博德奇之间 - Phrase highlighting the reason why such stories are told despite their lack of credibility, especially for their broad appeal and strange nature.
    • 录其广异宏丽之靡矣 - Phrase emphasizing the literary value of such tales, recording their vast strangeness and magnificent beauty.
    • 能群飞衔土成丘坟 - Description of the bird's ability to fly in groups and carry soil to form mounds and tombs.
    • 吐五色之气，氤氲如云 - Description of the bird's magical appearance, emitting a five-colored mist, swirling like clouds.
    • 此鸟能反形变色，集于峻林之上 - Description of the bird's magical transformations, changing its shape and color, gathering on high trees.
    • 在木则为禽，行地则为兽，变化无常 - Description of the bird's versatility, on trees it is a bird; on the ground, it is an animal; its transformations are unpredictable.
    • 常游丹海之际，时来苍梧之野 - Description of the bird's habitat and movements, often roams the shores of Dan Sea and sometimes visits the wilds of Cangwu.
    • 衔青砂珠，积成垄阜 - Description of the bird's activity of creating structures, carrying blue sand pearls and accumulating them into hills and mounds.
    • 其珠轻细，风吹如尘起 - Description of the characteristics of the pearls, light and fine, blown by the wind like dust.
    • 今苍梧之外，山人采药，时有得青石，圆洁如珠，服之不死，带者身轻 - Description of the magical properties of the stones, found by mountain dwellers, round and clean like pearls, granting immortality if consumed, and those who wear them become lighter.
    • 故仙人方回《游南岳七言赞》曰 - Quotation from Fang Hui's poem, emphasizing the magical properties of the pearl dust, 'The pearl dust is round, clean, and light, and those who consume it gain eternal life.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 帝之商均 - Refers to Emperor Shang Jun, a historical figure whose reign was marked by chaos.
    • 舜 - Refers to Emperor Shun, one of the legendary Five Emperors of ancient China.
    • 苍梧之野 - Refers to the region where Shun was buried, a significant location in Chinese mythology.
    • 丹州 - Refers to a historical prefecture, possibly in modern-day Shanxi or Henan.
    • 丹海 - Refers to a mythical sea, possibly a reference to a body of water associated with the region.
    • 仙人方回 - Refers to Fang Hui, an immortal figure in Chinese mythology, known for his poetic works.

    Proper Nouns

    • 帝之商均 - Emperor Shang Jun
    • 舜 - Emperor Shun
    • 苍梧之野 - The region where Shun was buried
    • 丹州 - A historical prefecture
    • 丹海 - A mythical sea
    • 仙人方回 - Fang Hui, an immortal figure

    When Emperor Shang Jun was in power, the world was in chaos, and tales of a giant fish swallowing the sun and a dragon coiling around the sky circulated, which were baseless rumors. This story claims that the sun was swallowed and stars rained down, which is somewhat true but not entirely accurate. Thus, it caused later generations to be confused, attributing it to groundless tales, especially for their broad appeal and strange nature, recording their vast strangeness and magnificent beauty. Shun was buried in the wilds of Cangwu, and there was a bird like a sparrow that came from Dan Prefecture. It emitted a five-colored mist, swirling like clouds, and was called the "Pengxiao Sparrow." It could fly in groups and carry soil to form mounds and tombs. This bird could change its shape and color, gathering on high trees. On trees, it was a bird; on the ground, it was an animal; its transformations were unpredictable. It often roamed the shores of Dan Sea and sometimes visited the wilds of Cangwu. It carried blue sand pearls and accumulated them into hills and mounds, which were called "Zhuqiu" (Pearl Mound). The pearls were light and fine, blown by the wind like dust, and were called "Zhuchen" (Pearl Dust). Nowadays, in the outskirts of Cangwu, mountain dwellers who gather herbs sometimes find blue stones, round and clean like pearls, which, if consumed, grant immortality, and those who wear them become lighter. Therefore, the immortal Fang Hui wrote in his seven-character ode on the Southern Mountain: "The pearl dust is round, clean, and light, and those who consume it gain eternal life." [19]

    冀州之西二万里，有孝养之国。其俗人年三百岁，而织茅为衣，即《尚书》“岛夷卉服”之类也。死，葬之中野，百鸟衔土为坟，群兽为之掘穴，不封不树。有亲死者，克木为影，事之如生。其俗骁勇，能啮金石，其舌杪方而本小。手搏千钧，以爪画地，则洪泉涌流。善养禽兽，入海取虬龙，育于圜室，以充祭祀。昔黄帝伐蚩尤，除诸凶害，独表此处为孝养之乡，万国莫不钦仰，故舜封为孝让之国。舜受尧禅，其国执玉帛来朝，特加宾礼，异于余戎狄也。爰及鸟兽昆虫，以应阴阳。至亿万之夫，山一轮，海一竭，鱼、蛟陆居，有赤乌如鹏，以翼覆蛟、鱼之上。蛟以尾叩天求雨，鱼吸日之光，冥然则暗如薄蚀矣，众星与雨偕坠。舜乃祷海岳之灵，万国称圣。德之所洽，群祥咸至矣。 [20]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [20]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 冀州之西二万里 - Refers to a location two thousand miles west of Ji Province.
    • 孝养之国 - A country known for filial piety.
    • 织茅为衣 - Making clothes from reed or straw.
    • 岛夷卉服 - A reference to islanders wearing grass or leaf clothing, as mentioned in the *Book of Documents*.
    • 葬之中野 - Burial in the middle of the wilderness.
    • 百鸟衔土为坟 - Birds carrying soil to build a mound.
    • 群兽为之掘穴 - Beasts digging graves.
    • 不封不树 - Not marking the grave with mounds or trees.
    • 克木为影 - Carving wood to create a likeness.
    • 事之如生 - Treating it as if it were alive.
    • 骁勇 - Brave and fierce.
    • 能啮金石 - Able to bite through metal and stone.
    • 舌杪方而本小 - The tip of the tongue is square, and the base is small.
    • 手搏千钧 - Able to lift a thousand catties (a unit of weight).
    • 以爪画地，则洪泉涌流 - Drawing on the ground with claws causes a great spring to flow.
    • 善养禽兽 - Good at raising animals.
    • 入海取虬龙 - Going into the sea to catch dragons.
    • 育于圜室 - Raising them in circular chambers.
    • 以充祭祀 - For use in sacrifices.
    • 黄帝伐蚩尤 - The mythological battle between Huangdi (Yellow Emperor) and Chiyou.
    • 除诸凶害 - Eliminating all evil.
    • 孝养之乡 - A place known for filial piety.
    • 万国莫不钦仰 - All nations admire it.
    • 舜封为孝让之国 - Shun established it as a country of filial piety and courtesy.
    • 执玉帛来朝 - Bringing jade and silk to pay homage.
    • 特加宾礼 - Giving special guest treatment.
    • 异于余戎狄也 - Different from other barbarians.
    • 爰及鸟兽昆虫 - Including birds, beasts, and insects.
    • 以应阴阳 - To correspond with the balance of yin and yang.
    • 山一轮，海一竭 - Mythical events indicating the power of nature.
    • 鱼、蛟陆居 - Mythical creatures living on land, indicating a supernatural element.
    • 有赤乌如鹏 - A red bird like a Peng (a mythical bird).
    • 以翼覆蛟、鱼之上 - Covering the dragons and fish with its wings, indicating protection and nurturing.
    • 蛟以尾叩天求雨 - The dragon tapping the sky with its tail to request rain, indicating a connection between the divine and the natural world.
    • 鱼吸日之光 - Fish sucking in the sun's light, indicating a magical or supernatural ability.
    • 冥然则暗如薄蚀矣 - When it becomes dark, it is like a solar eclipse.
    • 众星与雨偕坠 - Stars and rain falling together, indicating a dramatic and supernatural event.
    • 舜乃祷海岳之灵 - Shun praying to the spirits of the sea and mountains, indicating a deep connection with nature and the divine.
    • 万国称圣 - All nations calling him a sage.
    • 德之所洽 - The virtue that spreads widely.
    • 群祥咸至矣 - All auspicious signs arriving.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 冀州 - One of the Nine Provinces of ancient China.
    • 黄帝伐蚩尤 - A famous mythological battle between Huangdi (Yellow Emperor) and Chiyou.
    • 舜 - Shun, a legendary ruler of ancient China.
    • 孝让之国 - A country known for filial piety and courtesy.
    • 执玉帛来朝 - A ritual of bringing jade and silk to pay homage, indicating submission and respect.
    • 特加宾礼 - Special guest treatment, indicating high status and honor.
    • 异于余戎狄也 - Different from other barbarians, indicating a higher level of civilization.
    • 爰及鸟兽昆虫 - Including birds, beasts, and insects, indicating a holistic view of nature.
    • 以应阴阳 - Corresponding with the balance of yin and yang, a fundamental concept in Chinese philosophy.
    • 山一轮，海一竭 - Mythical events indicating the power of nature.
    • 鱼、蛟陆居 - Mythical creatures living on land, indicating a supernatural element.
    • 有赤乌如鹏 - A red bird like a Peng, a mythical bird symbolizing longevity and power.
    • 以翼覆蛟、鱼之上 - Covering the dragons and fish with its wings, indicating protection and nurturing.
    • 蛟以尾叩天求雨 - The dragon tapping the sky with its tail to request rain, indicating a connection between the divine and the natural world.
    • 鱼吸日之光 - Fish sucking in the sun's light, indicating a magical or supernatural ability.
    • 冥然则暗如薄蚀矣 - When it becomes dark, it is like a solar eclipse.
    • 众星与雨偕坠 - Stars and rain falling together, indicating a dramatic and supernatural event.
    • 舜乃祷海岳之灵 - Shun praying to the spirits of the sea and mountains, indicating a deep connection with nature and the divine.
    • 万国称圣 - All nations calling him a sage.
    • 德之所洽 - The virtue that spreads widely.
    • 群祥咸至矣 - All auspicious signs arriving.

    Proper Nouns

    • 冀州 - One of the Nine Provinces of ancient China.
    • 黄帝 - Huangdi (Yellow Emperor), a legendary ruler of ancient China.
    • 蚩尤 - Chiyou, a mythological figure often depicted as an enemy of Huangdi.
    • 舜 - Shun, a legendary ruler of ancient China.
    • 尧 - Yao, a legendary ruler of ancient China.
    • 孝让之国 - A country known for filial piety and courtesy.
    • 《尚书》 - The *Book of Documents*, one of the Five Classics.
    • 岛夷 - Islanders, often used to describe people from distant regions.
    • 卉服 - Clothing made from grass or leaves.
    • 孝养之乡 - A place known for filial piety.
    • 万国 - All nations.
    • 玉帛 - Jade and silk, used as gifts or offerings.
    • 宾礼 - Special guest treatment, indicating high status and honor.
    • 异于余戎狄也 - Different from other barbarians.
    • 爰及鸟兽昆虫 - Including birds, beasts, and insects.
    • 以应阴阳 - To correspond with the balance of yin and yang.
    • 山一轮，海一竭 - Mythical events indicating the power of nature.
    • 鱼、蛟陆居 - Mythical creatures living on land, indicating a supernatural element.
    • 有赤乌如鹏 - A red bird like a Peng, a mythical bird symbolizing longevity and power.
    • 以翼覆蛟、鱼之上 - Covering the dragons and fish with its wings, indicating protection and nurturing.
    • 蛟以尾叩天求雨 - The dragon tapping the sky with its tail to request rain, indicating a connection between the divine and the natural world.
    • 鱼吸日之光 - Fish sucking in the sun's light, indicating a magical or supernatural ability.
    • 冥然则暗如薄蚀矣 - When it becomes dark, it is like a solar eclipse.
    • 众星与雨偕坠 - Stars and rain falling together, indicating a dramatic and supernatural event.
    • 舜乃祷海岳之灵 - Shun praying to the spirits of the sea and mountains, indicating a deep connection with nature and the divine.
    • 万国称圣 - All nations calling him a sage.
    • 德之所洽 - The virtue that spreads widely.
    • 群祥咸至矣 - All auspicious signs arriving.

    Two thousand miles west of Ji Province lies a land renowned for filial piety. The people there live to be three hundred years old, weaving reed into their garments, as described in the Book of Documents as "island people wearing grass and leaf clothing." When they die, they are buried in the wilderness, where birds carry soil to build their mounds, and beasts dig their graves, with no markers or trees placed over them. For those who have passed away, the living carve wooden likenesses to honor them as if they were still alive. The inhabitants are brave and fierce, able to bite through metal and stone, with their tongues square at the tip and narrow at the base. They can lift a thousand catties with their hands, and when they draw on the ground with their claws, great springs flow forth. They are skilled at raising animals, venturing into the sea to catch dragons, which they raise in circular chambers for use in sacrifices. In ancient times, Huangdi (Yellow Emperor) defeated Chiyou, eliminating all evil, and singled out this place as a land of filial piety. All nations admired it, and thus Shun established it as a country of filial piety and courtesy. When Shun succeeded Yao, the people of this land brought jade and silk to pay homage, receiving special guest treatment, setting them apart from other barbarians. The customs also extend to birds, beasts, and insects, all in harmony with the balance of yin and yang. In a moment, the mountains turn once, the seas dry up, and fish and dragons live on land. A red bird like a Peng covers the dragons and fish with its wings. The dragon taps the sky with its tail to request rain, while the fish suck in the sun's light. When darkness falls, it is as if a solar eclipse has occurred, and stars and rain fall together. Shun then prayed to the spirits of the sea and mountains, and all nations called him a sage. The virtue he spread widely brought forth all auspicious signs. [20]

    南浔之国，有洞穴阴源，其下通地脉。中有毛龙、毛鱼，时蜕骨于旷泽之中。鱼、龙同穴而处。其国献毛龙，一雌一雄，故置豢龙之官。至夏代养龙不绝，因以命族。至禹导川，乘此龙。及四海攸同，乃放河汭。 [21]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [21]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 时蜕骨于旷泽之中 - Periodically shed their skin or scales in the vast marshes.
    • 鱼、龙同穴而处 - Fish and dragons coexist in the same cave, symbolizing a harmonious or unusual relationship.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 南浔之国 - A fictional or symbolic kingdom used to set the scene.
    • 洞穴阴源 - A mystical or magical setting, common in classical Chinese literature.
    • 毛龙、毛鱼 - Mythical creatures that are not found in historical records but are part of Chinese mythology.
    • 夏代 - The Xia Dynasty, one of the earliest recorded dynasties in Chinese history, known for its legendary figures like Yu the Great.
    • 禹导川 - Refers to the legendary feat of Yu the Great taming the floods, a foundational myth in Chinese civilization.
    • 四海攸同 - A phrase often used to describe a time of peace and unity, here referring to the pacification of the four seas.

    Proper Nouns

    • 南浔之国 - A fictional or symbolic kingdom.
    • 夏代 - The Xia Dynasty.
    • 禹 - Yu the Great, a legendary figure in Chinese history.
    • 四海 - The four seas, often used symbolically to represent the entire world or empire.

    In the kingdom of Nanxun, there is a cave with a hidden source that connects to the earth's veins. Within this cave live hairy dragons and hairy fish, which periodically shed their skin in the vast marshes. Fish and dragons coexist in the same cave. The kingdom presented a pair of hairy dragons, one male and one female, leading to the establishment of officials responsible for caring for the dragons. This practice continued uninterrupted through the Xia Dynasty, and the clan named itself after it. When Yu the Great tamed the floods, he rode one of these dragons. When the four seas were pacified, the dragon was released into the river bend. [21]

    录曰：按《春秋传》云：“星陨如雨，而夜犹明。”《淮南子》云：“麒麟斗而日月蚀，鲸鱼死而彗星见。”夫盈虚薄蚀，未详变于圣典；孛彗妖沴，着灾异于图册。麒麟斗，鲸鱼死，靡闻于前经。求诸正诰，殆将昧焉。
    　　录曰：自稽考群籍，伏羲至于轩辕、少昊、高辛、唐、虞之际，禅业相袭，符表名类，未若尧之盛也。按《易纬》云：尧为阳精，叶德干道，粤若稽古，是谓上圣。惟天为大，惟尧则之。禅业有虞，所谓契叶符同，明象日月。盖其载籍遐旷，算纪绵远，德业异纪，神迹各殊。考传闻于前古，求佥言于中世，而教道参差，祥德递起，指明群说，能无彷佛！精灵冥昧，至圣之所不语，安以浅末，贬其有无者哉！刘子政曰：“凡传闻不如亲闻，亲闻不如亲见。 [22]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [22]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 錄曰 - A formal heading indicating that what follows is a record or note.
    • 按《春秋傳》云 - Means 'According to the *Spring and Autumn Annals* commentary,' introducing a citation from an authoritative source.
    • 星陨如雨，而夜犹明 - A vivid metaphor describing a celestial event where stars fall like rain, yet the night remains bright.
    • 麒麟斗而日月蚀，鲸鱼死而彗星见 - A poetic and symbolic description of natural phenomena linked to mythical creatures and celestial bodies.
    • 夫盈虚薄蚀，未详变于圣典 - A statement about the unknown changes in celestial phenomena as recorded in sacred texts.
    • 孛彗妖沴，着灾异于图册 - Refers to comets and other celestial anomalies being recorded as signs of calamity in historical records.
    • 麒麟斗，鲸鱼死，靡闻于前经 - Indicates that the events of the mythical creatures fighting and the whale dying are not mentioned in previous scriptures.
    • 求诸正诰，殆将昧焉 - Means 'Seeking in the true edicts, it seems obscure,' suggesting that these events are not clearly documented in authoritative sources.
    • 自稽考群籍 - Means 'After examining various books,' indicating a scholarly approach.
    • 伏羲至于轩辕、少昊、高辛、唐、虞之际 - Refers to the lineage of ancient Chinese rulers from Fuxi to Yao and Shun.
    • 禅业相袭，符表名类，未若尧之盛也 - Describes the succession of rulers and their symbols, noting that none were as great as Yao.
    • 按《易纬》云 - Means 'According to the *Yi Jing* commentary,' introducing another authoritative source.
    • 尧为阳精，叶德干道 - Describes Yao as the embodiment of yang energy and virtue, aligning with the cosmic order.
    • 粤若稽古，是谓上圣 - Means 'Examining ancient times, this is called the supreme sage,' emphasizing Yao's status.
    • 惟天为大，惟尧则之 - A statement about the greatness of heaven and Yao's role in emulating it.
    • 禅业有虞，所谓契叶符同 - Refers to the peaceful transfer of power from Yao to Shun, indicating a harmonious succession.
    • 明象日月 - Means 'Clearly representing the sun and moon,' suggesting a clear and stable governance.
    • 盖其载籍遐旷，算纪绵远 - Describes the vast and long history of these rulers.
    • 德业异纪，神迹各殊 - Indicates that the virtues and miracles of each ruler were distinct.
    • 考传闻于前古，求佥言于中世 - Means 'Examining ancient legends and seeking consensus in the middle ages,' indicating a comprehensive approach to historical research.
    • 而教道参差，祥德递起 - Describes the varying teachings and virtues that emerged over time.
    • 指明群说，能无彷佛 - Means 'Pointing out various explanations, it seems vague,' suggesting that the accounts are not entirely clear.
    • 精灵冥昧，至圣之所不语 - Refers to the mysterious and elusive nature of spiritual phenomena, even beyond the understanding of the wisest.
    • 安以浅末，贬其有无者哉 - Means 'How can we lightly dismiss their existence or non-existence?' questioning the validity of dismissing such phenomena.
    • 刘子政曰 - Means 'Liu Zizheng said,' introducing a quote from a historical figure.
    • 凡传闻不如亲闻，亲闻不如亲见 - A statement about the reliability of information, emphasizing that personal experience is more reliable than hearsay.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 春秋传 - The commentary on the *Spring and Autumn Annals*, a historical text.
    • 淮南子 - A philosophical text attributed to Liu An, the Prince of Huainan.
    • 伏羲、轩辕、少昊、高辛、唐、虞 - Ancient Chinese rulers and deities, including Fuxi, the Yellow Emperor, the Bird Clan, Emperor Ku, Emperor Yao, and Emperor Shun.
    • 禅业 - The practice of abdicating the throne to a worthy successor.
    • 易纬 - The commentary on the *Yi Jing* (Book of Changes).
    • 尧、舜 - Emperor Yao and Emperor Shun, two of the most revered rulers in Chinese history.
    • 刘子政 - Liu Zizheng, a historian and scholar from the Han dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 春秋传 - The commentary on the *Spring and Autumn Annals*.
    • 淮南子 - The philosophical text attributed to Liu An.
    • 伏羲 - Fuxi, the mythical first ruler of China.
    • 轩辕 - The Yellow Emperor, a legendary ruler and cultural hero.
    • 少昊 - The Bird Clan, a legendary ruler.
    • 高辛 - Emperor Ku, a legendary ruler.
    • 唐 - Emperor Yao, a legendary ruler.
    • 虞 - Emperor Shun, a legendary ruler.
    • 易纬 - The commentary on the *Yi Jing* (Book of Changes).
    • 尧 - Emperor Yao, a legendary ruler.
    • 舜 - Emperor Shun, a legendary ruler.
    • 刘子政 - Liu Zizheng, a historian and scholar from the Han dynasty.

    Record states: According to the Spring and Autumn Annals commentary, "Stars fell like rain, yet the night remained bright." The Huainanzi says, "When the qilin fought, the sun and moon eclipsed; when the whale died, a comet appeared." The waxing and waning, eclipses, and other celestial changes are not clearly detailed in the sacred texts. Comets and other celestial anomalies are recorded as signs of calamity in historical records. The qilin fighting and the whale dying are not mentioned in previous scriptures. Seeking in the true edicts, it seems obscure.

    Record further states: After examining various books, from Fuxi to the Yellow Emperor, the Bird Clan, Emperor Ku, and the time of Yao and Shun, the practice of abdicating the throne to a worthy successor was passed down, and their symbols and names were recorded, but none were as great as Yao. According to the Yi Jing commentary, Yao was the embodiment of yang energy and virtue, aligning with the cosmic order. Examining ancient times, this is called the supreme sage. Heaven is the greatest, and Yao emulated it. The peaceful transfer of power to Shun, the harmonious succession, clearly represented the sun and moon. Their records are vast and long, their virtues and miracles distinct. Examining ancient legends and seeking consensus in the middle ages, the teachings varied, and virtues emerged in succession. Pointing out various explanations, it seems vague. The mysterious and elusive nature of spiritual phenomena is beyond the understanding of the wisest. How can we lightly dismiss their existence or non-existence? Liu Zizheng said, "All hearsay is less reliable than personal experience, and personal experience is less reliable than direct observation." [22]

    ”何则？神化欻忽，出隐难常，非肤受之所考算，恒情之所思测。至如龙火鸟水之异，云凤麟虫之属，魍魉百怪之形，欻忽之像，凭风云而自生，因金玉而相化，未详备于夏鼎，信莫记于山经。贯月槎之诞，重明桂实之说，阳燎出于冰木，阴虫生于炎山，易肠倒舌之民，蜕骨龙肉之景，凭风云而托生，含雨露而蠢育，已表怪于众图，方见伟于群记。茫茫遐迩，眇眇流文，百家迂阔，各尚斯异，非守文于一说者矣。 [23]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [23]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 何則 - Rhetorical question meaning 'why is this so?' or 'what then?' used to introduce an explanation.
    • 神化欻忽 - Refers to divine transformations that are sudden and unpredictable.
    • 出隐难常 - Means 'appear and disappear without regularity,' describing the elusive nature of these phenomena.
    • 非肤受之所考算 - Means 'beyond the scope of superficial understanding or calculation,' emphasizing the depth and complexity of these events.
    • 恒情之所思测 - Means 'beyond the reach of ordinary human thought and measurement,' further emphasizing the extraordinary nature of these phenomena.
    • 龙火鸟水之异 - Refers to unusual occurrences involving dragons, fire, birds, and water.
    • 云凤麟虫之属 - Refers to various mythical creatures such as phoenixes, qilins, and insects.
    • 魍魉百怪之形 - Refers to a variety of ghostly and monstrous forms.
    • 欻忽之像 - Means 'sudden and fleeting appearances.'
    • 凭风云而自生 - Means 'arising spontaneously from wind and clouds,' describing the spontaneous nature of these phenomena.
    • 因金玉而相化 - Means 'transforming through gold and jade,' suggesting a transformation influenced by precious materials.
    • 未详备于夏鼎 - Means 'not fully recorded in the bronze vessels of the Xia dynasty,' indicating that these phenomena were not well-documented in ancient records.
    • 信莫记于山经 - Means 'not reliably recorded in the *Shanhaijing* (Classic of Mountains and Seas),' further emphasizing the lack of reliable documentation.
    • 贯月槎之诞 - Refers to the legend of the celestial boat that connects the heavens and earth.
    • 重明桂实之说 - Refers to the story of the double-brightened moon and the fruit of the osmanthus tree.
    • 阳燎出于冰木 - Means 'flames emerging from ice and wood,' describing a paradoxical phenomenon.
    • 阴虫生于炎山 - Means 'insects born in a fiery mountain,' another paradoxical phenomenon.
    • 易肠倒舌之民 - Refers to people with unusual physical characteristics, such as reversed intestines and tongues.
    • 蜕骨龙肉之景 - Refers to scenes of dragons shedding their bones and transforming into flesh.
    • 凭风云而托生 - Means 'arising and depending on wind and clouds,' describing the spontaneous and ethereal nature of these phenomena.
    • 含雨露而蠢育 - Means 'nurtured by rain and dew,' emphasizing the natural and elemental forces involved.
    • 已表怪于众图 - Means 'already depicted in many images,' indicating that these phenomena have been recorded in various forms.
    • 方见伟于群记 - Means 'now seen as remarkable in many records,' suggesting that these phenomena are gaining recognition.
    • 茫茫遐迩 - Means 'vast and far-reaching,' describing the widespread nature of these phenomena.
    • 眇眇流文 - Means 'dim and scattered writings,' referring to the sparse and often obscure records of these phenomena.
    • 百家迂阔 - Refers to the various schools of thought that are often abstract and impractical.
    • 各尚斯异 - Means 'each holds these differences in high regard,' indicating the diversity of opinions and beliefs.
    • 非守文于一说者矣 - Means 'not confined to a single interpretation,' emphasizing the multiplicity of perspectives.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 神化 - Refers to divine transformations, a common theme in Chinese mythology and religious texts.
    • 龙火鸟水之异 - These are specific examples of supernatural phenomena that are often found in Chinese folklore and legends.
    • 云凤麟虫之属 - These are mythical creatures that are often associated with auspicious signs and omens in Chinese culture.
    • 魍魉百怪之形 - These are ghostly and monstrous forms that are often found in Chinese ghost stories and legends.
    • 贯月槎之诞 - This refers to the legend of the celestial boat, which is a significant myth in Chinese astronomy and cosmology.
    • 重明桂实之说 - This refers to the story of the double-brightened moon and the fruit of the osmanthus tree, which is a popular legend in Chinese literature.
    • 阳燎出于冰木 - This describes a paradoxical phenomenon that is often found in Chinese alchemical and philosophical texts.
    • 阴虫生于炎山 - This describes another paradoxical phenomenon that is often found in Chinese alchemical and philosophical texts.
    • 易肠倒舌之民 - This refers to people with unusual physical characteristics, which are often found in Chinese medical and alchemical texts.
    • 蜕骨龙肉之景 - This refers to scenes of dragons shedding their bones and transforming into flesh, which is a common theme in Chinese mythology.
    • 夏鼎 - Refers to the bronze vessels of the Xia dynasty, which are important artifacts in Chinese archaeology and history.
    • 山经 - Refers to the *Shanhaijing* (Classic of Mountains and Seas), an ancient Chinese text that describes various geographical and mythological features.
    • 百家 - Refers to the various schools of thought during the Warring States period, including Confucianism, Daoism, and Legalism.
    • 斯异 - Refers to the diverse and often conflicting interpretations of these phenomena.

    Proper Nouns

    • 夏鼎 - The bronze vessels of the Xia dynasty.
    • 山经 - The *Shanhaijing* (Classic of Mountains and Seas).
    • 贯月槎 - The celestial boat.
    • 重明桂实 - The double-brightened moon and the fruit of the osmanthus tree.
    • 阳燎 - Flames emerging from ice and wood.
    • 阴虫 - Insects born in a fiery mountain.
    • 易肠倒舌之民 - People with unusual physical characteristics.
    • 蜕骨龙肉之景 - Scenes of dragons shedding their bones and transforming into flesh.
    • 百家 - Various schools of thought during the Warring States period.

    Why is this so? Divine transformations are sudden and unpredictable, appearing and disappearing without regularity, beyond the scope of superficial understanding or calculation, and beyond the reach of ordinary human thought and measurement. For example, unusual occurrences involving dragons, fire, birds, and water; mythical creatures such as phoenixes, qilins, and insects; ghostly and monstrous forms; sudden and fleeting appearances—all arise spontaneously from wind and clouds, transforming through gold and jade. These phenomena are not fully recorded in the bronze vessels of the Xia dynasty, nor are they reliably recorded in the Shanhaijing (Classic of Mountains and Seas). The legend of the celestial boat, the story of the double-brightened moon and the fruit of the osmanthus tree, flames emerging from ice and wood, insects born in a fiery mountain, people with unusual physical characteristics, scenes of dragons shedding their bones and transforming into flesh—all arise and depend on wind and clouds, nurtured by rain and dew, already depicted in many images, now seen as remarkable in many records. Vast and far-reaching, dim and scattered writings, various schools of thought that are often abstract and impractical, each holds these differences in high regard, not confined to a single interpretation. [23]

    拾遗记卷二

    夏禹

    　　尧命夏鲧治水，九载无绩。鲧自沉于羽渊，化为玄鱼，时扬须振鳞，横修波之上，见者谓为“河精”。羽渊与河海通源也。海民于羽山之中，修立鲧庙，四时以致祭祀。常见玄鱼与蛟龙跳跃而出，观者惊而畏矣。至舜命禹疏川奠岳，济巨海则鼋鼍而为梁，逾翠岑则神龙而为驭，行遍日月之墟，惟不践羽山之地，皆圣德之感也。鲧之灵化，其事互说，神变犹一，而色状不同。玄鱼黄能，四音相乱，传写流文，“鲧”字或“鱼”边“玄”也。群疑众说，并略记焉。 [24]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [24]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 拾遺記 - A historical text that records unusual or forgotten events and figures.
    • 夏禹 - The legendary founder of the Xia Dynasty and a key figure in Chinese mythology.
    • 堯命夏鯀治水 - A reference to the story of Emperor Yao appointing Yu's father, Gun, to control the floods.
    • 九載無績 - Nine years without success, indicating that Gun failed to achieve his mission.
    • 鯀自沉於羽淵 - Gun drowned himself in the Yu marsh, a dramatic event in the story.
    • 化為玄魚 - Transformed into a black fish, a supernatural transformation.
    • 時揚須振鱗 - Sometimes raising its whiskers and shaking its scales, a vivid description of the fish's movements.
    • 橫修波之上 - Across the long waves, describing the fish's position in the water.
    • 見者謂為“河精” - Those who saw it called it the 'river spirit,' a supernatural interpretation.
    • 羽淵與河海通源也 - The Yu marsh is connected to the rivers and seas, explaining the fish's connection to larger water systems.
    • 海民於羽山之中，修立鯀廟 - The people by the sea built a temple to Gun in the Yu mountains, a religious response to the event.
    • 四時以致祭祀 - Offering sacrifices throughout the four seasons, indicating ongoing veneration.
    • 常見玄魚與蛟龍躍而出 - Often seen with black fish and dragons leaping out, a description of the supernatural activity.
    • 觀者驚而畏矣 - Spectators were amazed and fearful, a reaction to the supernatural events.
    • 至舜命禹疏川奠岳 - When Emperor Shun appointed Yu to clear the rivers and stabilize the mountains, a transition to Yu's role.
    • 濟巨海則鼉而為梁 - Crossing vast seas by using turtles as bridges, a metaphor for Yu's engineering feats.
    • 逾翠岑則神龍而為駕 - Scaling green mountains by riding on divine dragons, another metaphor for Yu's achievements.
    • 行遍日月之墟 - Traveling across the realms of the sun and moon, a grand description of Yu's journeys.
    • 惟不踐羽山之地 - Except for not treading on the land of Yu, a symbolic act of respect.
    • 皆聖德之感也 - All due to the influence of Yu's virtue, explaining the supernatural phenomena.
    • 鯀之靈化，其事互說 - The transformations of Gun are described differently in various accounts, acknowledging the variability in the stories.
    • 神變猶一，而色狀不同 - The transformations are similar, but the appearance differs, a nuanced description of the variations.
    • 玄魚黃能 - Black fish and yellow fish, a reference to the different forms of the transformed Gun.
    • 四音相亂 - The four sounds are confused, a play on the similarity in pronunciation of '玄' (xuan) and '鲧' (gun).
    • 傳寫流文 - In written transmission, the text has flowed, acknowledging the changes in the text over time.
    • “鯀”字或“魚”邊“玄”也 - The character for 'Gun' sometimes has a 'fish' radical and a 'xuan' radical, a note on the character's construction.
    • 群疑眾說 - Various doubts and interpretations, a summary of the different views on the story.
    • 並略記焉 - All are briefly recorded, indicating the comprehensive nature of the account.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 堯 - Emperor Yao, a legendary ruler of ancient China known for his virtue and governance.
    • 夏鯀 - Gun, the father of Yu, a key figure in the legend of flood control.
    • 禹 - Yu, the legendary founder of the Xia Dynasty and a hero in Chinese mythology.
    • 羽淵 - The Yu marsh, a significant location in the story.
    • 蛟龍 - Dragons, often associated with water and supernatural power in Chinese mythology.
    • 舜 - Emperor Shun, another legendary ruler who succeeded Yao and appointed Yu to control the floods.
    • 翠岑 - Green mountains, symbolizing natural beauty and challenges.
    • 日月之墟 - The realms of the sun and moon, representing the vastness of the world.
    • 羽山 - The Yu mountains, a place of significance in the story.
    • 聖德 - Virtue, a central concept in Chinese moral and political thought.
    • 靈化 - Transformations, often associated with supernatural beings in Chinese mythology.
    • 四音相亂 - A reference to the phonetic similarities between characters, highlighting the complexity of the Chinese writing system.
    • 傳寫流文 - The process of written transmission, which can lead to variations in the text.

    Proper Nouns

    • 拾遺記 - Shiyiji, a historical text.
    • 夏禹 - Xia Yu, the legendary founder of the Xia Dynasty.
    • 堯 - Emperor Yao.
    • 夏鯀 - Gun, the father of Yu.
    • 羽淵 - The Yu marsh.
    • 舜 - Emperor Shun.
    • 翠岑 - Green mountains.
    • 羽山 - The Yu mountains.
    • 聖德 - Virtue.
    • 靈化 - Transformations.
    • 四音相亂 - The phonetic similarities between characters.
    • 傳寫流文 - The process of written transmission.
    • “鯀”字或“魚”邊“玄”也 - The character for 'Gun'.
    • 群疑眾說 - Various doubts and interpretations.
    • 並略記焉 - Briefly recorded.

    Shiyiji, Volume 2

    Xia Yu

    Emperor Yao appointed Xia Gun to control the floods, but after nine years, there was no success. Gun drowned himself in the Yu marsh and transformed into a black fish. Sometimes raising its whiskers and shaking its scales, it would leap across the long waves. Those who saw it called it the "river spirit." The Yu marsh is connected to the rivers and seas. The people by the sea built a temple to Gun in the Yu mountains and offered sacrifices throughout the four seasons. Often seen with black fish and dragons leaping out, spectators were amazed and fearful. When Emperor Shun appointed Yu to clear the rivers and stabilize the mountains, he crossed vast seas by using turtles as bridges and scaled green mountains by riding on divine dragons. He traveled across the realms of the sun and moon, except for not treading on the land of Yu, all due to the influence of Yu's virtue. The transformations of Gun are described differently in various accounts, but the transformations are similar, and the appearance differs. Black fish and yellow fish, the four sounds are confused, in written transmission, the text has flowed, the character for "Gun" sometimes has a "fish" radical and a "xuan" radical. Various doubts and interpretations, all are briefly recorded. [24]

    录曰：书契之作，肇迹轩史，道朴风淳，文用尚质。降及唐、虞，爰迄三代，世祀遐绝，载历绵远。列圣通儒，忧乎道缺。故使玉牒金绳之书，虫章鸟篆之记，或秘诸岩薮，藏于屋壁；或逢丧乱，经籍事寝。前史旧章，或流散异域。故字体与俗讹移，其音旨随方互改。历商、周之世，又经嬴、汉，简帛焚裂，遗坟残泯。详其朽蠹之余，采捃传闻之说。是以“己亥”正于前疑，“三豕”析于后谬。子年所述，涉乎万古，与圣叶同，擿文求理，斯言如或可据。《尚书》云：“尧殛鲧于羽山。”《春秋传》曰：“其神化为黄能，以入羽渊。 [25]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [25]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 書契之作 - Refers to the creation of written records or inscriptions, marking the beginning of written communication.
    • 肇迹轩史 - Means 'originating in the records of the Yellow Emperor,' indicating the earliest known records in Chinese history.
    • 道朴风淳 - Describes a time when the Dao (way) was simple and the customs were pure.
    • 文用尚质 - Indicates that writing was valued for its substance and simplicity.
    • 列圣通儒 - Refers to successive wise rulers and scholars.
    • 忧乎道缺 - Expresses concern over the decline of the Dao (way).
    • 玉牒金绳之书 - Refers to precious and sacred texts, often inscribed on jade tablets or gold ropes.
    • 虫章鸟篆之记 - Describes ancient scripts resembling insects and birds, symbolizing early forms of writing.
    • 秘诸岩薮 - Means 'hidden in remote mountains and forests.'
    • 藏于屋壁 - Means 'hidden in the walls of houses.'
    • 逢丧乱 - Refers to encountering times of chaos and disorder.
    • 经籍事寝 - Means 'the affairs of books and learning were put aside.'
    • 前史旧章 - Refers to the records and traditions of the past.
    • 流散异域 - Means 'scattered to foreign lands.'
    • 字体与俗讹移 - Describes how the forms of characters changed with the times.
    • 其音旨随方互改 - Indicates that the pronunciation and meaning of words varied by region.
    • 历商、周之世 - Refers to the periods of the Shang and Zhou dynasties.
    • 嬴、汉 - Refers to the Qin and Han dynasties.
    • 简帛焚裂 - Means 'bamboo slips and silk books were burned and torn.'
    • 遗坟残泯 - Describes how ancient burial sites and texts were destroyed.
    • 详其朽蠹之余 - Means 'examining the remains of decayed texts.'
    • 采捃传闻之说 - Refers to gathering hearsay and oral traditions.
    • 己亥正于前疑 - A reference to the correction of doubts about the character '己亥.'
    • 三豕析于后谬 - A reference to the correction of errors regarding the character '三豕.'
    • 子年所述 - Refers to the account given in the *Zi Nian* (年表) or chronicles.
    • 与圣叶同 - Means 'in agreement with the sages.'
    • 擿文求理 - Refers to analyzing texts to understand their underlying principles.
    • 斯言如或可据 - Means 'this statement can be relied upon if it is true.'
    • 尧殛鲧于羽山 - Refers to the story of Emperor Yao exiling Gun to Yu Mountain.
    • 其神化为黄能，以入羽渊 - Describes how Gun's spirit transformed into a yellow dragon and entered Yu Lake.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 轩史 - Refers to the records of the Yellow Emperor, one of the legendary founders of Chinese civilization.
    • 尧殛鲧于羽山 - A historical event from the *Shangshu* (Book of Documents) where Emperor Yao exiles his son Gun for failing to control floods.
    • 黄能 - A mythical creature, often interpreted as a yellow dragon.
    • 羽渊 - Refers to Yu Lake, a significant body of water in ancient Chinese mythology.
    • 三豕 - A reference to the character '三' (three) and '豕' (pig), used in the context of textual corrections.

    Proper Nouns

    • 轩史 - The records of the Yellow Emperor.
    • 尧 - Emperor Yao, a legendary ruler of ancient China.
    • 鲧 - Gun, the son of Emperor Yao, who was exiled for failing to control floods.
    • 羽山 - Yu Mountain, the place where Gun was exiled.
    • 黄能 - A mythical creature, often interpreted as a yellow dragon.
    • 羽渊 - Yu Lake, a significant body of water in ancient Chinese mythology.
    • 三豕 - A reference to the character '三' (three) and '豕' (pig), used in the context of textual corrections.
    • 子年 - The *Zi Nian* (年表) or chronicles, a historical record.

    The Record states: The creation of written records began in the annals of the Yellow Emperor, when the Dao was simple and the customs were pure, and writing was valued for its substance. Descending to the era of Tang and Yu, and continuing through the Three Dynasties, the world's rituals and sacrifices have long been forgotten, and the records span a vast expanse of time. Successive wise rulers and scholars have been concerned about the decline of the Dao. Therefore, precious texts inscribed on jade tablets and gold ropes, and ancient scripts resembling insects and birds, have been hidden in remote mountains and forests, or concealed within the walls of houses; some have been lost in times of chaos and disorder, and the affairs of books and learning have been put aside. The records and traditions of the past have been scattered to foreign lands. Thus, the forms of characters have changed with the times, and the pronunciation and meaning of words vary by region. Through the periods of the Shang and Zhou dynasties, and again during the Qin and Han dynasties, bamboo slips and silk books were burned and torn, and ancient burial sites and texts were destroyed. Examining the remains of decayed texts and gathering hearsay and oral traditions, it is said that the character "己亥" was corrected in earlier doubts, and the character "三豕" was clarified in later errors. The Zi Nian chronicles, spanning a thousand years, are in agreement with the sages, and by analyzing texts to understand their underlying principles, this statement can be relied upon if it is true. The Shangshu (Book of Documents) states: "Emperor Yao exiled Gun to Yu Mountain." The Spring and Autumn Annals commentary says: "His spirit transformed into a yellow dragon and entered Yu Lake." [25]

    ”是在山变为能，入水化为鱼也。兽之依山，鱼之附水，各因其性而变化焉。详之正典，爰访杂说，若真若似，并略录焉。禹铸九鼎，五者以应阳法，四者以象阴数。使工师以雌金为阴鼎，以雄金为阳鼎。鼎中常满，以占气象之休否。当夏桀之世，鼎水忽沸。及周将末，九鼎咸震：皆应灭亡之兆。后世圣人，因禹之迹，代代铸鼎焉。禹尽力沟洫，导川夷岳。黄龙曳尾于前，玄龟负青泥于后。玄龟，河精之使者也。龟颔下有印，文皆古篆，字作九州岛山川之字。禹所穿凿之处，皆以青泥封记其所，使玄龟印其上。今人聚土为界，此之遗像也。 [26]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [26]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 是在山變為能，入水化為魚也 - Classical structure of transformation, where '能' (neng) can refer to a mythical creature or a general ability, and '化為魚' (hua wei yu) means 'transform into a fish.'
    • 獸之依山，魚之附水 - Classical structure of metaphor, where '依山' (yi shan) means 'rely on the mountain' and '附水' (fu shui) means 'attach to the water,' symbolizing the natural habitat of animals and fish.
    • 各因其性而變化焉 - Classical structure of causality, meaning 'each changes according to its nature.'
    • 詳之正典，爰訪雜說 - Classical structure of sequential actions, meaning 'examine the canonical texts, and then consult various sayings.'
    • 若真若似，並略錄焉 - Classical structure of inclusiveness, meaning 'whether true or similar, all are briefly recorded.'
    • 禹铸九鼎，五者以應陽法，四者以象阴数 - Describes the casting of the Nine Tripods, where '應陽法' (ying yang fa) means 'correspond to the yang principle' and '象阴数' (xiang yin shu) means 'represent the yin number.'
    • 使工师以雌金为阴鼎，以雄金为阳鼎 - Classical structure of assignment, meaning 'let the master craftsman use female metal for the yin tripod and male metal for the yang tripod.'
    • 鼎中常满，以占气象之休否 - Classical structure of function, meaning 'the tripod always remains full, used to predict the auspiciousness or inauspiciousness of the weather.'
    • 当夏桀之世，鼎水忽沸 - Classical structure of historical context, meaning 'during the reign of King Jie of Xia, the water in the tripod suddenly boiled.'
    • 及周将末，九鼎咸震 - Classical structure of historical context, meaning 'by the end of the Zhou dynasty, all nine tripods trembled.'
    • 皆应灭亡之兆 - Classical structure of consequence, meaning 'all were signs of impending doom.'
    • 后世圣人，因禹之迹，代代铸鼎焉 - Classical structure of continuation, meaning 'later sages, following Yu's example, cast tripods generation after generation.'
    • 禹尽力沟洫，导川夷岳 - Classical structure of action, meaning 'Yu made great efforts to dig canals and divert rivers, leveling mountains.'
    • 黄龙曳尾于前，玄龟负青泥于后 - Classical structure of mythological imagery, meaning 'a yellow dragon trailed its tail in front, and a black turtle carried green mud behind.'
    • 玄龟，河精之使者也 - Classical structure of attribution, meaning 'the black turtle is the messenger of the river spirit.'
    • 龟颔下有印，文皆古篆，字作九州岛山川之字 - Classical structure of description, meaning 'the turtle's neck has a seal, the characters are all ancient seals, and the words are the names of the nine provinces and mountains and rivers.'
    • 禹所穿凿之处，皆以青泥封记其所，使玄龟印其上 - Classical structure of action, meaning 'wherever Yu excavated, he sealed it with green mud, and the black turtle stamped it.'
    • 今人聚土为界，此之遗像也 - Classical structure of legacy, meaning 'today, people gather earth to mark boundaries, which is a remnant of this practice.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 禹 - Refers to Yu the Great, a legendary figure in Chinese history known for controlling floods.
    • 九鼎 - Refers to the Nine Tripods, symbolic artifacts of power and authority in ancient China.
    • 夏桀 - Refers to King Jie of Xia, the last ruler of the Xia dynasty, known for his tyranny.
    • 周 - Refers to the Zhou dynasty, one of the most significant dynasties in Chinese history.
    • 黄龙 - Refers to a yellow dragon, a mythical creature often associated with the emperor and imperial power.
    • 玄龟 - Refers to a black turtle, a symbol of longevity and wisdom in Chinese culture.
    • 河精 - Refers to the river spirit, a deity associated with water and fertility.
    • 九州岛 - Refers to the nine provinces of ancient China, a concept used to describe the entire country.

    Proper Nouns

    • 禹 - Yu the Great
    • 九鼎 - Nine Tripods
    • 夏桀 - King Jie of Xia
    • 周 - Zhou dynasty
    • 黄龙 - Yellow Dragon
    • 玄龟 - Black Turtle
    • 河精 - River Spirit
    • 九州岛 - Nine Provinces

    In the mountains, one transforms into a mythical creature, and upon entering the water, one changes into a fish. Animals rely on the mountains, and fish depend on the water, each changing according to its nature. Examining the canonical texts and consulting various sayings, whether true or similar, all are briefly recorded. Yu the Great cast the Nine Tripods, five of which corresponded to the yang principle, and four represented the yin number. He instructed the master craftsman to use female metal for the yin tripod and male metal for the yang tripod. The tripod always remained full, used to predict the auspiciousness or inauspiciousness of the weather. During the reign of King Jie of Xia, the water in the tripod suddenly boiled. By the end of the Zhou dynasty, all nine tripods trembled—these were all signs of impending doom. Later sages, following Yu's example, cast tripods generation after generation. Yu made great efforts to dig canals and divert rivers, leveling mountains. A yellow dragon trailed its tail in front, and a black turtle carried green mud behind. The black turtle is the messenger of the river spirit. The turtle's neck has a seal, the characters are all ancient seals, and the words are the names of the nine provinces and mountains and rivers. Wherever Yu excavated, he sealed it with green mud, and the black turtle stamped it. Today, people gather earth to mark boundaries, which is a remnant of this practice. [26]

    禹凿龙关之山，亦谓之龙门。至一空岩，深数十里，幽暗不可复行，禹乃负火而进。有兽状如豕，衔夜明之珠，其光如烛。又有青犬，行吠于前。禹计可十里，迷于昼夜。既觉渐明，见向来豕犬变为人形，皆着玄衣。又见一神，蛇身人面。禹因与语，神即示禹八卦之图，列于金版之上。又有八神侍侧。禹曰：“华胥生圣子，是汝耶？”答曰：“华胥是九河神女，以生余也。”乃探玉简授禹，长一尺二寸，以合十二时之数，使量度天地。禹即执持此简，以平定水土。蛇身之神，即羲皇也。 [27]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [27]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 禹凿龙关之山，亦谓之龙门 - Historical reference to Yu carving the Dragon Gate mountain, also known as Longmen.
    • 至一空岩，深数十里，幽暗不可复行 - Describes a vast cave, deep for dozens of miles, dark and impossible to continue, emphasizing the difficulty of the journey.
    • 禹乃负火而进 - Shows Yu's determination and resourcefulness in carrying fire and proceeding.
    • 有兽状如豕，衔夜明之珠，其光如烛 - Mythological creature, symbolizing guidance and enlightenment, with a luminous pearl like a candle.
    • 又有青犬，行吠于前 - Suggests a protective or guiding presence with a blue dog barking ahead.
    • 禹计可十里，迷于昼夜 - Indicates the disorienting nature of the journey, with Yu calculating it was about ten miles but being confused by day and night.
    • 既觉渐明，见向来豕犬变为人形，皆着玄衣 - Transformation suggests a divine or supernatural encounter, with the pig and dog transforming into human form, all wearing black clothes.
    • 又见一神，蛇身人面 - Description of a deity, often associated with wisdom and transformation, with a snake body and a human face.
    • 禹因与语，神即示禹八卦之图，列于金版之上 - Interaction with the deity and the presentation of the *I Ching* diagram, showing the diagram of the Eight Trigrams arranged on a golden tablet.
    • 又有八神侍侧 - Signifies a grand and divine presence with eight gods attending.
