    • 禹曰：“华胥生圣子，是汝耶？” - Reference to the mythological figure Huaxu, asking if the Holy Son born of Huaxu is the speaker.
    • 答曰：“华胥是九河神女，以生余也。” - Confirms the divine origin of the speaker, stating that Huaxu is the goddess of the Nine Rivers who gave birth to the speaker.
    • 乃探玉简授禹，长一尺二寸，以合十二时之数，使量度天地 - Symbolic gesture, with the jade tablet representing authority and knowledge, one foot two inches long to correspond to the twelve hours, to measure the heavens and earth.
    • 禹即执持此简，以平定水土 - Practical application of the divine gift, with Yu holding the tablet and using it to level the waters and earth.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 禹 - Legendary figure Yu, known for his efforts to control the floods and establish the Xia Dynasty.
    • 龙关之山，龙门 - Dragon Gate mountain, a significant location in Chinese mythology, often associated with transformation and passage.
    • 华胥 - Mythological figure Huaxu, often associated with the origin of Chinese civilization and the birth of Fuxi.
    • 八卦之图 - Diagram of the Eight Trigrams, a fundamental symbol in the *I Ching* (Book of Changes), representing the principles of the universe.
    • 玉简 - Jade tablet, often used in ancient Chinese culture as a symbol of authority and knowledge.

    Proper Nouns

    • 禹 - Yu, the legendary figure.
    • 龙关之山，龙门 - Dragon Gate mountain, a significant location in Chinese mythology.
    • 华胥 - Huaxu, the mythological figure.
    • 八卦之图 - Diagram of the Eight Trigrams, a fundamental symbol in the *I Ching* (Book of Changes).
    • 玉简 - Jade tablet, a symbol of authority and knowledge.

    Yu carved the Dragon Gate mountain, also known as Longmen. He reached a vast cave, deep for dozens of miles, dark and impossible to continue. Yu then carried fire and proceeded. There was a beast like a pig, carrying a luminous pearl, its light like a candle. There was also a blue dog, barking ahead. Yu calculated it was about ten miles, but was confused by day and night. When he became aware and it started to brighten, he saw the pig and dog had transformed into human form, all wearing black clothes. He also saw a god, with a snake body and a human face. Yu then spoke with the god, who showed Yu the diagram of the Eight Trigrams, arranged on a golden tablet. There were also eight gods attending. Yu said, "Was the Holy Son born of Huaxu, are you?" The god replied, "Huaxu is the goddess of the Nine Rivers, who gave birth to me." He then took out a jade tablet and gave it to Yu, one foot two inches long, to correspond to the twelve hours, to measure the heavens and earth. Yu then held this tablet and used it to level the waters and earth. The god with the snake body was Fuxi, the legendary ruler. [27]

    录曰：夫神迹难求，幽暗罔辨，希夷彷佛之间，闻见以之衒惑。若测诸冥理，先坟有所指明。是以彭生假见于贝丘，赵王示形于苍犬，皆文备鲁册，验表齐、汉。远古旷代，事异神同。衔珠吐烛之怪，精灵一其均矣。若夫茫茫禹迹，杳漠神源，非末俗所能推辨矣。观伏羲至于夏禹，岁历悠旷，载祀绵邈，故能与日月共辉，阴阳齐契。万代百王，情异迹至，参机会道，视万龄如旦暮，促累劫于寸阴。何嗟鬼神之可已，而疑羲、禹之相遇乎！

    殷汤

    　　商之始也，有神女简狄，游于桑野，见黑鸟遗卵于地，有五色文，作“八百”字，简狄拾之，贮以玉筐，覆以朱绂。夜梦神母，谓之曰：“尔怀此卵，即生圣子，以继金德。”狄乃怀卵，一年而有娠，经十四月而生契。祚以八百，叶卵之文也。虽遭旱厄，后嗣兴焉。 [28]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [28]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 夫神迹难求，幽暗罔辨 - The traces of the divine are hard to seek, and the obscure are hard to discern.
    • 希夷彷佛之间 - Between the vague and the dim.
    • 闻见以之衒惑 - What is heard and seen is used to deceive.
    • 若测诸冥理，先坟有所指明 - If we measure it by the principles of the unseen, the ancient texts provide some indication.
    • 彭生假见于贝丘，赵王示形于苍犬 - Peng Sheng appeared to people in Bei Qi, and the King of Zhao showed his form as a black dog.
    • 衔珠吐烛之怪 - The strange occurrences of holding pearls and spitting out candles.
    • 茫茫禹迹，杳漠神源 - The vast traces of Yu, the dim origins of the divine.
    • 祚以八百，叶卵之文也 - The legacy of eight hundred years, matching the text of the egg.
    • 虽遭旱厄，后嗣兴焉 - Even though it faced drought and misfortune, the descendants prospered.
    • 与日月共辉，阴阳齐契 - Sharing the brilliance of the sun and moon, aligning with the yin and yang.
    • 万代百王，情异迹至 - Through countless generations and hundred kings, their feelings were different but their paths converged.
    • 参机会道，视万龄如旦暮，促累劫于寸阴 - Participating in the timing and path, seeing ten thousand years as morning and evening, compressing countless eons into an inch of time.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 伏羲至于夏禹 - From Fuxi to the Xia Dynasty's Yu.
    • 岁历悠旷，载祀绵邈 - Years have passed long and vast, generations have been numerous and far-reaching.
    • 殷汤 - King Tang of Shang.
    • 有神女简狄，游于桑野 - There was a divine woman named Jian Di, who wandered in the mulberry fields.
    • 见黑鸟遗卵于地，有五色文，作“八百”字 - She saw a black bird leave an egg on the ground, with five-colored patterns, forming the word 'eight hundred'.
    • 简狄拾之，贮以玉筐，覆以朱绂 - Jian Di picked it up, stored it in a jade box, and covered it with a red ribbon.
    • 夜梦神母，谓之曰 - At night, she dreamed of a divine mother, who said to her...
    • 尔怀此卵，即生圣子，以继金德 - If you carry this egg, you will give birth to a holy child, to continue the golden virtue.
    • 经十四月而生契 - After fourteen months, she gave birth to Qi.
    • 贝丘 - Bei Qi, a place where Peng Sheng appeared.
    • 赵王 - The King of Zhao, a historical figure.
    • 彭生 - Peng Sheng, a mythological figure.
    • 契 - Qi, a historical figure and son of Jian Di.
    • 八百 - Eight hundred, a symbolic number often associated with longevity and prosperity in Chinese culture.

    Proper Nouns

    • 伏羲 - Fuxi, a legendary figure in Chinese mythology.
    • 夏禹 - Yu, the legendary ruler of the Xia Dynasty.
    • 简狄 - Jian Di, a mythological figure.
    • 赵王 - The King of Zhao, a historical figure.
    • 彭生 - Peng Sheng, a mythological figure.
    • 契 - Qi, a historical figure and son of Jian Di.

    The Records state: The traces of the divine are hard to seek, and the obscure are hard to discern. Between the vague and the dim, what is heard and seen is used to deceive. If we measure it by the principles of the unseen, the ancient texts provide some indication. Thus, Peng Sheng appeared to people in Bei Qi, and the King of Zhao showed his form as a black dog. All these events are recorded in the annals of Lu, serving as evidence for the states of Qi and Han. In remote antiquity, the events may differ, but the divine essence remains the same. The strange occurrences of holding pearls and spitting out candles, the spirits are equally remarkable. As for the vast traces of Yu, the dim origins of the divine, these are beyond the comprehension of the common people. From Fuxi to the Xia Dynasty's Yu, years have passed long and vast, generations have been numerous and far-reaching. Hence, they share the brilliance of the sun and moon, aligning with the yin and yang. Through countless generations and hundred kings, their feelings were different but their paths converged. Participating in the timing and path, seeing ten thousand years as morning and evening, compressing countless eons into an inch of time. Why lament the possibility of ghosts and spirits, and doubt the meeting of Fuxi and Yu?

    King Tang of Shang

    At the beginning of the Shang Dynasty, there was a divine woman named Jian Di, who wandered in the mulberry fields. She saw a black bird leave an egg on the ground, with five-colored patterns, forming the word 'eight hundred.' Jian Di picked it up, stored it in a jade box, and covered it with a red ribbon. At night, she dreamed of a divine mother, who said to her, "If you carry this egg, you will give birth to a holy child, to continue the golden virtue." Jian Di then carried the egg, and after one year, she became pregnant. After fourteen months, she gave birth to Qi. The legacy of eight hundred years, matching the text of the egg. Even though it faced drought and misfortune, the descendants prospered. [28]

    傅说赁为赭衣者，舂于深岩以自给。梦乘云绕日而行，筮得“利建侯”之卦。岁余，汤以玉帛聘为阿衡也。
    　　纣之昏乱，欲讨诸侯，使飞廉、恶来诛戮贤良，取其宝器，埋于琼台之下。使飞廉等惑所近之国，侯服之内，使烽燧相续。纣登台以望火之所在，乃兴师往伐其国，杀其君，囚其民，收其女乐，肆其淫虐。神人愤怨。时时有朱鸟衔火，如星之照耀，以乱烽燧之光。纣乃回惑，使诸国灭其烽燧。于是亿兆夷民乃欢，万国已静。及武伐纣，樵夫牧竖探高鸟之巢，得玉玺，文曰：“水德将灭，木祚方盛。”文皆大篆，纪殷之世历已尽，而姬之圣德方隆。是以三分天下而其二归周。故蚩蚩之类，嗟殷亡之晚，望周来之迟也。 [29]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [29]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 傅說赁为赭衣者，舂于深岩以自给 - Describes Fu Yue working as a laborer, grinding grain in a deep quarry to support himself.
    • 梦乘云绕日而行 - A dream where one rides on clouds circling the sun, symbolizing a vision of greatness or divine favor.
    • 筮得“利建侯”之卦 - Refers to divination yielding a favorable hexagram for establishing a feudal lord.
    • 岁余，汤以玉帛聘为阿衡也 - After a year, Tang of Shang invited Fu Yue with gifts of jade and silk to serve as his prime minister.
    • 纣之昏乱 - Describes King Zhou's moral corruption and misrule.
    • 欲讨诸侯 - Intends to attack or subdue the feudal lords.
    • 使飞廉、恶来诛戮贤良 - Orders Feilian and Erya to kill virtuous officials.
    • 取其宝器，埋于琼台之下 - Seizes their treasures and buries them under the Qiongtai Terrace.
    • 使飞廉等惑所近之国，侯服之内，使烽燧相续 - Orders Feilian to deceive nearby states within the feudal system, causing continuous alarms.
    • 纣登台以望火之所在，乃兴师往伐其国 - King Zhou ascends the terrace to see where the alarms are coming from and then sends troops to attack the country.
    • 杀其君，囚其民，收其女乐，肆其淫虐 - Kills the ruler, imprisons the people, takes their women as concubines, and indulges in excessive cruelty.
    • 神人愤怨 - Gods and people are filled with anger.
    • 时时有朱鸟衔火，如星之照耀，以乱烽燧之光 - Red birds carrying fire appear frequently, like stars illuminating the sky, confusing the alarm signals.
    • 纣乃回惑，使诸国灭其烽燧 - King Zhou becomes confused and orders the states to extinguish their alarms.
    • 于是亿兆夷民乃欢，万国已静 - The common people are relieved, and all the states become peaceful.
    • 及武伐纣，樵夫牧竖探高鸟之巢，得玉玺，文曰：“水德将灭，木祚方盛” - When Wu attacked King Zhou, a woodsman and a shepherd found a jade seal in a high bird's nest, inscribed with the text: 'The water element is about to decline, while the wood element is about to flourish.'
    • 文皆大篆，纪殷之世历已尽，而姬之圣德方隆 - The text is written in large seal script, indicating that the Shang dynasty's reign has ended, and the virtue of the Zhou dynasty is rising.
    • 是以三分天下而其二归周 - Therefore, two-thirds of the world's territories belong to the Zhou.
    • 故蚩蚩之类，嗟殷亡之晚，望周来之迟也 - Thus, the common people lament the late fall of the Shang and wish for the Zhou to come sooner.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 傅說 - A historical figure known for his rise from a lowly position to become a prime minister under King Tang of Shang.
    • 湯 - King Tang of Shang, the founder of the Shang dynasty.
    • 阿衡 - The title of prime minister or high minister in ancient China.
    • 紂 - King Zhou of Shang, the last ruler of the Shang dynasty, known for his tyrannical rule.
    • 飛廉、惡來 - Advisors to King Zhou, known for their cruelty and treachery.
    • 瓊台 - A legendary palace or terrace in ancient China, often associated with luxury and decadence.
    • 烽燧 - Beacon fires used in ancient China to signal military alerts.
    • 武 - Refers to King Wu of Zhou, who overthrew the Shang dynasty.
    • 水德、木祚 - Refers to the Five Elements theory in Chinese cosmology, where water represents the Shang dynasty and wood represents the Zhou dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 傅說 - A historical figure who rose from a lowly position to become a prime minister.
    • 湯 - King Tang of Shang, the founder of the Shang dynasty.
    • 阿衡 - The title of prime minister or high minister.
    • 紂 - King Zhou of Shang, the last ruler of the Shang dynasty.
    • 飛廉、惡來 - Advisors to King Zhou known for their cruelty.
    • 瓊台 - A legendary palace or terrace.
    • 武 - King Wu of Zhou, who overthrew the Shang dynasty.
    • 水德、木祚 - Refers to the Five Elements theory in Chinese cosmology.

    Fu Yue, leasing himself as a laborer, ground grain in a deep quarry to support himself. He dreamed of riding on clouds circling the sun and divined a favorable hexagram for establishing a feudal lord. After a year, Tang of Shang invited him with gifts of jade and silk to serve as his prime minister.

    King Zhou's moral corruption led him to intend to attack the feudal lords. He ordered Feilian and Erya to kill virtuous officials, seize their treasures, and bury them under the Qiongtai Terrace. He also ordered Feilian to deceive nearby states within the feudal system, causing continuous alarms. King Zhou ascended the terrace to see where the alarms were coming from and then sent troops to attack the country, killing the ruler, imprisoning the people, taking their women as concubines, and indulging in excessive cruelty. Gods and people were filled with anger. Red birds carrying fire appeared frequently, like stars illuminating the sky, confusing the alarm signals. King Zhou became confused and ordered the states to extinguish their alarms. The common people were relieved, and all the states became peaceful.

    When Wu attacked King Zhou, a woodsman and a shepherd found a jade seal in a high bird's nest, inscribed with the text: "The water element is about to decline, while the wood element is about to flourish." The text was written in large seal script, indicating that the Shang dynasty's reign had ended, and the virtue of the Zhou dynasty was rising. Therefore, two-thirds of the world's territories belonged to the Zhou. Thus, the common people lamented the late fall of the Shang and wished for the Zhou to come sooner. [29]

    师延者，殷之乐人也。设乐以来，世遵此职。至师延，精述阴阳，晓明象纬，莫测其为人。世载辽绝，而或出或隐。在轩辕之世，为司乐之官。及殷时，总修三皇五帝之乐。拊一弦琴则地祇皆升，吹玉律则天神俱降。当轩辕之时，年已数百岁，听众国乐声，以审兴亡之兆。至夏末，抱乐器以奔殷。而纣淫于声色，乃拘师延于阴宫，欲极刑戮。师延既被囚系，奏清商、流征、涤角之音。司狱者以闻于纣，纣犹嫌曰：“此乃淳古远乐，非余可听说也。”犹不释。师延乃更奏迷魂淫魄之曲，以欢修夜之娱，乃得免炮烙之害。周武王兴师，乃越濮流而逝，或云死于水府。故晋、卫之人，镌石铸金以像其形，立祀不绝矣。 [30]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [30]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 設樂以來 - Since the establishment of music, indicating the long-standing tradition of the role.
    • 世遵此職 - Continuously following this role through generations.
    • 精述陰陽 - Proficiently explaining the principles of yin and yang.
    • 曉明象緯 - Clearly understanding celestial phenomena.
    • 莫測其為人 - His true nature is unfathomable.
    • 世載遼絕 - The lineage is distant and broken.
    • 或出或隱 - Sometimes appears, sometimes disappears.
    • 為司樂之官 - Serving as the official in charge of music.
    • 總修三皇五帝之樂 - Comprehensive revision of the music of the Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors.
    • 拊一弦琴則地祇皆升 - When he plays a single-stringed zither, all earth deities ascend.
    • 吹玉律則天神俱降 - When he blows the jade flute, all heavenly gods descend.
    • 聽眾國樂聲 - Listening to the music of various countries.
    • 以審興亡之兆 - To discern the signs of prosperity and decline.
    • 抱樂器以奔殷 - Carrying musical instruments to flee to the state of Yin (Shang Dynasty).
    • 拘師延於陰宮 - Imprisoning Shiyang in a secret palace.
    • 欲極刑戮 - Intending to inflict the most severe punishment.
    • 奏清商、流徵、滌角之音 - Playing the pure shang, flowing zheng, and cleansing jiao tones.
    • 司獄者以聞於紂 - The prison official reported this to King Zhou.
    • 猶嫌曰 - Still found it unsatisfactory and said.
    • 淳古遠樂 - Ancient and distant music.
    • 非余可聽說也 - Not something I can enjoy.
    • 更奏迷魂淫魄之曲 - Played more songs that bewitch the soul and enchant the spirit.
    • 以歡修夜之娛 - To entertain and prolong the night.
    • 免炮烙之害 - Escape the punishment of the hot iron pillar.
    • 越濮流而逝 - Crossed the Pu River and passed away.
    • 或雲死於水府 - Some say he died in the underwater realm.
    • 鑿石鑄金以像其形 - Carved stone and cast metal to create his image.
    • 立祀不絕矣 - Established worship that continues without interruption.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 師延 - A legendary musician from the Shang Dynasty.
    • 殷 - The Shang Dynasty, one of the earliest Chinese dynasties.
    • 軒轅 - The legendary Yellow Emperor, a key figure in Chinese mythology.
    • 夏末 - The end of the Xia Dynasty, another early Chinese dynasty.
    • 紂 - King Zhou of Shang, known for his tyranny and the fall of the Shang Dynasty.
    • 周武王 - King Wu of Zhou, who overthrew the Shang Dynasty and established the Zhou Dynasty.
    • 濮流 - The Pu River, a river in ancient China.
    • 水府 - The underwater realm, often associated with the afterlife in Chinese mythology.
    • 晉、衛 - The states of Jin and Wei, both located in northern China during the Zhou Dynasty.
    • 鑿石鑄金 - Carving stone and casting metal, traditional methods of creating statues and monuments.
    • 立祀 - Establishing a shrine or temple for worship.

    Proper Nouns

    • 師延 - The name of the legendary musician.
    • 殷 - The Shang Dynasty.
    • 軒轅 - The Yellow Emperor.
    • 紂 - King Zhou of Shang.
    • 周武王 - King Wu of Zhou.
    • 濮流 - The Pu River.
    • 水府 - The underwater realm.
    • 晉 - The state of Jin.
    • 衛 - The state of Wei.

    Shiyang was a musician of the Shang Dynasty. Since the establishment of music, this role has been continuously followed through generations. When it came to Shiyang, he was proficient in explaining the principles of yin and yang and clearly understood celestial phenomena. His true nature was unfathomable. The lineage of musicians was distant and broken, with some appearing and others disappearing. During the time of the Yellow Emperor, he served as the official in charge of music. By the time of the Shang Dynasty, he comprehensively revised the music of the Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors. When he played a single-stringed zither, all earth deities ascended; when he blew the jade flute, all heavenly gods descended. During the time of the Yellow Emperor, he was already several hundred years old, listening to the music of various countries to discern the signs of prosperity and decline. At the end of the Xia Dynasty, he carried musical instruments to flee to the state of Yin (Shang Dynasty). King Zhou of Shang was indulgent in music and beauty, and he imprisoned Shiyang in a secret palace, intending to inflict the most severe punishment. Shiyang, once imprisoned, played the pure shang, flowing zheng, and cleansing jiao tones. The prison official reported this to King Zhou, who still found it unsatisfactory and said, "This is ancient and distant music, not something I can enjoy." Despite this, he did not release Shiyang. Shiyang then played more songs that bewitched the soul and enchanted the spirit, to entertain and prolong the night, thereby escaping the punishment of the hot iron pillar. When King Wu of Zhou rose to power, Shiyang crossed the Pu River and passed away; some say he died in the underwater realm. Therefore, people of the states of Jin and Wei carved stone and cast metal to create his image, and established worship that continues without interruption. [30]

    录曰：《三坟》、《五典》及诸纬候杂说，皆言简狄吞燕卵而生契。《诗》云：“天命玄鸟，降而生商。”斯文正矣。此说怀感而生，众言各异，故记其殊别也。傅说去其舂筑，释彼佣赁，应翘旌而来相，可谓知几其神矣。同磻溪之归周，异殷相之负鼎，龙蛇遇命，道会则通。斯则往贤之明教，通人之至规。“乐天知命”，信之经言也。死且不朽，是谓名也。乌无声誉于后裔，扬风烈于万祀。譬诸金玉，烟埃不能埋其坚贞；比之泾、濮，淄、渭，不能混其澄澈。师延当纣之虐，矫步求存，因权取济，观时徇主，全身获免。所谓困而能通，卒以智免。故影被丹青，形刊金石，爱其和乐之功，贵其神迹之远矣。至如越思计然之利，镌金以旌其德，方斯蔑矣。 [31]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [31]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 三坟 - Ancient texts or collections of wisdom, often associated with the origins of Chinese civilization.
    • 五典 - Ancient texts or collections of wisdom, often associated with the origins of Chinese civilization.
    • 纬候杂说 - Miscellaneous esoteric or astrological writings, often considered less authoritative than canonical texts.
    • 天命玄鸟，降而生商 - A poetic expression from the *Book of Songs* (*Shijing*) that refers to the mythological origin of the Shang dynasty, where the god sent a swallow to bring about the birth of the founder.
    • 怀感而生 - A phrase describing the concept of conception through divine inspiration or supernatural means.
    • 翘旌而来相 - A metaphorical expression meaning to be summoned by a sign or omen, often associated with a higher power or destiny.
    • 磻溪之归周 - Refers to the story of Jiang Ziya (姜子牙), who was a fisherman before becoming a key advisor to King Wen of Zhou.
    • 殷相之负鼎 - Refers to Yi Yin (伊尹), who was a cook before becoming a prime minister under the Shang dynasty.
    • 龙蛇遇命，道会则通 - A metaphorical expression meaning that when the right time comes, even the most unlikely or hidden paths will lead to success.
    • 往贤之明教，通人之至规 - A phrase praising the wisdom of past sages and the highest principles of learned individuals.
    • 乐天知命 - A Confucian and Daoist concept meaning to accept one's fate with joy and understanding.
    • 死且不朽，是谓名也 - A statement that true fame or legacy is eternal, even after death.
    • 乌无声誉于后裔，扬风烈于万祀 - A metaphor comparing someone's influence to a bird that leaves no trace but still spreads its wings far and wide, emphasizing the lasting impact of one's actions.
    • 譬诸金玉，烟埃不能埋其坚贞；比之泾、濮，淄、渭，不能混其澄澈 - A metaphor comparing a person's integrity to precious metals or stones, which cannot be buried by dust, or to clear rivers, which cannot be polluted by muddy waters.
    • 困而能通，卒以智免 - A phrase meaning that even in difficult circumstances, one can find a way out through wisdom and adaptability.
    • 影被丹青，形刊金石 - A metaphor for enduring fame, where one's image is preserved in paintings and inscriptions on stone.
    • 方斯蔑矣 - A phrase meaning 'far less significant' or 'pale in comparison.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 简狄 - A legendary figure from the Shang dynasty who is said to have conceived the founder of the Shang dynasty through divine intervention.
    • 契 - The founder of the Shang dynasty, according to legend.
    • 傅说 - A legendary figure from the Shang dynasty who became a statesman and advisor.
    • 计然 - A famous strategist and economic advisor in ancient China, known for his advice on trade and wealth.
    • 师延 - A musician during the late Shang dynasty who survived the tyranny of King Zhou by adapting his actions.
    • 江子牙 - Also known as Jiang Ziya, a legendary figure who became a key advisor to King Wen of Zhou.
    • 伊尹 - A legendary figure who became a prime minister under the Shang dynasty.
    • 乐天知命 - A concept that combines Confucian and Daoist ideas of accepting one's fate with joy and understanding.

    Proper Nouns

    • 三坟 - Ancient texts or collections of wisdom.
    • 五典 - Ancient texts or collections of wisdom.
    • 纬候杂说 - Miscellaneous esoteric or astrological writings.
    • 简狄 - A legendary figure from the Shang dynasty.
    • 契 - The founder of the Shang dynasty.
    • 傅说 - A legendary figure from the Shang dynasty.
    • 计然 - A famous strategist and economic advisor.
    • 师延 - A musician during the late Shang dynasty.
    • 江子牙 - Also known as Jiang Ziya.
    • 伊尹 - A legendary figure who became a prime minister.
    • 乐天知命 - A concept that combines Confucian and Daoist ideas.

    The Records state: The Three Caves, Five Classics, and various esoteric and astrological writings all claim that Jian Di (简狄) conceived the founder of the Shang dynasty, Xi (契), by swallowing a swallow's egg. The Book of Songs says: "By the decree of heaven, the black bird descended and gave birth to the Shang." This is the correct version of the text. This account speaks of conception through divine inspiration, with differing opinions among various sources, so it is recorded to highlight their distinctions. Fu Yue (傅说) left behind his labor as a commoner and was released from his position as a hired worker, responding to the omen and coming to serve as a minister, truly demonstrating his prescience. He is similar to Jiang Ziya (姜子牙) who returned to Zhou after fishing, yet different from Yi Yin (伊尹) who carried a tripod before becoming a prime minister. When the right time comes, even the most unlikely paths will lead to success. This is the clear teaching of past sages and the highest principle of learned individuals. "To accept one's fate with joy and understanding" is indeed a timeless truth. To achieve immortality in death is what we call fame. A bird may leave no reputation for its descendants, but its wings spread far and wide through the ages. Like precious metals and stones, dust cannot bury their steadfastness; like the clear rivers of Jing and Pu, the muddy waters of Zi and Wei cannot mix with their purity. Shiren (师延) faced the tyranny of King Zhou and adapted his steps to survive, using his authority to navigate and gain assistance, observing the times and serving his master, preserving his life and escaping. This is what is meant by being able to find a way out in adversity and ultimately escaping through wisdom. Therefore, his image is preserved in paintings and inscriptions on stone, valued for his harmonious and joyful contributions and esteemed for the far-reaching influence of his divine actions. As for the rewards given to Jixian (计然) for his wisdom, inscribed in gold to honor his virtue, they pale in comparison. [31]

    周

    　　周武王东伐纣，夜济河。时云明如昼，八百之族，皆齐而歌。有大蜂状如丹鸟，飞集王舟，因以鸟画其旗。翌日而枭纣，名其船曰蜂舟。鲁哀公二年，郑人击赵简子，得其蜂旗，则其类也。 【 事出《太公六韬》。】 武王使画其像于幡旗，以为吉兆。今人幡信皆为鸟画，则遗像也。
    　　成王即政三年，有泥离之国来朝。其人称：自发其国，常从云里而行，闻雷霆之声在下；或入潜穴，又闻波涛之声在上。视日月以知方国所向，计寒暑以知年月。考国之正朔，则序历与中国相符。王接以外宾礼也。 [32]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [32]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 周武王东伐纣 - King Wu of Zhou launched an eastern campaign against King Zhou of Shang.
    • 夜济河 - Crossed the river at night.
    • 云明如昼 - The clouds were as bright as day, describing a clear and bright night sky.
    • 八百之族，皆齐而歌 - Eight hundred clans all sang together.
    • 有大蜂状如丹鸟，飞集王舟 - There was a large bee resembling a red bird, flying and landing on King Wu's boat.
    • 翌日而枭纣 - On the next day, King Zhou was defeated and executed.
    • 名其船曰蜂舟 - He named his boat 'Bee Boat.'
    • 鲁哀公二年，郑人击赵简子，得其蜂旗 - In the second year of Duke Ai of Lu, the people of Zheng attacked Zhao Jianzi and captured his bee flag.
    • 武王使画其像于幡旗，以为吉兆 - King Wu ordered the image of the bee to be painted on his banners, considering it a good omen.
    • 今人幡信皆为鸟画，则遗像也 - Today, people's banners and letters all feature bird paintings, which are remnants of this tradition.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 周武王 - King Wu of Zhou, the founder of the Zhou dynasty, known for overthrowing the Shang dynasty.
    • 纣 - King Zhou of Shang, the last ruler of the Shang dynasty, known for his tyranny and the fall of the Shang.
    • 鲁哀公 - Duke Ai of Lu, a ruler of the state of Lu during the Spring and Autumn period.
    • 郑人 - The people of Zheng, a state during the Spring and Autumn period.
    • 赵简子 - Zhao Jianzi, a prominent noble during the Spring and Autumn period.
    • 太公六韬 - A classic military treatise attributed to Jiang Ziya (also known as Taigong Wang), a key advisor to King Wu of Zhou.

    Proper Nouns

    • 周武王 - King Wu of Zhou.
    • 纣 - King Zhou of Shang.
    • 鲁哀公 - Duke Ai of Lu.
    • 郑人 - The people of Zheng.
    • 赵简子 - Zhao Jianzi.
    • 太公六韬 - A classic military treatise.

    Zhou

    King Wu of Zhou launched an eastern campaign against King Zhou of Shang and crossed the river at night. At that time, the clouds were as bright as day, and eight hundred clans all sang together. There was a large bee resembling a red bird, which flew and landed on King Wu's boat. He then painted the image of the bird on his flag. On the next day, King Zhou was defeated and executed, and King Wu named his boat "Bee Boat." In the second year of Duke Ai of Lu, the people of Zheng attacked Zhao Jianzi and captured his bee flag, which was of the same type. [This event is recorded in the Taigong Liutao (Six Secret Teachings of Taigong).] King Wu ordered the image of the bee to be painted on his banners, considering it a good omen. Today, people's banners and letters all feature bird paintings, which are remnants of this tradition.

    Three years after King Cheng of Zhou ascended the throne, a delegation from the country of Nili came to pay homage. They said: Since leaving their country, they often traveled through the clouds, hearing the sound of thunder below; or entering hidden caves, they heard the sound of waves above. They determined the direction of their country by observing the sun and moon, and calculated the seasons by the cold and heat. When examining the calendar of their country, it matched the Chinese calendar. The king received them with the courtesy due to foreign guests. [32]

    四年。旃涂国献凤雏，载以瑶华之车，饰以五色之玉，驾以赤象，至于京师。育于灵禽之苑，饮以琼浆，饴以云实，二物皆出上元仙。方凤初至之时，毛色文彩未彪发；及成王封泰山、禅社首之后，文彩炳耀。中国飞走之类，不复喧鸣，咸服神禽之远至也。及成王崩，冲飞而去。孔子相鲁之时，有神凤游集。至哀公之末，不复来翔，故云：“凤鸟不至。”可为悲矣！
    　　五年。有因祇之国，去王都九万里，献女工一人。体貌轻洁，被纤?杂绣之衣，长袖修裾，风至则结其衿带，恐飘飖不能自止也。其人善织，以五色丝内于口中，手引而结之，则成文锦。其国人来献，有云昆锦，文似云从山岳中出也；有列堞锦，文似云霞覆城雉楼堞也；有杂珠锦，文似贯珠佩也；有篆文锦，文似大篆之文也；有列明锦，文似列灯烛也。幅皆广三尺。其国丈夫勤于耕稼，一日锄十顷之地。又贡嘉禾，一茎盈车。故时俗四言诗曰：“力勤十顷，能致嘉颖。” [33]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [33]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 四年 - A year in the reign of a ruler.
    • 凤雏 - A young phoenix, symbolizing nobility and virtue.
    • 瑶华之车 - A chariot made of precious jade, symbolizing luxury and elegance.
    • 五色之玉 - Jade in five colors, representing harmony and completeness.
    • 赤象 - A red elephant, possibly a mythical or symbolic animal.
    • 京师 - The capital city.
    • 灵禽之苑 - A garden for sacred birds.
    • 琼浆 - Heavenly nectar, symbolizing divine sustenance.
    • 云实 - Cloud fruit, a mythical or rare delicacy.
    • 上元仙 - Heavenly beings from the Upper Yuan realm, a Taoist concept.
    • 文彩炳耀 - Bright and splendid colors, symbolizing brilliance.
    • 飞走之类 - Flying and running creatures, referring to all animals.
    • 冲飞而去 - To soar away suddenly, indicating a sudden departure.
    • 孔子相鲁之时 - When Confucius served as the governor of Lu.
    • 哀公之末 - The end of Duke Ai's reign.
    • 凤鸟不至 - A phrase from the Analects, meaning the phoenix does not come, symbolizing a lack of moral order.
    • 五年 - Another year in the reign of a ruler.
    • 因祇之国 - A country named '因祇,' likely a fictional or less-known kingdom.
    • 去王都九万里 - Nine thousand miles from the capital.
    • 女工 - A skilled woman, often a weaver or artisan.
    • 体貌轻洁 - Graceful and clean appearance.
    • 被纤?杂绣之衣 - Wearing finely woven and embroidered clothes.
    • 长袖修裾 - Long sleeves and flowing skirts.
    • 结其衿带 - Tying their sash to prevent being blown away.
    • 云昆锦 - Cloud-patterned brocade, with designs resembling clouds emerging from mountains.
    • 列堞锦 - Wall-patterned brocade, with designs resembling clouds over city walls.
    • 杂珠锦 - Pearl-patterned brocade, with designs resembling pearls.
    • 篆文锦 - Seal script-patterned brocade, with designs resembling ancient Chinese characters.
    • 列明锦 - Lamp-patterned brocade, with designs resembling rows of lamps.
    • 幅皆广三尺 - Each piece is three feet wide.
    • 丈夫勤于耕稼 - Men diligently work in farming.
    • 一日锄十顷之地 - Can till ten acres of land in a day.
    • 贡嘉禾 - Offering auspicious rice, symbolizing good harvest and prosperity.
    • 一茎盈车 - One stalk fills a cart, indicating exceptional yield.
    • 四言诗 - Four-character verse, a traditional form of Chinese poetry.
    • 力勤十顷，能致嘉颖 - Diligence in farming ten acres can bring forth excellent crops.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 凤雏 - The phoenix, a symbol of nobility and virtue in Chinese culture.
    • 瑶华之车 - A luxurious chariot, symbolizing wealth and status.
    • 五色之玉 - Jade in five colors, representing harmony and completeness.
    • 赤象 - A red elephant, possibly a mythical or symbolic animal.
    • 灵禽之苑 - A sacred bird garden, often associated with imperial gardens.
    • 琼浆 - Heavenly nectar, symbolizing divine sustenance.
    • 云实 - Cloud fruit, a mythical or rare delicacy.
    • 上元仙 - Heavenly beings from the Upper Yuan realm, a Taoist concept.
    • 文彩炳耀 - Bright and splendid colors, symbolizing brilliance.
    • 飞走之类 - Flying and running creatures, referring to all animals.
    • 冲飞而去 - To soar away suddenly, indicating a sudden departure.
    • 孔子相鲁之时 - When Confucius served as the governor of Lu, a significant period in Chinese history.
    • 哀公之末 - The end of Duke Ai's reign, a time of decline in the state of Lu.
    • 凤鸟不至 - A phrase from the Analects, meaning the phoenix does not come, symbolizing a lack of moral order.
    • 因祇之国 - A country named '因祇,' likely a fictional or less-known kingdom.
    • 去王都九万里 - Nine thousand miles from the capital, emphasizing the distance.
    • 女工 - A skilled woman, often a weaver or artisan.
    • 体貌轻洁 - Graceful and clean appearance.
    • 被纤?杂绣之衣 - Wearing finely woven and embroidered clothes.
    • 长袖修裾 - Long sleeves and flowing skirts.
    • 结其衿带 - Tying their sash to prevent being blown away.
    • 云昆锦 - Cloud-patterned brocade, with designs resembling clouds emerging from mountains.
    • 列堞锦 - Wall-patterned brocade, with designs resembling clouds over city walls.
    • 杂珠锦 - Pearl-patterned brocade, with designs resembling pearls.
    • 篆文锦 - Seal script-patterned brocade, with designs resembling ancient Chinese characters.
    • 列明锦 - Lamp-patterned brocade, with designs resembling rows of lamps.
    • 幅皆广三尺 - Each piece is three feet wide.
    • 丈夫勤于耕稼 - Men diligently work in farming.
    • 一日锄十顷之地 - Can till ten acres of land in a day.
    • 贡嘉禾 - Offering auspicious rice, symbolizing good harvest and prosperity.
    • 一茎盈车 - One stalk fills a cart, indicating exceptional yield.
    • 四言诗 - Four-character verse, a traditional form of Chinese poetry.
    • 力勤十顷，能致嘉颖 - Diligence in farming ten acres can bring forth excellent crops.

    Final Translation

    In the fourth year, the country of Zhantu offered a young phoenix. It was transported in a jade chariot adorned with five-colored jade, driven by a red elephant, and arrived at the capital. The phoenix was raised in a sacred bird garden, fed with heavenly nectar and cloud fruit, both from the Upper Yuan realm. When the phoenix first arrived, its feathers were not yet brilliant; after King Cheng's enfeoffment of Mount Tai and the establishment of the Altar of the Earth, its feathers became splendid. All flying and running creatures in China ceased to make noise, all submitting to the arrival of the divine bird. When King Cheng died, the phoenix suddenly flew away. During the time when Confucius served as the governor of Lu, a divine phoenix appeared. By the end of Duke Ai's reign, the phoenix no longer appeared, hence the saying "The phoenix does not come," which is a cause for sorrow.

    In the fifth year, there was a country named Yinqi, nine thousand miles from the capital, which offered a skilled woman. She had a graceful and clean appearance, wearing finely woven and embroidered clothes with long sleeves and flowing skirts. When the wind came, she tied her sash to prevent herself from being blown away. This woman was skilled in weaving, using five-colored silk in her mouth and threading it with her hands to create patterned brocades. The people of the country offered various brocades: cloud-patterned brocade, with designs resembling clouds emerging from mountains; wall-patterned brocade, with designs resembling clouds over city walls; pearl-patterned brocade, with designs resembling pearls; seal script-patterned brocade, with designs resembling ancient Chinese characters; and lamp-patterned brocade, with designs resembling rows of lamps. Each piece was three feet wide. The men of the country diligently worked in farming and could till ten acres of land in a day. They also offered auspicious rice, with one stalk filling a cart. Thus, the contemporary four-character verse said, "Diligence in farming ten acres can bring forth excellent crops." [33]

    六年。燃丘之国献比翼鸟，雌雄各一，以玉为樊。其国使者皆拳头尖鼻，衣云霞之布，如今朝霞也。经历百有余国，方至京师。其中路山川不可记。越铁岘，泛沸海，蛇洲、蜂岑。铁岘峭砺，车轮刚金为辋，比至京师，轮皆铫锐几尽。又沸海汹涌如煎，鱼鳖皮骨坚强如石，可以为铠。泛沸海之时，以铜薄舟底，蛟龙不能近也。又经蛇洲，则以豹皮为屋，于屋内推车。又经蜂岑，燃胡苏之木，此木烟能杀百虫。经途五十余年，乃至洛邑。成王封泰山，禅社首。使发其国之并童稚，至京师，须皆白。及还至燃丘，容貌还复少壮。比翼鸟多力，状如鹊，衔南海之丹泥，巢昆岑之玄木，遇圣则来集，以表周公辅圣之祥异也。 [34]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [34]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 六年 - Specific year in the reign of a monarch, indicating the time frame of the narrative.
    • 燃丘之國 - Refers to the kingdom of Ranqiu, a place name that may require additional research for context.
    • 比翼鳥 - A mythical bird that flies with its mate, symbolizing loyalty and partnership.
    • 雌雄各一 - Indicates that there is one male and one female of the bird.
    • 以玉為樊 - Means 'enclosed by jade,' describing the cage or enclosure for the birds.
    • 衣云霞之布 - Refers to clothing made of clouds and mist, a poetic description of the fabric.
    • 如今朝霞也 - Compares the fabric to the morning glow, emphasizing its beauty.
    • 經歷百有余國 - Means 'traveled through more than a hundred countries,' indicating a long journey.
    • 越鐵岘 - Means 'crossed the Iron Pass,' referring to a difficult mountain pass.
    • 泛沸海 - Means 'crossed the boiling sea,' describing a perilous sea journey.
    • 蛇洲、蜂岑 - Refers to the Snake Island and the Bee Mountain, specific locations on the journey.
    • 鐵岘峭砺 - Describes the Iron Pass as steep and rugged.
    • 車輪剛金為辋 - Means 'wheels made of hard metal,' indicating the robust construction of the vehicles.
    • 輪皆铫锐几尽 - Means 'the wheels were almost entirely worn down,' emphasizing the difficulty of the journey.
    • 沸海汹涌如煎 - Describes the boiling sea as turbulent and boiling like a cauldron.
    • 魚鳖皮骨坚强如石 - Means 'fish and turtles with skin and bones as hard as stone,' indicating the toughness of the marine life.
    • 可以为铠 - Means 'can be used as armor,' suggesting the practical value of the marine life.
    • 泛沸海之时，以铜薄舟底 - Means 'when crossing the boiling sea, the bottom of the boat was lined with copper,' describing a protective measure.
    • 蛟龙不能近也 - Means 'dragons could not approach,' indicating the effectiveness of the copper lining.
    • 以豹皮为屋 - Means 'with a leopard skin roof,' describing the shelter on the Snake Island.
    • 于屋内推车 - Means 'pushing the cart inside the house,' indicating a unique method of transportation.
    • 燃胡苏之木 - Means 'burning the hulusu wood,' referring to a specific type of wood used for fire.
    • 此木烟能杀百虫 - Means 'this wood when burned can kill all insects,' indicating the wood's insecticidal properties.
    • 经途五十余年 - Means 'traveled for more than fifty years,' emphasizing the long duration of the journey.
    • 乃至洛邑 - Means 'finally arrived at Luoyi,' indicating the destination.
    • 成王封泰山，禅社首 - Refers to King Cheng's ritual of worshipping Mount Tai and offering sacrifices at the She Altar, a significant historical event.
    • 使发其国之并童稚 - Means 'sent young children from their country,' indicating the participants in the ritual.
    • 至京师，须皆白 - Means 'upon arriving in the capital, their hair turned white,' suggesting the passage of time.
    • 及还至燃丘，容貌还复少壮 - Means 'upon returning to Ranqiu, their appearance returned to youthful vigor,' indicating a miraculous transformation.
    • 比翼鸟多力 - Means 'the winged bird is strong,' describing the bird's physical prowess.
    • 状如鹊 - Means 'looks like a magpie,' providing a visual description of the bird.
    • 衔南海之丹泥 - Means 'carries red clay from the South Sea,' indicating the bird's material.
    • 巢昆岑之玄木 - Means 'nests in the dark wood of Kun岑,' providing a specific nesting location.
    • 遇圣则来集 - Means 'appears when encountering a sage,' indicating the bird's association with wisdom and virtue.
    • 以表周公辅圣之祥异也 - Means 'to signify the auspicious and extraordinary nature of Duke Zhou assisting a sage,' indicating the bird's symbolic significance.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 六年 - The specific year in the reign of a monarch, which may require additional research to identify the exact ruler and period.
    • 燃丘之國 - The kingdom of Ranqiu, a place name that may have historical or mythological significance.
    • 比翼鳥 - A mythical bird often associated with loyalty and partnership, commonly found in Chinese folklore.
    • 成王 - King Cheng, a historical figure in the Zhou dynasty known for his reign and rituals.
    • 泰山 - Mount Tai, a sacred mountain in China associated with imperial rituals and worship.
    • 社首 - The She Altar, a site for offering sacrifices to the land god.
    • 周公 - Duke Zhou, a revered statesman and advisor in the Zhou dynasty, known for his wisdom and assistance to King Cheng.

    Proper Nouns

    • 六年 - The specific year in the reign of a monarch.
    • 燃丘之國 - The kingdom of Ranqiu.
    • 比翼鳥 - The winged bird.
    • 成王 - King Cheng.
    • 泰山 - Mount Tai.
    • 社首 - The She Altar.
    • 周公 - Duke Zhou.

    Sixth Year. The Kingdom of Ranqiu presented a pair of winged birds, one male and one female, enclosed in a jade cage. The envoys from the kingdom were all with sharp noses and wore clothing made of cloud and mist, as beautiful as the morning glow. They traveled through more than a hundred countries before finally reaching the capital. The journey through the mountains and rivers cannot be fully recorded. They crossed the Iron Pass, sailed across the boiling sea, and passed through Snake Island and Bee Mountain. The Iron Pass was steep and rugged, and the wheels of the vehicles were made of hard metal. By the time they reached the capital, the wheels were almost entirely worn down. The boiling sea was turbulent and boiling like a cauldron, with fish and turtles whose skin and bones were as hard as stone, suitable for making armor. When crossing the boiling sea, the bottom of the boat was lined with copper, preventing dragons from approaching. On Snake Island, they used a leopard skin roof and pushed the cart inside the house. On Bee Mountain, they burned hulusu wood, which, when burned, could kill all insects. The journey took more than fifty years, and they finally arrived at Luoyi. King Cheng performed a ritual at Mount Tai and offered sacrifices at the She Altar. He sent young children from their country, and upon arriving in the capital, their hair turned white. Upon returning to Ranqiu, their appearance returned to youthful vigor. The winged bird was strong, looking like a magpie, carrying red clay from the South Sea, and nesting in the dark wood of Kun岑. It appeared when encountering a sage, signifying the auspicious and extraordinary nature of Duke Zhou assisting a sage. [34]

    七年。南陲之南，有扶娄之国。其人善能机巧变化，易形改服，大则兴云起雾，小则入于纤毫之中。缀金玉毛羽为衣裳。能吐云喷火，鼓腹则如雷霆之声。或化为犀、象、狮子、龙、蛇、犬、马之状。或变为虎、兕，口中生人，备百戏之乐，宛转屈曲于指掌间。人形或长数分，或复数寸，神怪欻忽，衒丽于时。乐府皆传此伎，至末代犹学焉，得粗亡精，代代不绝，故俗谓之婆候伎，则扶娄之音，讹替至今。 [35]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [35]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 七年 - Specific year or time period, likely referring to the seventh year of a reign or era.
    • 南陲之南 - Southernmost part of the southern frontier, emphasizing a distant and exotic location.
    • 善能机巧变化 - Skilled in ingenious transformations.
    • 易形改服 - To change form and attire.
    • 大则兴云起雾 - When large, they can summon clouds and mists.
    • 小则入于纤毫之中 - When small, they can enter the tiniest spaces.
    • 缀金玉毛羽为衣裳 - Adorn themselves with gold, jade, feathers, and fur.
    • 能吐云喷火 - Can spew clouds and fire.
    • 鼓腹则如雷霆之声 - When they roar, it sounds like thunder.
    • 或化为犀、象、狮子、龙、蛇、犬、马之状 - Can transform into the shapes of rhinos, elephants, lions, dragons, snakes, dogs, and horses.
    • 或变为虎、兕，口中生人 - Can turn into tigers, buffaloes, and even give birth to humans from their mouths.
    • 备百戏之乐 - Can perform all kinds of entertainments.
    • 宛转屈曲于指掌间 - Twist and turn in the palm of one's hand.
    • 人形或长数分，或复数寸 - Human forms can be several inches or even a few centimeters long.
    • 神怪欻忽 - Full of supernatural and sudden changes.
    • 衒丽于时 - Display their beauty and splendor.
    • 乐府皆传此伎 - The Music Bureau has all transmitted this art.
    • 至末代犹学焉 - Even in later generations, people still learn it.
    • 得粗亡精 - Get the rough outline but lose the essence.
    • 代代不绝 - Generation after generation, it never ceases.
    • 故俗谓之婆候伎 - Therefore, it is commonly called the 'Bao Hou' art.
    • 扶娄之音，讹替至今 - The name 'Fulu' has been distorted and passed down to this day.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 七年 - Specific year, likely during a particular dynasty or reign.
    • 南陲之南 - Southernmost part of the southern frontier, which could be a reference to a distant and exotic land.
    • 扶娄之国 - Mythical or foreign land of Fulu, known for its peculiar inhabitants.
    • 乐府 - Music Bureau, an institution responsible for collecting and preserving music and poetry in ancient China.
    • 婆候伎 - Specific type of performance art, likely a form of acrobatics or illusion.

    Proper Nouns

    • 扶娄 - Name of the mythical or foreign land.
    • 婆候伎 - Common name for the performance art.

    In the seventh year, in the southernmost part of the southern frontier, there was a country called Fulu. The people of this country were skilled in ingenious transformations, able to change their form and attire. When they were large, they could summon clouds and mists; when they were small, they could enter the tiniest spaces. They adorned themselves with gold, jade, feathers, and fur. They could spew clouds and fire, and when they roared, it sounded like thunder. They could transform into the shapes of rhinos, elephants, lions, dragons, snakes, dogs, and horses, or turn into tigers, buffaloes, and even give birth to humans from their mouths. They could perform all kinds of entertainments, twisting and turning in the palm of one's hand. Their human forms could be several inches or even a few centimeters long, full of supernatural and sudden changes, displaying their beauty and splendor. The Music Bureau had all transmitted this art, and even in later generations, people still learned it, though they might only capture the rough outline and lose the essence. Generation after generation, it never ceased, and therefore, it is commonly called the "Bao Hou" art. The name "Fulu" has been distorted and passed down to this day. [35]

    昭王即位二十年，王坐祇明之室，昼而假寐。忽梦白云蓊蔚而起，有人衣服并皆毛羽，因名羽人。王梦中与语，问以上仙之术。羽人曰：“大王精智未开，欲求长生久视，不可得也。”王跪而请受绝欲之教。羽人乃以指画王心，应手即裂。王乃惊寤，而血湿衿席，因患心疾，即却膳撤乐。移于旬日，忽见所梦者复来，语王曰：“先欲易王之心。”乃出方寸绿囊，中有续脉明丸、补血精散，以手摩王之臆，俄而即愈。王即请此药，贮以玉罐，缄以金绳。王以涂足，则飞天地万里之外，如游咫尺之内。有得服之，后天而死。 [36]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [36]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 昭王即位二十年 - Formal statement indicating the time period, meaning 'King Zhao reigned for twenty years.'
    • 王坐祇明之室，昼而假寐 - Describes King Zhao sitting in a bright room during the day and taking a light sleep.
    • 忽梦白云蓊蔚而起 - Vivid description of a dream where thick, lush clouds rise, with '蓊蔚' meaning dense and lush.
    • 有人衣服并皆毛羽，因名羽人 - Describes a person in the dream with clothing made of feathers, referred to as '羽人' (Feathered One).
    • 欲求长生久视，不可得也 - Expresses the desire for immortality, with '长生久视' meaning eternal life.
    • 绝欲之教 - Refers to teachings on abstaining from desires.
    • 先欲易王之心 - Indicates the intention to replace the king's heart.
    • 续脉明丸、补血精散 - Refers to medicinal pills and powders that restore pulse and replenish blood.
    • 涂足，则飞天地万里之外，如游咫尺之内 - Describes a magical effect where touching the feet allows one to travel vast distances as if they were nearby.
    • 后天而死 - A phrase indicating death after a certain period, possibly referring to a limited lifespan.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 昭王 - Refers to King Zhao, a historical figure whose reign is being described.
    • 祇明之室 - Likely refers to a specific room or hall associated with King Zhao, possibly a place of significance in his reign.
    • 羽人 - A term often used in Chinese mythology to describe immortals or beings with supernatural abilities, often associated with flying or living in the heavens.
    • 长生久视 - A common theme in Chinese literature and philosophy, referring to the pursuit of immortality or eternal life.
    • 绝欲之教 - Refers to teachings on asceticism and self-discipline, often associated with Daoist or Buddhist practices.
    • 续脉明丸、补血精散 - These are likely fictional or mythical medicinal remedies, possibly inspired by traditional Chinese medicine.

    Proper Nouns

    • 昭王 - The title of the king, King Zhao.
    • 祇明之室 - The name of the room or hall where King Zhao was sitting.
    • 羽人 - The name given to the figure in the dream, referring to a mythical being.
    • 续脉明丸、补血精散 - The names of the fictional medicinal remedies.

    King Zhao had reigned for twenty years. One day, while seated in the Bright Hall during the day, he took a light nap. Suddenly, he dreamed of thick, lush clouds rising, and a person appeared with clothing made entirely of feathers. This person was named "Feathered One." In the dream, the Feathered One spoke to King Zhao, asking about the ways of achieving immortality. The Feathered One said, "Your Majesty's wisdom and spirit have not yet been fully cultivated. If you wish to attain eternal life, it cannot be achieved." King Zhao knelt and asked for teachings on abstaining from desires. The Feathered One then pointed to King Zhao's heart, and to his shock, it split open. King Zhao woke up with blood soaking his robe and bedding, and he fell ill with heart trouble. He immediately stopped eating and withdrew from all pleasures. After ten days, the Feathered One appeared again in his dream and said, "I wished to replace your heart." The Feathered One then produced a small green bag, containing pills to restore the pulse and powders to replenish the blood. With a gentle touch, King Zhao's heart was healed. King Zhao asked for these remedies, which were stored in a jade vase and sealed with a golden cord. The Feathered One explained that if the remedies were applied to the feet, one could travel across thousands of miles of the world as if they were nearby. However, those who used them would eventually die after a certain period. [36]

    二十四年。涂修国献青凤、丹鹊各一雌一雄。孟夏之时，凤、鹊皆脱易毛羽。聚鹊翅以为扇，缉凤羽以饰车盖也。扇一名游飘，二名条翮，三名亏光，四名仄影。时东瓯献二女，一名延娟，二名延娱。使二人更摇此扇，侍于王侧，轻风四散，泠然自凉。此二人辩口丽辞，巧善歌笑，步尘上无迹，行日中无影。及昭王沦于汉水，二女与王乘舟，夹拥王身，同溺于水。故江汉之人，到今思之，立祀于江湄。数十年间，人于江汉之上，犹见王与二女乘舟戏于水际。至暮春上巳之日，褉集祠间。或以时鲜甘味，采兰杜包裹，以沉水中。或结五色纱囊盛食，或用金铁之器，并沉水中，以惊蛟龙水虫，使畏之不侵此食也。其水傍号曰招祇之祠。缀青凤之毛为二裘，一名燠质，二名暄肌，服之可以却寒。至厉王流于彘，彘人得而奇之，分裂此裘，遍于彘土。罪入大辟者，抽裘一毫以赎其死，则价值万金。 [37]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [37]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 二十四年 - Specific year in the reign of a ruler; rendered as 'In the twenty-fourth year' or 'During the twenty-fourth year.'
    • 脱易毛羽 - Literal phrase meaning 'shed and change feathers'; translated as 'molt and change their plumage.'
    • 聚鹊翅以为扇，缉凤羽以饰车盖 - Descriptive phrases; translated to convey the imagery of gathering magpie wings to make fans and weaving phoenix feathers to decorate car canopies.
    • 扇一名游飘，二名条翮，三名亏光，四名仄影 - Alternative names for the fan; transliterated and explained in a footnote if necessary.
    • 辩口丽辞，巧善歌笑 - Describes eloquent and beautiful speech and skillful singing and laughing; translated to capture the elegance and skill.
    • 步尘上无迹，行日中无影 - Poetic descriptions of grace and lightness; translated to convey the imagery.
    • 惊蛟龙水虫，使畏之不侵此食也 - Describes scaring away dragons and water creatures to protect food; translated to convey the purpose.
    • 缀青凤之毛为二裘 - Describes weaving blue phoenix feathers into two coats; translated to convey the imagery.
    • 一名燠质，二名暄肌 - Alternative names for the coats; transliterated and explained in a footnote if necessary.
    • 至暮春上巳之日，褉集祠间 - Describes a specific ritual gathering on the third day of the third month in the lunar calendar; rendered to convey the cultural significance.
    • 或以时鲜甘味，采兰杜包裹，以沉水中 - Describes rituals performed; translated to convey the imagery.
    • 或结五色纱囊盛食，或用金铁之器，并沉水中 - Describes rituals performed; translated to convey the imagery.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 涂修国 - Kingdom or state; transliterated as 'Tu Xiu Kingdom.'
    • 东瓯 - Kingdom or state; transliterated as 'Dongou Kingdom.'
    • 昭王 - King; rendered as 'King Zhao.'
    • 厉王 - King; rendered as 'King Li.'
    • 彘 - Place; transliterated as 'Zhi.'
    • 暮春上巳之日 - Specific day in the lunar calendar; rendered as 'the third day of the third month in the lunar calendar.'
    • 褉集祠间 - Ritual gathering; rendered as 'gather for a ritual at the shrine.'
    • 招祇之祠 - Shrine; rendered as 'Shrine of Invoking the Spirits.'

    In the twenty-fourth year, the Tu Xiu Kingdom presented a pair of blue phoenixes and a pair of red magpies, each consisting of one male and one female. During the first month of summer, both the phoenixes and magpies molted and changed their plumage. The wings of the magpies were gathered to make fans, and the feathers of the phoenixes were woven to adorn the car canopies. The fans were known by four names: "Yoyou," "Tiaohé," "Kuiguāng," and "Zèyǐng." At that time, the kingdom of Dongou presented two women, one named Yanjuan and the other named Yanwu. The king had these two women take turns waving the fans, standing by his side, and the gentle breeze spread, cooling him pleasantly. These two women were eloquent and skilled in beautiful speech, adept at singing and laughing, and their footsteps left no trace on the ground, and their shadows were barely visible in the sunlight. When King Zhao drowned in the Han River, the two women accompanied him on the boat, holding him close, and they all drowned together. Therefore, the people of Jianghan still think of them today, and a shrine was established by the riverside. For several decades, people on the banks of the Jiang and Han rivers still saw King Zhao and the two women playing on a boat by the water. On the third day of the third month in the lunar calendar, during the ritual gathering at the shrine, some offered fresh and sweet delicacies, wrapped in fragrant herbs, and sank them in the water. Others used colorful silk pouches to hold the food, or metal and iron vessels, and sank them in the water to scare away dragons and water creatures, making them fear and not touch the food. The shrine by the water was called the Shrine of Invoking the Spirits. The feathers of the blue phoenix were woven into two coats, known as "Yùzhì" and "Xuānjī." Wearing these coats could ward off the cold. When King Li was exiled to Zhi, the people of Zhi found these coats strange, and they divided them, spreading them throughout the land of Zhi. Those who committed crimes punishable by death could redeem their lives by pulling out a single hair from the coat, which was worth ten thousand gold. [37]

    录曰：武王资圣智而克伐，观天命以行诛。不驱熊罴之师，不劳三战之旅，一戎衣而定王业，凭神力而协符瑞。至于成王，制礼崇乐，姬德方盛，营洛邑而居九鼎，寝刑庙而万国来宾。虽大禹之隆夏绩，帝乙之兴殷道，未足方焉。故能继后稷之先基，绍公刘之盛德，文、武之迹不坠，故《大雅》称为“令德”。播声教于八荒之外，流仁惠于九围之表。神智之所绥化，遐迩之所来服，靡不越岳航海，交赆于辽险之路。 [38]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [38]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 資聖智 - Classical expression meaning 'endowed with divine wisdom.'
    • 一戎衣 - Classical expression meaning 'one campaign' or 'one military action.'
    • 協符瑞 - Meaning 'harmonized auspicious signs.'
    • 制禮崇樂 - Meaning 'established rituals and elevated music.'
    • 姬德方盛 - Meaning 'the virtue of the House of Ji was flourishing.'
    • 居九鼎 - Meaning 'housed the Nine Tripods,' symbols of imperial power.
    • 寝刑廟 - Meaning 'abolished capital punishment in the temple.'
    • 隆夏績 - Meaning 'flourishing summer achievements.'
    • 帝乙之興殷道 - Meaning 'the rise of the Shang dynasty under Emperor Yi.'
    • 不墜 - Meaning 'did not decline.'
    • 播聲教於八荒之外 - Meaning 'disseminated the sound and teachings beyond the eight corners of the world.'
    • 流仁惠於九圍之表 - Meaning 'spread benevolence and kindness beyond the nine regions.'
    • 神智之所綏化 - Meaning 'the domain of divine wisdom and governance.'
    • 遐邇之所來服 - Meaning 'the far and near came to submit.'
    • 靡不越嶽航海 - Meaning 'none failed to cross mountains and seas.'
    • 交贄於遼險之路 - Meaning 'offering tribute on the distant and perilous roads.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 武王 - King Wu of Zhou, a legendary figure who overthrew the Shang dynasty and founded the Zhou dynasty.
    • 文王 - King Wen of Zhou, the father of King Wu, known for his wisdom and virtue.
    • 成王 - King Cheng of Zhou, the son of King Wu, who continued the reforms and established the capital at Luoyang.
    • 大禹 - The legendary founder of the Xia dynasty, known for his flood control efforts and establishment of the first Chinese state.
    • 帝乙 - The last king of the Shang dynasty before its fall to the Zhou.
    • 九鼎 - Nine bronze tripods that symbolized the power and legitimacy of the Chinese emperor.
    • 八荒 - The eight corners of the world, a metaphor for the entire known world.
    • 九圍 - The nine regions of China, a traditional division of the Chinese land.
    • 神智 - Divine wisdom, often associated with the Mandate of Heaven.
    • 綏化 - Governance and pacification, a concept of ruling with benevolence and order.
    • 遐邇 - Far and near, a metaphor for the entire world.
    • 越嶽航海 - Crossing mountains and seas, a metaphor for overcoming great distances.
    • 交贄 - Offering tribute, a practice of sending gifts or offerings to show respect and submission.

    Proper Nouns

    • 武王 - King Wu of Zhou.
    • 文王 - King Wen of Zhou.
    • 成王 - King Cheng of Zhou.
    • 大禹 - The legendary founder of the Xia dynasty.
    • 帝乙 - The last king of the Shang dynasty.
    • 九鼎 - Nine bronze tripods that symbolized the power and legitimacy of the Chinese emperor.
    • 八荒 - The eight corners of the world.
    • 九圍 - The nine regions of China.
    • 神智 - Divine wisdom.
    • 綏化 - Governance and pacification.
    • 遐邇 - Far and near.
    • 越嶽航海 - Crossing mountains and seas.
    • 交贄 - Offering tribute.

    Record states: King Wu, endowed with divine wisdom, achieved victory and observed the mandate of heaven to carry out punishment. He did not mobilize a fierce army or engage in multiple battles; with one campaign, he established the royal cause, relying on divine power to harmonize auspicious signs. When it came to King Cheng, he established rituals and elevated music, and the virtue of the House of Ji was flourishing. He established Luoyang and housed the Nine Tripods, abolished capital punishment in the temple, and attracted the nations. Even the grand achievements of the great Yu in the flourishing summer and the rise of the Shang dynasty under Emperor Yi were not sufficient to match the achievements of King Wu and King Cheng. Thus, they could continue the legacy of Houji, follow the great virtue of Gong Liu, and the legacy of Wen and Wu did not decline, hence the Da Ya (Great Odes) called them "virtuous." They disseminated the sound and teachings beyond the eight corners of the world and spread benevolence and kindness beyond the nine regions. The domain of divine wisdom and governance, the far and near came to submit, none failed to cross mountains and seas, offering tribute on the distant and perilous roads. [38]

    瑰宝殊怪之物，充于王庭；灵禽神兽之类，游集林蘌。诡丽殊用之物，镌斫异于人功。方册未之或载，篆素或所不绝。及乎王人风举之使，直指逾于日月之陲，穷昏明之际，觇风星以望路，凭云波而远逝。所谓道通幽微，德被冥昧者也。成、康以降，世祀陵衰。昭王不能弘远业，垂声教，南游荆楚，义乖巡狩，溺精灵于江汉，且极于幸由。水滨所以招问，《春秋》以为深贬。嗟二姬之殉死，三良之贞节。精诚一至，视殒若生。格之正道，不如强谏。楚人怜之，失其死矣。 [39]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [39]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 瑰宝殊怪之物 - Rare and extraordinary treasures.
    • 充于王庭 - Filled the royal court.
    • 灵禽神兽之类 - Divine birds and mythical beasts.
    • 游集林蘌 - Roamed and gathered in the forests.
    • 诡丽殊用之物 - Strange and beautiful objects with unique uses.
    • 镌斫异于人功 - Carved and shaped differently from human craftsmanship.
    • 方册未之或载 - Not recorded in any books.
    • 篆素或所不绝 - Not entirely absent from inscriptions and writings.
    • 王人风举之使 - Royal envoy who traveled swiftly.
    • 直指逾于日月之陲 - Traveled beyond the borders of the sun and moon.
    • 穷昏明之际 - At the boundary of darkness and light.
    • 觇风星以望路 - Observed the stars to navigate.
    • 凭云波而远逝 - Relied on the clouds and waves to travel far.
    • 道通幽微 - The path connects the subtle and profound.
    • 德被冥昧者也 - Virtue permeates the dark and obscure.
    • 成、康以降 - Reigns of King Cheng and King Kang of Zhou.
    • 世祀陵衰 - The world's sacrifices and offerings declined.
    • 昭王不能弘远业 - King Zhao could not expand his distant legacy.
    • 垂声教 - Establish a reputation and influence.
    • 南游荆楚 - Traveled south to Jing and Chu.
    • 义乖巡狩 - Departed from the proper duty of inspecting the regions.
    • 溺精灵于江汉 - Became entranced by the spirits of the Yangtze and Han rivers.
    • 且极于幸由 - Indulged excessively in pleasure.
    • 水滨所以招问 - At the water's edge, he was summoned to ask.
    • 春秋以为深贬 - The *Spring and Autumn Annals* deeply criticized this.
    • 嗟二姬之殉死 - Alas, the two consorts who died in sacrifice.
    • 三良之贞节 - Three loyal retainers who remained steadfast.
    • 精诚一至 - Their sincere devotion.
    • 视殒若生 - They regarded death as if it were life.
    • 格之正道 - To conform to the righteous path.
    • 不如强谏 - It was better to strongly advise.
    • 楚人怜之 - The people of Chu pitied them.
    • 失其死矣 - They lost their lives in vain.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 瑰宝殊怪之物 - Rare and extraordinary treasures, often associated with the wealth and power of a ruler.
    • 灵禽神兽之类 - Divine birds and mythical beasts are common in Chinese mythology and often symbolize the supernatural or divine.
    • 游集林蘌 - Forests are often depicted as places of wonder and mystery in classical Chinese literature.
    • 诡丽殊用之物 - Unique and beautiful objects often symbolize the ingenuity and craftsmanship of a culture.
    • 镌斫异于人功 - Carving and shaping techniques that differ from human craftsmanship may refer to divine or supernatural methods.
    • 方册未之或载 - The *Spring and Autumn Annals* and other historical records are often referenced in classical Chinese texts.
    • 篆素或所不绝 - Inscriptions and writings are important sources of historical and cultural information.
    • 王人风举之使 - Royal envoys were often depicted as traveling swiftly and efficiently.
    • 直指逾于日月之陲 - The concept of traveling beyond the borders of the sun and moon is a metaphor for traveling to distant and unknown lands.
    • 穷昏明之际 - The boundary of darkness and light is a metaphor for the transition between day and night or the unknown and the known.
    • 觇风星以望路 - Observing the stars to navigate is a common theme in classical Chinese literature, symbolizing wisdom and foresight.
    • 凭云波而远逝 - Traveling by cloud and wave is a metaphor for traveling far and fast, often associated with divine or supernatural abilities.
    • 道通幽微 - The path connecting the subtle and profound is a metaphor for understanding deep and hidden truths.
    • 德被冥昧者也 - Virtue permeating the dark and obscure is a metaphor for the far-reaching influence of moral integrity.
    • 成、康以降 - King Cheng and King Kang of Zhou are historical figures known for their wise rule.
    • 世祀陵衰 - The decline of sacrifices and offerings is a metaphor for the decline of moral and social order.
    • 昭王不能弘远业 - King Zhao of Zhou is a historical figure whose reign is often criticized for its lack of expansion and influence.
    • 垂声教 - Establishing a reputation and influence is a key goal for rulers in classical Chinese thought.
    • 南游荆楚 - Jing and Chu were regions in ancient China, often associated with cultural and political significance.
    • 义乖巡狩 - The proper duty of inspecting the regions is a key responsibility of rulers in classical Chinese thought.
    • 溺精灵于江汉 - The Yangtze and Han rivers are important rivers in Chinese history and mythology.
    • 且极于幸由 - Indulging excessively in pleasure is a common criticism of rulers in classical Chinese literature.
    • 水滨所以招问 - Being summoned to ask at the water's edge is a metaphor for being called upon to answer questions or face judgment.
    • 春秋以为深贬 - The *Spring and Autumn Annals* is a historical text that often includes moral and political critiques.
    • 嗟二姬之殉死 - The two consorts who died in sacrifice are historical figures whose loyalty and devotion are celebrated.
    • 三良之贞节 - The three loyal retainers who remained steadfast are historical figures whose loyalty and integrity are celebrated.
    • 精诚一至 - Sincere devotion is a key virtue in classical Chinese thought.
    • 视殒若生 - Regarding death as if it were life is a metaphor for the ultimate sacrifice and devotion.
    • 格之正道 - Conforming to the righteous path is a key goal in classical Chinese thought.
    • 不如强谏 - Strongly advising is a key responsibility of loyal retainers in classical Chinese thought.
    • 楚人怜之 - The people of Chu pitied them, indicating the impact of their actions on the people.
    • 失其死矣 - Losing their lives in vain is a metaphor for the futility of their actions.

    Proper Nouns

    • 成、康 - King Cheng and King Kang of Zhou.
    • 昭王 - King Zhao of Zhou.
    • 荆楚 - Regions in ancient China, often associated with cultural and political significance.
    • 江汉 - Yangtze and Han rivers.
    • 二姬 - Two consorts who died in sacrifice.
    • 三良 - Three loyal retainers who remained steadfast.

    Rare and extraordinary treasures filled the royal court; divine birds and mythical beasts roamed and gathered in the forests. Strange and beautiful objects, carved and shaped differently from human craftsmanship, were not recorded in any books but were not entirely absent from inscriptions and writings. When the royal envoy, traveling swiftly, reached beyond the borders of the sun and moon, at the boundary of darkness and light, he observed the stars to navigate and relied on the clouds and waves to travel far. This is what is meant by the path connecting the subtle and profound, and virtue permeating the dark and obscure. From the reigns of King Cheng and King Kang of Zhou onward, the world's sacrifices and offerings declined. King Zhao could not expand his distant legacy, establish a reputation and influence, and traveled south to Jing and Chu, departing from the proper duty of inspecting the regions, becoming entranced by the spirits of the Yangtze and Han rivers, and indulging excessively in pleasure. At the water's edge, he was summoned to ask, and the Spring and Autumn Annals deeply criticized this. Alas, the two consorts who died in sacrifice, and the three loyal retainers who remained steadfast. Their sincere devotion made them regard death as if it were life. To conform to the righteous path, it was better to strongly advise. The people of Chu pitied them, and they lost their lives in vain. [39]

    拾遗记卷三

    周穆王

    　　穆王即位三十二年，巡行天下。驭黄金碧玉之车，傍气乘风，起朝阳之岳，自明及晦，穷?禹县之表。有书史十人，记其所行之地。又副以瑶华之轮十乘，随王之后，以载其书也。王驭八龙之骏：一名绝地，足不践土；二名翻羽，行越飞禽；三名奔霄，夜行万里；四名超影，逐日而行；五名逾辉，毛色炳耀；六名超光，一形十影；七名腾雾，乘云而奔；八名挟翼，身有肉翅。递而驾焉，按辔徐行，以匝天地之域。王神智远谋，使迹毂遍于四海，故绝异之物，不期而自服焉。 [40]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [40]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 巡行天下 - Formal expression meaning 'to tour the entire world' or 'to travel throughout the land.'
    • 驭黄金碧玉之车 - Literally means 'to drive a golden and jade chariot,' but likely refers to a luxurious or divine vehicle.
    • 傍气乘风 - Poetic expression meaning 'to ride the wind and mist,' suggesting a supernatural or highly advanced mode of transportation.
    • 起朝阳之岳 - Literally means 'to rise from the mountain of the rising sun,' which is a metaphor for starting a journey at dawn.
    • 自明及晦 - Means 'from dawn to dusk,' indicating the passage of a full day.
    • 穷禹县之表 - Literally means 'to reach the boundary of Yu's county,' but likely refers to exploring the farthest reaches of the known world.
    • 记其所行之地 - Means 'to record the places he traveled to.'
    • 瑶华之轮十乘 - Literally means 'ten chariots of jade flowers,' which is a metaphor for ten luxurious or divine vehicles.
    • 王驭八龙之骏 - Means 'the king drives eight divine horses.'
    • 绝地 - Means 'to not step on the ground,' suggesting the horse can fly or move without touching the ground.
    • 翻羽 - Means 'to fly faster than birds,' indicating the horse can fly.
    • 奔霄 - Means 'to run through the sky,' suggesting the horse can travel at great speeds, possibly in the air.
    • 超影 - Means 'to surpass shadows,' indicating the horse can move so fast that it outpaces shadows.
    • 逐日而行 - Means 'to follow the sun,' suggesting the horse can travel at the speed of the sun.
    • 逾辉 - Means 'to surpass brilliance,' indicating the horse has a very bright or colorful appearance.
    • 超光 - Means 'to surpass light,' suggesting the horse can move so fast that it outpaces light.
    • 一形十影 - Means 'one form with ten shadows,' indicating the horse can create multiple illusions or reflections.
    • 腾雾 - Means 'to rise through the mist,' suggesting the horse can move through or above clouds.
    • 挟翼 - Means 'to have wings,' indicating the horse has physical wings.
    • 递而驾焉 - Means 'to take turns driving them,' suggesting the horses are used in rotation.
    • 按辔徐行 - Means 'to gently control the reins and move slowly,' suggesting a controlled and deliberate pace.
    • 以匝天地之域 - Means 'to encircle the entire domain of heaven and earth,' indicating a comprehensive exploration.
    • 神智远谋 - Means 'divine wisdom and far-reaching plans,' suggesting the king's exceptional intelligence and strategic thinking.
    • 使迹毂遍于四海 - Means 'to make the tracks of the wheels spread across the four seas,' indicating extensive travel.
    • 绝异之物 - Means 'extraordinary things,' suggesting rare or unique items.
    • 不期而自服焉 - Means 'without being expected, they submit naturally,' indicating that extraordinary things come to the king without effort.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 周穆王 - Refers to King Mu of Zhou, a legendary ruler of the Zhou Dynasty known for his travels and conquests.
    • 黄金碧玉之车 - Refers to a luxurious or divine vehicle, possibly a metaphor for a chariot made of precious materials.
    • 八龙之骏 - Refers to eight divine horses, each with unique abilities, which are often associated with mythological or legendary figures.
    • 天地之域 - Refers to the entire universe or the known world, emphasizing the king's extensive travels.
    • 神智远谋 - Refers to the king's exceptional intelligence and strategic thinking, suggesting he is a wise and capable ruler.
    • 四海 - Refers to the four seas, a metaphor for the entire world, emphasizing the king's global reach.
    • 绝异之物 - Refers to extraordinary or unique items, suggesting that the king's travels brought him many rare and valuable things.
    • 不期而自服焉 - Refers to the idea that extraordinary things come to the king without effort, suggesting his divine favor or exceptional status.

    Proper Nouns

    • 周穆王 - King Mu of Zhou, a legendary ruler of the Zhou Dynasty.
    • 黄金碧玉之车 - A luxurious or divine vehicle.
    • 八龙之骏 - Eight divine horses, each with unique abilities.
    • 绝地、翻羽、奔霄、超影、逾辉、超光、腾雾、挟翼 - Names of the eight divine horses.
    • 天地之域 - The entire universe or the known world.
    • 四海 - The four seas, a metaphor for the entire world.
    • 绝异之物 - Extraordinary or unique items.

    The Book of Lost Tales, Volume III

    King Mu of Zhou

    In the thirty-second year of King Mu's reign, he toured the entire world. He drove a golden and jade chariot, riding the wind and mist, rising from the mountain of the rising sun, traveling from dawn to dusk, reaching the boundary of Yu's county. He had ten scribes to record the places he traveled to. Additionally, he had ten chariots of jade flowers following him, to carry his books.

    The king drove eight divine horses: one was named "绝地" (Unearthly), whose hooves did not touch the ground; the second was named "翻羽" (Sky Soarer), which could fly faster than birds; the third was named "奔霄" (Sky Runner), which could travel a thousand miles at night; the fourth was named "超影" (Shadow Surpasser), which could follow the sun; the fifth was named "逾辉" (Radiant), whose coat was brilliantly shining; the sixth was named "超光" (Light Surpasser), which could create ten shadows with one form; the seventh was named "腾雾" (Mist Rider), which could rise through the mist; the eighth was named "挟翼" (Winged), which had physical wings. They took turns driving them, and he gently controlled the reins and moved slowly, encircling the entire domain of heaven and earth. The king had divine wisdom and far-reaching plans, making the tracks of the wheels spread across the four seas, thus extraordinary things came to him without being expected. [40]

    录曰：夫因气含生，罕不以形相别。至于比德方事，龙马则同类焉。是以蔡昙观其智，忌卫相其才。抑亦昭发于图纬，而刊载于宝牒。章皇王之符瑞，叶河洛之祯祥。故以丹青列其形，铜玉传其象。至如騄耳、骅骝、赤骥、白驎之绝，黄渠、山子、逾轮之异，不可得而比也。故能遥碣石而轹倒晷，排阊阖而轶姑徐。非夫归风弥应之迹，超虚送日之步，安能若是哉！望绛宫而骧首，指琼台而一息，繄可得而齐影矣。至于《诗》、《书》所记，名色实多，骍骆丽乎垧野，皎质耀乎空谷。 [41]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [41]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 夫因气含生 - A philosophical statement meaning 'all life arises from qi (vital energy).'
    • 罕不以形相别 - Means 'rarely does it not distinguish itself through form.'
    • 比德方事 - Means 'to compare virtues and match deeds.'
    • 龙马则同类焉 - Means 'dragons and horses are of the same kind.'
    • 昭发于图纬 - Means 'manifest in the celestial diagrams and auguries.'
    • 刊载于宝牒 - Means 'recorded in the precious scrolls.'
    • 章皇王之符瑞 - Means 'display the auspicious signs of the sovereigns.'
    • 叶河洛之祯祥 - Means 'harmonize with the auspicious signs of the Yellow River and Luo River.'
    • 丹青列其形 - Means 'depict its form in paintings.'
    • 铜玉传其象 - Means 'transmit its image in bronze and jade.'
    • 騄耳、骅骝、赤骥、白驎之绝 - Refers to legendary horses like Lu'er, Hualiu, Chijì, and Baidi, known for their extraordinary qualities.
    • 黄渠、山子、逾轮之异 - Refers to unique horses like Huangqu, Shanzi, and Yulun.
    • 遥碣石而轹倒晷 - Means 'travel far to Jieshi and surpass the moving shadow.'
    • 排阊阖而轶姑徐 - Means 'push open the celestial gates and surpass Gu and Xu.'
    • 归风弥应之迹 - Means 'returning to the wind and fulfilling its destined path.'
    • 超虚送日之步 - Means 'leaping through the void and pacing the sun.'
    • 望绛宫而骧首 - Means 'gazing at the Crimson Palace and raising its head.'
    • 指琼台而一息 - Means 'pointing to the Jade Terrace and resting for a moment.'
    • 繄可得而齐影矣 - Means 'only then can it match its shadow.'
    • 名色实多 - Means 'there are many names and types.'
    • 骍骆丽乎垧野 - Means 'the red and black horses are beautiful in the open fields.'
    • 皎质耀乎空谷 - Means 'their bright forms shine in the empty valleys.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 龙马 - A legendary creature, often associated with wisdom and auspiciousness.
    • 蔡昙、忌卫 - Likely historical or mythological figures associated with observing and assessing extraordinary beings.
    • 图纬 - Refers to celestial diagrams and auguries, often used in divination and cosmology.
    • 宝牒 - Refers to precious scrolls or records, often containing important historical or mythological information.
    • 皇王 - Refers to sovereigns or rulers, often in a mythological or historical context.
    • 河洛 - Refers to the Yellow River and Luo River, significant in Chinese cosmology and mythology.
    • 绛宫、琼台 - Mythological or symbolic places, often associated with the celestial or divine realms.
    • 騄耳、骅骝、赤骥、白驎 - Legendary horses known for their extraordinary qualities.
    • 黄渠、山子、逾轮 - Unique horses with distinctive characteristics.
    • 碣石、阊阖、姑徐 - Mythological or geographical locations, often associated with celestial or divine realms.

    Proper Nouns

    • 蔡昙 - A historical or mythological figure associated with observing intelligence.
    • 忌卫 - A historical or mythological figure associated with assessing talent.
    • 图纬 - Refers to celestial diagrams and auguries.
    • 宝牒 - Refers to precious scrolls or records.
    • 皇王 - Refers to sovereigns or rulers.
    • 河洛 - Refers to the Yellow River and Luo River.
    • 绛宫 - A mythological or symbolic place.
    • 琼台 - A mythological or symbolic place.
    • 騄耳、骅骝、赤骥、白驎 - Legendary horses.
    • 黄渠、山子、逾轮 - Unique horses.
    • 碣石、阊阖、姑徐 - Mythological or geographical locations.

    The Record states: All life arises from qi, rarely does it not distinguish itself through form. When it comes to comparing virtues and matching deeds, dragons and horses are of the same kind. Therefore, Cai Tan observed its intelligence, and Ji Wei assessed its talent. It is also manifest in the celestial diagrams and auguries, and recorded in the precious scrolls. It displays the auspicious signs of the sovereigns, harmonizes with the auspicious signs of the Yellow River and Luo River. Hence, its form is depicted in paintings, and its image is transmitted in bronze and jade. As for the legendary horses like Lu'er, Hualiu, Chijì, and Baidi, and the unique horses like Huangqu, Shanzi, and Yulun, they are incomparable. Thus, it can travel far to Jieshi and surpass the moving shadow, push open the celestial gates and surpass Gu and Xu. Without its destined path and pacing the sun, how could it be so! Gazing at the Crimson Palace and raising its head, pointing to the Jade Terrace and resting for a moment, only then can it match its shadow. As for the Book of Poetry and Book of Documents, there are many names and types. The red and black horses are beautiful in the open fields, and their bright forms shine in the empty valleys. [41]

    或表形騧紫，被乎青玄，难可尽言矣。其有龙文、騕袅之伦，取其电逝而飙逸，驎、骝、駃騠之俦，亦腾骧以称骏。莫不待盛明而皆出，历代之神宝矣。次有薄梢、啮膝、鱼文、骊驹之类，或擅名于汉古，或珍生于冀北，备饰于涓正，填列于帝皂，进则充服于上襄，而骖骊于瑶辂，退则羁弃于下圉，思驭于帝闲，俟吴班、秦公之见识，仰天门而弥远，窥云路而可难哉！使乎韩哀、孙阳之复执靶，岂伤吻弊策，伏匿而不进焉。非神彻幽遐，体照冥远，驱驾群龙，穷观天域，详搜迥古，靡得俦焉。 [42]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [42]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 或表形騧紫，被乎青玄 - Describes a horse with a unique and complex coloration, where '表形' means 'to display form' and '被乎青玄' means 'clothed in blue and black,' suggesting a horse with a distinctive and possibly supernatural appearance.
    • 难可尽言矣 - Means 'it is difficult to fully describe' or 'it cannot be fully expressed,' indicating the complexity and uniqueness of the horse's appearance.
    • 龙文、騕袅之倫 - Refers to horses with dragon-like patterns or markings, emphasizing their noble and majestic qualities.
    • 電逝而飙逸 - Describes the horse's speed and agility, where '電逝' means 'swift as lightning' and '飙逸' means 'swift and graceful.'
    • 驎、骝、駃騠之俦 - Refers to different types of horses, including the qilin (a mythical horse), the luo (a black horse), and the jue (a fast horse), all of which are considered noble and swift.
    • 騰骧以称骏 - Means 'to leap and gallop, thus being called swift,' describing the horse's dynamic and impressive movements.
    • 薄梢、啮膝、鱼文、骊驹之类 - Refers to various types of horses, each with its own unique characteristics and names.
    • 擅名于汉古 - Means 'to be famous in ancient Han times,' indicating historical recognition.
    • 珍生于冀北 - Means 'to be precious and born in Jibei,' referring to a region known for producing fine horses.
    • 备饰于涓正 - Means 'to be adorned by the imperial court,' indicating the horse's importance and status.
    • 填列于帝皂 - Means 'to be lined up in the imperial stables,' further emphasizing the horse's high status.
    • 进则充服于上襄 - Means 'to serve in the imperial carriage,' indicating the horse's role in official and ceremonial contexts.
    • 骖骊于瑶辂 - Means 'to pull the imperial carriage,' describing the horse's role in royal transportation.
    • 退则羁弃于下圉 - Means 'to be abandoned in the lower stables,' indicating the horse's potential fall from grace.
    • 思驭于帝闲 - Means 'to long for service in the imperial stables,' expressing the horse's desire to regain its former status.
    • 俟吴班、秦公之见识 - Means 'to wait for the recognition of Wu Ban and Qin Gong,' referring to specific historical figures who could recognize the horse's value.
    • 仰天门而弥远 - Means 'to look up to the Heavenly Gate and feel increasingly distant,' suggesting a sense of unattainable aspiration.
    • 窥云路而可难哉 - Means 'to look at the path to the clouds and find it difficult,' further emphasizing the challenges and obstacles.
    • 使乎韩哀、孙阳之复执靶 - Means 'if only Han Ai and Sun Yang could once again hold the reins,' referring to historical horse experts who could appreciate the horse's qualities.
    • 岂伤吻弊策，伏匿而不进焉 - Means 'would not they be harmed by worn bits and broken reins, hidden and not advancing,' suggesting that the horse's true potential is not being recognized.
