    • 蛇门 - A gate in the Wu city.
    • 望乌台 - A platform in the Wu city.
    • 宝井 - Wells or trenches where valuable items were hidden.
    • 游宫 - Inner chambers or palaces in the Yue capital.

    Yue plotted to conquer Wu, collecting rare treasures, beautiful women, and exotic delicacies to present to Wu. They sacrificed three animals to pray to heaven and earth, and sacrificed dragons and snakes to worship rivers and mountains. They pretended to mobilize millions of households from southern China to serve as laborers for Wu. Yue also had two beautiful women, one named Yiguang and the other named Xiuming, [who were also known as Xi Shi and Zheng Dan.] They were presented to Wu. Wu housed them in a room fragrant with pepper and adorned with fine pearl-hung curtains, lowering them in the morning to block the sun and raising them at night to welcome the moon. The two women sat together in front of the window, tidying their mirrors and applying makeup within the pearl-hung curtains. Those who secretly peeped at them were all moved and terrified, calling them divine beings. The King of Wu was bewitched and neglected his duties. When Yue's troops entered the country, he took the two women and fled to the Wu palace. As Yue's army entered chaotically, they saw the two women under a tree and all called them divine women, gazing at them but daring not to approach. Now inside the snake gate of the Wu city, there is a decaying tree stump that is still a place of worship for the divine women. At first, when the Yue king entered Wu, there were red birds flanking him and flying. When King Goujian became a hegemon, he built the Lookout for the Red Birds, saying that the red birds were unusual. Fan Li served as chancellor of Yue, bringing in thousands of gold each day. There were ten thousand household servants skilled in arithmetic. They collected rare goods from all over the world, accumulating them in the Yue capital to make them into objects. Copper and iron were piled up like mountains, sometimes hidden in wells and trenches, called 'Treasure Wells'. Beautiful faces and colors overflowed the inner chambers, called 'Wandering Palaces'. Since ancient times, there has never been anything like this. [56]

    录曰：《易》尚谦益，《书》着明谟，人臣之体，以斯为上。《传》曰：“知无不为，忠也。”范蠡陈工术之本，而勾践乃霸，卒王百越，称为富强，斯其力矣。故能佯狂以晦迹，浮海以避世，因三徙以别名，功遂身退，斯其义也。至如“宝井”、“游宫”，虽奢不惑。夫兴亡之道，匪推之历数，亦由才力而致也。覌越之灭吴，屈柔之礼尽焉，荐非世之绝姬，收历代之神宝，斯皆迹殊而事同矣。博识君子，验斯言焉。 [57]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [57]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 錄曰 - Formal introductory phrase in classical texts, similar to 'It is recorded that' or 'The record states that'.
    • 《易》尚謙益 - From the *Yijing* (Book of Changes), it emphasizes the value of humility and benefit.
    • 《書》著明謨 - From the *Shujing* (Book of Documents), it highlights the importance of wise counsel.
    • 人臣之體，以斯為上 - Indicates that the behavior of ministers should be guided by these principles.
    • 知無不為，忠也 - Quote from the *Zuo Zhuan* (Zuo's Commentary on the Spring and Autumn Annals), meaning 'to know nothing one should not do is loyalty'.
    • 范蠡陳工術之本，而勾踐乃霸，卒王百越 - Refers to the historical account of Fan Li (范蠡) and King Goujian (勾踐) of Yue, emphasizing their strategies and achievements.
    • 佯狂以晦迹，浮海以避世 - Describes the act of feigning madness to hide one's identity and sailing away to avoid the world.
    • 因三徙以别名 - Indicates the practice of changing one's name and location three times to avoid recognition.
    • 功遂身退，斯其义也 - Meaning 'to withdraw after achieving success, this is righteousness'.
    • 寶井、游宫 - Refers to the luxurious well and pleasure palace, symbolizing extravagance.
    • 興亡之道，匪推之歷數，亦由才力而致也 - Discusses the principles of rise and fall, attributing them to both destiny and personal ability.
    • 覌越之滅吴，屈柔之礼尽焉 - Describes the fall of Wu by the state of Yue, highlighting the importance of diplomacy and flexibility.
    • 荐非世之绝姬，收历代之神宝 - Refers to the acquisition of rare and precious items, emphasizing the value of collecting.
    • 博识君子，验斯言焉 - A concluding phrase encouraging learned individuals to verify the truth of these statements.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 《易》（Yijing） - The *Book of Changes*, an ancient Chinese divination text and philosophical work.
    • 《书》（Shujing） - The *Book of Documents*, a collection of ancient Chinese historical documents and sayings.
    • 范蠡（Fan Li） - A famous statesman and strategist during the Spring and Autumn period, known for his wisdom and strategic advice to King Goujian.
    • 勾踐（Goujian） - The king of Yue, who, with the help of Fan Li, defeated the powerful state of Wu.
    • 百越 - Refers to the various tribes and states in southern China during the Spring and Autumn and Warring States periods.
    • 寶井（Baojing） - A luxurious well, often associated with wealth and luxury.
    • 游宫（Yougong） - A pleasure palace, symbolizing indulgence and luxury.
    • 覌越之滅吴 - The historical event where the state of Yue, under the leadership of King Goujian, defeated the state of Wu.
    • 屈柔之礼 - Refers to the flexible and diplomatic approach taken by Yue in its dealings with Wu.

    Proper Nouns

    • 《易》（Yijing） - The *Book of Changes*.
    • 《书》（Shujing） - The *Book of Documents*.
    • 范蠡（Fan Li） - A famous statesman and strategist.
    • 勾踐（Goujian） - The king of Yue.
    • 百越 - Various tribes and states in southern China.
    • 寶井（Baojing） - A luxurious well.
    • 游宫（Yougong） - A pleasure palace.
    • 覌越之滅吴 - The historical event of Yue defeating Wu.
    • 屈柔之礼 - The flexible and diplomatic approach.

    The Record states: The Yijing (Book of Changes) extols humility and benefit, while the Shujing (Book of Documents) emphasizes wise counsel; thus, the conduct of ministers should be guided by these principles. It is said, "To know nothing one should not do is loyalty." Fan Li expounded the fundamentals of statecraft, and King Goujian of Yue rose to dominance, eventually becoming the ruler of the states of the south, known for his wealth and strength, which were the result of his abilities. Therefore, he was able to feign madness to conceal his identity, sail away to avoid the world, change his name and location three times, and withdraw after achieving success, which is the essence of righteousness. Even in matters such as the "Luxurious Well" and the "Pleasure Palace," he remained undistracted by luxury. The principles of rise and fall are not solely determined by fate, but also by personal ability. The fall of Wu by the state of Yue was due to the full use of diplomacy and flexibility, and the acquisition of rare and precious items from various dynasties, all of which, despite their different appearances, share the same underlying principles. Learned gentlemen, verify these words. [57]

    拾遗记卷四

    燕昭王(五事)

    　　王即位二年，广延国来献善舞者二人：一名旋娟，一名提谟，并玉质凝肤，体轻气馥，绰约而窈窕，绝古无伦。或行无迹影，或积年不饥。昭王处以单绡华幄，饮以瓀鈱之膏，饴以丹泉之粟。王登崇霞之台，乃召二人，徘徊翔舞，殆不自支。王以缨?娄拂之，二人皆舞。容冶妖丽，靡于鸾翔，而歌声轻扬。乃使女伶代唱其曲，清响流韵，虽飘梁动木，未足嘉也。其舞一名《萦尘》，言其体轻与尘相乱；次曰《集羽》，言其婉转若羽毛之从风；末曰《旋怀》，言其肢体缠曼，若入怀袖也。 [58]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [58]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 燕昭王 - King of Yan, known for his efforts to strengthen his state.
    • 廣延國 - A fictional or allegorical country, likely created for the narrative.
    • 旋娟，提谟 - Names of the dancers, which may have symbolic or allegorical significance.
    • 玉质凝肤 - Describes their skin as smooth and beautiful, like jade.
    • 体轻气馥 - Describes their lightness and fragrance, suggesting ethereal beauty.
    • 绰约而窈窕 - Describes their graceful and slender appearance.
    • 绝古无伦 - Means 'without equal in ancient times,' emphasizing their extraordinary beauty.
    • 行无迹影 - Describes their movements as leaving no trace or shadow, suggesting supernatural qualities.
    • 积年不饥 - Describes their ability to survive for years without food, another supernatural quality.
    • 单绡华幄 - Refers to a luxurious silk tent or room.
    • 瓀鈱之膏 - Refers to a precious liquid, possibly a metaphor for a special drink.
    • 丹泉之粟 - Refers to a special grain, possibly a metaphor for a special food.
    • 崇霞之台 - Refers to a high and beautiful platform, possibly a metaphor for a grand stage.
    • 萦尘 - A dance name meaning 'entwined with dust,' suggesting a light and airy movement.
    • 集羽 - A dance name meaning 'gathered feathers,' suggesting a graceful and flowing movement.
    • 旋怀 - A dance name meaning 'spinning in the embrace,' suggesting a close and intimate movement.
    • 容冶妖丽 - Describes their appearance as beautiful and alluring.
    • 靡于鸾翔 - Describes their movements as more graceful than those of a phoenix.
    • 清响流韵 - Describes their singing as clear and melodious, with a flowing tone.
    • 飘梁动木 - Describes the effect of their singing as causing beams and trees to move, suggesting a powerful and magical sound.
    • 无不足以嘉也 - Means 'nothing is insufficiently praiseworthy,' emphasizing the excellence of their performance.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 燕昭王 - A historical figure known for his efforts to strengthen his state, particularly through the recruitment of talented individuals.
    • 廣延國 - A fictional or allegorical country, possibly created to highlight the exotic and supernatural qualities of the dancers.
    • 旋娟，提谟 - Names of the dancers, which may have symbolic or allegorical significance, though their exact meaning is unclear.
    • 崇霞之台 - A metaphorical description of a grand and beautiful platform, possibly inspired by classical Chinese poetry and literature.

    Proper Nouns

    • 燕昭王 - The King of Yan.
    • 廣延國 - The fictional or allegorical country.
    • 旋娟，提谟 - The names of the dancers.
    • 崇霞之台 - The name of the grand platform.

    Translation:

    The Book of Lost Memories, Volume Four

    King Zhao of Yan (Five Matters)

    Two years after ascending the throne, King Zhao received two skilled dancers from the distant land of Guangyan: one named Xuan Juan and the other Ti Mo. Both had skin as smooth as jade, light as air, and exuded a fragrant aura. They were graceful and slender, unparalleled in ancient times. Their movements left no trace or shadow, and they could survive for years without food. King Zhao provided them with a luxurious silk tent and offered them precious liquid and special grains. Upon ascending the magnificent platform of Chong Xia, he summoned the two dancers, who performed a dance that was so enchanting it seemed they could not bear the weight of their own beauty. The king waved a silk ribbon at them, and they began to dance. Their appearance was so beautiful and alluring that it surpassed even the grace of a phoenix, and their singing was clear and melodious, with a flowing tone. He then had a female singer perform their song, which was so clear and resonant that it seemed to cause the beams and trees to move, but it was still not sufficient to praise their performance. Their dance had three names: "Ying Chen," which described how their light bodies seemed to intertwine with the dust; "Ji Yu," which described how their movements were as graceful as feathers dancing in the wind; and "Xuan Huai," which described how their limbs were so entwined and delicate that they seemed to enter the folds of one's sleeves. [58]

    乃设麟文之席，散荃芜之香。香出波弋国，浸地则土石皆香，着朽木腐草，莫不郁茂，以熏枯骨，则肌肉皆生。以屑喷地，厚四五寸，使二女舞其上，弥日无迹，体轻故也。时有白鸾孤翔，衔千茎穟。穟于空中自生，花实落地，则生根叶。一岁百获，一茎满车，故曰“盈车嘉穟”。麟文者，错杂宝以饰席也，皆为云霞麟凤之状。昭王复以衣袖麾之，舞者皆止。昭王知其神异，处于崇霞之台，设枕席以寝宴，遣侍人以卫之。王好神仙之术，玄天之女，托形作此二人。昭王之末，莫知所在。或云游于汉江，或伊洛之滨。 [59]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [59]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 乃设麟文之席 - Indicates a sequence of events; '设' means 'to set up' or 'to arrange'; '麟文之席' refers to a luxurious or ornate seating arrangement decorated with patterns of qilin (a mythical creature).
    • 散荃芜之香 - '散' means 'to disperse' or 'to spread'; '荃芜之香' refers to a fragrant substance, possibly a perfume or incense.
    • 香出波弋国 - '波弋国' is likely a fictional or mythical place name, possibly referring to a distant land known for its unique perfumes.
    • 浸地则土石皆香 - '浸地' means 'to soak into the ground'; '土石皆香' means 'the soil and stones all become fragrant.'
    • 着朽木腐草，莫不郁茂 - '着' means 'to stick to' or 'to adhere to'; '朽木腐草' refers to decaying wood and grass; '莫不郁茂' means 'nothing fails to flourish.'
    • 以熏枯骨，则肌肉皆生 - '熏' means 'to fumigate' or 'to smoke'; '枯骨' means 'dry bones'; '肌肉皆生' means 'the flesh and muscles all grow back.'
    • 以屑喷地，厚四五寸 - '屑' means 'powder' or 'crushed material'; '喷地' means 'to sprinkle on the ground'; '厚四五寸' means 'about four to five inches thick.'
    • 使二女舞其上，弥日无迹，体轻故也 - '使' means 'to make' or 'to cause'; '二女' means 'two women'; '舞其上' means 'dance on it'; '弥日无迹' means 'for a whole day without leaving any trace'; '体轻故也' means 'because their bodies are light.'
    • 时有白鸾孤翔，衔千茎穟 - '时有' means 'sometimes there is'; '白鸾' means 'white phoenix'; '孤翔' means 'flying alone'; '衔千茎穟' means 'carrying thousands of ears of grain.'
    • 穟于空中自生，花实落地，则生根叶 - '穟' means 'ear of grain'; '自生' means 'to grow by itself'; '花实落地' means 'the flowers and fruits fall to the ground'; '生根叶' means 'sprout roots and leaves.'
    • 一岁百获，一茎满车，故曰“盈车嘉穟” - '一岁百获' means 'a hundred harvests in one year'; '一茎满车' means 'one stalk fills a cart'; '故曰”盈车嘉穟”' means 'therefore it is called 'abundant grains filling the cart.'
    • 麟文者，错杂宝以饰席也，皆为云霞麟凤之状 - '麟文者' means 'the qilin pattern'; '错杂宝' means 'a mixture of precious materials'; '饰席' means 'to decorate the seating'; '云霞麟凤之状' means 'in the shapes of clouds, rainbows, qilin, and phoenixes.'
    • 昭王复以衣袖麾之，舞者皆止 - '复' means 'again'; '以衣袖麾之' means 'with his sleeve he waved'; '舞者皆止' means 'the dancers all stopped.'
    • 昭王知其神异，处于崇霞之台，设枕席以寝宴，遣侍人以卫之 - '知其神异' means 'knew of their supernatural nature'; '处于崇霞之台' means 'resided on the high platform of the towering clouds'; '设枕席以寝宴' means 'arranged pillows and seating for sleeping and feasting'; '遣侍人以卫之' means 'sent attendants to guard them.'
    • 王好神仙之术，玄天之女，托形作此二人 - '好神仙之术' means 'enjoyed the practices of immortals'; '玄天之女' means 'the daughters of the Heavenly Realm'; '托形作此二人' means 'took the forms of these two women.'
    • 昭王之末，莫知所在 - '昭王之末' means 'at the end of King Zhao's reign'; '莫知所在' means 'no one knew where they were.'
    • 或云游于汉江，或伊洛之滨 - '或云' means 'it is said'; '游于汉江' means 'traveling along the Han River'; '伊洛之滨' means 'along the banks of the Yi and Luo rivers.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 麟文 - Refers to a decorative pattern featuring the qilin, a mythical creature symbolizing peace and prosperity.
    • 波弋国 - A fictional or mythical place known for its unique perfumes.
    • 白鸾 - A mythical white phoenix, often associated with good fortune and purity.
    • 昭王 - Likely refers to a historical or legendary king, possibly a ruler of a certain dynasty.
    • 神仙之术 - Refers to practices and beliefs related to immortality and the supernatural.
    • 玄天之女 - Refers to celestial beings or deities from the Heavenly Realm.
    • 崇霞之台 - A high platform or pavilion, often associated with divine or royal residences.
    • 汉江 - The Han River, a major river in China.
    • 伊洛之滨 - The banks of the Yi and Luo rivers, significant locations in Chinese history and mythology.

    Proper Nouns

    • 昭王 - The name of a historical or legendary king.
    • 波弋国 - A fictional or mythical place name.
    • 汉江 - The Han River.
    • 伊洛之滨 - The banks of the Yi and Luo rivers.
    • 玄天之女 - The daughters of the Heavenly Realm.

    King Zhao then arranged seating with a qilin pattern and scattered the fragrant substance of Qunwu. The fragrance came from the distant land of Boyige, and when it soaked into the ground, the soil and stones all became fragrant. It adhered to decaying wood and grass, and nothing failed to flourish. When used to fumigate dry bones, the flesh and muscles all grew back. When the powder was sprinkled on the ground, about four to five inches thick, two women could dance on it for a whole day without leaving any trace, because their bodies were light. Sometimes a white phoenix would fly alone, carrying thousands of ears of grain. The grain grew by itself in the air, and when the flowers and fruits fell to the ground, they sprouted roots and leaves. In one year, a hundred harvests were gathered, and one stalk filled a cart, hence it was called "abundant grains filling the cart." The qilin pattern was a mixture of precious materials used to decorate the seating, all in the shapes of clouds, rainbows, qilin, and phoenixes. King Zhao then waved his sleeve, and the dancers all stopped. Knowing of their supernatural nature, King Zhao had them reside on the high platform of the towering clouds, arranging pillows and seating for sleeping and feasting, and sending attendants to guard them. The king enjoyed the practices of immortals, and the daughters of the Heavenly Realm took the forms of these two women. At the end of King Zhao's reign, no one knew where they were. It is said they traveled along the Han River or along the banks of the Yi and Luo rivers. [59]

    四年，王居正寝，召其臣甘需曰：“寡人志于仙道，欲学长生久视之法，可得遂乎？”需曰：“臣游昆台之山，见有垂白之叟，宛若少童，貌如冰雪，形如处子。血清骨劲，肤实肠轻，乃历蓬、瀛而超碧海，经涉升降，游往无穷，此为上仙之人也。盖能去滞欲而离嗜爱，洗神灭念，常游于太极之门。今大王以妖容惑目，美味爽口，列女成群，迷心动虑，所爱之容，恐不及玉，纤腰皓齿，患不如神。而欲却老云游，何异操圭爵以量沧海，执毫厘而回日月，其可得乎！”昭王乃彻色减味，居乎正寝，赐甘需羽衣一袭，表其墟为“明真理”也。 [60]

    In the fourth year, King Zhao resided in his main hall and summoned his minister Gan Xu, saying, "I have set my heart on the path of immortality and wish to learn the method of long life and enduring vision. Can I achieve this?" Gan Xu replied, "I once traveled to Mount Kuntuo and saw an elderly man with long white hair, who appeared as if he were a young boy, with a face as pure as ice and snow and a body as that of a maiden. His blood was clear and his bones strong, his skin firm and his intestines light. He had journeyed through the lands of Peng and Ying and beyond the blue sea, experiencing the ups and downs of the spiritual realm, traveling endlessly. This is a supreme immortal. He has the ability to eliminate desires and detach from attachments, to cleanse his spirit and extinguish his thoughts, and to frequently travel through the gates of Taiji. Now, Your Majesty is bewitched by beautiful appearances that deceive the eyes, indulges in delicious food that pleases the palate, and is surrounded by a group of beautiful women, bewiling your heart and confusing your mind. The beauty of the beloved is feared to be less than jade, and the slender waist and white teeth are worrying that they are not as divine. And yet you wish to resist aging and wander in the clouds. How is this different from measuring the vast ocean with a small measure or turning the sun and moon with a tiny tool? Can it be achieved?" King Zhao then abandoned colors and flavors, resided in his main hall, bestowed a robe made of feathers upon Gan Xu, and designated the place as "The Clarification of Truth." [60]

    七年，沐胥之国来朝，则申毒国之一名也。道术人名尸?。问其年，云：“百三十岁。”荷锡持瓶，云：“发其国五年乃至燕都。“善衒惑之术。于其指端出浮屠十层，高三尺，及诸天神仙，巧丽特绝。人皆长五六分，列幢盖，鼓舞，绕塔而行，歌唱之音，如真人矣。尸?喷水为雰雾，暗数里间。俄而复吹为疾风，雰雾皆止。又吹指上浮屠，渐入云里。又于左耳出青龙，右耳出白虎。始入之时，才一二寸，稍至八九尺。俄而风至云起，即以一手挥之，即龙虎皆入耳中。又张口向日，则见人乘羽盖，驾螭、鹄，直入于口内。复以手抑胸上，而闻怀袖之中，轰轰雷声。更张口，则见羽盖、螭、鹄相随从口中而出。尸?常坐日中，渐渐觉其形小，或化为老叟，或为婴儿，倏忽而死，香气盈室，时有清风来吹之，更生如向之形。咒术衒惑，神怪无穷。 [61]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [61]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 七年 - Indicates a specific year, which may be part of a larger historical context.
    • 沐胥之国来朝 - Refers to a nation called '沐胥' paying homage, a classical diplomatic act.
    • 申毒国之一名也 - Indicates that '沐胥' is another name for '申毒国'.
    • 道术人名尸? - Describes a person named '尸?' who practices a certain art or skill.
    • 问其年，云：‘百三十岁。’ - A direct question about age, with an answer indicating an unusually long lifespan.
    • 荷锡持瓶 - Describes a person carrying a staff and a bottle, likely for alchemy or other practices.
    • 云：‘发其国五年乃至燕都。’ - Indicates a journey from '其国' to '燕都' taking five years.
    • 善衒惑之术 - Describes the skill of '尸?' as being adept at deception or illusion.
    • 于其指端出浮屠十层，高三尺 - Describes a multi-layered pagoda emerging from the fingertips, a fantastical element.
    • 及诸天神仙，巧丽特绝 - Describes the appearance of various celestial beings and deities, emphasizing their beauty and uniqueness.
    • 人皆长五六分，列幢盖，鼓舞，绕塔而行，歌唱之音，如真人矣 - Describes miniature figures performing around the pagoda, with their sounds and movements being lifelike.
    • 尸?喷水为雰雾，暗数里间 - Describes '尸?' creating a mist that obscures visibility over several miles.
    • 俄而复吹为疾风，雰雾皆止 - Describes the mist being dispersed by a strong wind.
    • 又吹指上浮屠，渐入云里 - Describes the pagoda rising into the clouds.
    • 又于左耳出青龙，右耳出白虎 - Describes dragons and tigers emerging from the ears, a fantastical element.
    • 始入之时，才一二寸，稍至八九尺 - Describes the initial size of the dragons and tigers, growing larger over time.
    • 俄而风至云起，即以一手挥之，即龙虎皆入耳中 - Describes the wind and clouds rising, and '尸?' waving his hand to make the dragons and tigers disappear back into his ears.
    • 又张口向日，则见人乘羽盖，驾螭、鹄，直入于口内 - Describes people riding on feathered canopies and mythical creatures entering '尸?''s mouth.
    • 复以手抑胸上，而闻怀袖之中，轰轰雷声 - Describes '尸?' pressing his hand against his chest, producing thunder-like sounds.
    • 更张口，则见羽盖、螭、鹄相随从口中而出 - Describes the people and creatures re-emerging from '尸?''s mouth.
    • 尸?常坐日中，渐渐觉其形小，或化为老叟，或为婴儿，倏忽而死，香气盈室，时有清风来吹之，更生如向之形 - Describes '尸?''s ability to change form, die, and be reborn, with a focus on his transformation and the mystical elements involved.
    • 咒术衒惑，神怪无穷 - Describes the use of spells and illusions, emphasizing the endless variety of supernatural phenomena.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 沐胥之国 - A fictional or historical nation, possibly a reference to a known region or a mythical place.
    • 申毒国 - Another name for '沐胥之国,' possibly indicating a historical or mythological connection.
    • 浮屠 - Refers to a pagoda, a common architectural feature in Chinese culture, often associated with Buddhist temples.
    • 诸天神仙 - Refers to various celestial beings and deities, common in Chinese mythology and folklore.
    • 青龙、白虎 - Refers to the Four Symbols in Chinese cosmology, representing the four cardinal directions and elements.
    • 螭、鹄 - Refers to mythical creatures, often associated with Chinese mythology and folklore.
    • 咒术 - Refers to spells or incantations, common in various forms of Chinese magic and ritual practices.

    Proper Nouns

    • 沐胥之国 - A fictional or historical nation.
    • 申毒国 - Another name for '沐胥之国.'
    • 尸? - The name of the person practicing a certain art or skill.
    • 燕都 - A place, possibly a capital city.
    • 青龙 - A mythical creature representing the east.
    • 白虎 - A mythical creature representing the west.
    • 螭 - A mythical creature.
    • 鹄 - A mythical creature.
    • 咒术 - A form of magic or ritual practice.

    In the seventh year, the nation of Muxu came to pay homage, which is another name for the nation of Shendu. A person named Shi practiced a certain art or skill. When asked about his age, he said, "One hundred and thirty years." He carried a staff and a bottle, and said, "It took me five years to travel from my country to Yan Capital." He was skilled in the art of deception and illusion. From the tips of his fingers emerged a ten-story pagoda, three feet high, along with various celestial beings and deities, all exquisitely beautiful and unique. The figures were all about five or six inches tall, arranged in a procession, drumming and dancing, circling the pagoda, and singing in a voice as real as that of living people. Shi exhaled water to create a mist that obscured visibility for several miles. Soon, he blew it away with a strong wind, dispersing the mist. He then blew the pagoda on his fingers, making it rise into the clouds. From his left ear emerged a green dragon, and from his right ear emerged a white tiger. Initially, they were only an inch or two long, but they grew to eight or nine feet in length. As the wind rose and the clouds gathered, he waved his hand, and the dragon and tiger disappeared back into his ears. He then opened his mouth toward the sun, and people riding on feathered canopies and driving mythical creatures entered his mouth. He pressed his hand against his chest, and a thunderous sound could be heard from within his sleeves. When he opened his mouth again, the people and creatures emerged from his mouth, following each other. Shi often sat in the sun, gradually feeling his form shrink, sometimes transforming into an old man, sometimes into an infant, dying suddenly, filling the room with fragrance, and occasionally being blown by a cool breeze, reborn in the same form as before. His spells and illusions were endless, and the supernatural phenomena were boundless. [61]

    八年，卢扶国来朝，渡河万里方至。云其国中山川无恶禽兽。水不扬波，风不折木。人皆寿三百岁，结草为衣，是谓卉服。至死不老，咸知孝让。寿登百岁以上，相敬如至亲之礼。死葬于野外，以香木灵草瘗掩其尸。闾里助送，号泣之音，动于林谷，河源为之流止，春木为之改色。居丧水浆不入于口，至死者骨为尘埃，然后乃食。昔大禹随山导川，乃旌其地为无老纯孝之国。
    　　录曰：夫含灵禀气，取象二仪；受命因生，包乎五德。故守淳明以循身，资施以为本。义缘天属，生尽爱敬之容；体自心慈，死结追终之慕。盖处物之常情，有识之常道。是以忠谏一至，则会理以通幽；神义由心，则祇灵为之昭感。迹显神着，表降群祥，行道不违，远迩旌德。美乎异国之人，隔绝王化，阙闻大道，语其国法，华戎有殊，观其政教，颇令殊俗。礼在四夷，事存诸诰，孝让之风，莫不尚也。 [62]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [62]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 八年 - Refers to the eighth year of a specific reign period, likely the reign of a Chinese emperor.
    • 渡河萬里方至 - Literally 'crossing the river for ten thousand miles to arrive,' used to describe a long and arduous journey.
    • 無惡禽獸 - Means 'no evil beasts,' indicating a peaceful and harmonious environment.
    • 水不揚波，風不折木 - Describes a serene natural environment where the water does not create waves and the wind does not break trees.
    • 人皆壽三百歲 - Means 'people all live for three hundred years,' indicating a long lifespan.
    • 結草為衣，是謂卉服 - Means 'binding grass to make clothing, which is called 'grass clothes,' describing a simple and natural way of dressing.
    • 至死不老 - Means 'until death, they do not age,' indicating eternal youth.
    • 咸知孝讓 - Means 'all know filial piety and yielding,' indicating a society that values respect and humility.
    • 壽登百岁以上，相敬如至亲之礼 - Means 'those who live beyond a hundred years, they respect each other as if they were closest relatives,' describing a deep respect among the elderly.
    • 死葬于野外，以香木灵草瘗掩其尸 - Means 'they bury the dead in the wild, using fragrant wood and grass to cover the body,' describing a respectful and natural burial practice.
    • 闾里助送，号泣之音，动于林谷，河源为之流止，春木为之改色 - Describes the emotional impact of the funeral, where the community mourns, and the natural world responds.
    • 居丧水浆不入于口，至死者骨为尘埃，然后乃食 - Means 'during mourning, they do not eat or drink until the bones of the deceased turn to dust, then they resume eating,' describing a strict period of mourning.
    • 昔大禹随山导川，乃旌其地为无老纯孝之国 - Means 'In ancient times, Yu the Great followed the mountains and guided the rivers, and thus he honored this land as the country of the ageless and pure filial piety,' describing a legendary event that honors the country.
    • 录曰 - A formal introduction to a commentary or record.
    • 含灵禀气，取象二仪 - Means 'those who contain spirit and receive breath, taking their form from the two instruments,' referring to the natural world.
    • 受命因生，包乎五德 - Means 'receiving the mandate and thus living, encompassing the five virtues,' referring to the moral and ethical principles.
    • 守淳明以循身，资施以为本 - Means 'maintaining purity and clarity to guide one's actions, and using generosity as the foundation,' describing a virtuous way of life.
    • 义缘天属，生尽爱敬之容 - Means 'righteousness stemming from natural connections, showing love and respect throughout life,' describing the importance of familial and social bonds.
    • 体自心慈，死结追终之慕 - Means 'having a kind heart, even in death, there is a longing for the end,' describing the emotional aspect of death.
    • 盖处物之常情，有识之常道 - Means 'this is the constant nature of things and the common path of those who understand,' describing a universal truth.
    • 是以忠谏一至，则会理以通幽 - Means 'thus, when a loyal advisor speaks once, it brings understanding and illuminates the hidden,' describing the impact of wise counsel.
    • 神义由心，则祇灵为之昭感 - Means 'when righteousness comes from the heart, the spirits are moved and respond,' describing the spiritual aspect of righteousness.
    • 迹显神着，表降群祥 - Means 'the signs are clear and the spirits are evident, bringing down many good omens,' describing the positive outcomes of virtuous actions.
    • 行道不违，远迩旌德 - Means 'following the path without deviation, both near and far, they are praised for their virtue,' describing the widespread recognition of virtue.
    • 美乎异国之人，隔绝王化，阙闻大道 - Means 'how admirable are the people of a foreign land, isolated from the king's influence, lacking knowledge of the great way,' describing the admiration for a distant and isolated society.
    • 语其国法，华戎有殊 - Means 'describing their laws, there is a difference between the Chinese and the barbarians,' highlighting the cultural differences.
    • 观其政教，颇令殊俗 - Means 'observing their political and educational systems, they are quite different from the usual customs,' describing the unique governance and education.
    • 礼在四夷，事存诸诰 - Means 'rites exist among the four barbarian regions, and these matters are recorded in the edicts,' describing the presence of rituals in distant lands.
    • 孝让之风，莫不尚也 - Means 'the spirit of filial piety and yielding is highly valued,' emphasizing the importance of these virtues.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 大禹 - Refers to Yu the Great, a legendary Chinese ruler and founder of the Xia Dynasty, known for his efforts in flood control and the establishment of a centralized government.
    • 五德 - Refers to the five virtues, which are often associated with Confucian ethics: benevolence (仁), righteousness (义), propriety (礼), wisdom (智), and faithfulness (信).
    • 四夷 - Refers to the four barbarian regions, traditionally used to describe the non-Chinese peoples surrounding the Chinese civilization.
    • 王化 - Refers to the king's influence or the cultural and political influence of the central government.
    • 大道 - Refers to the great way or the path of righteousness and virtue.

    In the eighth year, envoys from Lu Fu Guo came to pay homage, having traveled a distance of ten thousand miles across the river to arrive. They reported that in their country, the mountains and rivers were serene, with no evil beasts. The waters did not create waves, and the winds did not break trees. The people all lived for three hundred years, binding grass to make clothing, which is called "grass clothes." They remained eternally young until death, and all knew the values of filial piety and yielding. Those who lived beyond a hundred years respected each other as if they were closest relatives. They buried the dead in the wild, using fragrant wood and grass to cover the body. The community assisted in the funeral, and the sounds of weeping echoed through the forests and valleys, causing the river's source to cease flowing and the spring trees to change color. During the mourning period, they did not eat or drink until the bones of the deceased turned to dust, then they resumed eating. In ancient times, Yu the Great followed the mountains and guided the rivers, and thus he honored this land as the country of the ageless and pure filial piety.

    The record states: Those who contain spirit and receive breath, taking their form from the two instruments; receiving the mandate and thus living, encompassing the five virtues. Therefore, maintaining purity and clarity to guide one's actions, and using generosity as the foundation. Righteousness stemming from natural connections, showing love and respect throughout life; having a kind heart, even in death, there is a longing for the end. This is the constant nature of things and the common path of those who understand. Thus, when a loyal advisor speaks once, it brings understanding and illuminates the hidden; when righteousness comes from the heart, the spirits are moved and respond. The signs are clear and the spirits are evident, bringing down many good omens; following the path without deviation, both near and far, they are praised for their virtue. How admirable are the people of a foreign land, isolated from the king's influence, lacking knowledge of the great way. Describing their laws, there is a difference between the Chinese and the barbarians; observing their political and educational systems, they are quite different from the usual customs. Rites exist among the four barbarian regions, and these matters are recorded in the edicts; the spirit of filial piety and yielding is highly valued. [62]

    九年，昭王思诸神异。有谷将子，学道之人也，言于王曰：“西王母将来游，必语虚无之术。”不逾一年，王母果至。与昭王游于燧林之下，说炎帝钻火之术。取绿桂之膏，燃以照夜。忽有飞蛾衔火，状如丹雀，来拂于桂膏之上。此蛾出于员丘之穴。穴洞达九天，中有细珠如流沙，可穿而结，因用为佩，此是神蛾之矢也。蛾凭气饮露，飞不集下，群仙杀此蛾合丹药。西王母与群仙游员丘之上，聚神蛾，以琼筐盛之，使玉童负筐，以游四极，来降燕庭，出此蛾以示昭王。王曰：“今乞此蛾以合九转神丹！”王母弗与。 [63]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [63]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 昭王思諸神異 - King Zhuang contemplates various divine wonders.
    • 學道之人也 - Refers to someone who studies the Dao (the Way), often translated as a 'Daoist scholar' or 'one who practices Daoism.'
    • 西王母將來游 - The Queen Mother of the West will come to visit.
    • 必語虛無之術 - Will surely speak about techniques or methods of achieving a state of emptiness or nothingness, often associated with Daoist practices.
    • 不逾一年 - Within a year.
    • 與昭王游於燧林之下 - Wanders with King Zhuang in a forest of firewood.
    • 說炎帝鑽火之術 - Discusses the technique of making fire by drilling, attributed to the Flame Emperor.
    • 取綠桂之膏，燃以照夜 - Burns the resin of the green osmanthus to light up the night.
    • 忽有飛蛾 - Suddenly there was a moth.
    • 狀如丹雀 - Appears like a red bird.
    • 來拂於桂膏之上 - Came to touch the osmanthus resin.
    • 此蛾出於員丘之穴 - The moth comes from a cave on Mount Yuanqiu.
    • 穴洞達九天 - The cave penetrates the highest heavens.
    • 中有細珠如流沙 - Inside the cave, there are tiny pearls like flowing sand.
    • 可穿而結 - Can be pierced and knotted.
    • 因用為佩 - Therefore used as an ornament.
    • 此是神蛾之矢也 - These are the arrows of the divine moth.
    • 蛾憑氣飲露 - The moth relies on qi (vital energy) and drinks dew.
    • 飛不集下 - Does not land.
    • 群仙殺此蛾合丹藥 - The group of immortals kills the moth to make elixirs.
    • 西王母與群仙游員丘之上 - The Queen Mother and immortals wander on Mount Yuanqiu.
    • 聚神蛾 - Gather divine moths.
    • 以琼筐盛之 - Place them in a jade basket.
    • 使玉童負筐 - Make a jade boy carry the basket.
    • 以游四极 - Wander to the four corners of the earth.
    • 來降燕庭 - Came down to the court of Yan.
    • 出此蛾以示昭王 - Show this moth to King Zhuang.
    • 王曰：“今乞此蛾以合九转神丹！” - King Zhuang says, 'I request this moth to make the Nine Transformation Elixir!'
    • 王母弗与 - The Queen Mother does not give it to him.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 昭王 - King Zhuang of Zhou, a ruler of the Zhou dynasty.
    • 西王母 - The Queen Mother of the West, a significant figure in Chinese mythology, often associated with longevity and immortality.
    • 炎帝 - The Flame Emperor, a legendary figure in Chinese mythology, known for his contributions to agriculture and fire-making.
    • 員丘 - A mythical mountain, often associated with the abode of immortals.
    • 九天 - The highest heavens, symbolizing the ultimate realm of spiritual attainment.
    • 九转神丹 - A legendary elixir of immortality, often mentioned in Daoist texts.
    • 燕庭 - The court of Yan, a state during the Warring States period.

    Proper Nouns

    • 昭王 - King Zhuang of Zhou.
    • 西王母 - The Queen Mother of the West.
    • 炎帝 - The Flame Emperor.
    • 員丘 - Mount Yuanqiu.
    • 九天 - The highest heavens.
    • 九转神丹 - The Nine Transformation Elixir.
    • 燕庭 - The court of Yan.

    Nine years later, King Zhuang pondered the various divine wonders. A Daoist scholar named Gu Zang spoke to the king, saying, "The Queen Mother of the West will come to visit, and she will surely speak of the techniques of emptiness." Within a year, the Queen Mother indeed arrived. She wandered with King Zhuang in the forest of firewood, discussing the method of making fire by drilling, attributed to the Flame Emperor. They collected the resin of the green osmanthus and burned it to light the night. Suddenly, a moth appeared, carrying a flame, its appearance like that of a red bird, and it touched the resin. This moth emerged from a cave on Mount Yuanqiu. The cave penetrated the highest heavens, and inside were tiny pearls like flowing sand, which could be pierced and knotted, used as ornaments, and were the arrows of the divine moth. The moth relied on qi and drank dew, never landing, and was killed by immortals to make elixirs. The Queen Mother and the immortals gathered divine moths, placed them in a jade basket, and had a jade boy carry it to wander the four corners of the earth. They descended to the court of Yan and showed the moth to King Zhuang. The king said, "I request this moth to make the Nine Transformation Elixir!" But the Queen Mother did not give it to him. [63]

    昭王坐握日之台参云，上可扪日。时有黑鸟白头，集王之所，衔洞光之珠，圆径一尺。此珠色黑如漆，悬照于室内，百神不能隐其精灵。此珠出阴泉之底，阴泉在寒山之北，员水之中，言水波常圆转而流也。有黑蚌飞翔，来去于五岳之上。昔黄帝时，雾成子游寒山之岭，得黑蚌在高崖之上，故知黑蚌能飞矣。至燕昭王时，有国献于昭王。王取瑶漳之水，洗其沙泥，乃嗟叹曰：“自悬日月以来，见黑蚌生珠已八九十遇，此蚌千岁一生珠也。”珠渐轻细。昭王常怀此珠，当隆暑之月，体自轻凉，号曰“销暑招凉之珠”也。 [64]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [64]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 昭王坐握日之台参云 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'King Zhao sat on the *Worri Sun* platform, which reached into the clouds.'
    • 上可扪日 - Hyperbolic expression meaning 'one could touch the sun,' indicating the platform's great height.
    • 黑鸟白头 - Literal description of a bird with a black body and white head.
    • 洞光之珠 - Pearl with the property of illuminating darkness, suggesting it is highly valuable and magical.
    • 圆径一尺 - Specific measurement, meaning the pearl has a diameter of one foot.
    • 百神不能隐其精灵 - Superlative phrase meaning 'no gods or spirits could conceal its spirit,' emphasizing the pearl's supernatural power.
    • 阴泉之底 - Refers to the bottom of a dark spring, indicating a mysterious and hidden location.
    • 寒山之北 - Indicates a specific geographical location, the north side of Cold Mountain.
    • 员水之中 - Refers to a circular or round body of water, suggesting a unique and possibly mystical setting.
    • 黑蚌飞翔 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'black abalone flying,' indicating a rare and extraordinary phenomenon.
    • 五岳之上 - Refers to the highest points of the Five Great Mountains, emphasizing the bird's ability to fly to great heights.
    • 黄帝时 - Refers to the time of the legendary Yellow Emperor, a significant figure in Chinese mythology.
    • 雾成子 - Name that could be a historical or mythological figure, possibly a hermit or sage.
    • 高崖之上 - Refers to a high cliff, indicating a dramatic and elevated setting.
    • 悬日月 - Hyperbolic expression meaning 'since the time of the sun and moon,' indicating an extremely long period.
    • 销暑招凉之珠 - Descriptive name for the pearl, meaning 'pearl that dispels heat and attracts coolness.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 昭王 - Refers to King Zhao of Yan, a historical figure known for his efforts to strengthen the state of Yan.
    • 燕昭王 - Another reference to King Zhao of Yan, emphasizing his reign.
    • 瑶漳之水 - Refers to the Jade River, a mythical or significant body of water in the story.
    • 黄帝 - The legendary Yellow Emperor, a central figure in Chinese mythology and the founder of Chinese civilization.
    • 雾成子 - Name that could be a historical or mythological figure, possibly a hermit or sage.
    • 寒山 - A specific mountain, possibly a real or mythical location.
    • 五岳 - Refers to the Five Great Mountains, which are Mount Tai, Mount Hua, Mount Heng (Shanxi), Mount Heng (Hunan), and Mount Song, significant in Chinese culture and religion.

    Proper Nouns

    • 昭王 - King Zhao of Yan.
    • 燕昭王 - Another reference to King Zhao of Yan.
    • 瑶漳之水 - The Jade River.
    • 黄帝 - The Yellow Emperor.
    • 雾成子 - A name that could be a historical or mythological figure.
    • 寒山 - A specific mountain.
    • 五岳 - The Five Great Mountains.

    King Zhao sat on the Worri Sun platform, which reached into the clouds, and from where one could touch the sun. At that time, a bird with a black body and white head came to the king's place, carrying a pearl that illuminated darkness, with a diameter of one foot. This pearl was black like lacquer and illuminated the room when hung inside, with no gods or spirits able to conceal its spirit. This pearl came from the bottom of a dark spring, located in the north of Cold Mountain, in a round body of water, where the water waves always flowed in a circular motion. There was a black abalone that flew, coming and going among the Five Great Mountains. In the time of the Yellow Emperor, the hermit Fog Chengzi traveled the peaks of Cold Mountain and found the black abalone on a high cliff, thus knowing that the black abalone could fly. By the time of King Zhao of Yan, a state presented the pearl to King Zhao. The king washed the sand and mud with water from the Jade River and then exclaimed, "Since the time of the sun and moon, I have seen the black abalone produce pearls eighty to ninety times, and this abalone produces a pearl once every thousand years." The pearl gradually became lighter and finer. King Zhao often carried this pearl, and during the hottest months, his body felt cool and light, and it was called the "Pearl that Dispels Heat and Attracts Coolness." [64]

    秦始皇(四事)

    　　始皇元年，骞霄国献刻玉善画工名裔。使含丹青以漱地，即成魑魅及诡怪群物之像；刻玉为百兽之形，毛发宛若真矣。皆铭其臆前，记以日月。工人以指画地，长百丈，直如绳墨。方寸之内，画以四渎五岳列国之图。又画为龙凤，骞翥若飞。皆不可点睛，或点之，必飞走也。始皇嗟曰：“刻画之形，何得飞走！”使以淳漆各点两玉虎一眼眼，旬日则失之，不知所在。山泽之人云：“见二白虎，各无一目，相随而行，毛色相似，异于常见者。”至明年，西方献两白虎，各无一目。始皇发槛视之，疑是先所失者，乃刺杀之。检其胸前，果是元年所刻玉虎。迄胡亥之灭，宝剑神物，随时散乱也。 [65]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [65]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 含丹青以漱地 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'to rinse the ground with cinnabar and verdigris,' referring to the magical ability of the artist to create images on the ground.
    • 魑魅及诡怪群物之像 - Refers to images of mythical and monstrous creatures.
    • 刻玉为百兽之形 - Means 'carving jade into the shapes of a hundred beasts,' referring to the artist's skill in carving jade.
    • 毛发宛若真矣 - Means 'the fur is as real as if it were alive,' emphasizing the lifelike quality of the carvings.
    • 皆铭其臆前，记以日月 - Means 'all inscribed on their chests, marked with the date and month,' indicating the artist's method of recording the date of creation.
    • 工人以指画地，长百丈，直如绳墨 - Means 'the artist drew on the ground with his finger, a hundred paces long, straight as a string and plumb line,' emphasizing the artist's skill in drawing.
    • 方寸之内，画以四渎五岳列国之图 - Means 'within a square inch, he drew maps of the four rivers, five mountains, and various states,' indicating the artist's ability to create detailed maps.
    • 又画为龙凤，骞翥若飞 - Means 'he also drew dragons and phoenixes, soaring as if flying,' emphasizing the artist's skill in drawing mythical creatures.
    • 皆不可点睛，或点之，必飞走也 - Means 'none of them could have their eyes painted, or if they were, they would fly away,' indicating the magical nature of the drawings.
    • 始皇嗟曰 - Means 'the First Emperor sighed and said,' indicating his reaction to the artist's work.
    • 刻画之形，何得飞走 - Means 'how can the carved images fly away?' expressing his disbelief.
    • 使以淳漆各点两玉虎一眼眼 - Means 'he ordered them to paint the eyes of two jade tigers with pure lacquer,' indicating his attempt to test the magical nature of the drawings.
    • 旬日则失之，不知所在 - Means 'after ten days, they were lost, and their whereabouts were unknown,' indicating the disappearance of the tigers.
    • 山泽之人云 - Means 'the people of the mountains and marshes said,' indicating the report of common people.
    • 见二白虎，各无一目，相随而行，毛色相似，异于常见者 - Means 'they saw two white tigers, each missing one eye, walking together, with similar fur colors, different from what is commonly seen,' indicating the appearance of the tigers.
    • 至明年，西方献两白虎，各无一目 - Means 'the following year, two white tigers, each missing one eye, were offered from the west,' indicating the arrival of the tigers.
    • 始皇发槛视之，疑是先所失者，乃刺杀之 - Means 'the First Emperor opened the cage to look at them, suspecting they were the ones previously lost, and then had them killed,' indicating his reaction to the tigers.
    • 检其胸前，果是元年所刻玉虎 - Means 'he checked their chests, and indeed they were the jade tigers carved in the first year,' confirming the identity of the tigers.
    • 迄胡亥之灭，宝剑神物，随时散乱也 - Means 'until the death of Hu Hai, the precious swords and divine objects were scattered at the time,' indicating the fate of the objects.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 秦始皇 - The First Emperor of Qin, known for his unification of China and the construction of the Great Wall.
    • 始皇元年 - The first year of the First Emperor's reign, marking the beginning of the Qin dynasty.
    • 骞霄国 - A mythical or distant country, possibly referring to a foreign land.
    • 刻玉善画工名裔 - A skilled artist from the country of Qianxiao, known for his ability to carve jade and draw.
    • 丹青 - Refers to cinnabar and verdigris, traditional pigments used in painting.
    • 魑魅 - Mythical and monstrous creatures, often associated with evil or supernatural beings.
    • 诡怪群物之像 - Images of strange and mythical creatures.
    • 四渎 - The four major rivers of China: the Yellow River, the Yangtze River, the Huai River, and the Ji River.
    • 五岳 - The five sacred mountains of China: Mount Tai, Mount Hua, Mount Heng (in Hunan), Mount Heng (in Shanxi), and Mount Song.
    • 列国 - Various states or regions within China.
    • 龙凤 - Dragons and phoenixes, mythical creatures often associated with power and prosperity.
    • 胡亥 - The second emperor of the Qin dynasty, known for his short and tyrannical reign.
    • 宝剑神物 - Precious swords and divine objects, often associated with power and mysticism.

    Proper Nouns

    • 秦始皇 - The First Emperor of Qin.
    • 始皇元年 - The first year of the First Emperor's reign.
    • 骞霄国 - A mythical or distant country.
    • 刻玉善画工名裔 - A skilled artist from the country of Qianxiao.
    • 胡亥 - The second emperor of the Qin dynasty.

    The First Emperor of Qin (Four Events)

    In the first year of the First Emperor's reign, a skilled artist from the country of Qianxiao was offered to him. This artist could create images on the ground by rinsing it with cinnabar and verdigris, instantly forming pictures of mythical and monstrous creatures. He also carved jade into the shapes of a hundred beasts, with fur so lifelike that it seemed real. All his works were inscribed on their chests, marked with the date and month. He drew on the ground with his finger, creating a hundred-paces-long line as straight as a string and plumb line. Within a square inch, he could draw maps of the four rivers, five mountains, and various states. He also drew dragons and phoenixes, soaring as if flying. None of his drawings could have their eyes painted, or if they were, they would fly away. The First Emperor sighed and said, "How can the carved images fly away?" He ordered them to paint the eyes of two jade tigers with pure lacquer. After ten days, the tigers were lost, and their whereabouts were unknown. The people of the mountains and marshes reported, "We saw two white tigers, each missing one eye, walking together, with similar fur colors, different from what is commonly seen." The following year, two white tigers, each missing one eye, were offered from the west. The First Emperor opened the cage to look at them, suspecting they were the ones previously lost, and then had them killed. He checked their chests, and indeed they were the jade tigers carved in the first year. Until the death of Hu Hai, the precious swords and divine objects were scattered at the time. [65]

    始皇好神仙之事，有宛渠之民，乘螺舟而至。舟形似螺，沉行海底，而水不浸入，一名“沦波舟”。其国人长十丈，编鸟兽之毛以蔽形。始皇与之语，及天地初开之时，瞭如亲睹。曰：“臣少时蹑虚却行，日游万里。及其老朽也，坐见天地之外事。臣国在咸池日没之所九万里，以万岁为一日。俗多阴雾，遇其晴日，则天豁然云裂，耿若江汉。则有玄龙黑凤，翻翔而下。及夜，燃石以继日光。此石出燃山，其土石皆自光澈，扣之则碎，状如粟，一粒辉映一堂。昔炎帝始变生食，用此火也。国人今献此石。或有投其石于溪涧中，则沸沫流于数十里，名其水为焦渊。臣国去轩辕之丘十万里，少典之子采首山之铜，铸为大鼎。臣先望其国有金火气动，奔而往视之，三鼎已成。又见冀州有异气，应有圣人生，果有庆都生尧。又见赤云入于酆镐，走而往视，果有丹雀瑞昌之符。”始皇曰：“此神人也。”弥信仙术焉。 [66]

    The First Emperor was fond of matters related to immortals and immortality. People from the land of Wanyu arrived by means of a shell-like boat. The boat's shape resembled a shell, and it could travel underwater without being submerged. It was also known as a "Lunbo Boat." The people from this country were exceptionally tall, each being ten zhang (approximately 30 meters) tall. They covered their bodies with the feathers and fur of birds and beasts. The First Emperor conversed with them and discussed the creation of the world, as if he had personally witnessed it. One of them said, "When I was young, I could walk on air and travel vast distances in a day. Now, while seated, I can see beyond the known world. My country is ten million li away from the mountain of Xuanyuan (Yellow Emperor's) and considers a year to be equivalent to a single day in our time. Our country is often shrouded in mist, but when the sun shines, the sky clears and the clouds part, revealing a brightness like the rivers and lakes. Then, there appear black dragons and phoenixes, flying down from the sky. At night, we use stones to provide light. These stones come from a mountain called Ranshan, and the soil and stones there emit light naturally. When struck, the stones break into small pieces, resembling millet grains, and a single piece can illuminate an entire hall. In ancient times, the Yellow Emperor used this fire to transform raw food into cooked food. Now, the people of our country present this stone to you. If one were to throw this stone into a stream, boiling foam would flow for tens of miles, and the water would be named 'Jiao Yuan.' My country is ten million li away from the mountain of Xuanyuan (Yellow Emperor's). The son of Shao Dian mined copper from Mount Shou and cast three great cauldrons. I sensed the movement of metal and fire in my country and rushed to witness the completion of the three cauldrons. I also saw unusual energy in Yizhou, indicating the birth of a sage, which turned out to be Emperor Yao. Additionally, I saw red clouds entering Feng and Hao, indicating the appearance of auspicious signs of prosperity." The First Emperor said, "These are divine beings." He became even more convinced of the art of immortality. [66]

    始皇起云明台，穷四方之珍木，搜天下之巧工。南得烟丘碧树，郦水燃沙，贲都朱泥，云冈素竹；东得葱峦锦柏，漂檖龙松，寒河星柘，岏云之梓；西得漏海浮金，狼渊羽璧，涤嶂霞桑，沉塘员筹；北得冥阜干漆，阴阪文杞，褰流黑魄，暗海香琼，珍异是集。二人腾虚缘木，挥斤斧于空中，子时起工，午时已毕。秦人谓之“子午台”，亦言于子午之地，各起一台，二说疑也。 [67]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [67]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 始皇起云明台 - Qin Shi Huang, the first emperor of China, built the Cloudy Bright Platform.
    • 穷四方之珍木 - Gathered the finest trees from all directions.
    • 搜天下之巧工 - Sought out the most skilled craftsmen from across the land.
    • 南得烟丘碧树 - Obtained smoky hills and green trees in the south.
    • 郦水燃沙 - Describes a scene of burning sand along the Li River.
    • 贲都朱泥 - Refers to red clay from the city or region of Bendi.
    • 云冈素竹 - Refers to white bamboo from the mountain or region of Yungang.
    • 东得葱峦锦柏 - Obtained lush mountains and colorful cypress trees in the east.
    • 漂檖龙松 - Describes a type of tree, possibly a metaphor for something else.
    • 寒河星柘 - Describes a scene of star-shaped zelkova in a cold river.
    • 岏云之梓 - Describes a type of tree, possibly a metaphor for something else.
    • 西得漏海浮金 - Describes a scene of leaking gold from the sea in the west.
    • 狼渊羽璧 - Describes a type of jade from the wolf abyss.
    • 涤嶂霞桑 - Describes a scene of clearing cliffs and sunset mulberry trees.
    • 沉塘员筹 - Describes a type of pond, possibly a metaphor for something else.
    • 北得冥阜干漆 - Obtained dark hills and dry lacquer in the north.
    • 阴阪文杞 - Describes a type of tree, possibly a metaphor for something else.
    • 褰流黑魄 - Describes a type of substance, possibly a metaphor for something else.
    • 暗海香琼 - Describes a type of jade from the dark sea.
    • 珍异是集 - Precious and rare items are gathered.
    • 二人腾虚缘木 - Two people soaring through the void and climbing trees.
    • 挥斤斧于空中 - Swinging axes in the air.
    • 子时起工，午时已毕 - Starting work at midnight and completing it at noon.
    • 秦人谓之“子午台” - The Qin people called it the Subei Platform.
    • 亦言于子午之地，各起一台，二说疑也 - Also says that each built a platform in the Subei area, both explanations are doubtful.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 始皇 - Refers to Qin Shi Huang, the first emperor of China.
    • 云明台 - A platform built by Qin Shi Huang, possibly named for its appearance or significance.
    • 四方 - Refers to the four directions, symbolizing the entire empire.
    • 天下 - Refers to the entire empire or world.
    • 巧工 - Refers to skilled craftsmen, often associated with the Qin dynasty's architectural and engineering feats.
    • 烟丘碧树 - Describes a scenic landscape, possibly a metaphor for the beauty and richness of the materials gathered.
    • 郦水 - Likely refers to the Li River, a famous river in China.
    • 贲都 - A place name, possibly a city or region known for its red clay.
    • 云冈 - A place name, possibly a mountain or region known for its white bamboo.
    • 葱峦锦柏 - Describes a scenic landscape, possibly a metaphor for the beauty and richness of the materials gathered.
    • 漂檖龙松 - Describes a type of tree, possibly a metaphor for something else.
    • 寒河星柘 - Describes a scenic landscape, possibly a metaphor for the beauty and richness of the materials gathered.
    • 岏云之梓 - Describes a type of tree, possibly a metaphor for something else.
    • 漏海浮金 - Describes a scenic landscape, possibly a metaphor for the wealth and resources gathered.
    • 狼渊羽璧 - Describes a type of jade, possibly a metaphor for something else.
    • 涤嶂霞桑 - Describes a scenic landscape, possibly a metaphor for the beauty and richness of the materials gathered.
    • 沉塘员筹 - Describes a type of pond, possibly a metaphor for something else.
    • 冥阜干漆 - Describes a type of lacquer, possibly a metaphor for something else.
    • 阴阪文杞 - Describes a type of tree, possibly a metaphor for something else.
    • 褰流黑魄 - Describes a type of substance, possibly a metaphor for something else.
    • 暗海香琼 - Describes a type of jade, possibly a metaphor for something else.
    • 珍异 - Refers to precious and rare items, often associated with the Qin dynasty's wealth and power.
    • 腾虚缘木 - Describes a type of movement, possibly a metaphor for something else.
    • 挥斤斧于空中 - Describes a type of action, possibly a metaphor for something else.
    • 子时 - Refers to midnight, a specific time of day.
    • 午时 - Refers to noon, a specific time of day.
    • 秦人 - Refers to the people of the Qin dynasty.
    • 子午台 - A platform named for its location or significance.
    • 子午之地 - Refers to the Subei area, a specific location.

    Qin Shi Huang built the Cloudy Bright Platform, gathering the finest trees from all directions and seeking out the most skilled craftsmen from across the land. From the south, he obtained smoky hills with green trees, the Li River with burning sand, red clay from Bendi, and white bamboo from Yun Gang; from the east, he obtained lush mountains with colorful cypress trees, floating and drifting dragon pines, cold rivers with star-shaped zelkova, and Chinese catalpa from the cloudy mountains; from the west, he obtained leaking gold from the sea, feathered jade from Wolf Abyss, clearing cliffs with sunset mulberry, and sinking ponds with round tally; from the north, he obtained dark hills with dry lacquer, shady slopes with patterned Chinese tamarisk, lifting flow with black soul, and dark sea with fragrant jade, all precious and rare items were gathered. Two people soared through the void and climbed trees, swinging axes in the air, starting work at midnight and completing it by noon. The people of Qin called it the "Subei Platform," or it was said that in the Subei area, each built a platform, and both explanations are doubtful. [67]

    张仪、苏秦二人，同志好学，迭剪发而鬻之，以相养。或佣力写书，非圣人之言不读。遇见《坟》《典》，行途无所题记，以墨书掌及股里，夜还而写之，析竹为简。二人每假食于路，剥树皮编以为书帙，以盛天下良书。尝息大树之下，假息而寐有一先生问：“二子何勤苦也？”仪、秦又问之：“子何国人？”答曰：“吾生于归谷。”亦云鬼谷，鬼者，归也。又云，归者，谷名也。乃请其术，教以干世出俗之辩，即探胸内，得二卷说书，言辅时之事。《古史考》云：“鬼谷子也，鬼、归，音相近也。”秦王子婴立，凡百日，郎中赵高谋杀之。 [68]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [68]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 同志好学 - Having the same aspirations and loving learning, indicating a shared educational goal.
    • 迭剪发而鬻之，以相养 - Taking turns cutting their hair and selling it to support each other, a literal but idiomatic expression describing a mutual support system.
    • 或佣力写书 - Sometimes working as hired laborers to write books, indicating a combination of manual labor and scholarly activity.
    • 非圣人之言不读 - They would not read anything unless it was the words of sages, emphasizing their selective approach to reading.
    • 遇见《坟》《典》，行途无所题记，以墨书掌及股里，夜还而写之 - When they encountered the *Fen* and *Dian* (ancient texts), they would inscribe them on their palms and inner thighs with ink, then write them down at night, describing a method of memorization and recording.
    • 析竹为简 - Splitting bamboo to make writing tablets, a literal description of making writing materials.
    • 二人每假食于路，剥树皮编以为书帙 - Whenever they ate on the road, they would peel tree bark and weave it into book covers, describing a practical method of creating book covers.
    • 尝息大树之下，假息而寐 - They once rested under a large tree and slept while resting, indicating a moment of rest.
    • 二子何勤苦也 - Why are you so hardworking and diligent? a rhetorical question expressing admiration.
    • 请其术，教以干世出俗之辩 - They asked him for his skills, and he taught them how to debate and argue in the world, describing the teaching of rhetorical skills.
    • 探胸内，得二卷说书 - They found two scrolls of persuasive writing inside his chest, a metaphorical description of receiving knowledge.
    • 言辅时之事 - They contained advice for the times, indicating the content of the scrolls.
    • 秦王子婴立，凡百日，郎中赵高谋杀之 - Prince Ziying of Qin ascended the throne, but after one hundred days, the eunuch Zhao Gao plotted to kill him, describing a historical event.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 张仪、苏秦 - Historical figures known for their diplomatic and rhetorical skills during the Warring States period.
    • 鬼谷子 - A legendary figure associated with the Warring States period, known for his teachings in strategy and rhetoric.
    • 《坟》《典》 - Ancient texts, possibly referring to the *Shujing* (Book of Documents) and *Shijing* (Book of Poetry).
    • 秦王子婴 - The last ruler of the Qin dynasty, who briefly reigned after the death of Qin Er Shi.
    • 赵高 - A powerful eunuch who played a significant role in the fall of the Qin dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 张仪、苏秦 - Historical figures.
    • 鬼谷子 - A legendary figure.
    • 《坟》《典》 - Ancient texts.
    • 秦王子婴 - The last ruler of the Qin dynasty.
    • 赵高 - A powerful eunuch.

    Zhang Yi and Su Qin, both having the same aspirations and loving learning, took turns cutting their hair and selling it to support each other. Sometimes they worked as hired laborers to write books, but they would not read anything unless it was the words of sages. When they encountered the Fen and Dian (ancient texts), they would inscribe them on their palms and inner thighs with ink, then write them down at night. They split bamboo to make writing tablets. Whenever they ate on the road, they would peel tree bark and weave it into book covers to hold the good books of the world. Once, they rested under a large tree and fell asleep. An old man asked them, "Why are you so hardworking and diligent?" Zhang Yi and Su Qin asked him, "What country are you from?" He replied, "I was born in Guigǔ." It is also known as Guigǔ, where "鬼" means "return" or "come back." Another interpretation is that Guigǔ is the name of a valley. They then asked him for his skills, and he taught them how to debate and argue in the world. They found two scrolls of persuasive writing inside his chest, which contained advice for the times. The Ancient History Examination states, "He was also known as Guigǔ, where '鬼' means 'return' or 'come back.' Another interpretation is that Guigǔ is the name of a valley." Prince Ziying of Qin ascended the throne, but after one hundred days, the eunuch Zhao Gao plotted to kill him. [68]

    子婴寝于望夷之宫，夜梦有人身长十丈，须鬓绝青，纳玉舄而乘丹车，驾朱马而至宫门。云欲见秦王子婴，阍者许进焉。子婴乃与言。谓子婴曰：“余是天使也，从沙丘来。天下将乱，当有同姓名欲相诛暴。”翌日乃起，子婴则疑赵高，囚高于咸阳狱，悬于井中，七日不死；更以镬汤煮，七日不沸，乃戮之。子婴问狱吏曰：“高其神乎？”狱吏曰：“初囚高之时，见高怀有一青丸，大如雀卵。”时方士说云：“赵高先世受韩终丹法，冬月坐于坚冰，夏日卧于炉上，不觉寒热。”及高死，子婴弃高尸于九达之路，泣送者千家。或见一青雀从高尸中出，直入云。九转之验，信于是乎。子婴所梦，即始皇之灵；所著玉舄，则安期先生所遗也。鬼魅之理，万世一时。 [69]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [69]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 子婴寝于望夷之宫 - Ziying slept in the Palace of Wangyi; indicates a state of rest or seclusion.
    • 纳玉舄而乘丹车 - Wearing jade shoes and riding in a red carriage; describes a grand and divine appearance.
    • 云欲见秦王子婴 - Declaring that he wanted to see the prince of Qin; indicates direct speech.
    • 阍者许进焉 - The gatekeeper allowed him to enter; indicates permission to enter.
    • 当有同姓名欲相诛暴 - There will be people with the same name who will kill each other violently; suggests a future conflict.
    • 翌日乃起 - The next day then got up; indicates the passage of time.
    • 疑赵高 - Suspected Zhao Gao; indicates growing distrust.
    • 囚高于咸阳狱 - Imprisoned Zhao Gao in the prison in Xianyang; indicates confinement.
    • 悬于井中 - Hung him in the well; describes the method of execution.
    • 七日不死 - Did not die after seven days; suggests supernatural resilience.
    • 更以镬汤煮 - Then boiled him in a large pot of water; describes another method of execution.
    • 七日不沸 - Did not boil after seven days; again suggests supernatural resilience.
    • 乃戮之 - Then killed him; indicates the final act of execution.
    • 高其神乎 - Is he a god?; expresses doubt about Zhao Gao's supernatural nature.
    • 初囚高之时 - At the beginning of imprisoning Zhao Gao; indicates the initial imprisonment.
    • 见高怀有一青丸 - Seeing Zhao Gao carrying a green pill; describes a mysterious object.
    • 赵高先世受韩终丹法 - Zhao Gao's ancestors received elixir techniques from Han Zhong; explains Zhao Gao's alleged connection to alchemy.
    • 冬月坐于坚冰 - Sitting on frozen ice in winter; describes a harsh winter condition.
    • 夏日卧于炉上 - Lying down on a stove in summer; describes a hot summer condition.
    • 不觉寒热 - Unaware of cold and heat; suggests that Zhao Gao was unaffected by extreme temperatures.
    • 弃高尸于九达之路 - Abandoning Zhao Gao's body at a crossroads; describes the disposal of the body.
    • 泣送者千家 - Crying and sending off thousands of families; describes the mournful reaction of the people.
    • 或见一青雀从高尸中出 - Seeing a green bird emerging from Zhao Gao's body; describes a supernatural event.
    • 直入云 - Entering directly into the clouds; describes the bird flying into the sky.
    • 九转之验 - Nine-fold transformation; symbolizes the process of achieving immortality.
    • 信于是乎 - Believing in this case; expresses belief in the supernatural events.
    • 子婴所梦 - Ziying's dream; indicates that the dream was a significant event.
    • 即始皇之灵 - Is the spirit of Qin Shi Huang; suggests that the figure in the dream was the spirit of Qin Shi Huang.
    • 所著玉舄 - Wearing jade shoes; describes the divine attire.
    • 则安期先生所遗也 - Is left behind by Master Anqi; suggests that the jade shoes were a gift from a legendary figure.
    • 鬼魅之理 - Principles of ghosts and spirits; refers to the principles governing ghosts and spirits.
    • 万世一时 - Rare and significant event; suggests that the event was rare and significant.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 子婴 - Refers to Ziying, the last emperor of the Qin dynasty, who ruled briefly after the death of Qin Er Shi.
    • 望夷之宫 - Refers to the Palace of Wangyi, a palace in Xianyang, the capital of the Qin dynasty.
    • 秦王子婴 - Refers to Ziying, the prince of Qin.
    • 赵高 - Refers to Zhao Gao, a powerful eunuch who played a significant role in the downfall of the Qin dynasty.
    • 咸阳狱 - Refers to the prison in Xianyang, the capital of the Qin dynasty.
    • 韩终 - Refers to a legendary alchemist and one of the Eight Immortals in Chinese mythology.
    • 安期先生 - Refers to a legendary figure known for his wisdom and longevity, often associated with alchemy and immortality.
    • 始皇 - Refers to Qin Shi Huang, the first emperor of a unified China.
    • 九转之验 - Refers to the nine-fold transformation, a concept in alchemy that symbolizes the process of achieving immortality.
    • 鬼魅之理 - Refers to the principles governing ghosts and spirits, a common theme in Chinese folklore and religious beliefs.

    Ziying resided in the Palace of Wangyi, where one night he dreamt of a figure whose body was ten zhang long, with hair and beard of an extraordinary green hue. The figure wore jade shoes and rode in a red carriage, driven by vermilion horses, arriving at the palace gates. Declaring that he wished to see the prince of Qin, the gatekeeper allowed him to enter. Ziying then engaged in conversation with the figure, who said to Ziying, "I am an envoy from heaven, coming from Shachou. The world is about to descend into chaos, and there will be people with the same name who will engage in violent conflict." The next day, Ziying awoke, and he began to suspect Zhao Gao. He imprisoned Zhao Gao in the prison of Xianyang, suspending him in a well, but he did not die after seven days. They then boiled him in a large pot of water, but it did not boil after seven days, and only then did they kill him. Ziying asked the prison officials, "Is Zhao Gao a god?" The officials replied, "When we first imprisoned Zhao Gao, we saw him carrying a green pill as large as a sparrow's egg." At the time, Daoist practitioners said, "Zhao Gao's ancestors received elixir techniques from Han Zhong, allowing him to sit on frozen ice in winter and lie on a stove in summer, unaware of the cold or heat." After Zhao Gao's death, Ziying abandoned his body at a crossroads, and thousands of families wept as they sent him off. Some saw a green bird emerge from Zhao Gao's body and fly directly into the clouds. The nine-fold transformation, indeed, was proven true in this case. Ziying's dream was the spirit of Qin Shi Huang; the jade shoes he wore were left behind by Master Anqi. The principles of ghosts and spirits are rare and significant, occurring once in ten thousand generations. [69]

    录曰：夫含灵挺质，罕不羡乎久视，祈以长生。苟乖才性，企之弥远。何者？夫层宫峻宇肆其奢，绰约柔曼纵其惑，《九韶》、《六度》悦其耳，喜怒刑赏示其威，精灵溺于常滞，志意疲于驰策，销竭神虑，翦刻天和。秦正自以功高三皇，世逾五帝，取惑徐市，身殒沙丘。燕昭能延礼群神，百灵响集。并欲弃机事以游真极，去尘垢而望云飞。譬犹等沟浍于天河，齐朝菌于椿木，超二仪于昆峦，升一匮而扳重汉。何则望之与无阶矣。《抱朴子》曰：“学若牛毛，得如麟角。”至如秦皇、燕昭之智，虽微鉴仙体，而未入玄真。 [70]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [70]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 含灵挺质 - Classical expression meaning 'possessing a soul and a natural form,' referring to sentient beings.
    • 罕不羡乎久视 - Means 'rarely does one not envy long life' or 'long life is highly desired.'
    • 祈以长生 - Means 'to pray for eternal life' or 'to seek immortality.'
    • 苟乖才性 - Means 'if one's talent and nature are not in harmony' or 'if one lacks the natural disposition.'
    • 企之弥远 - Means 'the more one strives, the farther it seems,' indicating the futility of striving for something unattainable.
    • 层宫峻宇 - Refers to grand and tall palaces, symbolizing luxury and extravagance.
    • 绰约柔曼 - Describes graceful and gentle beauty, often used to describe women.
    • 九韶、六度 - Refers to the ancient musical compositions 'Jiu Shao' and 'Liu Du,' which are highly esteemed and considered to be very pleasing to the ear.
    • 悦其耳 - Means 'to please the ears.'
    • 喜怒刑赏示其威 - Means 'to show authority through rewards and punishments, as well as expressions of joy and anger.'
    • 精灵溺于常滞 - Means 'the spirit is trapped in mundane entanglements.'
    • 志意疲于驰策 - Means 'the will and aspirations are exhausted by constant striving.'
    • 销竭神虑 - Means 'to exhaust one's mental and spiritual energy.'
    • 翦刻天和 - Means 'to cut and carve away natural harmony.'
    • 秦正自以功高三皇 - Refers to Qin Shi Huang, the first emperor of China, who believed his achievements surpassed those of the Three Sovereigns.
    • 世逾五帝 - Means 'his reign exceeded that of the Five Emperors.'
    • 取惑徐市 - Means 'he was deceived by Xu Shi,' referring to the alchemist Xu Shi who promised him immortality.
    • 身殒沙丘 - Means 'he died at Shaoqiu,' the place where Qin Shi Huang died.
    • 燕昭能延礼群神 - Refers to Yan Zhaowang, who was known for his ability to attract and honor various deities.
    • 百灵响集 - Means 'a hundred spirits responded and gathered.'
    • 弃机事以游真极 - Means 'to abandon worldly affairs and seek the ultimate truth.'
    • 去尘垢而望云飞 - Means 'to leave behind the dirt and dust and aspire to fly like clouds.'
    • 等沟浍于天河 - Means 'to equate small streams with the Milky Way,' indicating a comparison of the insignificant with the vast.
    • 齐朝菌于椿木 - Means 'to equate morning mushrooms with cypress trees,' indicating a comparison of short-lived things with long-lived ones.
    • 超二仪于昆峦 - Means 'to transcend the two fundamental principles (heaven and earth) among the mountains.'
    • 升一匮而扳重汉 - Means 'to climb one step and reach the distant stars,' indicating a small effort leading to great success.
    • 望之与无阶矣 - Means 'it is as if there is no way to achieve it.'
    • 学若牛毛 - Means 'learning is as numerous as the hairs on a cow.'
    • 得如麟角 - Means 'those who succeed are as rare as the horn of a unicorn.'
    • 秦皇、燕昭之智 - Refers to the intelligence of Qin Shi Huang and Yan Zhaowang.
    • 虽微鉴仙体 - Means 'although they had a slight understanding of the divine body.'
    • 未入玄真 - Means 'they did not enter the realm of the profound truth.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 含灵挺质 - Refers to sentient beings, emphasizing the human condition.
    • 久视 - The concept of longevity, often associated with immortality in Chinese culture.
    • 长生 - The pursuit of eternal life, a common theme in Daoist and other Chinese philosophical traditions.
    • 秦正 - Qin Shi Huang, the first emperor of China, known for his ambitious projects and quest for immortality.
    • 燕昭 - Yan Zhaowang, a ruler known for his wisdom and ability to attract talented individuals.
    • 徐市 - Xu Shi, an alchemist who promised Qin Shi Huang immortality but failed to deliver.
    • 九韶、六度 - Ancient musical compositions that were highly valued for their aesthetic and spiritual qualities.
    • 三皇五帝 - The Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors, legendary figures in Chinese history representing the ideal rulers.
    • 仙体 - The divine or immortal body, often associated with Daoist practices and beliefs.
    • 玄真 - The profound truth or ultimate reality, often associated with Daoist philosophy.

    Proper Nouns

    • 秦正 - Qin Shi Huang, the first emperor of China.
    • 燕昭 - Yan Zhaowang, a ruler known for his wisdom.
    • 徐市 - Xu Shi, an alchemist who promised Qin Shi Huang immortality.
    • 三皇 - The Three Sovereigns, legendary rulers in Chinese history.
    • 五帝 - The Five Emperors, legendary rulers in Chinese history.
    • 九韶 - An ancient musical composition.
    • 六度 - Another ancient musical composition.
    • 《抱朴子》 - A Daoist text written by Ge Hong, which discusses alchemy, medicine, and immortality.

    录曰：夫含灵挺质，罕不羡乎久视，祈以长生。苟乖才性，企之弥远。何者？夫层宫峻宇肆其奢，绰约柔曼纵其惑，《九韶》、《六度》悦其耳，喜怒刑赏示其威，精灵溺于常滞，志意疲于驰策，销竭神虑，翦刻天和。秦正自以功高三皇，世逾五帝，取惑徐市，身殒沙丘。燕昭能延礼群神，百灵响集。并欲弃机事以游真极，去尘垢而望云飞。譬犹等沟浍于天河，齐朝菌于椿木，超二仪于昆峦，升一匮而扳重汉。何则望之与无阶矣。《抱朴子》曰：“学若牛毛，得如麟角。”至如秦皇、燕昭之智，虽微鉴仙体，而未入玄真。

    Translation:
    The record states: Sentient beings, possessing a soul and a natural form, rarely do not envy long life and pray for eternal existence. If one's talent and nature are not in harmony, the more one strives, the farther it seems. Why is this so? Because grand palaces and tall buildings indulge in luxury, graceful and gentle beauty indulges in temptation, the ancient musical compositions "Jiu Shao" and "Liu Du" please the ears, and the display of joy, anger, rewards, and punishments shows authority. The spirit is trapped in mundane entanglements, and the will and aspirations are exhausted by constant striving, leading to the exhaustion of mental and spiritual energy and the cutting away of natural harmony. Qin Shi Huang, believing his achievements surpassed those of the Three Sovereigns and his reign exceeded that of the Five Emperors, was deceived by Xu Shi and died at Shaoqiu. Yan Zhaowang, known for his ability to attract and honor various deities, had a hundred spirits respond and gather. Both wished to abandon worldly affairs and seek the ultimate truth, leaving behind the dirt and dust to aspire to fly like clouds. It is like equating small streams with the Milky Way, morning mushrooms with cypress trees, transcending the two fundamental principles among the mountains, and climbing one step to reach the distant stars. Why is it as if there is no way to achieve it? The Baopu Zi says, "Learning is as numerous as the hairs on a cow, but those who succeed are as rare as the horn of a unicorn." Even the intelligence of Qin Shi Huang and Yan Zhaowang, though they had a slight understanding of the divine body, did not enter the realm of the profound truth. [70]

    盖犹褊惑尚多，滞情未尽。至于神通玄化，说变万端。故曰徐行云垂之俦，驾影乘霞之侣，可得齐肩比步焉，与之栖息也。穷神绝异，随方而来；衒绝殊形，越境而至。托神以尽变，因变以穷神，触象难名，灵怪莫测。《淮南子》云：“含雷吐火之术，出于万毕之家。”方毳羽于洪炉，炎烟火于冰水，漏海螺船之属，飞珠沉霞之类，千途万品，书籍之所未详，自神化以来，神奇莫与为例，岂末代浮诬所能窥仰，夭龄促知之所效哉！今观子年之记，苏、张二人，异辞同迹，或以字音相类，或以土俗为殊，验诸坟史，岂惟秦、仪之见异者哉！ [71]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [71]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 盖犹褊惑尚多，滞情未尽 - Complex phrase meaning 'still, there are many narrow-minded and confused people, and their emotions are not fully resolved.' '褊惑' (biǎn huò) refers to narrow-mindedness and confusion, while '滞情' (zhì qíng) means emotional stagnation or unresolved feelings.
    • 神通玄化 - Compound term meaning 'supernatural transformations' or 'mystical changes.' '神通' (shén tōng) refers to supernatural abilities, and '玄化' (xuán huà) means profound or mysterious transformation.
    • 说变万端 - Means 'to speak of countless transformations' or 'to describe myriad changes.' '说变' (shuō biàn) means to speak of changes, and '万端' (wàn duān) means countless or myriad.
    • 徐行云垂之俦 - Metaphorical phrase meaning 'companions who move slowly like clouds hanging in the sky.' '徐行' (xú xíng) means to move slowly, and '云垂' (yún chuí) means clouds hanging low in the sky.
    • 驾影乘霞之侣 - Metaphorical phrase meaning 'companions who ride shadows and clouds.' '驾影' (jià yǐng) means to ride shadows, and '乘霞' (chéng xiá) means to ride clouds.
    • 穷神绝异 - Means 'to exhaust the supernatural and the extraordinary.' '穷神' (qióng shén) means to exhaust the supernatural, and '绝异' (jué yì) means extraordinary or unparalleled.
    • 随方而来 - Means 'to come from all directions' or 'to arrive from various places.' '随方' (suí fāng) means from all directions.
    • 衒绝殊形 - Means 'to display extraordinary and unique forms.' '衒绝' (xuàn jué) means to display extraordinarily, and '殊形' (shū xíng) means unique forms.
    • 越境而至 - Means 'to come from beyond the borders' or 'to arrive from distant places.' '越境' (yuè jìng) means beyond the borders, and '至' (zhì) means to arrive.
    • 托神以尽变 - Means 'to rely on the supernatural to achieve complete transformation.' '托神' (tuō shén) means to rely on the supernatural, and '尽变' (jìn biàn) means complete transformation.
    • 因变以穷神 - Means 'to use transformation to exhaust the supernatural.' '因变' (yīn biàn) means to use transformation, and '穷神' (qióng shén) means to exhaust the supernatural.
    • 触象难名 - Means 'to touch upon phenomena that are hard to name.' '触象' (chù xiàng) means to touch upon phenomena, and '难名' (nán míng) means hard to name.
    • 灵怪莫测 - Means 'mysterious and unpredictable.' '灵怪' (líng guài) means mysterious and strange, and '莫测' (mò cè) means unpredictable.
    • 含雷吐火之术 - Metaphorical phrase meaning 'the art of containing thunder and spewing fire.' '含雷' (hán léi) means to contain thunder, and '吐火' (tǔ huǒ) means to spew fire.
    • 出于万毕之家 - Means 'originating from the family of Wan Bi.' '出于' (chū yú) means originating from, and '万毕之家' (wàn bì zhī jiā) refers to a specific family.
    • 毳羽于洪炉 - Metaphorical phrase meaning 'to place downy feathers in a great furnace.' '毳羽' (cuì yǔ) means downy feathers, and '洪炉' (hóng lú) means a great furnace.
    • 炎烟火于冰水 - Metaphorical phrase meaning 'to place fire and smoke in ice water.' '炎烟火' (yán yān huǒ) means fire and smoke, and '冰水' (bīng shuǐ) means ice water.
    • 漏海螺船之属 - Metaphorical phrase meaning 'ships that leak into the sea.' '漏海' (lòu hǎi) means to leak into the sea, and '螺船之属' (luó chuán zhī shǔ) means ships.
    • 飞珠沉霞之类 - Metaphorical phrase meaning 'pearls flying and clouds sinking.' '飞珠' (fēi zhū) means pearls flying, and '沉霞' (chén xiá) means clouds sinking.
    • 千途万品 - Means 'thousands of paths and myriad kinds.' '千途' (qiān tú) means thousands of paths, and '万品' (wàn pǐn) means myriad kinds.
    • 书籍之所未详 - Means 'details not fully explained in books.' '书籍' (shū jí) means books, and '未详' (wèi xiáng) means not fully explained.
    • 自神化以来 - Means 'since the era of deification.' '自神化' (zì shén huà) means since the era of deification, and '以来' (yǐ lái) means since.
    • 神奇莫与为例 - Means 'miraculous and unmatched.' '神奇' (shén qí) means miraculous, and '莫与为例' (mò yǔ wéi lì) means unmatched.
    • 夭龄促知之所效哉 - Means 'how could they be imitated by those with short lives and limited knowledge?' '夭龄' (yāo líng) means short lives, '促知' (cù zhī) means limited knowledge, and '之所效哉' (zhī suǒ xiào zāi) means how could they be imitated.
    • 今观子年之记 - Means 'now, observing the records of Zi Nian.' '今观' (jīn guān) means now observing, and '子年之记' (zǐ nián zhī jì) means the records of Zi Nian.
    • 苏、张二人 - Refers to two people named Su and Zhang.
    • 异辞同迹 - Means 'different words but the same deeds.' '异辞' (yì cí) means different words, and '同迹' (tóng jì) means the same deeds.
    • 或以字音相类 - Means 'sometimes due to similar sounds of characters.' '或以' (huò yǐ) means sometimes due to, '字音' (zì yīn) means sounds of characters, and '相类' (xiāng lèi) means similar.
    • 或以土俗为殊 - Means 'sometimes due to different local customs.' '或以' (huò yǐ) means sometimes due to, '土俗' (tǔ sú) means local customs, and '为殊' (wéi shū) means different.
    • 验诸坟史 - Means 'to verify in ancient texts.' '验诸' (yàn zhū) means to verify, and '坟史' (fén shǐ) means ancient texts.
    • 岂惟秦、仪之见异者哉 - Means 'how could it be only that Qin and Yi were seen as different?' '岂惟' (qǐ wéi) means how could it be only, '秦、仪' (qín, yí) means Qin and Yi, '之见异者' (zhī jiàn yì zhě) means those seen as different, and '哉' (zāi) is an exclamation particle.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 神通玄化 - Refers to supernatural transformations, often associated with Daoist and Buddhist traditions.
    • 徐行云垂之俦，驾影乘霞之侣 - Metaphorical references to companions who move slowly like clouds and ride shadows and clouds, often used to describe supernatural beings or deities.
    • 穷神绝异 - Refers to the concept of exhausting the supernatural and the extraordinary, often found in Daoist texts.
    • 随方而来，衒绝殊形，越境而至 - Describes the arrival of supernatural beings from various places, often associated with Daoist and Buddhist cosmology.
    • 托神以尽变，因变以穷神 - Describes the process of using the supernatural to achieve complete transformation and then using transformation to exhaust the supernatural, a concept often found in Daoist and Buddhist texts.
    • 含雷吐火之术 - Refers to the art of containing thunder and spewing fire, often associated with Daoist alchemy and magic.
    • 万毕之家 - Refers to a specific family, possibly a historical or legendary figure, known for their supernatural abilities.
