    • 毳羽于洪炉，炎烟火于冰水 - Metaphorical descriptions of placing downy feathers in a great furnace and fire and smoke in ice water, often used to describe the contrast between the mundane and the supernatural.

    There are still many narrow-minded and confused people, and their emotions are not fully resolved. As for supernatural transformations and myriad changes, it is said that companions who move slowly like clouds hanging in the sky and those who ride shadows and clouds can be compared in status and can be associated with them. They come from all directions, displaying extraordinary and unique forms, arriving from distant places. They rely on the supernatural to achieve complete transformation and use transformation to exhaust the supernatural, touching upon phenomena that are hard to name, mysterious and unpredictable. The Huainanzi states: "The art of containing thunder and spewing fire originates from the family of Wan Bi." To place downy feathers in a great furnace and fire and smoke in ice water, ships that leak into the sea, pearls flying and clouds sinking, thousands of paths and myriad kinds, details not fully explained in books, since the era of deification, miraculous and unmatched, how could they be imitated by those with short lives and limited knowledge? Now, observing the records of Zi Nian, Su and Zhang, two people, have different words but the same deeds, sometimes due to similar sounds of characters, sometimes due to different local customs, verifying in ancient texts, how could it be only that Qin and Yi were seen as different? [71]

    拾遗记卷五

    前汉上

    　　汉太上皇微时，佩一刀，长三尺，上有铭，其字难识，疑是殷高宗伐鬼方之时所作也。上皇游酆沛山中。寓居穷谷里有人冶铸。上皇息其傍，问曰：“此铸何器？”工者笑而答曰：“为天子铸剑，慎勿泄言？”上皇谓为戏言，而无疑色。工人曰：“今所铸铁钢砺难成，若得公腰间佩刀杂而治之，即成神器，可以克定天下，星精为辅佐，以歼三猾。木衰火盛，此为异兆也。”上皇曰：“余此物名为匕首，其利难俦，水断虬龙，陆斩虎兕，魑魅罔两，莫能逢之。斫玉镌金，其刃不卷。 [72]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [72]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 拾遺記 - Title of a classical Chinese text, likely a collection of historical or legendary anecdotes.
    • 前漢上 - Indicates this is part of a larger work, specifically the first part of a section dealing with the Western Han Dynasty.
    • 太上皇 - Refers to the father of Emperor Gaozu of Han, Liu Bang, who was posthumously given the title 'Taishang Huang'.
    • 微時 - Means 'in humble times' or 'when he was not yet famous'.
    • 佩刀 - Literally 'worn knife', referring to a sword or dagger carried by a person.
    • 殷高宗伐鬼方 - Refers to the reign of King Wu Ding of the Shang Dynasty and his military campaigns against the tribe of Guifang.
    • 寓居 - Means 'to live temporarily' or 'to reside temporarily'.
    • 窮谷 - Refers to a remote valley or a secluded place.
    • 冶铸 - Means 'to smelt and cast metal'.
    • 天子铸剑 - Refers to the king or emperor making a sword.
    • 星精为辅佐 - Means 'star spirits as assistants' or 'star deities as helpers'.
    • 三猾 - Refers to three cunning or deceitful individuals, possibly alluding to specific historical figures or a general term for treacherous people.
    • 木衰火盛 - Refers to a balance or imbalance of wood and fire elements, often used in Chinese philosophy and divination.
    • 匕首 - A type of short dagger or dagger-like weapon.
    • 魑魅罔两 - Refers to spirits or monsters, often used to describe supernatural beings.
    • 斫玉镌金 - Means 'to cut jade and engrave gold', describing the sharpness and precision of the blade.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 太上皇 - Liu Bang's father, Liu Zhong, who was given the posthumous title 'Taishang Huang' after his death.
    • 殷高宗 - King Wu Ding (1250–1192 BCE), a famous ruler of the Shang Dynasty known for his military prowess.
    • 鬼方 - A nomadic tribe that resisted the Shang Dynasty during King Wu Ding's reign.
    • 汉太上皇微时 - Refers to the early life of Liu Zhong, before he became the father of Emperor Gaozu of Han.
    • 佩刀 - In Chinese culture, swords and daggers were often symbolic of status and power, even in humble times.
    • 星精为辅佐 - Alludes to the belief in star deities or spirits that could aid in significant endeavors.
    • 三猾 - Could refer to specific historical figures or a general term for treacherous individuals, often used in historical narratives.
    • 木衰火盛 - Refers to the balance of the five elements in Chinese philosophy, where wood and fire are two of the elements.

    Proper Nouns

    • 拾遺記 - Title of the text.
    • 前汉上 - Part of the text.
    • 太上皇 - Title of Liu Zhong.
    • 殷高宗 - Title of King Wu Ding.
    • 鬼方 - Name of the tribe.
    • 酆沛山 - Name of the mountain.
    • 匕首 - Type of weapon.

    Translation:

    The Shiyi Ji Volume V

    Han Dynasty, Part I

    When the Taishang Huang of Han was in humble times, he wore a dagger, three feet long, with inscriptions on it that were difficult to read. It was suspected to have been made during the time when King Wu Ding of Yin (Shang) was waging war against the tribe of Guifang. The Taishang Huang was traveling in the mountains of Feng and Pei. In a remote valley, there was someone smelting and casting metal. The Taishang Huang rested nearby and asked, "What are you casting?" The worker laughed and replied, "I am casting a sword for the Son of Heaven. Be careful not to reveal this!" The Taishang Huang thought it was a joke and showed no sign of doubt. The worker said, "The iron and steel we are casting is difficult to refine. If we mix in your dagger from your waist, it will become a divine weapon capable of conquering the world. Star spirits will assist, and we can defeat the three cunning individuals. The decline of wood and the dominance of fire are signs of an extraordinary omen." The Taishang Huang said, "This dagger is called a bishou (dagger-axe). Its sharpness is unparalleled; it can cut through dragons in water and tigers on land. No spirits or monsters can withstand it. It can cut jade and engrave gold without dulling its edge." [72]

    ”工人曰：“若不得此匕首以和铸，虽欧冶专精，越砥敛锷，终为鄙器。”上皇则解匕首，投于炉中。俄而烟焰冲天，日为之昼晦。及乎剑成，杀三牲以衅祭之。铸工问上皇：“何时得此匕首？”上皇云：“秦昭襄王时，余行逢一野人，于陌上授余，云是殷时灵物，世世相传，上有古字，记其年月。”及成剑，工人视之，其铭尚存，叶前疑也。工人即持剑授上皇。上皇以赐高祖，高祖长佩于身，以歼三猾。及天下已定，吕后藏于宝库。库中守藏者见白气如云，出于户外，状如龙蛇。吕后改库名曰“灵金藏”。及诸吕擅权，白气亦灭。及惠帝即位，以此库贮禁兵器，名曰“灵金内府”也。 [73]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [73]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 工人曰 - Means 'the craftsman said,' introducing a direct speech.
    • 若不得此匕首以和铸 - Translates to 'if one does not get this dagger to blend and cast,' indicating a necessary condition.
    • 虽欧冶专精 - Means 'even if Ou Yi (a famous swordsmith) were to focus his expertise,' suggesting a hypothetical scenario.
    • 越砥敛锷 - Refers to sharpening the edge with a fine grindstone and folding the blade, a process in sword-making.
    • 终为鄙器 - Means 'it would still be a coarse tool,' indicating the inferior quality of the result.
    • 上皇则解匕首，投于炉中 - Translates to 'the emperor then removed the dagger and threw it into the furnace,' describing an action.
    • 俄而烟焰冲天，日为之昼晦 - Means 'soon the smoke and flames rose to the sky, making the day as dark as night,' describing a dramatic event.
    • 及乎剑成 - Means 'when the sword was completed,' marking a transition.
    • 杀三牲以衅祭之 - Translates to 'slaughter three animals to consecrate it,' indicating a ritual practice.
    • 铸工问上皇 - Means 'the craftsman asked the emperor,' introducing a question.
    • 何时得此匕首 - Translates to 'when did you obtain this dagger,' asking for a specific time.
    • 秦昭襄王时 - Refers to the reign of King Zhaoxiang of Qin, a historical period.
    • 余行逢一野人 - Means 'I encountered a rustic person,' indicating a meeting.
    • 于陌上授余 - Translates to 'who gave it to me on the road,' describing the transfer.
    • 云是殷时灵物 - Means 'who said it was a divine object from the Shang Dynasty,' providing a claim.
    • 世世相传 - Translates to 'passed down through generations,' indicating continuity.
    • 上有古字，记其年月 - Means 'there are ancient characters on it, recording the year and month,' describing inscriptions.
    • 及成剑，工人视之 - Translates to 'when the sword was completed, the craftsman examined it,' describing an action.
    • 其铭尚存 - Means 'the inscription was still there,' indicating the presence of markings.
    • 叶前疑也 - Translates to 'confirming the earlier doubts,' resolving uncertainty.
    • 工人即持剑授上皇 - Means 'the craftsman then held the sword and presented it to the emperor,' describing an action.
    • 上皇以赐高祖 - Translates to 'the emperor then bestowed it upon Gaozu,' indicating a gift.
    • 高祖长佩于身 - Means 'Gaozu wore it on his person,' describing a possession.
    • 以歼三猾 - Translates to 'to eliminate the three wicked ones,' indicating a purpose.
    • 及天下已定 - Means 'when the world was pacified,' marking a transition.
    • 吕后藏于宝库 - Translates to 'Empress Lü hid it in the treasure house,' describing an action.
    • 库中守藏者见白气如云 - Means 'the guard in the treasure house saw white vapor like clouds,' describing a sight.
    • 出于户外，状如龙蛇 - Translates to 'emanating from outside the door, resembling dragons and snakes,' describing a form.
    • 吕后改库名曰“灵金藏” - Translates to 'Empress Lü renamed the treasure house 'Spiritual Gold Repository',' indicating a change.
    • 及诸吕擅权 - Means 'when the relatives of Lü gained power,' marking a transition.
    • 白气亦灭 - Translates to 'the white vapor also disappeared,' describing a change.
    • 及惠帝即位 - Means 'when Emperor Hui ascended the throne,' marking a transition.
    • 以此库贮禁兵器 - Translates to 'this treasure house stored forbidden weapons,' describing a function.
    • 名曰“灵金内府”也 - Translates to 'it was called 'Spiritual Gold Inner Treasury',' indicating a name.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 欧冶 - Refers to Ou Yi, a legendary Chinese swordsmith.
    • 秦昭襄王时 - Refers to the reign of King Zhaoxiang of Qin, a historical period.
    • 殷时灵物 - Refers to a divine object from the Shang Dynasty, indicating a historical and cultural artifact.
    • 高祖 - Refers to Emperor Gaozu of Han, the founder of the Han Dynasty.
    • 吕后 - Refers to Empress Lü, the wife of Emperor Gaozu and regent during the early Han Dynasty.
    • 惠帝 - Refers to Emperor Hui of Han, the son of Emperor Gaozu and successor to the throne.

    Proper Nouns

    • 欧冶 - The name of a famous swordsmith.
    • 秦昭襄王 - The title of a historical figure, King Zhaoxiang of Qin.
    • 高祖 - The title of Emperor Gaozu of Han.
    • 吕后 - The name of Empress Lü.
    • 惠帝 - The title of Emperor Hui of Han.
    • 灵金藏 - The name of the treasure house.
    • 灵金内府 - The name of the inner treasury.

    The craftsman said, "If one does not obtain this dagger to blend and cast, even if Ou Yi were to focus his expertise, sharpen the edge with a fine grindstone, and fold the blade, it would still be a coarse tool." The emperor then removed the dagger and threw it into the furnace. Soon the smoke and flames rose to the sky, making the day as dark as night. When the sword was completed, three animals were slaughtered to consecrate it. The craftsman asked the emperor, "When did you obtain this dagger?" The emperor replied, "During the reign of King Zhaoxiang of Qin, I encountered a rustic person who gave it to me on the road, saying it was a divine object from the Shang Dynasty, passed down through generations, with ancient characters on it, recording the year and month." When the sword was completed, the craftsman examined it, and the inscription was still there, confirming the earlier doubts. The craftsman then held the sword and presented it to the emperor. The emperor bestowed it upon Gaozu, who wore it on his person to eliminate the three wicked ones. When the world was pacified, Empress Lü hid it in the treasure house. The guard in the treasure house saw white vapor like clouds, emanating from outside the door, resembling dragons and snakes. Empress Lü renamed the treasure house "Spiritual Gold Repository." When the relatives of Lü gained power, the white vapor also disappeared. When Emperor Hui ascended the throne, this treasure house stored forbidden weapons, and it was called "Spiritual Gold Inner Treasury." [73]

    录曰：夫精灵变化，其途非一；冥会之感，理故难常。至如《坟》谶所载，咸取验于已往；歌谣俚说，皆求征于未来。考图披籍，往往而编列矣。观乎工人之说，谅妖言之远效焉。三尺之剑，以应天地之数。故三为阳数，亦应天地之德。按《钩命诀》曰：“萧何为昴星精，项羽、陈胜、胡亥为三猾。”国为木德，汉叶火位，此其征也。
    　　孝惠帝二年，四方咸称车书同文轨，天下太平，干戈偃息。远国殊乡，重译来贡。时有道士，姓韩名稚，则韩终之胤也。越海而来，云是东海神使，闻圣德洽乎区宇，故悦服而来庭。时有东极，出扶桑之外，有泥离之国来朝。其人长四尺，两角如茧，牙出于唇，自乳以来，有灵毛自蔽，居于深穴，其寿不可测也。帝云：“方士韩稚解绝国人言，令问人寿几何？经见几代之事？”答曰：“五运相承，迭生迭死，如飞尘细雨，存殁不可论算。 [74]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [74]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 夫精灵变化 - Refers to the transformations of spirits, indicating supernatural or mystical changes.
    • 冥会之感 - Refers to a deep, intuitive feeling or connection that transcends ordinary understanding.
    • 理故难常 - Means the principle is inherently difficult to maintain or predict.
    • 考图披籍 - Means to examine maps and study records, indicating a thorough investigation of historical or geographical sources.
    • 工人之说 - Refers to the sayings or teachings of craftsmen or workers, possibly in a metaphorical sense.
    • 谅妖言之远效焉 - Means to believe that the effects of such prophecies are far-reaching.
    • 三尺之剑 - A metaphorical reference to a sword, often symbolizing authority or power.
    • 应天地之数 - Means corresponding to the numbers of heaven and earth, suggesting a cosmic or divine order.
    • 三为阳数 - Refers to the number three as a yang (positive) number in Chinese cosmology.
    • 应天地之德 - Means corresponding to the virtues of heaven and earth.
    • 按《钩命诀》曰 - Means according to the *Gouming Jue* (a classic text), it says.
    • 萧何为昴星精 - Refers to Xiao He, a historical figure, as being the essence of the star Altair (昴星).
    • 项羽、陈胜、胡亥为三猾 - Refers to Xiang Yu, Chen Sheng, and Hu Hai as the three cunning or deceitful individuals.
    • 国为木德，汉叶火位 - Means the state is governed by the virtue of wood, and the Han dynasty by the virtue of fire, referring to the Five Elements theory.
    • 孝惠帝二年 - Refers to the second year of Emperor Hui of Han's reign.
    • 车书同文轨 - Means unified writing and cart tracks, symbolizing a harmonized society.
    • 干戈偃息 - Means swords and spears are laid down, indicating peace.
    • 重译来贡 - Means to come bearing tribute through multiple translations, referring to distant foreigners.
    • 自乳以来 - Means since birth, indicating from the time of birth.
    • 灵毛自蔽 - Means a divine hair that provides protection.
    • 居于深穴 - Means living in a deep cave.
    • 其寿不可测也 - Means their lifespan cannot be measured.
    • 方士韩稚解绝国人言 - Means the Daoist Han Zhi understands the language of the distant foreigners.
    • 令问人寿几何 - Means to ask how long their life is.
    • 经见几代之事 - Means to have seen events of several generations.
    • 五运相承 - Refers to the Five Elements (Wu Xing) theory, where each element gives way to the next.
    • 迭生迭死 - Means alternately born and dying, referring to the cyclical nature of life and death.
    • 如飞尘细雨 - Means like flying dust and fine rain, describing something as fleeting or imperceptible.
    • 存殁不可论算 - Means existence and death cannot be calculated, suggesting the unpredictability of life and death.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 夫精灵变化 - Refers to the concept of spirits and their transformations, a common theme in Chinese mythology and philosophy.
    • 冥会之感 - Refers to a deep, intuitive feeling, often associated with mystical or spiritual experiences.
    • 理故难常 - Reflects the idea that certain principles are inherently difficult to maintain or predict, a common theme in Chinese philosophical texts.
    • 考图披籍 - Indicates a thorough investigation of historical and geographical records, reflecting the importance of historical documentation in Chinese culture.
    • 工人之说 - Refers to the sayings or teachings of craftsmen or workers, possibly in a metaphorical sense, suggesting the value of practical knowledge.
    • 谅妖言之远效焉 - Reflects the belief in the far-reaching effects of prophecies or mystical predictions, a common theme in Chinese folklore.
    • 三尺之剑 - A metaphorical reference to a sword, often symbolizing authority or power, reflecting the symbolic use of objects in Chinese culture.
    • 应天地之数 - Refers to the cosmic or divine order, reflecting the influence of Chinese cosmology on literature.
    • 三为阳数 - Refers to the number three as a yang (positive) number in Chinese cosmology, reflecting the importance of numbers in Chinese philosophy.
    • 应天地之德 - Refers to the virtues of heaven and earth, reflecting the moral and ethical dimensions of Chinese thought.
    • 按《钩命诀》曰 - Refers to the *Gouming Jue* (a classic text), indicating the reliance on authoritative sources in Chinese literature.
    • 萧何为昴星精 - Refers to Xiao He, a historical figure, as being the essence of the star Altair (昴星), reflecting the integration of historical and mythological figures in Chinese literature.
    • 项羽、陈胜、胡亥为三猾 - Refers to Xiang Yu, Chen Sheng, and Hu Hai as the three cunning or deceitful individuals, reflecting the moral judgments in Chinese historical narratives.
    • 国为木德，汉叶火位 - Refers to the Five Elements theory, where the state is governed by the virtue of wood, and the Han dynasty by the virtue of fire, reflecting the influence of the Five Elements on Chinese political and philosophical thought.
    • 孝惠帝二年 - Refers to the second year of Emperor Hui of Han's reign, providing a historical context for the text.
    • 车书同文轨 - Symbolizes a harmonized society, reflecting the ideal of unity in Chinese political thought.
    • 干戈偃息 - Indicates peace, reflecting the ideal of peace and stability in Chinese political thought.
    • 重译来贡 - Refers to distant foreigners coming bearing tribute, reflecting the cosmopolitan nature of the Han dynasty.
    • 自乳以来 - Indicates from the time of birth, reflecting the emphasis on the natural and the divine in Chinese mythology.
    • 灵毛自蔽 - Refers to a divine hair that provides protection, reflecting the belief in divine protection in Chinese mythology.
    • 居于深穴 - Refers to living in a deep cave, reflecting the natural and mystical settings in Chinese mythology.
    • 其寿不可测也 - Means their lifespan cannot be measured, reflecting the mystery and unpredictability of life in Chinese mythology.
    • 方士韩稚解绝国人言 - Refers to the Daoist Han Zhi understanding the language of the distant foreigners, reflecting the role of Daoists in Chinese mythology and folklore.
    • 令问人寿几何 - Means to ask how long their life is, reflecting the curiosity about the lifespan of mythical beings.
    • 经见几代之事 - Means to have seen events of several generations, reflecting the long lifespan and wisdom of mythical beings.
    • 五运相承 - Refers to the Five Elements (Wu Xing) theory, where each element gives way to the next, reflecting the cyclical nature of life and the universe in Chinese philosophy.
    • 迭生迭死 - Means alternately born and dying, reflecting the cyclical nature of life and death in Chinese philosophy.
    • 如飞尘细雨 - Means like flying dust and fine rain, describing something as fleeting or imperceptible, reflecting the transient nature of life in Chinese philosophy.
    • 存殁不可论算 - Means existence and death cannot be calculated, reflecting the unpredictability of life and death in Chinese philosophy.

    The Record states: The transformations of spirits are manifold; the profound feelings that transcend the ordinary are inherently difficult to maintain. As for the records in the Fen (a text on divination) and the prophecies, they all serve as proof of past events; folk songs and simple sayings all seek evidence of future occurrences. By examining maps and studying records, one often finds them compiled and listed. Considering the sayings of craftsmen, one can believe in the far-reaching effects of such prophecies. A sword three feet long corresponds to the numbers of heaven and earth. Therefore, the number three is a yang number, corresponding to the virtues of heaven and earth. According to the Gouming Jue (a classic text), it says: "Xiao He is the essence of the star Altair, while Xiang Yu, Chen Sheng, and Hu Hai are the three cunning individuals." The state is governed by the virtue of wood, and the Han dynasty by the virtue of fire, which is evident in this.

    In the second year of Emperor Hui's reign, all directions praised the unification of writing and cart tracks, indicating a peaceful and harmonious world. Distant and foreign lands sent tributes through multiple translations. At that time, there was a Daoist named Han Zhi, a descendant of Han Zhong (a legendary figure). He came across the sea, claiming to be a messenger from the Eastern Sea, and having heard of the emperor's virtue spreading throughout the land, he willingly submitted himself. There was a land called Nili beyond the Land of the Rising Sun, whose people were four feet tall, had two horns like cocoons, and teeth protruding from their lips. Since birth, they had a divine hair that provided protection, lived in deep caves, and their lifespan could not be measured. The emperor said, "The Daoist Han Zhi understands the language of the distant foreigners. Let him ask how long their life is and what they have seen across several generations." The answer was, "The Five Elements succeed each other, with life and death alternating, like flying dust and fine rain, making existence and death impossible to calculate." [74]

    ”问：“女娲以前可闻乎？”对曰：“蛇身已上，八风均，四时序，不以威悦揽乎精运。”又问燧人以前，答曰：“自钻火变腥以来，父老而慈，子寿而孝。自轩皇以来，屑屑焉以相诛灭，浮靡嚣动，淫于礼，乱于乐，世德浇讹，淳风坠矣。”稚以答闻于帝。帝曰：“悠哉杳昧，非通神达理者，难可语乎！斯远矣。”稚于斯而退，莫知其所之。帝使诸方士立仙坛于长安城北，名曰“祠韩馆”。俗云：“司寒之神，祀于城阴。”按《春秋传》曰“以享司寒”，其音相乱也，定是“祠韩馆”。至二年，诏宫女百人，文锦万匹，楼船十艘，以送泥离之使，大赦天下。 [75]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [75]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 悠哉杳昧 - Distant and obscure, describing the past.
    • 非通神达理者，难可语乎 - Only those who understand profound truths can discuss such matters.
    • 斯远矣 - Emphasizes the distance and difficulty of understanding.
    • 屑屑焉以相诛灭 - Constant warfare.
    • 浮靡嚣动 - Decadent and restless society.
    • 淫于礼，乱于乐 - Excessive and disorderly rituals and music.
    • 世德浇讹，淳风坠矣 - Moral values have deteriorated and pure customs have declined.
    • 蛇身已上 - A state beyond the physical world.
    • 八风均 - The eight winds are balanced, symbolizing harmony.
    • 四时序 - The four seasons are in order, representing natural balance.
    • 不以威悦揽乎精运 - The essence of the universe operates without the need for force or pleasure.
    • 自钻火变腥以来 - The time when fire was first used to cook food.
    • 父老而慈，子寿而孝 - Describes a harmonious family life.
    • 自轩皇以来 - Marks the beginning of the Yellow Emperor's era.
    • 稚以答闻于帝 - The young man reported the answers to the emperor.
    • 稚于斯而退 - The young man withdrew.
    • 莫知其所之 - No one knew where he went.
    • 帝使诸方士立仙坛于长安城北 - The emperor ordered various scholars to build a sacred platform in the north of Chang'an.
    • 名曰‘祠韩馆’ - Names the platform 'Cishi Hall.'
    • 俗云‘司寒之神，祀于城阴’ - The common belief that the god of cold is worshipped in the shadow of the city.
    • 按《春秋传》曰‘以享司寒’ - Refers to a passage in the *Spring and Autumn Annals* that mentions offering to the god of cold.
    • 其音相乱也，定是‘祠韩馆’ - The pronunciation is similar, but it is definitely 'Cishi Hall.'
    • 至二年，诏宫女百人，文锦万匹，楼船十艘 - Lists the gifts sent.
    • 以送泥离之使 - To send a messenger to Mudili.
    • 大赦天下 - A general amnesty was declared.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 女娲 - Nüwa, a goddess in Chinese mythology, known for creating humans and repairing the sky.
    • 燧人 - Suiren, a legendary figure credited with discovering how to use fire.
    • 黄帝 - Huangdi, the Yellow Emperor, a semi-legendary figure considered the founder of Chinese civilization.
    • 《春秋传》 - The *Spring and Autumn Annals* with its commentary, a classic text in Chinese history and literature.
    • 长安 - Chang'an, the capital of several Chinese dynasties, including the Han and Tang.
    • 泥离 - Mudili, a place name or person, possibly referring to a foreign land or a specific individual.

    Proper Nouns

    • 女娲 - Nüwa
    • 燧人 - Suiren
    • 黄帝 - Huangdi
    • 《春秋传》 - *Spring and Autumn Annals* with Commentary
    • 长安 - Chang'an
    • 泥离 - Mudili
    • 祠韩馆 - Cishi Hall

    The young man asked, "Is anything known about the time before Nüwa?" The other replied, "Above the serpent's body, the eight winds are balanced, the four seasons are in order, and the essence of the universe operates without the need for force or pleasure." When asked about the time before Suiren, the response was, "Since the discovery of fire and the cooking of food, fathers were loving and sons were long-lived and filial. Since the era of Huangdi, there has been constant warfare, a decadent and restless society, excessive and disorderly rituals, and the decline of moral values and pure customs." The young man reported these answers to the emperor. The emperor said, "How distant and obscure! Only those who understand profound truths can discuss such matters. This is far beyond the reach of ordinary people." The young man then withdrew, and no one knew where he went. The emperor ordered various scholars to build a sacred platform in the north of Chang'an, naming it "Cishi Hall." Common belief held that the god of cold was worshipped in the shadow of the city. According to the Spring and Autumn Annals, it was said that offerings were made to the god of cold, and the pronunciation was similar, but it was definitely "Cishi Hall." In the second year, the emperor decreed that a hundred palace ladies, ten thousand bolts of brocade, and ten ships were to be sent as gifts to a messenger to Mudili, and a general amnesty was declared. [75]

    汉武帝思怀往者李夫人，不可复得。时始穿昆灵之池，泛翔禽之舟。帝自造歌曲，使女伶歌之。时日已西倾，凉风激水，女伶歌声甚遒，因赋《落叶哀蝉》之曲曰：“?袂兮无声，玉墀兮尘生。虚房冷而寂寞，落叶依于重扃。望彼美之女兮安得，感余心之未宁！”帝闻唱动心，闷闷不自支持，命龙膏之灯以照舟内，悲不自止。亲侍者觉帝容色愁怨，乃进洪梁之酒，酌以文螺之卮。卮出波祇之国。酒出洪梁之县，此属右扶风，至哀帝废此邑，南人受此酿法。今言“云阳出美酒”，两声相乱矣。帝饮三爵，色悦心欢，乃诏女伶出侍。帝息于延凉室，卧梦李夫人授帝蘅芜之香。帝惊起，而香气犹着衣枕，历月不歇。帝弥思求，终不复见，涕泣洽席，遂改延凉室为遗芳梦室。初，帝深嬖李夫人，死后常思梦之，或欲见夫人。帝貌憔悴，嫔御不宁。 [76]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [76]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 思怀 - Classical expression meaning 'to think of with longing' or 'to miss deeply.'
    • 不可复得 - Means 'cannot be regained' or 'cannot be recovered.'
    • 泛翔禽之舟 - Literally 'a boat of flying birds,' likely a metaphor for a luxurious or ornate vessel.
    • 自造歌曲 - Means 'composed his own songs.'
    • 使女伶歌之 - Means 'had a female singer perform it.'
    • 日已西倾 - Literally 'the sun has already tilted westward,' meaning 'the sun has set' or 'it is late in the day.'
    • 凉风激水 - Means 'cool wind stirs the water.'
    • 女伶歌声甚遒 - Means 'the female singer's voice was very strong' or 'the female singer's voice was very powerful.'
    • 赋《落叶哀蝉》之曲 - Means 'composed the song 'Falling Leaves and Mourning Cicadas.'
    • 袂兮无声 - Literally 'sleeves, oh, without sound,' a poetic expression describing the absence of movement.
    • 玉墀兮尘生 - Literally 'jade steps, oh, dust rises,' a metaphor for the desolation of a place.
    • 虚房冷而寂寞 - Means 'the empty room is cold and lonely.'
    • 落叶依于重扃 - Means 'fallen leaves cling to the heavy doors.'
    • 望彼美之女兮安得 - Means 'how can I see that beautiful woman again?'
    • 感余心之未宁 - Means 'my heart is still unsettled.'
    • 闷闷不自支持 - Means 'feeling depressed and unable to bear it.'
    • 命龙膏之灯以照舟内 - Means 'ordered a lamp made of dragon fat to illuminate the boat.'
    • 悲不自止 - Means 'cannot stop being sad.'
    • 觉帝容色愁怨 - Means 'noticed the emperor's face was filled with sorrow and resentment.'
    • 进洪梁之酒 - Means 'presented wine from Hongliang.'
    • 酌以文螺之卮 - Means 'served in a cup made of seashells.'
    • 卮出波祇之国 - Means 'the cups come from the country of Boqi.'
    • 酒出洪梁之县 - Means 'the wine comes from the county of Hongliang.'
    • 此属右扶风 - Means 'this belongs to the Right Commandery.'
    • 至哀帝废此邑 - Means 'until Emperor Ai abolished this town.'
    • 南人受此酿法 - Means 'southern people learned this brewing method.'
    • 今言“云阳出美酒” - Means 'now people say 'Yunyang produces fine wine.'
    • 两声相乱矣 - Means 'the two sounds have become confused.'
    • 色悦心欢 - Means 'his face brightened and his heart was joyful.'
    • 诏女伶出侍 - Means 'ordered the female singer to attend.'
    • 息于延凉室 - Means 'rested in the Cool Extension Room.'
    • 卧梦李夫人授帝蘅芜之香 - Means 'dreamed that Lady Li gave him the fragrance of *hengwu* (a type of herb).'
    • 涕泣洽席 - Means 'tears wet the mat.'
    • 改延凉室为遗芳梦室 - Means 'changed the Cool Extension Room to the Fragrance of Remembrance Room.'
    • 深嬖 - Means 'deeply favored' or 'deeply loved.'
    • 死后常思梦之 - Means 'often thought of and dreamed of her after her death.'
    • 或欲见夫人 - Means 'sometimes wanted to see her.'
    • 貌憔悴 - Means 'looked emaciated.'
    • 嫔御不宁 - Means 'the consorts were uneasy.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 汉武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han, a prominent ruler of the Western Han dynasty.
    • 李夫人 - Lady Li, a concubine of Emperor Wu of Han, known for her beauty and the emperor's deep affection for her.
    • 昆灵之池 - A mythical or symbolic lake, possibly a reference to a specific location or a metaphor for a place of beauty.
    • 龙膏之灯 - A lamp made of dragon fat, a luxurious and rare item.
    • 洪梁之酒 - Wine from Hongliang, a specific region known for its wine.
    • 文螺之卮 - Cups made of seashells, a luxurious item.
    • 波祇之国 - The country of Boqi, a mythical or distant land.
    • 右扶风 - The Right Commandery, an administrative division during the Han dynasty.
    • 哀帝 - Emperor Ai of Han, a later emperor who abolished the town of Hongliang.
    • 云阳 - A place name, possibly referring to a region known for its wine.
    • 蘅芜之香 - The fragrance of *hengwu*, a type of herb or plant.
    • 延凉室 - The Cool Extension Room, a specific room in the palace.
    • 遗芳梦室 - The Fragrance of Remembrance Room, a room named after the dream.
    • 嫔御 - The consorts or concubines of the emperor.

    Proper Nouns

    • 汉武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 李夫人 - Lady Li.
    • 昆灵之池 - A mythical or symbolic lake.
    • 龙膏之灯 - A lamp made of dragon fat.
    • 洪梁之酒 - Wine from Hongliang.
    • 文螺之卮 - Cups made of seashells.
    • 波祇之国 - The country of Boqi.
    • 右扶风 - The Right Commandery.
    • 哀帝 - Emperor Ai of Han.
    • 云阳 - A place name.
    • 蘅芜之香 - The fragrance of *hengwu*.
    • 延凉室 - The Cool Extension Room.
    • 遗芳梦室 - The Fragrance of Remembrance Room.
    • 嫔御 - The consorts or concubines of the emperor.

    Emperor Wu of Han deeply missed Lady Li, whom he could no longer see. At that time, he had just excavated the Kunling Pool and was boating on a vessel adorned like a flying bird. The emperor composed his own songs and had a female singer perform them. As the sun had already set and a cool wind stirred the water, the female singer's voice was very powerful, and he composed the song "Falling Leaves and Mourning Cicadas," which went: "Silent are the sleeves, dust rises on the jade steps. The empty room is cold and lonely, fallen leaves cling to the heavy doors. How can I see that beautiful woman again? My heart is still unsettled!" The emperor was deeply moved by the song, feeling depressed and unable to bear it, and ordered a lamp made of dragon fat to illuminate the boat, unable to stop his sadness. His attendants noticed his face was filled with sorrow and resentment, and they presented wine from Hongliang, served in cups made of seashells. These cups came from the country of Boqi, and the wine came from the county of Hongliang, which belonged to the Right Commandery. Until Emperor Ai abolished this town, southern people learned this brewing method. Now people say "Yunyang produces fine wine," but the two sounds have become confused. The emperor drank three cups, his face brightened, and his heart was joyful, and he ordered the female singer to attend. He rested in the Cool Extension Room and dreamed that Lady Li gave him the fragrance of hengwu. He woke up, and the fragrance still lingered on his clothes and pillow, lasting for months. The emperor continued to long for her, but she never appeared again, and he wept until the mat was wet, and he changed the Cool Extension Room to the Fragrance of Remembrance Room. Initially, the emperor deeply favored Lady Li, and after her death, he often thought of and dreamed of her, sometimes wanting to see her. He looked emaciated, and his consorts were uneasy. [76]

    诏李少君，与之语曰；“朕思李夫人，其可得见乎？”少君曰：“可遥见，不可同于帷幄。暗海有潜英之石，其色青，轻如毛羽。寒盛则石温，暑盛则石冷。刻之为人像，神悟不异真人。使此石像往，则夫人至矣。此石人能传译人言语，有声无气，故知神异也。”帝曰：“此石像可得否？”少君曰：“愿得楼船百艘，巨力千人，能浮水登木者，皆使明于道术，赍不死之药。”乃至暗海，经十年而还。昔之去人，或升云不归，或托形假死，获反者四五人。得此石，即命工人依先图刻作夫人形。刻成，置于轻纱幕里，宛若生时。帝大悦，问少君曰：“可得近乎？“少君曰：“譬如中宵忽梦，而昼可得近观乎？此石毒，宜远望，不可逼也。勿轻万乘之尊，惑此精魅之物！”帝乃从其谏。见夫人毕，少君乃使舂此石人为丸，服之，不复思梦。乃筑灵梦台，岁时祀之。 [77]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [77]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 诏李少君 - Formal command to summon Li Shaojun.
    • 与之语曰 - To speak with him and say...
    • 朕思李夫人，其可得见乎 - I miss Lady Li; can I see her again?
    • 可遥见，不可同于帷幄 - She can be seen from afar but not in the same room.
    • 暗海有潜英之石 - In the Dark Sea, there is a stone called the Hidden Pearl.
    • 其色青，轻如毛羽 - It is blue in color and as light as feathers.
    • 寒盛则石温，暑盛则石冷 - When it is cold, the stone is warm; when it is hot, the stone is cool.
    • 刻之为人像，神悟不异真人 - Carving it into a human likeness, it has a divine understanding no different from a real person.
    • 使此石像往，则夫人至矣 - If this stone figure goes, then Lady Li will come.
    • 此石人能传译人言语，有声无气，故知神异也 - This stone figure can translate human speech, having sound but no breath, thus proving its supernatural nature.
    • 帝曰：‘此石像可得否？’ - The emperor said, 'Can I get this stone figure?'
    • 愿得楼船百艘，巨力千人，能浮水登木者，皆使明于道术，赍不死之药 - I wish to have a hundred ships, a thousand strong men, and those who can swim and climb trees, all skilled in Daoist arts, bringing the elixir of immortality.
    • 乃至暗海，经十年而还 - They went to the Dark Sea and returned after ten years.
    • 昔之去人，或升云不归，或托形假死，获反者四五人 - Those who went before either ascended to the clouds and did not return, or took on false forms and pretended to die; only four or five returned.
    • 得此石，即命工人依先图刻作夫人形 - When they obtained the stone, they ordered the craftsmen to carve it according to the original design into the likeness of Lady Li.
    • 刻成，置于轻纱幕里，宛若生时 - Once carved, it was placed behind a sheer curtain, looking just like her when alive.
    • 帝大悦，问少君曰：‘可得近乎？’ - The emperor was very pleased and asked Li Shaojun, 'Can I get close to her?'
    • 譬如中宵忽梦，而昼可得近观乎 - It's like having a dream in the middle of the night; can you see it clearly during the day?
    • 此石毒，宜远望，不可逼也 - This stone is toxic; it's best to look at it from a distance and not get too close.
    • 勿轻万乘之尊，惑此精魅之物 - Do not underestimate the majesty of the emperor; be wary of this supernatural entity.
    • 帝乃从其谏 - The emperor then followed his advice.
    • 见夫人毕，少君乃使舂此石人为丸，服之，不复思梦 - After seeing Lady Li, Li Shaojun had the stone figure ground into pills, which were taken, and the emperor no longer dreamed of her.
    • 乃筑灵梦台，岁时祀之 - They then built a Spirit Dream Terrace and worshipped it regularly.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 诏 - A formal imperial edict.
    • 李少君 - A figure associated with Daoist practices and alchemy.
    • 李夫人 - A concubine of Emperor Wu of Han, known for her beauty and the emperor's deep affection for her.
    • 暗海 - A mythical sea, often associated with the realm of the immortals.
    • 潜英之石 - A stone believed to have magical properties.
    • 楼船 - Large warships used in ancient China.
    • 巨力千人 - Strong men, likely referring to physically powerful individuals.
    • 能浮水登木者 - People skilled in swimming and climbing trees, often associated with Daoist practices.
    • 道术 - Daoist arts and practices.
    • 不死之药 - The elixir of immortality, a common theme in Daoist alchemy.
    • 灵梦台 - A platform for spiritual and dream-related rituals.

    Proper Nouns

    • 李少君 - A historical figure known for his Daoist practices and alchemy.
    • 李夫人 - A concubine of Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 暗海 - A mythical sea.
    • 潜英之石 - A magical stone.
    • 楼船 - Large warships.
    • 道术 - Daoist arts and practices.
    • 不死之药 - The elixir of immortality.
    • 灵梦台 - A memorial platform.

    The emperor issued an edict to summon Li Shaojun and spoke with him, saying, "I miss Lady Li; can I see her again?" Li Shaojun replied, "She can be seen from afar but not in the same room. In the Dark Sea, there is a stone called the Hidden Pearl, which is blue in color and as light as feathers. When it is cold, the stone is warm; when it is hot, the stone is cool. Carving it into a human likeness, it has a divine understanding no different from a real person. If this stone figure goes, then Lady Li will come. This stone figure can translate human speech, having sound but no breath, thus proving its supernatural nature." The emperor said, "Can I get this stone figure?" Li Shaojun replied, "I wish to have a hundred ships, a thousand strong men, and those who can swim and climb trees, all skilled in Daoist arts, bringing the elixir of immortality." They went to the Dark Sea and returned after ten years. Those who went before either ascended to the clouds and did not return, or took on false forms and pretended to die; only four or five returned. When they obtained the stone, they ordered the craftsmen to carve it according to the original design into the likeness of Lady Li. Once carved, it was placed behind a sheer curtain, looking just like her when alive. The emperor was very pleased and asked Li Shaojun, "Can I get close to her?" Li Shaojun said, "It's like having a dream in the middle of the night; can you see it clearly during the day? This stone is toxic; it's best to look at it from a distance and not get too close. Do not underestimate the majesty of the emperor; be wary of this supernatural entity!" The emperor then followed his advice. After seeing Lady Li, Li Shaojun had the stone figure ground into pills, which were taken, and the emperor no longer dreamed of her. They then built a Spirit Dream Terrace and worshipped it regularly. [77]

    元封元年，浮忻国贡兰金之泥。此金出汤泉，盛夏之时，水常沸涌，有若汤火，飞鸟不能过。国人常见水边有人冶此金为器，金状混混若泥，如紫磨之色；百铸，其色变白，有光如银，即“银烛”是也。常以此泥封诸函匣及诸宫门，鬼魅不敢干。当汉世，上将出征及使绝国，多以此泥为玺封。卫青、张骞、苏武、傅介子之使，皆受金泥之玺封也。武帝崩后，此泥乃绝焉。
    　　日南之南，有淫泉之浦。言其水浸淫从地而出成渊，故曰“淫泉”。或言此水甘软，男女饮之则淫。其水小处可滥觞褰涉，大处可方舟沿泝，随流屈直。其水激石之声，似人之歌笑，闻者令人淫动，故俗谓之“淫泉”。时有凫雁，色如金，群飞戏于沙濑，?者得之，乃真金凫也。当秦破骊山之坟，行野者见金凫向南而飞，至淫泉。后宝鼎元年，张善为日南太守，郡民有得金凫以献。张善该博德通，考其年月，即秦始皇墓之金凫也。 [78]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [78]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 元封元年 - The first year of the Yuánfēng era (110 BCE) during the reign of Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 浮忻国 - A fictional or lesser-known country, contributing to the exotic and mysterious nature of the narrative.
    • 兰金之泥 - A type of gold ore, possibly a metaphor for something valuable and rare.
    • 盛夏之时 - The hottest part of summer, setting the scene for the boiling springs.
    • 飞鸟不能过 - The intense heat, a common description in classical literature to emphasize extreme conditions.
    • 国人常见水边有人冶此金为器 - A common sight among the locals, reflecting the daily life and activities of the people.
    • 金状混混若泥 - The gold ore appears as if it were mud, emphasizing its form before processing.
    • 百铸，其色变白，有光如银 - The transformation process, highlighting the alchemical nature of the gold.
    • 银烛 - A term for the silver-like object, possibly a candle or a similar item made from the processed gold.
    • 封诸函匣及诸宫门 - Sealing various chests and palace doors, suggesting a protective or ceremonial use.
    • 鬼魅不敢干 - Ghosts and evil spirits dare not approach, implying a supernatural protection.
    • 上将出征及使绝国 - High-ranking officials going to war or on missions to distant lands, indicating significant events.
    • 多以此泥为玺封 - Often using this gold mud to seal imperial seals, highlighting its importance.
    • 卫青、张骞、苏武、傅介子之使 - Famous historical figures who were sent on missions, adding historical context.
    • 日南之南 - The southernmost part of the territory, indicating a distant and remote location.
    • 淫泉之浦 - A place where the water flows out of the ground, forming a pond or stream, with a suggestive name.
    • 浸淫从地而出成渊 - Water seeping out of the ground and forming a deep pool, describing the formation of the spring.
    • 小处可滥觞褰涉 - Small areas can be easily crossed, suggesting the shallow parts of the water.
    • 大处可方舟沿泝 - Larger areas can accommodate boats, indicating the navigability of the water.
    • 随流屈直 - Following the course of the water, which can be straight or winding.
    • 激石之声，似人之歌笑 - The sound of water hitting rocks is like human singing and laughing, creating a vivid auditory image.
    • 闻者令人淫动 - Those who hear it feel a sense of desire, adding a moral or cautionary note.
    • 秦破骊山之坟 - The time when the Qin dynasty destroyed the mausoleum of Emperor Qin Shi Huang, a significant historical event.
    • 行野者见金凫向南而飞 - A traveler saw a golden duck flying south, leading to a discovery.
    • 宝鼎元年 - The first year of the Bǎodǐng era (148 CE) during the reign of Emperor Huan of Han.
    • 张善为日南太守 - Zhang Shan served as the governor of Rìnán, providing a specific historical context.
    • 郡民有得金凫以献 - A local resident found and presented the golden duck, continuing the narrative.
    • 张善该博德通 - Zhang Shan was knowledgeable and virtuous, adding character depth.
    • 考其年月，即秦始皇墓之金凫也 - Determining the date and confirming that it was the golden duck from the tomb of Emperor Qin Shi Huang, providing a historical link.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 元封元年 - The first year of the Yuánfēng era (110 BCE) during the reign of Emperor Wu of Han, marking a significant period in Chinese history.
    • 浮忻国 - A fictional or lesser-known country, contributing to the exotic and mysterious nature of the narrative.
    • 兰金之泥 - A type of gold ore, possibly a metaphor for something valuable and rare.
    • 盛夏之时 - The hottest part of summer, setting the scene for the boiling springs.
    • 飞鸟不能过 - The intense heat, a common description in classical literature to emphasize extreme conditions.
    • 国人常见水边有人冶此金为器 - A common sight among the locals, reflecting the daily life and activities of the people.
    • 金状混混若泥 - The gold ore appears as if it were mud, emphasizing its form before processing.
    • 百铸，其色变白，有光如银 - The transformation process, highlighting the alchemical nature of the gold.
    • 银烛 - A term for the silver-like object, possibly a candle or a similar item made from the processed gold.
    • 封诸函匣及诸宫门 - Sealing various chests and palace doors, suggesting a protective or ceremonial use.
    • 鬼魅不敢干 - Ghosts and evil spirits dare not approach, implying a supernatural protection.
    • 上将出征及使绝国 - High-ranking officials going to war or on missions to distant lands, indicating significant events.
    • 多以此泥为玺封 - Often using this gold mud to seal imperial seals, highlighting its importance.
    • 卫青、张骞、苏武、傅介子之使 - Famous historical figures who were sent on missions, adding historical context.
    • 日南之南 - The southernmost part of the territory, indicating a distant and remote location.
    • 淫泉之浦 - A place where the water flows out of the ground, forming a pond or stream, with a suggestive name.
    • 浸淫从地而出成渊 - Water seeping out of the ground and forming a deep pool, describing the formation of the spring.
    • 小处可滥觞褰涉 - Small areas can be easily crossed, suggesting the shallow parts of the water.
    • 大处可方舟沿泝 - Larger areas can accommodate boats, indicating the navigability of the water.
    • 随流屈直 - Following the course of the water, which can be straight or winding.
    • 激石之声，似人之歌笑 - The sound of water hitting rocks is like human singing and laughing, creating a vivid auditory image.
    • 闻者令人淫动 - Those who hear it feel a sense of desire, adding a moral or cautionary note.
    • 秦破骊山之坟 - The time when the Qin dynasty destroyed the mausoleum of Emperor Qin Shi Huang, a significant historical event.
    • 行野者见金凫向南而飞 - A traveler saw a golden duck flying south, leading to a discovery.
    • 宝鼎元年 - The first year of the Bǎodǐng era (148 CE) during the reign of Emperor Huan of Han, marking another significant period in Chinese history.
    • 张善为日南太守 - Zhang Shan served as the governor of Rìnán, providing a specific historical context.
    • 郡民有得金凫以献 - A local resident found and presented the golden duck, continuing the narrative.
    • 张善该博德通 - Zhang Shan was knowledgeable and virtuous, adding character depth.
    • 考其年月，即秦始皇墓之金凫也 - Determining the date and confirming that it was the golden duck from the tomb of Emperor Qin Shi Huang, providing a historical link.

    In the first year of the Yuánfēng era, the country of Fúxīn presented the mud of lánjīn gold. This gold comes from the hot springs, during the hottest part of summer, the water often boils and surges, as if it were boiling water or fire, so intense that birds cannot fly over it. The locals often see people working by the water to make objects from this gold, the gold appearing as if it were mud, with a color like polished purple; after many castings, its color turns white, shining like silver, which is known as the "silver candle." This mud is commonly used to seal various chests and palace doors, and ghosts and evil spirits dare not approach. During the Han dynasty, high-ranking officials going to war or on missions to distant lands often used this mud to seal imperial seals. The envoys of Wei Qing, Zhang Qian, Su Wu, and Fu Jiezi all received seals sealed with this gold mud. After the death of Emperor Wu, this mud ceased to be used.

    To the south of Rìnnán, there is a pond called Yínquán. It is said that the water seeps out of the ground and forms a deep pool, hence the name "Yínquán." Some say that this water is sweet and soft, and if men and women drink it, they become lustful. In small areas, it can be easily crossed, and in larger areas, boats can navigate along its course, following its winding path. The sound of the water hitting the rocks is like human singing and laughter, and those who hear it feel a sense of desire, hence the common name "Yínquán." Occasionally, there are ducks and geese with a golden color, flying in groups and playing on the sandy banks. A traveler once saw a golden duck flying south and arriving at Yínquán. Later, in the first year of the Bǎodǐng era, Zhang Shan served as the governor of Rìnnán, and a local resident found and presented a golden duck to him. Zhang Shan was knowledgeable and virtuous, and upon examining the date, he confirmed that it was the golden duck from the tomb of Emperor Qin Shi Huang. [78]

    昔始皇为冢，敛天下瑰异，生殉工人，倾远方奇宝于冢中，为江海川渎及列山岳之形。以沙棠沉檀为舟楫，金银为凫雁，以琉璃杂宝为龟鱼。又于海中作玉象鲸鱼，衔火珠为星，以代膏烛，光出墓中，精灵之伟也。昔生埋工人于冢内，至被开时，皆不死。工人于冢内琢石为龙凤仙人之像，及作碑文辞赞。汉初发此冢，验诸史传，皆无列仙龙凤之制，则知生埋匠人之所作也。后人更写此碑文，而辞多怨酷之言，乃谓为“怨碑”。《史记》略而不录。 [79]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [79]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 昔始皇为冢 - Refers to the past, specifically to the time of Qin Shi Huang, and describes the construction of his tomb.
    • 敛天下瑰异 - Means to gather rare and precious things from all over the world.
    • 生殉工人 - Refers to the practice of burying living workers alive with the deceased.
    • 倾远方奇宝于冢中 - Means to fill the tomb with rare and exotic treasures from distant lands.
    • 为江海川渎及列山岳之形 - Describes the tomb's interior as being modeled after rivers, seas, lakes, and mountains.
    • 以沙棠沉檀为舟楫 - Means to use sandalwood and fragrant wood to make boats and paddles.
    • 金银为凫雁 - Means to use gold and silver to make ducks and geese.
    • 以琉璃杂宝为龟鱼 - Means to use glass and various precious materials to make turtles and fish.
    • 又于海中作玉象鲸鱼，衔火珠为星，以代膏烛，光出墓中 - Describes the creation of jade elephants and whales in the sea, holding fire pearls as stars to replace oil lamps, with light shining out of the tomb.
    • 精灵之伟也 - Means the greatness of the spirit or the grandeur of the spiritual aspect.
    • 昔生埋工人于冢内，至被开时，皆不死 - Refers to the workers who were buried alive but survived when the tomb was opened.
    • 工人于冢内琢石为龙凤仙人之像，及作碑文辞赞 - Means the workers carved stone images of dragons, phoenixes, immortals, and wrote inscriptions and praises.
    • 汉初发此冢，验诸史传，皆无列仙龙凤之制 - Means that when the tomb was opened at the beginning of the Han dynasty, it was checked against historical records, and there were no records of such divine figures.
    • 后人更写此碑文，而辞多怨酷之言，乃谓为“怨碑” - Means that later people rewrote the inscription, which contained many words of resentment and cruelty, and it was called the 'Resentment Stele.'
    • 《史记》略而不录 - Means that the *Records of the Grand Historian* (Shiji) briefly mentioned it but did not record it in detail.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 始皇 - Refers to Qin Shi Huang, the first emperor of China.
    • 汉初 - Refers to the early years of the Han dynasty.
    • 《史记》 - Refers to the *Records of the Grand Historian*, a historical text compiled by Sima Qian.
    • 列仙龙凤 - Refers to divine figures such as immortals, dragons, and phoenixes, which are common in Chinese mythology.
    • 怨碑 - Refers to the 'Resentment Stele,' a term used to describe an inscription that expresses resentment or criticism.

    Proper Nouns

    • 始皇 - Qin Shi Huang.
    • 汉初 - Early Han dynasty.
    • 《史记》 - *Records of the Grand Historian*.
    • 怨碑 - Resentment Stele.

    In the past, Qin Shi Huang constructed his tomb, gathering rare and precious things from all over the world, and burying living workers alive with him. He filled the tomb with exotic treasures from distant lands, creating an interior modeled after rivers, seas, lakes, and mountains. He used sandalwood and fragrant wood to make boats and paddles, gold and silver to make ducks and geese, and glass and various precious materials to make turtles and fish. In the sea, he created jade elephants and whales, holding fire pearls as stars to replace oil lamps, with light shining out of the tomb, showcasing the grandeur of the spiritual aspect. When the tomb was opened, the workers who had been buried alive were found to be still alive. These workers carved stone images of dragons, phoenixes, immortals, and wrote inscriptions and praises inside the tomb. When the tomb was opened at the beginning of the Han dynasty, it was checked against historical records, and there were no records of such divine figures. It was concluded that these carvings and inscriptions were the work of the surviving workers. Later, the inscription was rewritten, containing many words of resentment and cruelty, and it was called the "Resentment Stele." The Records of the Grand Historian briefly mentioned it but did not record it in detail. [79]

    董偃常卧延清之室，以画石为床，文如锦也。石体甚轻，出郅支国。上设紫琉璃帐，火齐屏风，列灵麻之烛，以紫玉为盘，如屈龙，皆用杂宝饰之。侍者于户外扇偃。偃曰：“玉石岂须扇而后凉耶？”侍者乃却扇，以手摸，方知有屏风。又以玉精为盘，贮冰于膝前。玉精与冰同其洁澈。侍者谓冰之无盘，必融湿席，乃合玉盘拂之，落阶下，冰玉俱碎，偃以为乐。此玉精，千涂国所贡也。武帝以此赐偃。哀、平之世，民家犹有此器，而多残破。及王莽之世，不复知其所在。 [80]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [80]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 董偃常卧延清之室 - Idiomatic expression indicating a habitual action in a specific location.
    • 以画石为床，文如锦也 - Descriptive phrase emphasizing the luxurious nature of the bed.
    • 石体甚轻，出郅支国 - Factual statement about the origin and properties of the stone.
    • 上设紫琉璃帐，火齐屏风 - Descriptive phrase highlighting the luxurious setting.
    • 列灵麻之烛，以紫玉为盘，如屈龙，皆用杂宝饰之 - Detailed description of the luxurious furnishings.
    • 侍者于户外扇偃 - Descriptive phrase indicating the attentive service provided.
    • 玉石岂须扇而后凉耶？ - Rhetorical question emphasizing the natural coolness of the materials.
    • 侍者乃却扇，以手摸，方知有屏风 - Sequence of actions revealing the presence of a screen.
    • 又以玉精为盘，贮冰于膝前 - Descriptive phrase highlighting the cooling effect of the ice.
    • 玉精与冰同其洁澈 - Comparative statement emphasizing the clarity of the materials.
    • 侍者谓冰之无盘，必融湿席，乃合玉盘拂之，落阶下，冰玉俱碎，偃以为乐 - Sequence of actions leading to an amusing outcome.
    • 此玉精，千涂国所贡也 - Factual statement about the origin of the jade plate.
    • 武帝以此赐偃 - Factual statement about the gift.
    • 哀、平之世，民家犹有此器，而多残破 - Historical statement about the condition of the item.
    • 及王莽之世，不复知其所在 - Historical statement about the loss of the item.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 董偃 - Historical figure, likely a favorite of Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 延清 - Likely a person or a place, possibly a palace or a specific room.
    • 郅支国 - Ancient kingdom, possibly referring to the kingdom of Zhizhi.
    • 武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han, a powerful and influential ruler.
    • 哀、平之世 - Reigns of Emperors Ai and Ping of Han, marking the end of the Western Han dynasty.
    • 王莽之世 - Reign of Wang Mang, who established the Xin dynasty and briefly overthrew the Han dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 董偃 - Historical figure, likely a favorite of Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 延清 - Likely a person or a place, possibly a palace or a specific room.
    • 郅支国 - Ancient kingdom, possibly referring to the kingdom of Zhizhi.
    • 武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han, a powerful and influential ruler.
    • 哀、平之世 - Reigns of Emperors Ai and Ping of Han, marking the end of the Western Han dynasty.
    • 王莽之世 - Reign of Wang Mang, who established the Xin dynasty and briefly overthrew the Han dynasty.

    Dong Yan often slept in the room of Yan Qing, using painted stones as a bed, the patterns like brocade. The stone was very light, coming from the country of Zhizhi. Above, there were purple glass curtains and fire-screened screens, with candles made of lingma (a type of plant), and a purple jade plate shaped like a coiled dragon, all adorned with various treasures. The attendant fanned Dong Yan outside the door. Dong Yan asked, "Do jade and stone need to be fanned to cool down?" The attendant then stopped fanning and touched it, only then realizing there was a screen. There was also a jade plate, storing ice in front of his knees. The jade plate and the ice were equally pure and transparent. The attendant thought that without a plate, the ice would melt and wet the mat, so he tried to sweep it with the jade plate, but it fell down the steps, shattering both the ice and the jade, which Dong Yan found amusing. This jade plate was a gift from the country of Qiantu. Emperor Wu gave this to Dong Yan. During the reigns of Emperors Ai and Ping, common households still had this item, but it was mostly broken. By the time of Wang Mang, it was no longer known where it was. [80]

    太初二年，大月氏国贡双头鸡，四足一尾，鸣则俱鸣。武帝置于甘泉故馆，更以余鸡混之，得其种类而不能鸣。谏者曰：“《诗》云：‘牝鸡无晨。’一云：‘牝鸡之晨，惟家之索。’今雄类不鸣，非吉祥也。”帝乃送还西域。行至西关，鸡反顾望汉宫而哀鸣。故谣言曰：“三七末世，鸡不鸣，犬不吠，宫中荆刺乱相系，当有九虎争为帝。”至王莽篡位，将军有九虎之号。其后丧乱弥多，宫掖中生蒿棘，家无鸡鸣犬吠。此鸡未至月支国，乃飞于天汉，声似鹍鸡，翱翔云里。一名暄鸡，昆、暄之音相类。 [81]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [81]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 四足一尾，鸣则俱鸣 - Describes the unique physical characteristics of the chicken: four feet and one tail, and it would crow together when both heads did.
    • 《诗》云：‘牝鸡无晨。’一云：‘牝鸡之晨，惟家之索。’ - Quotes from the *Book of Songs*: 'A hen does not crow at dawn.' Another version says, 'If a hen crows at dawn, it is a sign of household decline.'
    • 三七末世，鸡不鸣，犬不吠，宫中荆刺乱相系，当有九虎争为帝 - Predicts a chaotic end times scenario where chickens do not crow, dogs do not bark, thorns and brambles grow in the palace, and nine tigers will vie for the throne.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 太初二年 - Refers to the second year of the Tai' An era during the reign of Emperor Wu of Han (141 BCE).
    • 大月氏国 - Refers to the Great Yuezhi, an ancient nomadic people who later established the Kushan Empire.
    • 武帝 - Refers to Emperor Wu of Han, a powerful and influential ruler known for his military campaigns and cultural achievements.
    • 甘泉故馆 - Refers to the Old Pavilion of Ganquan, a place in the Han capital where the emperor might have kept exotic animals.
    • 王莽篡位 - Refers to Wang Mang's usurpation of the Han throne, marking the end of the Western Han dynasty and the beginning of the Xin dynasty.
    • 九虎 - Refers to the title 'Nine Tigers' used by Wang Mang's generals, symbolizing their power and authority.
    • 天汉 - Refers to the Milky Way, often used in Chinese literature as a symbol of the heavens or the celestial realm.
    • 鹍鸡 - A type of bird, possibly a mythical or rare bird, known for its distinctive call.
    • 暄鸡 - Another name for the chicken, possibly derived from its call or appearance.

    Proper Nouns

    • 太初二年 - The second year of the Tai' An era.
    • 大月氏国 - The Great Yuezhi country.
    • 武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 甘泉故馆 - The Old Pavilion of Ganquan.
    • 《诗》 - The *Book of Songs*.
    • 王莽 - Wang Mang, a usurper who founded the Xin dynasty.
    • 九虎 - The title 'Nine Tigers' used by Wang Mang's generals.
    • 天汉 - The Milky Way.
    • 鹍鸡 - A type of bird.
    • 暄鸡 - Another name for the chicken.

    During the second year of the Tai' An era, the Great Yuezhi country presented a double-headed chicken to the Han court. This chicken had four feet and one tail, and both heads would crow together. Emperor Wu placed the chicken in the old Pavilion of Ganquan and mixed it with other chickens, but the others could not produce the same sound. Advisers said, "The Book of Songs states: 'A hen does not crow at dawn.' Another version says, 'If a hen crows at dawn, it is a sign of household decline.' Now that the male chickens do not crow, this is not a good omen." The emperor then sent the chicken back to the Western Regions. As it traveled to the Western Pass, the chicken looked back at the Han palace and mournfully crowed. Therefore, there was a folk saying which said: "In the chaotic end times, chickens do not crow, dogs do not bark, thorns and brambles grow in the palace, and nine tigers will vie for the throne." When Wang Mang usurped the throne, his generals were known by the title "Nine Tigers." Afterward, chaos and disorder increased, with thorns and brambles growing in the palace and no chickens crowing or dogs barking in the households. Before the chicken reached the Yuezhi country, it flew into the Milky Way, sounding like a kun chicken and soaring through the clouds. It is also known as a xuan chicken, with the sounds kun and xuan being similar. [81]

    天汉二年，渠搜国之西，有祈沦之国。其俗淳和，人寿三百岁。有寿木之林，一树千寻，日月为之隐蔽。若经憩此木下，皆不死不病。或有泛海越山来会其国，归怀其叶者，则终身不老。其国人缀草毛为绳，结网为衣，似今之?纨也。至元狩六年，渠搜国献网衣一袭。帝焚于九达之道，恐后人征求，以物奢费，烧之，烟如金石之气。
    　　太始二年，西方有因霄之国，人皆善啸。丈夫啸闻百里，妇人啸闻五十里，如笙竽之音，秋冬则声清亮，春夏则声沉下。人舌尖处倒向喉内，亦曰两舌重沓，以爪徐刮之，则啸声逾远。故《吕氏春秋》云“反舌殊乡之国”，即此谓也。有至圣之君，则来服其化。 [82]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [82]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 天漢二年 - The second year of the Tianhan era (101 BCE).
    • 渠搜國 - A kingdom in the Western Regions.
    • 祈淪之國 - A mythical kingdom.
    • 壽木之林 - A forest of longevity trees.
    • 網衣 - A type of clothing.
    • 九達之道 - A major thoroughfare.
    • 太始二年 - The second year of the Taishi era (95 BCE).
    • 因霄之國 - Another mythical kingdom.
    • 《吕氏春秋》 - The Spring and Autumn Annals of Mr. Lü.
    • 至聖之君 - A most holy or sage ruler.
    • 服其化 - To be influenced or transformed by their culture.
    • 泛海越山 - Crossing seas and mountains.
    • 網衣 - A type of clothing made from nets.
    • 焚於九達之道 - Burning on a major thoroughfare.
    • 燒之，煙如金石之氣 - The smoke resembled the vapor of precious metals and stones.
    • 善啸 - Good at whistling or making a high-pitched sound.
    • 丈夫啸闻百里，妇人啸闻五十里 - Men can be heard up to 100 miles away, women up to 50 miles away.
    • 笙竽之音 - The sound of a sheng (a reed pipe instrument) and a yu (a type of zither).
    • 秋冬則聲清亮，春夏則聲沉下 - In autumn and winter, the sound is clear and bright; in spring and summer, it is deep and low.
    • 舌尖處倒向喉內 - The tip of the tongue is inverted into the throat.
    • 兩舌重沓 - Two tongues overlapping.
    • 爪徐刮之 - Gently scratching with nails.
    • 反舌殊鄉之國 - A country where people have inverted tongues, found in different regions.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 天漢二年 - The second year of the Tianhan era (101 BCE) during Emperor Wu of Han's reign.
    • 渠搜國 - A kingdom mentioned in the *Records of the Grand Historian* (Shiji), often associated with the Western Regions.
    • 祈淪之國 - A fictional or mythical kingdom, possibly inspired by ancient Chinese legends.
    • 壽木之林 - Symbolizes longevity and immortality, common themes in Chinese mythology.
    • 網衣 - A type of clothing, possibly a reference to a specific garment or fabric.
    • 九達之道 - A major thoroughfare, often used in classical texts to describe important roads.
    • 太始二年 - The second year of the Taishi era (95 BCE) during Emperor Wu of Han's reign.
    • 因霄之國 - Another fictional or mythical kingdom, possibly inspired by ancient Chinese legends.
    • 善啸 - The ability to whistle or make a high-pitched sound, often associated with certain cultures or mythologies.
    • 反舌殊鄉之國 - A reference to the *Lüshi Chunqiu* (Spring and Autumn Annals of Mr. Lü), an ancient Chinese text that mentions countries with people who have inverted tongues.
    • 至聖之君 - A reference to sage rulers in Chinese philosophy, often associated with Confucian ideals.

    Proper Nouns

    • 天漢二年 - The second year of the Tianhan era (101 BCE).
    • 渠搜國 - A kingdom in the Western Regions.
    • 祈淪之國 - A mythical kingdom.
    • 壽木之林 - A forest of longevity trees.
    • 網衣 - A type of clothing.
    • 九達之道 - A major thoroughfare.
    • 太始二年 - The second year of the Taishi era (95 BCE).
    • 因霄之國 - Another mythical kingdom.
    • 《吕氏春秋》 - The Spring and Autumn Annals of Mr. Lü.

    In the second year of the Tianhan era (101 BCE), in the west of the Chushou Kingdom, there was the Qilun Kingdom. Its customs were pure and harmonious, and the people lived for three hundred years. There was a forest of longevity trees, each tree reaching a height of a thousand fathoms, hiding the sun and moon. If one rested under these trees, they would neither fall ill nor die. Some who crossed seas and mountains to visit this kingdom brought back leaves, and those who kept them would never age. The people of the kingdom wove grass and hair into ropes and made clothing from nets, similar to the fine silk of today. In the sixth year of the Yuanshou era (101 BCE), the Chushou Kingdom presented a set of net clothing. The emperor burned it on a major thoroughfare, fearing that future generations would covet such luxury, and the smoke resembled the vapor of precious metals and stones.

    In the second year of the Taishi era (95 BCE), in the west, there was the Yin Xiao Kingdom, where the people were all skilled at whistling. Men could be heard up to a hundred miles away, and women up to fifty miles away, with a sound like that of a sheng and a yu. In autumn and winter, the sound was clear and bright; in spring and summer, it was deep and low. The tip of their tongue was inverted into the throat, also called two tongues overlapping, and if gently scratched with nails, the whistling sound would travel even farther. Therefore, the Spring and Autumn Annals of Mr. Lü states, "A country with inverted tongues in different regions," referring to this. If there were a most holy ruler, they would be influenced by their culture. [82]

    录曰：汉兴，继六国之遗弊，天子思于圣德。是以黔黎嗟秦亡之晚，恨汉来之迟。高祖肇基帝业，恢张区宇。孝惠务宽刑辟，以成无为之治，德侔三王，教通四海。至于武帝，世载愈光，省方巡岳，标元崇号，闻礼乐以恢风，广文义以饰俗，改律历而建封禅，祀百神以招群瑞。虽“钦明”茂于《唐书》，“文思”称于《虞典》，岂尚兹焉！观乎周、孔之教，不贵虚无之学。武帝修黄老，治却老之方，求报无福之祀。是以张敞切言，使远斥仙术，指以苌弘、楚襄怀、秦皇、徐福之事，故辛垣之徒，卒见夷戮。夫仙者，尚冲静以忘形体，守寂寞而袪嚣务。武帝好微行而尚克伐，恢宫宇而广苑囿，永乖长生久视之法，失玄一守道之要，悔少翁之先诛，惑栾大之诡说。 [83]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [83]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 继六国之遗弊 - Following the legacy of the six states' misfortunes; refers to the decline of the Warring States period.
    • 黔黎嗟秦亡之晚 - The common people lamented the late fall of Qin; '黔黎' refers to the common people.
    • 恨汉来之迟 - Regretted the tardiness of the Han dynasty's arrival.
    • 肇基帝业 - Laid the foundation for imperial rule; indicates the establishment of the Han dynasty.
    • 恢张区宇 - Expanded the territory; refers to the expansion of the empire.
    • 务宽刑辟 - Focused on lenient laws; describes the policy of leniency in governance.
    • 无为之治 - Rule of non-action; a reference to the Daoist philosophy of minimal intervention.
    • 德侔三王 - Moral virtue equal to the three kings; refers to the moral achievements of the early Chinese rulers.
    • 教通四海 - Teachings spread throughout the world; indicates the widespread influence of the Han dynasty.
    • 世载愈光 - The legacy became even more illustrious; describes the increasing glory of the Han dynasty.
    • 省方巡岳 - Inspecting the provinces and visiting the mountains; refers to imperial tours.
    • 标元崇号 - Establishing the supreme title; indicates the elevation of the emperor's status.
    • 闻礼乐以恢风 - Promoting rites and music to revitalize the customs; emphasizes the role of culture in governance.
    • 广文义以饰俗 - Expanding the meaning of literature to adorn the customs; highlights the importance of literature in shaping society.
    • 改律历而建封禅 - Reforming the calendar and laws and establishing the Fengshan sacrifices; refers to significant religious and political rituals.
    • 祀百神以招群瑞 - Worshipping all the gods to attract various blessings; indicates the practice of religious ceremonies to bring good fortune.
    • 钦明茂于《唐书》 - The virtue of 'reverence and clarity' is prominent in the *Book of Tang*; refers to the moral qualities of the Tang dynasty.
    • 文思称于《虞典》 - The virtue of 'literary wisdom' is praised in the *Book of Yu*; refers to the moral qualities of the Yu dynasty.
    • 周、孔之教 - The teachings of Zhou and Confucius; refers to the moral and ethical teachings of these figures.
    • 不贵虚无之学 - Do not value the study of the void; indicates a rejection of Daoist philosophy.
    • 修黄老 - Study the teachings of Huang-Lao; refers to the combination of Daoist and Legalist philosophies.
    • 治却老之方 - Seek methods to avoid aging; indicates the pursuit of longevity practices.
    • 求报无福之祀 - Seek blessings from rituals that lack merit; suggests the futility of certain religious practices.
    • 张敞切言 - Zhang Chang spoke frankly; refers to a historical figure known for his direct advice.
    • 使远斥仙术 - To distance oneself from the art of immortality; indicates a rejection of Daoist practices.
    • 指以苌弘、楚襄怀、秦皇、徐福之事 - Pointing to the examples of Chang Hong, Chu Xianghui, Qin Shi Huang, and Xu Fu; refers to historical figures associated with Daoist practices.
    • 辛垣之徒 - The followers of Xin Yuan; refers to those who supported Daoist practices.
    • 卒见夷戮 - Ultimately, they were executed; indicates the punishment of those who supported Daoist practices.
    • 仙者 - Immortals; refers to those who seek immortality.
    • 尚冲静以忘形体 - Value tranquility and forget the body; indicates the Daoist ideal of detachment from the physical world.
    • 守寂寞而袪嚣务 - Maintain solitude and eliminate worldly affairs; emphasizes the importance of inner peace and detachment from material concerns.
    • 好微行而尚克伐 - Favored secret travels and valued conquests; indicates the emperor's preference for covert activities and military achievements.
    • 恢宫宇而广苑囿 - Expanded the palace and gardens; refers to the construction and expansion of imperial buildings.
    • 永乖长生久视之法 - Forever deviated from the method of long life and eternal vision; indicates the failure to achieve immortality.
    • 失玄一守道之要 - Lost the essence of adhering to the Dao; suggests a departure from the fundamental principles of Daoism.
    • 悔少翁之先诛 - Regretted the early execution of Shao Weng; refers to a historical figure who was executed for his Daoist practices.
    • 惑栾大之诡说 - Deceived by the deceptive words of Luan Da; indicates the emperor's susceptibility to false promises of immortality.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 汉兴 - The rise of the Han dynasty; marks the beginning of a new era in Chinese history.
    • 六国之遗弊 - The legacy of the Warring States period; characterized by political fragmentation and social unrest.
    • 黔黎 - The common people; often used to describe the general population.
    • 高祖 - Emperor Gaozu of Han; the founder of the Han dynasty.
    • 孝惠 - Emperor Hui of Han; the second emperor of the Han dynasty, known for his lenient policies.
    • 武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han; the seventh emperor of the Han dynasty, known for his military campaigns and cultural achievements.
    • 周、孔之教 - The teachings of Confucius and other sages; emphasizing moral and ethical conduct.
    • 黄老 - The combined teachings of Huangdi (Yellow Emperor) and Laozi; representing Daoist philosophy.
    • 苌弘、楚襄怀、秦皇、徐福 - Historical figures associated with Daoist practices and the pursuit of immortality.
    • 辛垣 - A historical figure known for supporting Daoist practices.
    • 少翁 - A historical figure who was executed for his Daoist practices.
    • 栾大 - A historical figure who deceived Emperor Wu of Han with false promises of immortality.

    Proper Nouns

    • 汉 - The Han dynasty.
    • 高祖 - Emperor Gaozu of Han.

    The Han dynasty rose, inheriting the legacy of the six states' misfortunes, and the emperor aspired to virtue. Thus, the common people lamented the late fall of Qin and regretted the tardiness of the Han dynasty's arrival. Emperor Gaozu laid the foundation for imperial rule and expanded the territory. Emperor Hui focused on lenient laws, achieving a rule of non-action, with moral virtue equal to the three kings and teachings spreading throughout the world. By the time of Emperor Wu, the dynasty's legacy became even more illustrious. He inspected the provinces and visited the mountains, establishing the supreme title. He promoted rites and music to revitalize the customs and expanded the meaning of literature to adorn the customs. He reformed the calendar and laws and established the Fengshan sacrifices, worshipping all the gods to attract various blessings. Although the virtue of "reverence and clarity" is prominent in the Book of Tang and the virtue of "literary wisdom" is praised in the Book of Yu, how can one surpass this? Observing the teachings of Confucius and other sages, they do not value the study of the void. Emperor Wu studied the teachings of Huang-Lao, sought methods to avoid aging, and sought blessings from rituals that lacked merit. Thus, Zhang Chang spoke frankly, urging him to distance himself from the art of immortality, pointing to the examples of Chang Hong, Chu Xianghui, Qin Shi Huang, and Xu Fu. Therefore, the followers of Xin Yuan were ultimately executed. Immortals value tranquility and forget the body, maintaining solitude and eliminating worldly affairs. Emperor Wu favored secret travels and valued conquests, expanding the palace and gardens. He forever deviated from the method of long life and eternal vision, losing the essence of adhering to the Dao. He regretted the early execution of Shao Weng and was deceived by the deceptive words of Luan Da. [83]

    至如李夫人，缅心昵爱，专媚兰闺，思沉魂之更生，饬新宫以延伫。盖犹嬖惑之宠过炽，累心之结未袪。欲竦身云霓之表，与天地而齐毕，由系风晷，其可阶乎？虽未及玄真，颇参神邃。是以幽明不能藏其殊妙，万象无所隐其精灵。考诸仙部，验以众说，未有异于斯乎！夫五运递兴，数之常理，金、土之兆，魏、晋当焉。董偃起自贩珠之徒，因庖宰而升宠，窃幸一时，富倾海宇，内蓄神异之珍，衒非世之宝；一朝绝爱，信盛衰之有兆乎！夫为棺椁者，以防蝼蚁之患，权敛骨之离，圣人使合其正礼，恶其逾费，疾其过薄。至如澹台灭明之俭，盛姬、秦皇之奢，皆失于节用。嗟乎！形销神灭，欻为一棺之土，为陵成谷，琼珣美宝，奄为烬尘，斯则费生加死，无益身名也。冥然长往，何忆曩时之盛？仲尼云：“不如速朽。”敛手足形，圣人以斯昭诫，岂不尚哉！ [84]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [84]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 至如李夫人 - Phrase introducing a specific example, meaning 'as for Lady Li.'
    • 缅心昵爱 - Compound phrase meaning 'cherish with deep affection.'
    • 专媚兰闺 - Literally 'devote oneself to flattering the inner chamber,' referring to being devoted to a woman.
    • 思沉魂之更生 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'to think of the soul's rebirth.'
    • 饬新宫以延伫 - Means 'to prepare a new palace to await.'
    • 盖犹嬖惑之宠过炽 - Means 'it is as if the excessive favor of a beloved has become too intense.'
    • 累心之结未袪 - Means 'the knots of the heart have not been untied.'
    • 欲竦身云霓之表 - Means 'to want to ascend above the clouds and rainbows.'
    • 与天地而齐毕 - Means 'to be equal to heaven and earth.'
    • 由系风晷，其可阶乎 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'it is like trying to catch the wind or measure time with a sundial; how can it be achieved?'
    • 虽未及玄真 - Means 'although not reaching the level of supreme truth.'
    • 颇参神邃 - Means 'to some extent, it participates in the profound mysteries.'
    • 幽明不能藏其殊妙 - Means 'the profound and subtle cannot be hidden.'
    • 万象无所隐其精灵 - Means 'all things cannot conceal their spirits.'
    • 考诸仙部 - Means 'to examine among the ranks of immortals.'
    • 验以众说 - Means 'to verify with various sayings.'
    • 未有异于斯乎 - Means 'there is nothing different from this.'
    • 夫五运递兴 - Means 'the five elements take turns in rise.'
    • 数之常理 - Means 'the constant principle of numbers.'
    • 金、土之兆 - Refers to the signs of the Metal and Earth elements.
    • 魏、晋当焉 - Means 'Wei and Jin dynasties should be here.'
    • 董偃起自贩珠之徒 - Means 'Dong Yan rose from a pearl-selling background.'
    • 因庖宰而升宠 - Means 'he gained favor through the kitchen and dining hall.'
    • 窃幸一时 - Means 'to secretly enjoy a momentary favor.'
    • 富倾海宇 - Means 'wealth that could overturn the world.'
    • 内蓄神异之珍 - Means 'to possess within rare and mystical treasures.'
    • 衒非世之宝 - Means 'to display treasures that are not of this world.'
    • 一朝绝爱 - Means 'one day, the favor was cut off.'
    • 信盛衰之有兆乎 - Means 'is it a sign of the rise and fall?'
    • 夫为棺椁者 - Means 'the purpose of making coffins.'
    • 以防蝼蚁之患 - Means 'to prevent the harm of ants and termites.'
    • 权敛骨之离 - Means 'to temporarily gather the bones.'
    • 圣人使合其正礼 - Means 'the sage commands the proper rites.'
    • 恶其逾费 - Means 'dislikes excessive spending.'
    • 疾其过薄 - Means 'dislikes being too thin.'
    • 至如澹台灭明之俭 - Means 'as for Dan Tai Mie Ming's frugality.'
    • 盛姬、秦皇之奢 - Refers to the extravagance of King Sheng Ji and Emperor Qin Shi Huang.
    • 皆失于节用 - Means 'all miss the point of moderation.'
    • 嗟乎 - Interjection expressing sorrow or regret.
    • 形销神灭 - Means 'the body decays and the spirit perishes.'
    • 欻为一棺之土 - Means 'suddenly becomes the soil of a coffin.'
    • 为陵成谷 - Means 'becomes a hill and then a valley.'
    • 琼珣美宝 - Refers to precious jade and beautiful treasures.
    • 奄为烬尘 - Means 'suddenly becomes ashes and dust.'
    • 斯则费生加死 - Means 'this is to waste life and add to death.'
    • 无益身名也 - Means 'it is of no benefit to one's reputation.'
    • 冥然长往 - Means 'to go away forever in darkness.'
    • 何忆曩时之盛 - Means 'how can one remember the past glory?'
    • 仲尼云 - Means 'Confucius said.'
    • 不如速朽 - Means 'it is better to decay quickly.'
    • 敛手足形 - Means 'to gather the hands and feet.'
    • 圣人以斯昭诫 - Means 'the sage uses this as a warning.'
    • 岂不尚哉 - Means 'isn't it commendable?'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 李夫人 - Refers to Lady Li, a concubine of Emperor Wu of Han, known for her beauty and the emperor's deep affection for her.
    • 董偃 - A historical figure who rose from a lowly background to gain favor at the court.
    • 澹台灭明 - A historical figure known for his frugality.
    • 盛姬 - Refers to King Sheng Ji, a historical figure known for his extravagance.
    • 秦皇 - Refers to Emperor Qin Shi Huang, the first emperor of China, known for his extravagance.
    • 五运 - Refers to the Five Elements (Wu Xing) in Chinese cosmology, which are Metal, Wood, Water, Fire, and Earth.
    • 魏、晋 - Refers to the Wei and Jin dynasties, which followed the Han dynasty.
    • 仲尼 - Refers to Confucius, the great philosopher and teacher of ancient China.

    Proper Nouns

    • 李夫人 - Lady Li, a concubine of Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 董偃 - A historical figure who rose from a lowly background to gain favor at the court.
    • 澹台灭明 - A historical figure known for his frugality.
    • 盛姬 - King Sheng Ji, a historical figure known for his extravagance.
    • 秦皇 - Emperor Qin Shi Huang, the first emperor of China.
    • 仲尼 - Confucius

    As for Lady Li, she cherished with deep affection, devoted herself to flattering the inner chamber, thinking of the soul's rebirth, and prepared a new palace to await. It is as if the excessive favor of a beloved has become too intense, and the knots of the heart have not been untied. She wanted to ascend above the clouds and rainbows, to be equal to heaven and earth, but it is like trying to catch the wind or measure time with a sundial; how can it be achieved? Although not reaching the level of supreme truth, she to some extent participated in the profound mysteries. Therefore, the profound and subtle cannot be hidden, and all things cannot conceal their spirits. Examining among the ranks of immortals and verifying with various sayings, there is nothing different from this! The five elements take turns in rise, the constant principle of numbers, the signs of the Metal and Earth elements, Wei and Jin dynasties should be here. Dong Yan rose from a pearl-selling background, he gained favor through the kitchen and dining hall, secretly enjoying a momentary favor, wealth that could overturn the world, possessing within rare and mystical treasures, displaying treasures that are not of this world; one day, the favor was cut off, is it a sign of the rise and fall? The purpose of making coffins is to prevent the harm of ants and termites, to temporarily gather the bones. The sage commands the proper rites, dislikes excessive spending, and dislikes being too thin. As for Dan Tai Mie Ming's frugality, the extravagance of King Sheng Ji and Emperor Qin Shi Huang, all miss the point of moderation. Alas! The body decays and the spirit perishes, suddenly becomes the soil of a coffin, becomes a hill and then a valley, precious jade and beautiful treasures, suddenly become ashes and dust. This is to waste life and add to death, it is of no benefit to one's reputation. To go away forever in darkness, how can one remember the past glory? Confucius said, "it is better to decay quickly." To gather the hands and feet, the sage uses this as a warning, isn't it commendable? [84]

    拾遗记卷六

    前汉下

    　　昭帝始元元年，穿淋池，广千步。中植分枝荷，一茎四叶，状如骈盖，日照则叶低荫根茎，若葵之卫足，名“低光荷”。实如玄珠，可以饰佩。花叶难萎，芬馥之气，彻十余里。食之令人口气常香，益脉理病。宫人贵之，每游宴出入，必皆含嚼。或剪以为衣，或折以蔽日，以为戏弄。《楚辞》所谓“折芰荷以为衣”，意在斯也。亦有倒生菱，茎如乱丝，一花千叶，根浮水上，实沉泥中，名“紫菱”，食之不老。帝时命水嬉，游宴永日。土人进一巨槽，帝曰：“桂楫松舟，其犹重朴；况乎此槽，可得而乘也？”乃命以文梓为船，木兰为柂。刻飞鸾翔鹢，饰于船首，随风轻漾，毕景忘归，乃至通夜。使宫人歌曰：“秋素景兮泛洪波，挥纤手兮折芰荷，凉风凄凄扬棹歌，云光开曙月低河，万岁为乐岂云多！”帝乃大悦。起商台于池上。及乎末岁，进谏者多，遂省薄游幸，堙毁池台，鸾舟荷芰，随时废灭。今台无遗址，沟池已平。 [85]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [85]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 始元元年 - The first year of the Shiyuan era during Emperor Zhao's reign.
    • 穿淋池 - Constructing or digging a pool.
    • 廣千步 - Extending a thousand steps in width.
    • 中植分枝荷 - Planting lotus flowers with multiple branches in the center.
    • 一茎四叶 - One stem with four leaves.
    • 状如骈盖 - Appearing like a double canopy.
    • 日照则叶低荫根茎 - When the sun shines, the leaves bend to shade the root and stem.
    • 若葵之卫足 - Like the sunflower protecting its base.
    • 名“低光荷” - Named 'Low Light Lotus.'
    • 实如玄珠 - The fruit is like black pearls.
    • 可以饰佩 - Can be used for decoration or as a pendant.
    • 花叶难萎 - Flowers and leaves are difficult to wither.
    • 芬馥之气 - Fragrant aroma.
    • 彻十余里 - Can be smelled for more than ten miles.
    • 食之令人口气常香 - Eating it makes one's breath always fragrant.
    • 益脉理病 - Benefits the body and treats illnesses.
    • 宫人贵之 - The palace ladies highly value it.
    • 每游宴出入，必皆含嚼 - Whenever they go out for banquets or outings, they always chew on it.
    • 或剪以为衣 - Some cut it to make clothes.
    • 或折以蔽日 - Others break it to shield themselves from the sun.
    • 以为戏弄 - For amusement.
    • 《楚辞》所谓“折芰荷以为衣” - As mentioned in the *Chuci* (Songs of Chu), 'cutting lotus leaves to make clothes.'
    • 意在斯也 - It means this.
    • 亦有倒生菱 - There are also upside-down-growing water lilies.
    • 茎如乱丝 - Stems like tangled threads.
    • 一花千叶 - One flower with a thousand petals.
    • 根浮水上，实沉泥中 - Roots floating on the water, fruits sinking in the mud.
    • 名“紫菱” - Named 'Purple Water Lily.'
    • 食之不老 - Eating it prevents aging.
    • 帝时命水嬉 - The emperor ordered water-based entertainment.
    • 游宴永日 - Banquet and entertain for the whole day.
    • 土人进一巨槽 - A local presented a large trough.
