    • 桂楫松舟，其犹重朴 - A boat with a paddle made of cassia and a hull made of pine, which is still heavy and crude.
    • 乃命以文梓为船，木兰为柂 - Then ordered to use fine sandalwood for the boat and magnolia for the rudder.
    • 刻飞鸾翔鹢 - Carved flying phoenixes and swimming cranes.
    • 饰于船首 - Decorated at the bow of the ship.
    • 随风轻漾 - Drifting gently with the wind.
    • 毕景忘归 - Forgetting to return until the end of the day.
    • 乃至通夜 - Even till the night.
    • 使宫人歌曰 - Ordered the palace ladies to sing:
    • 秋素景兮泛洪波 - In the clear autumn light, drifting on the vast waves.
    • 挥纤手兮折芰荷 - With delicate hands, cutting lotus leaves.
    • 凉风凄凄扬棹歌 - Cool winds blow, raising the oar songs.
    • 云光开曙月低河 - Clouds clear, dawn moon low over the river.
    • 万岁为乐岂云多 - A thousand years of joy, how could it be too much?
    • 帝乃大悦 - The emperor was greatly pleased.
    • 起商台于池上 - Built a commercial pavilion on the pool.
    • 及乎末岁，进谏者多 - By the end of the reign, many advised against it.
    • 遂省薄游幸 - Then reduced the frequency of outings.
    • 堙毁池台 - Dismantled the pools and pavilions.
    • 鸾舟荷芰，随时废灭 - The phoenix boats and lotus leaves were abandoned over time.
    • 今台无遗址，沟池已平 - Now the pavilion has no trace, and the pools and ditches are flat.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 昭帝 - Emperor Zhao, the ruler of the Western Han dynasty.
    • 楚辞 - The *Chuci* (Songs of Chu), a collection of ancient Chinese poetry.
    • 商台 - A commercial pavilion, possibly a place for trade or entertainment.
    • 末岁 - The end of the reign.
    • 进谏者多 - Many advisors who spoke out against the emperor's actions.
    • 省薄游幸 - Reduced the frequency of outings, indicating a change in policy.
    • 堙毁池台 - Dismantling the pools and pavilions, reflecting a shift in priorities.
    • 鸾舟荷芰 - Phoenix boats and lotus leaves, symbols of luxury and beauty.
    • 随时废灭 - Abandoned over time, indicating a decline in usage.

    Proper Nouns

    • 昭帝 - Emperor Zhao.
    • 楚辞 - The *Chuci* (Songs of Chu).
    • 商台 - A commercial pavilion.
    • 鸾舟 - Phoenix boats.
    • 荷芰 - Lotus leaves.

    Translation:

    The Shiyi Ji (拾遗记) Volume Six

    Western Han Dynasty, Part Two

    In the first year of the Shiyuan era during Emperor Zhao's reign, a pool was dug, extending a thousand steps in width. In the center, lotus flowers with multiple branches were planted, each stem bearing four leaves, resembling a double canopy. When the sun shone, the leaves bent to shade the root and stem, like the sunflower protecting its base, and were named "Low Light Lotus." The fruits were like black pearls and could be used for decoration or as pendants. The flowers and leaves were difficult to wither, and their fragrant aroma could be smelled for more than ten miles. Eating them made one's breath always fragrant and benefited the body, treating illnesses. The palace ladies highly valued them, and whenever they went out for banquets or outings, they always chewed on them. Some cut them to make clothes, others broke them to shield themselves from the sun, for amusement. As mentioned in the Chuci (Songs of Chu), "cutting lotus leaves to make clothes," it means this. There were also upside-down-growing water lilies, with stems like tangled threads, one flower with a thousand petals, roots floating on the water, and fruits sinking in the mud, named "Purple Water Lily." Eating it prevented aging. The emperor ordered water-based entertainment, and they banqueted and entertained for the whole day. A local presented a large trough, and the emperor said, "A boat with a paddle made of cassia and a hull made of pine is still heavy and crude; how much more so, then, for this trough, can it be used?" He then ordered to use fine sandalwood for the boat and magnolia for the rudder. Flying phoenixes and swimming cranes were carved and decorated at the bow of the ship. Drifting gently with the wind, they forgot to return until the end of the day, even till the night. The emperor ordered the palace ladies to sing:

    "In the clear autumn light, drifting on the vast waves,
    With delicate hands, cutting lotus leaves.
    Cool winds blow, raising the oar songs.
    Clouds clear, dawn moon low over the river.
    A thousand years of joy, how could it be too much!"

    The emperor was greatly pleased and built a commercial pavilion on the pool. By the end of the reign, many advised against it, and he then reduced the frequency of outings, dismantling the pools and pavilions. The phoenix boats and lotus leaves were abandoned over time. Now the pavilion has no trace, and the pools and ditches are flat. [85]

    宣帝地节元年，乐浪之东，有背明之国，来贡其方物。言其乡在扶桑之东，见日出于西方。其国昏昏常暗，宜种百谷，名曰“融泽”，方三千里。五谷皆良，食之后天而死。有浃日之稻，种之十旬而熟；有翻形稻，言食者死而更生，夭而有寿；有明清稻，食者延年也；清肠稻，食一粒历年不饥。有摇枝粟，其枝长而弱，无风常摇，食之益髓；有凤冠粟，似凤鸟之冠，食者多力；有游龙粟，叶屈曲似游龙也；有琼膏粟，白如银，食此二粟，令人骨轻。有绕明豆，其茎弱，自相萦缠；有挟剑豆，其荚形似人挟剑，横斜而生；有倾离豆，言其豆见日，叶垂覆地，食者不老不疾。有延精麦，延寿益气；有昆和麦，调畅六府；有轻心麦，食者体轻；有醇和麦，为曲以酿酒，一醉累月，食之凌冬可袒；有含露麦，穟中有露，味甘如饴。有紫沉麻，其实不浮；有云冰麻，实冷而有光，宜为油泽；有通明麻，食者夜行不持烛，是苣蕂也，食之延寿，后天而老。 [86]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [86]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 宣帝地节元年 - The first year of the Diji era during the reign of Emperor Xuan of Han, providing the historical context.
    • 背明之国 - A country that is 'away from light,' referring to a land where the sun rises in the west, creating perpetual darkness.
    • 宜种百谷 - Suitable for cultivating all grains, indicating the fertility of the land.
    • 名曰“融泽” - The name given to the land, meaning 'Melted Dew,' possibly referring to its fertile and moist nature.
    • 五谷皆良 - All grains are good, emphasizing the quality of the produce.
    • 食之后天而死 - Eating the grains leads to death after a short time, indicating a unique and perhaps dangerous property of the grains.
    • 有浃日之稻 - Rice that takes ten days to mature, emphasizing its rapid growth.
    • 有翻形稻 - Rice that causes the eater to die and then be reborn, suggesting a supernatural or transformative effect.
    • 有明清稻 - Rice that grants longevity, indicating its beneficial properties.
    • 清肠稻 - Rice that provides long-lasting nourishment, emphasizing its sustenance.
    • 摇枝粟 - Millet with branches that sway constantly, suggesting a unique physical characteristic.
    • 凤冠粟 - Millet that resembles a phoenix's crown, indicating its beauty and strength.
    • 游龙粟 - Millet whose leaves twist like a dragon, emphasizing its dynamic form.
    • 琼膏粟 - Millet that is white and cold, suggesting a cooling effect.
    • 绕明豆 - Beans with weak stems that intertwine, indicating their unique growth pattern.
    • 挟剑豆 - Beans whose pods resemble a person carrying a sword, emphasizing their distinctive shape.
    • 倾离豆 - Beans that lean over when exposed to sunlight, suggesting a unique response to light.
    • 延精麦 - Wheat that extends life and vitality, indicating its beneficial effects.
    • 昆和麦 - Wheat that harmonizes the six organs, emphasizing its medicinal properties.
    • 轻心麦 - Wheat that makes the body light, suggesting a physical effect.
    • 醇和麦 - Wheat that, when made into wine, causes prolonged intoxication, indicating its potent nature.
    • 含露麦 - Wheat with grains that contain dew, suggesting a unique and possibly refreshing quality.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宣帝地节元年 - The first year of the Diji era during the reign of Emperor Xuan of Han, providing the historical context.
    • 背明之国 - A reference to a mythical or distant land, possibly inspired by ancient Chinese cosmology and mythology.
    • 融泽 - A name that suggests a fertile and moist land, possibly drawing on classical Chinese descriptions of idealized lands.
    • 五谷 - The five grains (rice, wheat, millet, soybeans, and barley), which are fundamental to Chinese agriculture and culture.
    • 凤冠 - Phoenix crown, a symbol of nobility and beauty in Chinese culture.
    • 游龙 - Dragon, a powerful and revered creature in Chinese mythology.
    • 琼膏 - White and cold, possibly referring to a cooling or soothing effect, often used in classical Chinese to describe precious or rare substances.
    • 含露 - Dew, a symbol of freshness and purity in Chinese culture.

    Proper Nouns

    • 宣帝 - Emperor Xuan of Han.
    • 地节元年 - The first year of the Diji era during the reign of Emperor Xuan of Han.
    • 乐浪 - A region in ancient Korea.
    • 扶桑 - A mythical land in Chinese mythology, often associated with the sun rising in the east.
    • 融泽 - The name given to the land described in the text.
    • 凤冠 - Phoenix crown, a symbol of nobility and beauty in Chinese culture.
    • 游龙 - Dragon, a powerful and revered creature in Chinese mythology.
    • 琼膏 - White and cold, possibly referring to a cooling or soothing effect.
    • 含露 - Dew, a symbol of freshness and purity in Chinese culture.

    In the first year of the Diji era during the reign of Emperor Xuan, to the east of Lelang, there was a land known as the "Land of the Backward Light," which came to pay tribute with its local products. It was said that this land was located to the east of Fusang, where the sun rises in the west. The country is perpetually dark, suitable for cultivating all grains, and is named "Rongze," spanning three thousand li. All the grains are of high quality, but eating them leads to death shortly after. There is a type of rice called "Jia Ri Rice" that matures in ten days; another type, "Fan Xing Rice," is said to cause the eater to die and be reborn, thus extending their lifespan; there is also "Ming Qing Rice," which grants longevity to those who eat it; and "Qing Chang Rice," which provides long-lasting nourishment with just one grain. There is "Yao Zhi Millet," whose branches are long and weak, swaying constantly even without wind, and is said to strengthen the marrow when eaten; "Feng Guan Millet," which resembles a phoenix's crown, and is said to increase strength when consumed; "You Long Millet," whose leaves twist like a dragon; and "Qiong Gao Millet," which is white and cold, and is said to make bones light when eaten. There are also "Rao Ming Beans," whose weak stems intertwine; "Jia Jian Beans," whose pods resemble a person carrying a sword, growing sideways; and "Qing Li Beans," which lean over when exposed to sunlight, and are said to prevent aging and illness. There is "Yan Jing Wheat," which extends life and vitality; "Kun He Wheat," which harmonizes the six organs; "Qing Xin Wheat," which makes the body light; "Chun He Wheat," which, when made into wine, causes prolonged intoxication, and is said to allow one to go without clothing even in winter; and "Han Lu Wheat," which has grains containing dew, with a sweet taste like honey. There is "Zi Chen Hemp," whose seeds do not float; "Yun Bing Hemp," whose seeds are cold and have a lustrous appearance, suitable for oil production; and "Tong Ming Hemp," which allows one to walk at night without a torch. This is "Ju Lei," and eating it extends life, making one live longer than others. [86]

    其北有草，名虹草，枝长一丈，叶如车轮，根大如毂，花似朝虹之色。昔齐桓公伐山戎，国人献其种，乃植于庭，云霸者之瑞也。有宵明草，夜视如列烛，昼则无光，自消灭也。有紫菊，谓之日精，一茎一蔓，延及数亩，味甘，食者至死不饥渴。有焦茅，高五丈，燃之成灰，以水灌之，复成茅也，谓之灵茅。有黄渠草，映日如火，其铿韧若金，食者焚身不热；有梦草，叶如蒲，茎如蓍，采之以占吉凶，万不遗一；又有闻遐草，服者耳聪，香如桂，茎如兰。其国献之，多不生实，叶多萎黄，诏并除焉。元凤二年，于淋池之南起桂台，以望远气。东引太液之水。有一连理树，上枝跨于渠水，下枝隔岸而南，生与上枝同一株。帝常以季秋之月，泛蘅兰云鹢之舟，穷晷系夜，钓于台下。以香金为钩，霜丝为纶，丹鲤为饵，钓得白蛟，长三丈，若大蛇，无鳞甲。帝曰：“非祥也。”命太官为鲊，肉紫骨青，味甚香美，班赐群臣。帝思其美，渔者不能复得，知为神异之物。 [87]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [87]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 其北有草 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'there is grass to the north.'
    • 名虹草 - Literally 'named Rainbow Grass,' used to describe the plant.
    • 枝长一丈 - Means 'branches are one zhang long,' where 'zhang' is an ancient Chinese unit of length.
    • 叶如车轮 - Means 'leaves are like carriage wheels,' used to describe the size and shape of the leaves.
    • 根大如毂 - Means 'roots are as large as a wheel hub,' used to describe the size of the roots.
    • 花似朝虹之色 - Means 'flowers are like the color of a morning rainbow,' used to describe the color of the flowers.
    • 昔齐桓公伐山戎 - Means 'formerly Duke Huan of Qi attacked the Shanrong,' referring to a historical event.
    • 国人献其种 - Means 'the people of the state presented its seeds,' indicating a significant event.
    • 乃植于庭 - Means 'then planted it in the courtyard,' describing the action taken.
    • 云霸者之瑞也 - Means 'it is said to be an omen of a hegemon,' indicating a symbolic meaning.
    • 有宵明草 - Means 'there is also a grass called Night Brightness,' used to describe another plant.
    • 夜视如列烛 - Means 'at night it looks like rows of candles,' used to describe the appearance of the plant at night.
    • 昼则无光，自消灭也 - Means 'during the day it has no light and disappears by itself,' describing the behavior of the plant during the day.
    • 有紫菊 - Means 'there is also a purple chrysanthemum,' used to describe another plant.
    • 谓之日精 - Means 'it is called Solar Essence,' indicating a symbolic name.
    • 一茎一蔓，延及数亩 - Means 'one stem and one vine, extending over several mu,' describing the growth pattern of the plant.
    • 味甘，食者至死不饥渴 - Means 'its taste is sweet, and those who eat it will never feel hungry or thirsty,' describing the effects of eating the plant.
    • 有焦茅 - Means 'there is also a kind of burnt reed,' used to describe another plant.
    • 高五丈 - Means 'it is five zhang high,' describing the height of the plant.
    • 燃之成灰，以水灌之，复成茅也 - Means 'when burned, it turns to ash, and when water is poured on it, it turns back into reed,' describing a unique property of the plant.
    • 谓之灵茅 - Means 'it is called Divine Reed,' indicating a symbolic name.
    • 有黄渠草 - Means 'there is also a yellow channel grass,' used to describe another plant.
    • 映日如火 - Means 'it reflects sunlight like fire,' describing the appearance of the plant in sunlight.
    • 铿韧若金 - Means 'it is hard and resilient like metal,' describing the texture of the plant.
    • 食者焚身不热 - Means 'those who eat it do not feel heat even if they are burned,' describing the effects of eating the plant.
    • 有梦草 - Means 'there is also a dream grass,' used to describe another plant.
    • 叶如蒲，茎如蓍 - Means 'its leaves are like rushes, and its stems are like yarrow,' describing the appearance of the plant.
    • 采之以占吉凶，万不遗一 - Means 'it is collected to predict good or bad fortune, and it never fails,' describing the use of the plant.
    • 又有闻遐草 - Means 'there is also a distant-scented grass,' used to describe another plant.
    • 服者耳聪，香如桂，茎如兰 - Means 'those who consume it have keen hearing, it smells like cassia, and its stem is like orchid,' describing the effects and appearance of the plant.
    • 其国献之，多不生实，叶多萎黄，诏并除焉 - Means 'it was presented to the country, but many did not bear fruit, and the leaves often withered and turned yellow, so the emperor ordered them to be removed,' describing a historical event.
    • 元凤二年，于淋池之南起桂台 - Means 'in the second year of Yuan Feng, a platform was built south of Linchi,' describing a historical event.
    • 以望远气 - Means 'to observe distant qi (vital energy),' describing the purpose of the platform.
    • 东引太液之水 - Means 'it drew water from Taiye to the east,' describing the water source for the platform.
    • 有一连理树 - Means 'there was a tree with intertwined branches,' describing a unique tree.
    • 上枝跨于渠水，下枝隔岸而南，生与上枝同一株 - Means 'the upper branches spanned the water channel, and the lower branches extended across the bank to the south, growing from the same trunk as the upper branches,' describing the structure of the tree.
    • 帝常以季秋之月，泛蘅兰云鹢之舟 - Means 'the emperor often sailed in a boat named Henglan Yunyi during the last month of autumn,' describing a historical event.
    • 穷晷系夜，钓于台下 - Means 'he fished at the platform all night long,' describing the activity of the emperor.
    • 以香金为钩，霜丝为纶，丹鲤为饵 - Means 'he used fragrant gold as a hook, frost threads as a line, and red carp as bait,' describing the fishing equipment.
    • 钓得白蛟，长三丈，若大蛇，无鳞甲 - Means 'he caught a white dragon, three zhang long, resembling a large snake without scales or armor,' describing the catch.
    • 帝曰：“非祥也。” - Means 'the emperor said, 'It is not an auspicious sign,' describing the emperor's reaction.
    • 命太官为鲊，肉紫骨青，味甚香美，班赐群臣 - Means 'he ordered the imperial kitchen to make it into dried fish, which was purple meat and green bones, very fragrant and delicious, and distributed to the officials,' describing the preparation and distribution of the catch.
    • 帝思其美，渔者不能复得，知为神异之物 - Means 'the emperor thought about its beauty, and the fisherman could not catch it again, knowing it to be a divine and extraordinary creature,' describing the significance of the catch.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 齐桓公 - Refers to Duke Huan of Qi, a famous ruler of the Spring and Autumn period.
    • 山戎 - Refers to the Shanrong, a group of nomadic tribes that lived in the northern regions of China.
    • 元凤二年 - Refers to the second year of the Yuan Feng era, a period in the Western Han dynasty.
    • 淋池 - Refers to Linchi, a pool or lake in the imperial gardens.
    • 桂台 - Refers to a platform built for observing distant qi (vital energy).
    • 太液 - Refers to Taiye, a large artificial lake in the imperial gardens.
    • 连理树 - Refers to a tree with intertwined branches, symbolizing harmony and unity.
    • 季秋之月 - Refers to the last month of autumn, which is September in the lunar calendar.
    • 蘅兰云鹢之舟 - Refers to a boat named Henglan Yunyi, likely a luxurious and ornate vessel.
    • 太官 - Refers to the imperial kitchen, responsible for preparing food for the emperor and his court.
    • 白蛟 - Refers to a white dragon, a mythical creature in Chinese folklore.

    Proper Nouns

    • 齐桓公 - Duke Huan of Qi, a famous ruler of the Spring and Autumn period.
    • 山戎 - A group of nomadic tribes that lived in the northern regions of China.
    • 元凤二年 - The second year of the Yuan Feng era, a period in the Western Han dynasty.
    • 淋池 - Linchi, a pool or lake in the imperial gardens.
    • 桂台 - A platform built for observing distant qi (vital energy).
    • 太液 - Taiye, a large artificial lake in the imperial gardens.
    • 连理树 - A tree with intertwined branches, symbolizing harmony and unity.
    • 季秋之月 - The last month of autumn, which is September in the lunar calendar.
    • 蘅兰云鹢之舟 - A boat named Henglan Yunyi, likely a luxurious and ornate vessel.
    • 太官 - The imperial kitchen, responsible for preparing food for the emperor and his court.
    • 白蛟 - A white dragon, a mythical creature in Chinese folklore.

    To the north, there is a grass known as Rainbow Grass, with branches that stretch one zhang in length, leaves resembling carriage wheels, roots as large as a wheel hub, and flowers the color of a morning rainbow. In ancient times, Duke Huan of Qi attacked the Shanrong, and the people of the state presented its seeds, which were then planted in the courtyard, said to be an omen of a hegemon. There is also a grass called Night Brightness, which at night appears like rows of candles, but during the day has no light and disappears by itself. There is a purple chrysanthemum known as Solar Essence, with one stem and one vine extending over several mu, its taste being sweet, and those who eat it will never feel hungry or thirsty. There is a kind of burnt reed, five zhang high, which when burned turns to ash, and when water is poured on it, it turns back into reed, called Divine Reed. There is also a yellow channel grass that reflects sunlight like fire, hard and resilient like metal, and those who eat it do not feel heat even if they are burned. There is a dream grass, with leaves like rushes and stems like yarrow, collected to predict good or bad fortune, and it never fails. There is also a distant-scented grass, whose consumers have keen hearing, it smells like cassia, and its stem is like orchid. It was presented to the country, but many did not bear fruit, and the leaves often withered and turned yellow, so the emperor ordered them to be removed. In the second year of Yuan Feng, a platform was built south of Linchi to observe distant qi. It drew water from Taiye to the east. There was a tree with intertwined branches, with upper branches spanning the water channel and lower branches extending across the bank to the south, growing from the same trunk as the upper branches. The emperor often sailed in a boat named Henglan Yunyi during the last month of autumn, fishing at the platform all night long. He used fragrant gold as a hook, frost threads as a line, and red carp as bait, catching a white dragon, three zhang long, resembling a large snake without scales or armor. The emperor said, "It is not an auspicious sign," and ordered the imperial kitchen to make it into dried fish, which was purple meat and green bones, very fragrant and delicious, and distributed to the officials. The emperor thought about its beauty, and the fisherman could not catch it again, knowing it to be a divine and extraordinary creature. [87]

    二年，含涂国贡其珍怪。其使云：“去王都七万里。鸟兽皆能言语。鸡犬死者，埋之不朽。经历数世，其家人游于山阿海滨，地中闻鸡犬鸣吠，主乃掘取，还家养之，毛羽虽秃落更生，久乃悦泽。” [88]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [88]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 珍怪 - Rare and unusual treasures or curiosities, often found in distant lands.
    • 埋之不朽 - Buried without decay, indicating a place with supernatural properties.
    • 鸡犬死者，埋之不朽 - A unique burial custom where dead animals are buried and do not decay.
    • 游于山阿海滨 - Wandering in the mountains and by the sea, evoking a natural and adventurous setting.
    • 地中闻鸡犬鸣吠 - Hearing the sounds of chickens and dogs from underground, suggesting a mystical place.
    • 主乃掘取，还家养之，毛羽虽秃落更生，久乃悦泽 - A process where dead animals are dug up, brought home, and regrow their feathers or fur over time, indicating magical properties.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 含涂国 - A fictional or mythological country, possibly referring to a distant land with exotic customs.
    • 珍怪 - Common in ancient texts to describe exotic or rare items from distant lands.
    • 鸟兽皆能言语 - Reflects a theme of magical or enchanted places found in folklore and mythology.
    • 埋之不朽 - Suggests a place with supernatural properties, where decay does not occur.
    • 鸡犬死者，埋之不朽 - Describes a custom that is both unusual and magical, indicating a place with unique cultural practices.
    • 游于山阿海滨 - Natural settings that are often used in literature to evoke a sense of wonder or adventure.
    • 地中闻鸡犬鸣吠 - A supernatural element, suggesting a land with mystical properties.
    • 主乃掘取，还家养之，毛羽虽秃落更生，久乃悦泽 - Describes a process that is both magical and practical, indicating a place with unique customs and beliefs.

    Proper Nouns

    • 含涂国 - A fictional or mythological country.
    • 珍怪 - Rare and unusual treasures.
    • 王都 - The capital city.

    In the second year, the country of Han Tu presented its rare and unusual treasures. The envoy said, "It is seven ten-thousand li from the capital. The birds and beasts can all speak. When chickens and dogs die, they are buried and do not decay. Over many generations, the people of the country wander in the mountains and by the sea, and hear the sounds of chickens and dogs from underground. The owners then dig them up, bring them home, and raise them. Although their feathers and fur may fall out, they grow back over time, becoming smooth and lustrous." [88]

    张掖郡有郅族之盛，因以名也。郅奇字君珍，居丧尽礼。所居去墓百里，每夜行，常有飞鸟衔火夹之，登山济水，号泣不息，未尝以险难为忧，虽夜如昼之明也。以泪洒石则成痕，着朽木枯草，必皆重茂。以泪浸地即咸，俗谓之“咸乡”。至昭帝，嘉其孝异，表铭其邑曰“孝感乡”，四时祭祀，立庙焉。
    　　录曰：夫心迹所至，无幽不彻，理着于微，冥昧自显。玄曦回鲁阳之戈，严霜感匹夫之叹，在于凡伦，尚昭神迹。况求之精爽，以会蒸蒸之心，木石为之玄感，鸟兽为之驯集。伟元哀号，春花以之改叶；叔通晨兴，朝流欻生横石；辛缮表迹于栖鸾，卫农示德于梦虎。郅氏之行，类斯道焉。按汉昭帝时，有黄鹄下太液池；今云淋池，盖一水二名也。宣帝之世，有嘉谷玄稷之祥，亦不说今之所生，岂由神农、后稷播厥之功，抑亦王子所称，非近俗所食。诠其名，华而不实。及乎飞走之类，神木怪草，见奇而说，万世之瑰伟也。 [89]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [89]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 張掖郡有郅族之盛 - Literally, 'The Zhi clan was flourishing in Zhangye Prefecture,' indicating the prominence of the Zhi family in the region.
    • 因以名也 - A phrase meaning 'and thus it was named,' explaining the origin of the place name.
    • 以泪洒石则成痕 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'when tears are shed on stone, they leave marks,' symbolizing the emotional impact of grief.
    • 着朽木枯草，必皆重茂 - A phrase meaning 'when shed on withered wood and dead grass, they must all flourish again,' symbolizing the transformative power of emotion.
    • 以泪浸地即咸 - A phrase meaning 'when tears soak the ground, it becomes salty,' explaining the origin of the term 'Salty Village.'
    • 表铭其邑曰“孝感乡” - A phrase meaning 'to inscribe and commemorate the town as 'Filial Piety Village,' indicating the official recognition of the Zhi family's filial piety.
    • 四时祭祀，立庙焉 - A phrase meaning 'to perform seasonal sacrifices and establish a temple there,' indicating the establishment of a shrine to honor the Zhi family.
    • 夫心迹所至，无幽不彻 - A phrase meaning 'The heart's path, wherever it goes, penetrates even the most hidden places,' emphasizing the power of sincere emotion.
    • 理着于微，冥昧自显 - A phrase meaning 'Truth lies in the subtle, and the obscure becomes clear,' suggesting that profound truths can be revealed through small, seemingly insignificant actions.
    • 玄曦回鲁阳之戈 - A phrase meaning 'The dark light returns the sword of Luyang,' referring to a legendary event where the sun reversed its course to help a general.
    • 严霜感匹夫之叹 - A phrase meaning 'The severe frost stirs the sigh of an ordinary man,' suggesting that even the simplest of people can evoke powerful emotions.
    • 伟元哀号 - A phrase meaning 'Wei Yuan's mournful cries,' referring to a person named Wei Yuan who was known for his deep sorrow.
    • 春花以之改叶 - A phrase meaning 'Spring flowers change their leaves because of it,' indicating the impact of emotional intensity on nature.
    • 叔通晨兴 - A phrase meaning 'Shu Tong rises early in the morning,' referring to a person named Shu Tong.
    • 朝流欻生横石 - A phrase meaning 'The morning stream suddenly forms a horizontal stone,' describing a natural phenomenon that occurs due to emotional influence.
    • 辛缮表迹于栖鸾 - A phrase meaning 'Xin Shian's traces are seen in the roosting phoenix,' referring to a person named Xin Shian and the symbolic meaning of the phoenix.
    • 卫农示德于梦虎 - A phrase meaning 'Wei Nong shows virtue in the dream tiger,' referring to a person named Wei Nong and the symbolic meaning of the tiger in dreams.
    • 类斯道焉 - A phrase meaning 'Similar to this way,' indicating that the Zhi family's actions align with the aforementioned examples.
    • 按汉昭帝时 - A phrase meaning 'According to the reign of Emperor Zhao of Han,' indicating the historical context.
    • 黄鹄下太液池 - A phrase meaning 'A yellow crane descends into Taiye Pool,' describing a miraculous event.
    • 嘉谷玄稷之祥 - A phrase meaning 'The auspicious sign of good grain and black millet,' indicating a favorable omen.
    • 神农、后稷播厥之功 - A phrase meaning 'The achievements of Shen Nong and Hou Ji in planting,' referring to ancient agricultural deities.
    • 王子所称 - A phrase meaning 'What the prince calls,' indicating a reference to a specific authority or tradition.
    • 非近俗所食 - A phrase meaning 'Not what the common people eat,' suggesting that the grain is rare or special.
    • 诠其名，华而不实 - A phrase meaning 'To interpret its name, it is ornamental but not substantial,' criticizing the superficiality of the name.
    • 飞走之类 - A phrase meaning 'Birds and animals,' referring to various creatures.
    • 神木怪草 - A phrase meaning 'Divine trees and strange grasses,' describing unusual plants.
    • 见奇而说 - A phrase meaning 'To see the strange and speak of it,' indicating the reporting of unusual phenomena.
    • 万世之瑰伟 - A phrase meaning 'The extraordinary of the ages,' emphasizing the significance of these events.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 張掖郡 - Zhangye Prefecture, a historical region in modern-day Gansu Province, China.
    • 郅奇 - A historical figure from the Zhi clan, known for his filial piety.
    • 昭帝 - Emperor Zhao of Han, the ruler during the period described.
    • 孝感乡 - A village named after the Zhi family's filial piety.
    • 魯陽之戈 - A legendary event involving the sun reversing its course to help a general.
    • 嚴霜 - Severe frost, often used metaphorically to represent hardship or adversity.
    • 偉元 - A historical figure known for his deep sorrow.
    • 春花 - Spring flowers, often used to symbolize renewal and beauty.
    • 叔通 - A historical figure known for rising early.
    • 辛缮 - A historical figure known for his traces being seen in the roosting phoenix.
    • 衛農 - A historical figure known for showing virtue in dreams.
    • 太液池 - Taiye Pool, a famous garden pond in the imperial palace.
    • 嘉谷 - Good grain, often used to symbolize prosperity.
    • 玄稷 - Black millet, a type of grain.
    • 神農 - Shen Nong, the legendary agricultural deity.
    • 後稷 - Hou Ji, another legendary agricultural deity.
    • 王子 - A prince, often used to refer to a specific authority or tradition.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • This sentence introduces the prominence of the Zhi clan and the origin of the place name.
    • This sentence uses parallel structure to describe the transformative power of tears.
    • This sentence explains the origin of the term 'Salty Village.'
    • This sentence describes the official recognition and commemoration of the Zhi family's filial piety.
    • This sentence emphasizes the power of sincere emotion and the revelation of profound truths.
    • This sentence uses two examples to illustrate the impact of sincere emotion.
    • This sentence describes the impact of emotional intensity on nature.
    • This sentence describes a natural phenomenon that occurs due to emotional influence.
    • This sentence uses two examples to illustrate the symbolic meaning of the phoenix and the tiger.
    • This sentence indicates that the Zhi family's actions align with the aforementioned examples.
    • This sentence describes a miraculous event during the reign of Emperor Zhao of Han.
    • This sentence explains the dual naming of the pool.
    • This sentence describes a favorable omen during the reign of Emperor Xuan of Han.
    • This sentence questions the origin of the grain and its significance.
    • This sentence criticizes the superficiality of the name.
    • This sentence describes the reporting of unusual phenomena and their significance.

    Proper Nouns

    • 張掖郡 - Zhangye Prefecture.
    • 郅奇 - A historical figure from the Zhi clan.
    • 昭帝 - Emperor Zhao of Han.
    • 孝感乡 - A village named after the Zhi family's filial piety.
    • 魯陽 - A place name associated with the legendary event.
    • 偉元 - A historical figure known for his deep sorrow.
    • 叔通 - A historical figure known for rising early.
    • 辛缮 - A historical figure known for his traces being seen in the roosting phoenix.
    • 衛農 - A historical figure known for showing virtue in dreams.
    • 太液池 - Taiye Pool, a famous garden pond in the imperial palace.

    Zhangye Prefecture was home to the flourishing Zhi clan, and thus it was named. Zhi Qi, whose courtesy name was Junzhen, observed all the rituals during his mourning period. His residence was one hundred li from the tomb, and every night when he traveled, he was often accompanied by birds carrying fire, helping him climb mountains and cross rivers, wailing incessantly. He never worried about the dangers, as the night was as bright as day. When he shed tears on stones, they left marks; when he shed tears on withered wood and dead grass, they must all flourish again. When his tears soaked the ground, it became salty, and the locals called it "Salty Village." During the reign of Emperor Zhao, he was praised for his filial piety, and the town was inscribed and commemorated as "Filial Piety Village," with seasonal sacrifices performed and a temple established there.

    The record states: The heart's path, wherever it goes, penetrates even the most hidden places; truth lies in the subtle, and the obscure becomes clear. The dark light returns the sword of Luyang, and the severe frost stirs the sigh of an ordinary man. Even in the realm of common people, divine traces are still evident. How much more so when one seeks the essence of the spirit, to unite with the fervent heart, making even wood and stone respond mysteriously, and birds and beasts gather in harmony. Wei Yuan's mournful cries caused spring flowers to change their leaves; Shu Tong's early morning rise caused the morning stream to suddenly form a horizontal stone; Xin Shian's traces are seen in the roosting phoenix, and Wei Nong shows virtue in the dream tiger. The actions of the Zhi family are similar to this way. According to the reign of Emperor Zhao of Han, a yellow crane descended into Taiye Pool; today it is called Lin Pool, as it is one body of water with two names. During the reign of Emperor Xuan, there was the auspicious sign of good grain and black millet. It is not clear if this is due to the achievements of Shen Nong and Hou Ji in planting, or if it is what the prince calls, not what the common people eat. To interpret its name, it is ornamental but not substantial. As for birds and animals, divine trees and strange grasses, to see the strange and speak of it, these are the extraordinary of the ages. [89]

    汉成帝好微行，于太液池旁起宵游宫，以漆为柱，铺黑绨之幕，器服乘舆，皆尚黑色。既悦于暗行，憎灯烛之照。宫中美御，皆服皂衣，自班婕妤以下，咸带玄绶，簪佩虽如锦绣，更以木兰纱绡罩之。至宵游宫，乃秉烛。宴幸既罢，静鼓自舞，而步不扬尘。好夕出游。造飞行殿，方一丈，如今之辇，选羽林之士，负之以趋。帝于辇上，觉其行快疾，闻其中若风雷之声，言其行疾也，名曰“云雷宫”。所幸之宫，咸以毡绨藉地，恶车辙马迹之喧。虽惑于微行昵宴，在民无劳无怨。每乘舆返驾，以爱幸之姬宝衣珍食，舍于道傍，国人之穷老者皆歌“万岁”。是以鸿嘉、永始之间，国富家丰，兵戈长戢。故刘向、谷永指言切谏，于是焚宵游宫及飞行殿，罢宴逸之乐。所谓从绳则正，如转圜焉。 [90]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [90]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 好微行 - Preference for incognito or covert travel, often associated with the emperor's desire to observe the common people without being noticed.
    • 宵游宫 - A palace built for nighttime outings, emphasizing the theme of nocturnal activities.
    • 漆为柱，铺黑绨之幕 - Describes the use of lacquered columns and black silk curtains, creating a dark and luxurious atmosphere.
    • 器服乘舆，皆尚黑色 - Indicates that all furnishings, clothing, and vehicles were predominantly black, reinforcing the theme of darkness.
    • 静鼓自舞，而步不扬尘 - Describes a performance where the dancers' steps were so light that they did not raise dust, emphasizing the elegance and control of the dancers.
    • 好夕出游 - Another way of saying '好微行,' indicating a preference for evening outings.
    • 飞行殿 - A flying pavilion, a mythical or magical structure that could move through the air.
    • 恶车辙马迹之喧 - Expresses a dislike for the noise and disturbance caused by wheels and hooves.
    • 惑于微行昵宴，在民无劳无怨 - Suggests that while the emperor was indulging in covert pleasures, the people did not suffer or complain.
    • 万岁 - A common expression of praise or celebration, often used to show approval or loyalty.
    • 鸿嘉、永始之间 - Refers to specific periods during the reign of Emperor Cheng of Han, indicating a time of prosperity.
    • 焚宵游宫及飞行殿，罢宴逸之乐 - Describes the destruction of the palace and the flying pavilion, and the cessation of extravagant entertainment.
    • 从绳则正，如转圜焉 - A metaphorical expression suggesting that following the right path leads to smooth and easy progress, like a wheel turning effortlessly.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 汉成帝 - Emperor Cheng of Han, who reigned from 33 to 7 BCE. He is known for his indulgent lifestyle and the construction of the '宵游宫.'
    • 宵游宫 - A specific palace built by Emperor Cheng for nighttime outings, reflecting his preference for covert travel.
    • 飞行殿 - A mythical or magical structure that could move through the air, symbolizing the emperor's desire for luxurious and extraordinary experiences.
    • 鸿嘉、永始之间 - Specific periods during the reign of Emperor Cheng, known for their relative peace and prosperity.
    • 刘向、谷永 - Prominent officials and scholars during the Han dynasty who advised the emperor on various matters, including the need to reduce extravagance.

    Proper Nouns

    • 汉成帝 - Emperor Cheng of Han.
    • 宵游宫 - A specific palace built by Emperor Cheng.
    • 飞行殿 - A mythical or magical structure.
    • 鸿嘉、永始之间 - Specific periods during the reign of Emperor Cheng.
    • 刘向、谷永 - Prominent officials and scholars during the Han dynasty.

    Emperor Cheng of Han enjoyed incognito travel, and he built the "Xiaoyou Palace" by the Taiye Pool, with lacquered columns and black silk curtains, and all furnishings, clothing, and vehicles were predominantly black. He was pleased with the dark environment and disliked the light from lamps and candles. The palace women all wore black clothes, and from Ban Jieyu downwards, they all wore black ribbons, and their ornaments, though as splendid as brocade, were covered with sandalwood gauze. Only when they arrived at the "Xiaoyou Palace" did they light candles. After feasts and entertainments, they performed quiet dances, and their steps were so light that they did not raise dust. He enjoyed evening outings. He built a flying pavilion, one zhang square, like a modern carriage, and selected elite soldiers from the Yu Lin to carry it. On the carriage, he felt it moved swiftly, and he heard a sound like wind and thunder inside, indicating its speed, and it was called the "Yunlei Palace." In the palaces he favored, the ground was covered with felt and silk to avoid the noise of wheels and hooves. Although he was indulged in covert pleasures, the people did not suffer or complain. Whenever he returned from his outings, he distributed valuable clothes and delicacies to the women he favored, and left them by the roadside, and the poor and elderly people all sang "Long live the Emperor." Therefore, during the reigns of Hongjia and Yongshi, the country was prosperous, and weapons were rarely used. Thus, Liu Xiang and Gu Yong pointed out and earnestly advised, and the "Xiaoyou Palace" and the flying pavilion were burned down, and extravagant entertainment was abolished. This is what is meant by following the right path, which leads to smooth and easy progress, like a wheel turning effortlessly. [90]

    帝常以三秋闲日，与飞燕戏于太液池，以沙棠木为舟，贵其不沉没也。以云母饰于鹢首，一名“云舟”。又刻大桐木为虬龙，雕饰如真，以夹云舟而行。以紫桂为柂枻。及观云棹水，玩撷菱蕖，帝每忧轻荡，以惊飞燕，令佽飞之士，以金锁缆云舟于波上。每轻风时至，飞燕殆欲随风入水。帝以翠缨结飞燕之裙，游倦乃返。飞燕后渐见疏，常怨曰：“妾微贱，何复得预缨裙之游？”今太液池尚有避风台，即飞燕结裙之处。
    　　录曰：夫言端扆拱默者，人君之尊也。是故兴居有节，进止有度，出则太师奏登车之礼，入则少师荐升堂之仪，列旌门以周卫，修清宫以宴息。成帝轻南面之位，微游昵幸，好惑神仙之事，谷永因而抗谏。《书》不云乎：“弗矜细行，终累大德。”斯之谓矣。 [91]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [91]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 三秋闲日 - Leisure days of autumn; indicates a time of relaxation and leisure.
    • 轻荡 - Lightness and swaying; describes the boat's movement.
    • 翠缨结飞燕之裙 - Green silk ribbon tied to Fei Yan's skirt; method used to prevent her from falling into the water.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 帝 - Emperor Cheng of Han; the ruler of the Han dynasty.
    • 飞燕 - Zhao Feiyan; a famous concubine of Emperor Cheng of Han, known for her beauty and grace.
    • 太液池 - Taiye Pool; a large artificial lake in the imperial palace of the Han dynasty, used for recreational purposes.
    • 沙棠木 - Sandalwood; a type of wood known for its durability and resistance to water.
    • 云母 - Mica; a mineral used for decorative purposes.
    • 紫桂 - Purple osmanthus; a type of ornamental plant.
    • 风 - Gentle breeze; a common element in Chinese poetry and literature, often associated with beauty and tranquility.
    • 避风台 - Wind shelter; a structure built to protect against strong winds.

    Proper Nouns

    • 帝 - Emperor Cheng of Han.
    • 飞燕 - Zhao Feiyan.
    • 太液池 - Taiye Pool.
    • 沙棠木 - Sandalwood.
    • 云母 - Mica.
    • 紫桂 - Purple osmanthus.
    • 风 - Gentle breeze.
    • 避风台 - Wind shelter.

    The emperor often on the leisure days of autumn, played with Zhao Feiyan in Taiye Pool, using sandalwood to make a boat, valuing it for not sinking. He decorated the bow with mica, and it was also called "Cloud Boat." He also carved a large paulownia wood into a coiled dragon, carved and decorated to look real, to accompany the Cloud Boat. He used purple osmanthus for the rudder and paddle. When watching the Cloud Boat paddle through the water, playing and picking lotus flowers, the emperor always worried about the lightness and swaying, which might startle Fei Yan. He ordered the skilled watermen to use golden chains to tether the Cloud Boat on the waves. Each time a gentle breeze blew, Fei Yan almost seemed to want to follow the wind into the water. The emperor tied a green silk ribbon to Fei Yan's skirt, and they returned when they were tired of playing. Fei Yan was gradually neglected, and she often complained saying, "I am lowly, how can I again participate in the ribbon-skirt play?" Now Taiye Pool still has a wind shelter, the place where the ribbon was tied to Fei Yan's skirt.

    The record states: The dignity of a ruler is to sit facing the screen in silent reverence. Therefore, one's movements and rest have their limits, and one's actions and stops have their measures. When going out, the Grand Tutor presents the ceremony of mounting the carriage; when entering, the Junior Tutor recommends the ceremony of ascending the hall. Flags and banners are set up around the gates for protection, and a clean palace is prepared for rest. Emperor Cheng of Han, however, neglected his position as ruler, indulged in minor pleasures, and was easily swayed by tales of immortals. Gu Yong thus boldly remonstrated. Does not the Book of Documents say, "Do not disdain small actions, for they will ultimately tarnish great virtue"? This is precisely what it means. [91]

    哀帝尚淫奢，多进谄佞。幸爱之臣，竞以妆饰妖丽，巧言取容。董贤以雾绡单衣，飘若蝉翼。帝入宴息之房，命筵卿易轻衣小袖，示用奢带修裙，故使婉转便易也。宫人皆效其断袖。又曰，割袖恐惊其眠。

    后汉

    　　明帝阴贵人梦食瓜甚美。帝使求诸方国。时炖煌献异瓜种，恒山献巨桃核。瓜名“穹隆”，长三尺，而形屈曲，味美如饴。父老云：“昔道士从蓬莱山得此瓜，云是崆峒灵瓜，四劫一实，西王母遗于此地，世代遐绝，其实颇在。”又说：“巨桃霜下结花，隆暑方熟，亦云仙人所食。”帝使植于霜林园。园皆植寒果，积冰之节，百果方盛，俗谓之“相陵”，与霜林之声讹也。后曰：“王母之桃，王公之瓜，可得而食，吾万岁矣，安可植乎？”后崩，内侍者见镜奁中有瓜、桃之核，视之涕零，疑非其类耳。 [92]

    Emperor Ai indulged in luxury and debauchery, frequently promoting flatterers and sycophants. His favored officials competed to present themselves as beautifully adorned and eloquent. Dong Xian wore a fine, translucent garment, delicate as a cicada's wing. When the emperor entered the room where he rested, he ordered the attendant to change into light clothing with small sleeves, to show off the use of luxurious sashes and long skirts, making the attire graceful and easy to move. The attendants emulated cutting one's sleeve, and the phrase "cutting the sleeve to avoid waking him up" became a common expression.

    During the reign of Emperor Ming, Empress Dowager Yin had a dream in which she ate a very delicious melon. The emperor ordered to seek melon seeds from various countries. Dunhuang presented a strange variety of melon seeds, and Mount Heng presented a large peach pit. The melon was named "Qionglong," measuring three feet in length and curved in shape, with a taste sweet as syrup. The elders said, "In the past, a Taoist priest obtained this melon from Mount Penglai, saying it was a divine melon from Mount Kungtung, bearing fruit once every four great eons. The Queen Mother of the West left it in this place, far removed in time, and the fruit still exists." They also said, "The peach blooms under frost and ripens in the heat, and it is said to be food for immortals." The emperor ordered the melon to be planted in the Frosty Forest Garden. The garden was filled with cold fruits, blooming in winter and bearing fruit in the heat, a phenomenon known as "Xiangling," a phonetic variant of "Frosty Forest."

    The empress dowager remarked, "The peach of the Queen Mother of the West and the melon of the king, though they can be eaten, I shall never plant them here. After my death, the inner attendants saw melon and peach pits in the mirror casket. They looked at them and wept, suspecting they were not of their kind." [92]

    章帝永宁元年，条支国来贡异瑞。有鸟名鳷鹊，形高七尺，解人语。其国太平，则鳷鹊群翔。昔汉武帝时，四夷宾服，有献驯鹊，若有喜乐事，则鼓翼翔鸣。按庄周云“雕陵之鹊”，盖其类也。《淮南子》云：“鹊知人喜。”今之所记，大小虽殊，远近为异，故略举焉。
    　　安帝好微行，于郊垧或露宿，起帷宫，皆用锦罽文绣。至永初三年，国用不足，令吏民入钱者得为官。有琅琊王溥，即王吉之后。吉先为昌邑中尉。溥奕世衰凌，及安帝时，家贫不得仕，乃挟竹简插笔，于洛阳市佣书。美于形貌，又多文辞梾僦其书者，丈夫赠其衣冠，妇人遗其珠玉，一日之中，衣宝盈车而归。积粟于廪，九族宗亲，莫不仰其衣食，洛阳称为善笔而得富。溥先时家贫，穿井得铁印，铭曰：“佣力得富，钱至亿庾。一土三田，军门主簿。”后以一亿钱输官，得中垒校尉。三田一土，“垒”字也；中垒校尉掌北军垒门，故曰军门主簿。积善降福，神明报焉。 [93]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [93]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 條支國來貢異瑞 - The country of Tiaozhi came to offer unusual auspicious signs.
    • 形高七尺 - Standing seven feet tall.
    • 解人語 - Can understand human language.
    • 其國太平，則鳷鹊群翔 - When the country is at peace, the Zhi Que birds fly in groups.
    • 昔漢武帝時，四夷賓服 - In the time of Emperor Wu of Han, the four barbarian tribes submitted.
    • 有獻驯鹊 - There was a tame magpie offered.
    • 若有喜樂事，則鼓翼翔鳴 - If there is a joyful event, it flaps its wings and flies and cries.
    • 按庄周云“雕陵之鹊” - According to Zhuangzi, the 'Qiao Ling magpie.'
    • 盖其类也 - It is of the same kind.
    • 《淮南子》云：“鹊知人喜。” - The *Huainanzi* says, 'The magpie knows human joy.'
    • 今之所记，大小虽殊，远近为异 - What is recorded now, though different in size and distance, is the same in essence.
    • 故略举焉 - Therefore, I will briefly mention it.
    • 安帝好微行 - Emperor An enjoyed secret travels.
    • 于郊垧或露宿 - In the outskirts or slept in the open air.
    • 起帷宫，皆用锦罽文绣 - Set up tents made of brocade, felt, and embroidered fabrics.
    • 至永初三年，国用不足 - By the third year of Yongchu, the state treasury was insufficient.
    • 令吏民入钱者得为官 - Ordered officials and commoners to contribute money to become officials.
    • 有琅琊王溥，即王吉之后 - There was Wang Pu of Langya, the descendant of Wang Ji.
    • 吉先为昌邑中尉 - Wang Ji was first the Zhongwei of Changyi.
    • 溥奕世衰凌 - Wang Pu's family declined for generations.
    • 及安帝时，家贫不得仕 - By the time of Emperor An, he was poor and could not become an official.
    • 乃挟竹简插笔，于洛阳市佣书 - He then carried bamboo slips and a brush, working as a copyist in the market of Luoyang.
    • 美于形貌，又多文辞 - He was handsome in appearance and had many literary skills.
    • 僦其书者，丈夫赠其衣冠，妇人遗其珠玉 - Those who hired him to copy books, men gave him clothes, and women gave him jewels.
    • 一日之中，衣宝盈车而归 - In one day, he returned with a carriage full of clothes and treasures.
    • 积粟于廪，九族宗亲，莫不仰其衣食 - He stored grain in granaries, and his nine clans and relatives all depended on his food and clothing.
    • 洛阳称为善笔而得富 - In Luoyang, he was called a good copyist and became wealthy.
    • 溥先时家贫，穿井得铁印 - Wang Pu, when he was poor, dug a well and found an iron seal.
    • 铭曰：“佣力得富，钱至亿庾。一土三田，军门主簿。” - The inscription said, 'Through labor, one gains wealth, with money reaching the billions. One earth, three fields, military gate clerk.'
    • 后以一亿钱输官，得中垒校尉 - Later, he paid one billion coins to the government and became the Commander of the Central Camp.
    • 三田一土，“垒”字也 - Three fields and one earth, the character for 'camp'.
    • 中垒校尉掌北军垒门，故曰军门主簿 - The Commander of the Central Camp oversees the gates of the northern army camp, hence the title 'military gate clerk'.
    • 积善降福，神明报焉 - Accumulated virtue brings blessings, and the spirits repay him.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 條支國 - The country of Tiaozhi, likely referring to a distant or mythical land, possibly related to the Parthian Empire.
    • 漢武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han, a prominent Han dynasty emperor known for his military campaigns and cultural achievements.
    • 四夷 - The four barbarian tribes, a general term for non-Han Chinese peoples.
    • 庄周 - Zhuangzi, a philosopher of the Daoist tradition.
    • 雕陵之鹊 - The Qiao Ling magpie, a specific type of magpie mentioned in Zhuangzi.
    • 《淮南子》 - The *Huainanzi*, a philosophical and scientific text from the Han dynasty.
    • 安帝 - Emperor An of Han, a Han dynasty emperor known for his indulgent lifestyle.
    • 永宁元年 - The first year of Yongning, a period in the Han dynasty.
    • 永初三年 - The third year of Yongchu, a period in the Han dynasty.
    • 琅琊 - Langya, a region in present-day Shandong Province.
    • 王吉 - Wang Ji, a historical figure, possibly a Han dynasty official.
    • 昌邑 - Changyi, a place name, possibly referring to a region or city.
    • 中尉 - Zhongwei, a military officer rank in the Han dynasty.
    • 中垒校尉 - Commander of the Central Camp, a military rank in the Han dynasty.
    • 北军垒门 - Gates of the northern army camp, a specific military structure in the Han dynasty.

    In the first year of Yongning during the reign of Emperor Zhang, the country of Tiaozhi came to offer unusual auspicious signs. There was a bird called Zhi Que, standing seven feet tall and able to understand human language. When the country was at peace, the Zhi Que birds would fly in groups. In the time of Emperor Wu of Han, the four barbarian tribes submitted, and there was a tame magpie offered. If there was a joyful event, it would flap its wings and fly and cry. According to Zhuangzi, the "Qiao Ling magpie," it is of the same kind. The Huainanzi says, "The magpie knows human joy." What is recorded now, though different in size and distance, is the same in essence, so I will briefly mention it.

    Emperor An enjoyed secret travels, in the outskirts or slept in the open air, setting up tents made of brocade, felt, and embroidered fabrics. By the third year of Yongchu, the state treasury was insufficient, and he ordered officials and commoners to contribute money to become officials. There was Wang Pu of Langya, the descendant of Wang Ji. Wang Ji was first the Zhongwei of Changyi. Wang Pu's family declined for generations. By the time of Emperor An, he was poor and could not become an official. He then carried bamboo slips and a brush, working as a copyist in the market of Luoyang. He was handsome in appearance and had many literary skills. Those who hired him to copy books, men gave him clothes, and women gave him jewels. In one day, he returned with a carriage full of clothes and treasures. He stored grain in granaries, and his nine clans and relatives all depended on his food and clothing. In Luoyang, he was called a good copyist and became wealthy. Wang Pu, when he was poor, dug a well and found an iron seal. The inscription said, "Through labor, one gains wealth, with money reaching the billions. One earth, three fields, military gate clerk." Later, he paid one billion coins to the government and became the Commander of the Central Camp. Three fields and one earth, the character for "camp." The Commander of the Central Camp oversees the gates of the northern army camp, hence the title "military gate clerk." Accumulated virtue brings blessings, and the spirits repay him. [93]

    灵帝初平三年，游于西园。起裸游馆千间，采绿苔而被阶，引渠水以绕砌，周流澄澈。乘船以游漾，使宫人乘之，选玉色轻体者，以执篙楫，摇漾于渠中。其水清澄，以盛暑之时，使舟覆没，视宫人玉色。又奏《招商》之歌，以来凉气也。歌曰：“凉风起兮日照渠，青荷昼偃叶夜舒，惟日不足乐有余。清丝流管歌玉凫，千年万岁喜难逾。”渠中植莲，大如盖，长一丈，南国所献。其叶夜舒昼卷，一茎有四莲丛生，名曰“夜舒荷”。亦云月出则舒也，故曰“望舒荷”。 [94]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [94]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 游于西园 - Leisurely activity of strolling in the Western Garden.
    • 起裸游馆千间 - Building a thousand rooms for nude bathing, indicating luxury and extravagance.
    • 采绿苔而被阶 - Collecting green moss and covering the steps, describing decorative use of natural elements.
    • 引渠水以绕砌 - Drawing canal water to encircle the steps, indicating a water feature around the building.
    • 周流澄澈 - Circulating and clear, describing the water's appearance.
    • 乘船以游漾 - Riding a boat and floating around, indicating a leisurely boat ride.
    • 使宫人乘之 - Having palace attendants ride on them, referring to the boat ride.
    • 选玉色轻体者 - Selecting those with fair skin and slender bodies, describing the chosen attendants.
    • 以执篙楫 - Holding paddles and oars, indicating the attendants' roles.
    • 摇漾于渠中 - Swaying and floating in the canal, describing the movement of the boats.
    • 其水清澄 - The water is clear, describing the water's clarity.
    • 以盛暑之时 - During the hot summer, indicating the season.
    • 使舟覆没 - Making the boats capsize, describing a deliberate action.
    • 视宫人玉色 - Seeing the fair skin of the palace attendants, indicating the purpose of the capsize.
    • 又奏《招商》之歌 - Playing the song 'Inviting Coolness,' indicating a musical performance.
    • 以来凉气也 - Bringing cool air, describing the purpose of the song.
    • 歌曰 - The song says, introducing the lyrics.
    • 凉风起兮日照渠 - Cool winds rise, and the sun shines on the canal, describing the scene.
    • 青荷昼偃叶夜舒 - Green lotus leaves curl during the day and spread at night, describing the lotus leaves' behavior.
    • 惟日不足乐有余 - Only days are insufficient, but joy is abundant, expressing the idea that days are short but joy is plentiful.
    • 清丝流管歌玉凫 - Clear strings and flowing pipes sing the jade duck, describing the musical instruments and the song.
    • 千年万岁喜难逾 - For a thousand years and ten thousand ages, joy is hard to surpass, expressing the enduring nature of joy.
    • 渠中植莲 - Lotus plants grow in the canal, describing the vegetation.
    • 大如盖 - As large as an umbrella, describing the size of the lotus leaves.
    • 长一丈 - One zhang long, describing the length of the lotus stems.
    • 南国所献 - Offered by the southern regions, indicating the origin of the lotus.
    • 其叶夜舒昼卷 - The leaves spread at night and curl during the day, describing the lotus leaves' behavior.
    • 一茎有四莲丛生 - One stem has four lotus flowers growing together, describing the lotus flowers.
    • 名曰“夜舒荷” - Called 'Night-Opening Lotus,' giving the lotus a name.
    • 亦云月出则舒也 - Also said to open when the moon rises, providing another name for the lotus.
    • 故曰“望舒荷” - Thus called 'Moon-Opening Lotus,' giving the lotus another name.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 灵帝 - Emperor Ling of Han, the ruler of the late Eastern Han period.
    • 西园 - The Western Garden, a royal garden in the capital.
    • 裸游馆 - A luxurious facility for nude bathing, reflecting the decadence of the late Han period.
    • 《招商》之歌 - The song 'Inviting Coolness,' which was likely used to bring a sense of coolness during the hot summer.
    • 南国 - The southern regions of China, known for their lush vegetation and exotic flora.

    Proper Nouns

    • 灵帝 - Emperor Ling of Han.
    • 西园 - The Western Garden.
    • 《招商》之歌 - The song 'Inviting Coolness.'
    • 南国 - The southern regions of China.
    • 夜舒荷 - The name given to the lotus.
    • 望舒荷 - Another name given to the lotus.

    During the third year of the Chuping era of Emperor Ling, he strolled in the Western Garden. He built a thousand rooms for nude bathing, collected green moss to cover the steps, drew canal water to encircle the steps, and the water circulated clearly. He rode boats to float around, had palace attendants ride on them, selected those with fair skin and slender bodies, to hold paddles and oars, and sway and float in the canal. The water was clear, and during the hot summer, he made the boats capsize to see the fair skin of the palace attendants. He also played the song "Inviting Coolness" to bring cool air. The song says: "Cool winds rise, and the sun shines on the canal; green lotus leaves curl during the day and spread at night. Only days are insufficient, but joy is abundant. Clear strings and flowing pipes sing the jade duck; for a thousand years and ten thousand ages, joy is hard to surpass." Lotus plants grew in the canal, as large as umbrellas, one zhang long, offered by the southern regions. Their leaves spread at night and curled during the day, with one stem having four lotus flowers growing together, called "Night-Opening Lotus." Also said to open when the moon rises, thus called "Moon-Opening Lotus." [94]

    帝盛夏避暑于裸游馆，长夜饮宴。帝嗟曰：“使万岁如此，则上仙也。”宫人年二七已上，三六以下，皆靓妆，解其上衣，惟着内服，或共裸浴。西域所献茵墀香，煮以为汤，宫人以之浴浣毕，使以余汁入渠，名曰“流香渠”。又使内竖为驴鸣。于馆北又作鸡鸣堂，多畜鸡，每醉迷于天晓，内侍竞作鸡鸣，以乱真声也。乃以炬烛投于殿前，帝乃惊悟。及董卓破京师，散其美人，焚其宫馆。至魏咸熙中，先所投烛处，夕夕有光如星。后人以为神光，于此地立小屋，名曰“余光祠”，以祈福。至魏明末，稍扫除矣。 [95]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [95]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 盛夏避暑 - Classical phrase meaning 'to escape the heat of summer,' indicating a seasonal activity.
    • 使万岁如此 - Hypothetical scenario meaning 'if one could live like this forever.'
    • 上仙 - Refers to a legendary immortal or deity, often used metaphorically to describe someone who lives forever in bliss.
    • 二七已上 - Means 'aged fourteen or older,' using a traditional way of counting age in Chinese culture.
    • 三六以下 - Means 'aged eighteen or younger,' again using a traditional method of age calculation.
    • 解其上衣 - Means 'to remove their outer garments,' describing a state of undress.
    • 惟着内服 - Means 'wearing only undergarments,' indicating a state of partial undress.
    • 裸浴 - Means 'nude bathing,' describing a practice of bathing without clothing.
    • 西域所献茵墀香 - Refers to a fragrant herb from the Western Regions (西域) that was used for bathing.
    • 煮以为汤 - Means 'to boil it into a bathwater,' describing the preparation of the bath.
    • 以之浴浣毕 - Means 'after bathing and washing,' indicating the completion of the bathing process.
    • 流香渠 - Means 'the fragrant flowing channel,' describing the channel where the bathwater was poured.
    • 内竖 - Refers to eunuchs or palace attendants.
    • 为驴鸣 - Means 'to make the sound of a donkey,' describing a form of entertainment.
    • 鸡鸣堂 - Means 'the rooster-crowing hall,' a place where roosters were kept to signal the start of the day.
    • 畜鸡 - Means 'to keep chickens,' indicating the practice of raising roosters.
    • 天晓 - Means 'dawn' or 'break of day,' referring to the early morning.
    • 内侍 - Refers to palace attendants or eunuchs.
    • 竞作鸡鸣 - Means 'to compete in making the sound of a rooster,' describing a competitive activity.
    • 以乱真声 - Means 'to confuse the real sound,' indicating the intention to mimic the natural sound of a rooster.
    • 炬烛 - Refers to torches or candles.
    • 投于殿前 - Means 'to throw them in front of the hall,' describing the action of throwing the torches.
    • 惊悟 - Means 'to startle awake,' indicating the reaction to the torches being thrown.
    • 破京师 - Means 'to destroy the capital city,' referring to the destruction of Luoyang.
    • 散其美人 - Means 'to disperse the beauties,' indicating the dispersal of the emperor's concubines.
    • 焚其宫馆 - Means 'to burn his palaces and halls,' describing the destruction of the imperial buildings.
    • 夕夕有光如星 - Means 'every night there is light like stars,' describing the ongoing phenomenon of the candles.
    • 神光 - Refers to divine light, indicating a supernatural or mystical quality.
    • 余光祠 - Means 'the shrine of the remaining light,' a place of worship or veneration.
    • 祈福 - Means 'to pray for blessings,' indicating the purpose of the shrine.
    • 扫除 - Means 'to clean' or 'to clear away,' describing the gradual disappearance of the shrine.
    • 魏咸熙中 - Refers to the year 265 AD, during the reign of Emperor Yuan of Wei.
    • 魏明末 - Refers to the end of the reign of Emperor Ming of Wei, which was 239 AD.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 帝 - Refers to an emperor, specifically Emperor Ling of Han.
    • 裸游馆 - Refers to a palace or hall where nude bathing was practiced.
    • 西域 - Refers to the Western Regions, areas west of the Pamir Mountains, known for their exotic goods and influences.
    • 茵墀香 - A fragrant herb from the Western Regions, often used for its aromatic properties.
    • 董卓 - A powerful warlord who seized control of the Han dynasty and later destroyed the capital.
    • 京师 - Refers to the capital city, Luoyang in this context.
    • 美人 - Refers to the emperor's concubines or favored women.
    • 宫馆 - Refers to the imperial palaces and halls.
    • 流香渠 - A channel where the fragrant bathwater was poured, symbolizing the luxurious and sensual atmosphere of the palace.
    • 余光祠 - A shrine built to honor the divine light, reflecting the belief in supernatural phenomena.

    Proper Nouns

    • 帝 - Emperor Ling of Han.
    • 裸游馆 - A specific palace or hall.
    • 西域 - The Western Regions.
    • 茵墀香 - A specific fragrant herb.
    • 董卓 - A specific warlord.
    • 京师 - The capital city, Luoyang.
    • 美人 - The emperor's concubines.
    • 宫馆 - The imperial palaces and halls.
    • 流香渠 - A specific channel.
    • 余光祠 - A specific shrine.
    • 魏咸熙中 - A specific year in the Wei dynasty.
    • 魏明末 - A specific year in the Wei dynasty.

    During the hot summer, the emperor sought refuge in the Naked Play Hall, where he indulged in drinking and feasting throughout the night. The emperor lamented, "If one could live like this forever, then one would be an immortal." Palace women aged fourteen or older and eighteen or younger were all adorned with makeup, removed their outer garments, and wore only their underclothes, sometimes bathing naked together. A fragrant herb from the Western Regions, known as yinchi, was boiled into a bathwater. After bathing and washing, the remaining liquid was poured into a channel, which was named the "Fragrant Flowing Channel." The emperor also ordered the eunuchs to make the sound of a donkey. To the north of the hall, a Rooster-Crowing Hall was constructed, where many chickens were kept. Whenever the emperor was drunk and confused by the dawn, the palace attendants would compete in making the sound of a rooster, confusing the real sound. Then, torches were thrown in front of the hall, startling the emperor awake. When Dong Zhuo destroyed the capital, he dispersed the emperor's concubines and burned the imperial palaces and halls. By the year 265 AD, the place where the torches had been thrown still glowed with light like stars every night. People believed this was divine light and built a small shrine there, naming it the "Shrine of the Remaining Light" to pray for blessings. By the end of Emperor Ming's reign in the Wei dynasty, the shrine was gradually cleared away. [95]

    录曰：明、章两主，丕承前业，风被四海，威行八区，殊边异服，祥瑞辐凑。安、灵二帝，同为败德。夫悦目快心，罕不沦乎情欲，自非远鉴兴亡，孰能移隔下俗。佣才缘心，缅乎嗜欲，塞谏任邪，没情于淫靡。至如列代亡主，莫不凭威猛以丧家国，肆奢丽以覆宗祀。询考先坟，往往而载，佥求历古，所记非一。贩爵鬻官，乖分职之本；露宿郊居，违省方之义。成、安二帝，载世虽远，而乱政攸同。验之史牒，讯诸前记，迷情狗马，爱好龙鹤，非明王之所闻示于后也。内穷淫酷，外尽禽荒，取悦耳目，流贬万世。是以牝妖告祸，汉灵以巷伯倾宗。酒池裸逐之丑，鸣鸡长夜之惑，事由商乙，远仿燕丹，异代一时，可为悲矣。 [96]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [96]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 丕承前业 - Formal expression meaning 'to greatly inherit the previous achievements' or 'to faithfully carry on the legacy.'
    • 风被四海，威行八区 - Grandiloquent phrase meaning 'the influence extends to all corners of the world, and the authority is exercised in all directions.' Emphasizes widespread influence and power.
    • 殊边异服 - Refers to remote regions with different customs or attire, indicating the far reaches of the empire.
    • 祥瑞辐凑 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'auspicious signs converge like spokes of a wheel,' suggesting a gathering of good omens.
    • 同为败德 - Means 'both engaged in moral corruption' or 'both acted in ways that violated virtue.'
    • 悦目快心 - Refers to things that are visually pleasing and emotionally satisfying, often leading to indulgence.
    • 沦乎情欲 - Means 'to be submerged in desires,' indicating a loss of self-control due to sensual temptations.
    • 远鉴兴亡 - Means 'to have a far-sighted view of rise and fall,' implying wisdom gained from historical experience.
    • 移隔下俗 - Means 'to change or elevate the lower classes,' suggesting the ability to influence or reform society.
    • 佣才缘心 - Refers to talent that is guided by personal desires or whims.
    • 缅乎嗜欲 - Means 'to be deeply engrossed in desires,' indicating a preoccupation with sensual pleasures.
    • 塞谏任邪 - Means 'to block advice and favor evil,' suggesting a rejection of good counsel and an embrace of wrongdoing.
    • 没情于淫靡 - Means 'to be lost in debauchery,' indicating a complete immersion in excessive and immoral behavior.
    • 殊边异服，祥瑞辐凑 - Metaphorical expression that combines the idea of remote regions with auspicious signs converging.
    • 取悦耳目 - Means 'to delight the senses,' often referring to superficial pleasures.
    • 流贬万世 - Means 'to be condemned by future generations,' indicating a lasting negative impact.
    • 牝妖告祸 - Refers to a female demon or omen predicting disaster, often used to symbolize impending doom.
    • 巷伯倾宗 - Refers to a historical figure who brought ruin to his family, often used to illustrate the consequences of moral corruption.
    • 酒池裸逐之丑 - Refers to the infamous story of King Jie of Xia, who indulged in excessive drinking and naked dancing, symbolizing moral decay.
    • 鸣鸡长夜之惑 - Refers to the story of King You of Zhou, who used fire signals to amuse his concubine, leading to the fall of his kingdom.
    • 事由商乙 - Refers to the story of King Zhou of Shang, who was known for his cruelty and extravagance.
    • 远仿燕丹 - Refers to the story of Prince Dan of Yan, who sought to assassinate Qin Shi Huang, symbolizing futile resistance against inevitable decline.
    • 可为悲矣 - Means 'this is truly a cause for sorrow,' expressing deep regret or lamentation.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 明、章两主 - Refers to Emperor Ming and Emperor Zhang of Han, two emperors known for their wise governance.
    • 安、灵二帝 - Refers to Emperor An and Emperor Ling of Han, two emperors who were notorious for their moral failings.
    • 成、安二帝 - Refers to Emperor Cheng and Emperor An of Han, two emperors whose reigns were marked by political instability and moral decay.
    • 商乙 - Refers to King Zhou of Shang, a historical figure known for his tyranny and extravagance.
    • 燕丹 - Refers to Prince Dan of Yan, a historical figure who attempted to assassinate Qin Shi Huang, symbolizing futile resistance against inevitable decline.
    • 巷伯倾宗 - Refers to a historical figure who brought ruin to his family, often used to illustrate the consequences of moral corruption.
    • 酒池裸逐之丑 - Refers to the infamous story of King Jie of Xia, who indulged in excessive drinking and naked dancing, symbolizing moral decay.
    • 鸣鸡长夜之惑 - Refers to the story of King You of Zhou, who used fire signals to amuse his concubine, leading to the fall of his kingdom.

    Proper Nouns

    • 明、章两主 - Emperor Ming and Emperor Zhang of Han.
    • 安、灵二帝 - Emperor An and Emperor Ling of Han.
    • 成、安二帝 - Emperor Cheng and Emperor An of Han.
    • 商乙 - King Zhou of Shang.
    • 燕丹 - Prince Dan of Yan.
    • 巷伯 - A historical figure who brought ruin to his family.
    • 酒池裸逐之丑 - The story of King Jie of Xia.
    • 鸣鸡长夜之惑 - The story of King You of Zhou.

    The record states: "Emperor Ming and Emperor Zhang, inheriting the great achievements of their predecessors, extended their influence to all corners of the world and exercised their authority in all directions. Their influence reached remote regions with different customs, and auspicious signs converged like spokes of a wheel. However, Emperor An and Emperor Ling, both engaged in moral corruption. Indeed, things that delight the senses and satisfy the heart rarely do not lead one to be submerged in desires. Without a far-sighted view of rise and fall, who could change or elevate the lower classes? Talent guided by personal desires, deeply engrossed in desires, blocking advice and favoring evil, being lost in debauchery. Just as in past dynasties, no ruler who relied on tyranny and extravagance failed to lose his kingdom and destroy his ancestral rites. When consulting historical records and examining past accounts, it is often found that rulers who were obsessed with dogs and horses, and fond of dragons and cranes, were not the kind of wise kings whose deeds should be passed down to posterity. Internally, they indulged in cruel and excessive pleasures, and externally, they pursued frivolous and wasteful pursuits, delighting the senses and being condemned by future generations. Therefore, the female demon warned of disaster, and Emperor Ling brought ruin to his family through the actions of a notorious figure. The shameful acts of King Jie, who indulged in excessive drinking and naked dancing, and the deceptive actions of King You, who used fire signals to amuse his concubine, all point to the same pattern of moral decline. These events, whether in the Shang or Yan dynasties, are a cause for deep sorrow." [96]

    献帝伏皇后，聪惠仁明，有闻于内则。及乘舆为李?霍所败，昼夜逃走，宫人奔窜，万无一生。至河，无舟楫，后乃负帝以济河，河流迅急，惟觉脚下如有乘践，则神物之助焉。兵戈逼岸，后乃以身拥遏于帝。帝伤趾，后以绣拭血，刮玉钗以覆于疮，应手则愈。以泪湔帝衣及面，洁静如浣。军人叹伏：虽乱犹有明智妇人。精诚之至，幽祇之所感矣。 [97]

    source_title: "Empress Fu of Emperor Xian of Han"
source_author: "Unknown"
translator: "Your Name"

    Empress Fu of Emperor Xian of Han was known for her intelligence, kindness, and wisdom, and she was renowned for her virtue within the palace. When the imperial carriage was defeated by Li Jue and Guo Si, the emperor fled day and night, with the palace attendants in a panic, and there seemed to be no hope of survival. When they reached the river, there were no boats available, so Empress Fu carried the emperor across the river. The river was flowing rapidly, but she felt as if she were being carried by something supernatural, indicating divine assistance. As the soldiers approached the riverbank, she used her body to shield the emperor. The emperor had injured his toe, and Empress Fu wiped the blood with silk and used a jade hairpin to cover the wound, which healed instantly upon application. She washed the emperor's clothes and face with her tears, leaving them as clean and fresh as if they had been washed. The soldiers were deeply moved and impressed, remarking that even in chaos, there was a wise and virtuous woman. Her utmost sincerity and dedication had moved the divine beings. [97]

    录曰：夫丹石可磨，而不可夺其坚色；兰桂可折，而不可掩其贞芳。伏后履纯明之姿，怀忠亮之质，临危授命，壮夫未能加焉，知死不吝，冯媛之俦也。求之千古，亦所罕闻。汉兴，至于哀、平、元、成，尚以宫室，崇苑囿，而西京始有弘侈，东都继其繁奢，既违采椽不斫之制，尤异灵沼遵俭之风。考之皇图，求之志录，千家万户之书，台卫城隍之广，自重门构宇以来，未有若斯之费溢也。孝哀广四时之房，灵帝修裸游之馆，妖惑为之则神怨，工巧为之则人虐，夷国沦家，可为恸矣！及夫灵瑞、嘉禽、艳卉、殊木，生非其壤，诡色讹音，不禀正朔之地，无涉图书所记，或缘德业以来仪，由时俗以具质，咸得而备详矣。历览群经，披求方册，未若斯之宏丽矣。 [98]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [98]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 夫丹石可磨，而不可奪其堅色；蘭桂可折，而不可掩其貞芳 - Parallel structure comparing the unchangeable nature of jade and stones to the unyielding fragrance of orchids and cassia, emphasizing the inherent qualities of a person.
    • 伏後履純明之姿，懷忠亮之質，臨危授命，壯夫未能加焉 - Sequence describing Fu Hou's qualities and her courageous actions, with '臨危授命' being a key phrase that emphasizes her bravery.
    • 知死不吝，馮媛之儔也 - Phrase comparing Fu Hou's bravery to that of Feng Yuan, using '儔' to indicate similarity.
    • 求之千古，亦所罕聞 - Phrase emphasizing the rarity of such examples throughout history.
    • 漢興，至於哀、平、元、成，尚以宮室，崇苑囿 - Sequence describing the early Han Dynasty and the construction of palaces and gardens.
    • 而西京始有弘侈，東都繼其繁奢 - Phrase contrasting the grandeur of the Western Capital with the continued luxury of the Eastern Capital.
    • 既違采椽不斫之制，尤異靈沼遵儉之風 - Phrase criticizing the deviation from frugal traditions.
    • 考之皇圖，求之志錄，千家萬戶之書，台衛城隍之廣 - Sequence describing the extensive descriptions of palaces, fortifications, and cities.
    • 自重門構宇以來，未有若斯之費溢也 - Phrase emphasizing the unprecedented extravagance of the construction.
    • 孝哀廣四時之房，靈帝修裸遊之館 - Phrase describing the specific constructions of Emperor Ai and Emperor Ling.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 伏後 - Refers to Empress Fu (伏后), a historical figure known for her loyalty and integrity during the late Han Dynasty.
    • 馮媛 - Refers to Feng Yuan (馮媛), a historical figure known for her bravery and loyalty.
    • 漢興 - Refers to the establishment of the Han Dynasty.
    • 哀、平、元、成 - Refers to the reigns of Emperor Ai, Emperor Ping, Emperor Yuan, and Emperor Cheng of the Han Dynasty.
    • 西京 - Refers to Chang'an, the capital of the Western Han Dynasty.
    • 東都 - Refers to Luoyang, the capital of the Eastern Han Dynasty.
    • 孝哀 - Refers to Emperor Ai of Han.
    • 靈帝 - Refers to Emperor Ling of Han.
    • 靈瑞 - Refers to auspicious signs or omens.
    • 嘉禽 - Refers to rare or beautiful birds.
    • 艷卉 - Refers to beautiful flowers.
    • 殊木 - Refers to unique or rare trees.
    • 正朔 - Refers to the rules of the calendar, often used in historical contexts to denote the legitimacy of a dynasty.
    • 圖書 - Refers to historical records or books.

    The Record says: Just as jade and stones can be polished, but their inherent hardness and color cannot be altered, so too can orchids and cassia be broken, but their pure fragrance cannot be hidden. Empress Fu embodied purity and brightness in her appearance and loyalty and integrity in her character. Facing danger, she accepted her fate, a feat that even a brave man could not surpass. Knowing that death was inevitable without regret, she was like Feng Yuan. Such examples are rare throughout history. From the rise of the Han Dynasty to the reigns of Emperor Ai, Emperor Ping, Emperor Yuan, and Emperor Cheng, they still built palaces and gardens, with the Western Capital beginning to become grand and luxurious, followed by the Eastern Capital continuing this trend. They violated the principle of using simple, uncarved beams and deviated from the frugal tradition of the Sacred Pool. Examining the imperial plans and historical records, the extensive descriptions of palaces, fortifications, and cities, since the construction of multi-gate buildings, there has never been such extravagant spending. Emperor Ai expanded the four-season buildings, and Emperor Ling repaired the nude bathing pavilions. If one is deceived by illusions, the gods will resent it; if one is obsessed with craftsmanship, people will suffer. The destruction of nations and families is truly heart-wrenching! As for auspicious signs, rare birds, beautiful flowers, and unique trees, born outside their native soil, with strange colors and sounds, not bound by the rules of the calendar, and not mentioned in historical records, some come due to virtue and achievements, others due to contemporary customs. All these details are fully recorded. Studying all the classics and historical texts, nothing compares to this grandeur. [98]

    郭况，光武皇后之弟也。累金数亿，家僮四百余人，以黄金为器，工冶之声，震于都鄙。时人谓：“郭氏之室，不雨而雷。”言其铸锻之声盛也。庭中起高阁长庑，置衡石于其上，以称量珠玉也。阁下有藏金窟，列武士以卫之。错杂宝以饰台榭，悬明珠于四垂，昼视之如星，夜望之如月。里语曰：“洛阳多钱郭氏室，夜日昼星富无匹。”其宠者皆以玉器盛食，故东京谓郭家为“琼厨金穴”。况小心畏慎，虽居富势，闭门优游，未曾干世事，为一时之智也。 [99]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [99]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 累金數億 - Literally 'accumulated gold in the tens of millions,' used to describe immense wealth.
    • 家僮四百余人 - Literally 'over four hundred domestic servants,' indicating a large household staff.
    • 以黃金為器 - Literally 'using gold as vessels,' meaning that gold was used to make household items.
    • 工冶之聲，震于都鄙 - Literally 'the sound of smelting and forging, shaking the capital and its suburbs,' describing the loud noise of metalworking.
    • 郭氏之室，不雨而雷 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'De's house, where thunder roars without rain,' symbolizing the intense sound of metalworking.
    • 庭中起高閣長庑 - Literally 'a high pavilion and long corridor were built in the courtyard,' describing the grand architecture of the house.
    • 置衡石于其上，以稱量珠玉也 - Literally 'a balance and weights were placed on it to weigh pearls and jade,' indicating the use of the pavilion for weighing precious items.
    • 閣下有藏金窟 - Literally 'under the pavilion there is a hidden treasure vault,' referring to a secret storage place for gold.
    • 列武士以衛之 - Literally 'arranging warriors to guard it,' indicating the security measures in place.
    • 錯雜寶以飾台榭 - Literally 'adorn the pavilions and terraces with a mix of treasures,' describing the lavish decoration of the house.
    • 懸明珠于四垂 - Literally 'hanging pearls at the four corners,' indicating the luxurious decoration of the house.
    • 昼視之如星，夜望之如月 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'in the day it looks like stars, at night it looks like the moon,' describing the brilliance of the pearls.
    • 里語曰：“洛阳多钱郭氏室，夜日昼星富无匹。” - A folk saying meaning 'In Luoyang, the De household has more money than anyone else; at night it's like the moon, in the day it's like stars, unmatched in wealth.'
    • 其宠者皆以玉器盛食 - Literally 'those favored ones all use jade vessels to serve food,' indicating the luxurious lifestyle of the household.
    • 琼厨金穴 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'jade kitchen and gold cave,' referring to the luxurious and wealthy lifestyle of the De household.
    • 小心畏慎 - Literally 'careful and cautious,' describing De Kuang's character.
    • 闭门优游 - Literally 'shutting the door and living leisurely,' indicating a life of seclusion and ease.
    • 未曾干世事 - Literally 'never involved in worldly affairs,' indicating De Kuang's disinterest in politics.
    • 为一时之智也 - Literally 'to be wise for his time,' indicating that De Kuang's behavior was considered wise by contemporary standards.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 郭况 - Guo Kuang, a relative of Empress Guo of the Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 光武皇后 - Empress Guo of Emperor Guangwu (Liu Xiu) of the Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 洛阳 - Luoyang, the capital of the Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 琼厨金穴 - A metaphor for a wealthy and luxurious household.
    • 闭门优游 - A lifestyle of seclusion and ease, often associated with the wealthy elite.
    • 未曾干世事 - Refers to the practice of not engaging in political affairs, which was common among the wealthy and influential during the Eastern Han period.

    Proper Nouns

    • 郭况 - Guo Kuang, a relative of Empress Guo of the Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 光武皇后 - Empress Guo of Emperor Guangwu (Liu Xiu) of the Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 洛阳 - Luoyang, the capital of the Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 琼厨金穴 - A metaphor for a wealthy and luxurious household.

    Guo Kuang, the younger brother of Empress Guo of Emperor Guangwu, accumulated tens of millions in gold, had over four hundred domestic servants, and used gold to make household items. The sound of smelting and forging was so loud that it shook the capital and its suburbs. People said, "De's house, where thunder roars without rain," symbolizing the intense sound of metalworking. In the courtyard, a high pavilion and long corridor were built, and a balance and weights were placed on it to weigh pearls and jade. Under the pavilion, there was a hidden treasure vault, guarded by warriors. The pavilions and terraces were adorned with a mix of treasures, and pearls were hung at the four corners. In the day, they looked like stars; at night, they looked like the moon. Folk saying: "In Luoyang, the De household has more money than anyone else; at night it's like the moon, in the day it's like stars, unmatched in wealth." Those favored ones all used jade vessels to serve food, so in the Eastern Han, the De household was called "jade kitchen and gold cave." Guo Kuang was careful and cautious, and although he lived in wealth and power, he shut the door and lived leisurely, never involved in worldly affairs, to be wise for his time. [99]

    录曰：夫后族之盛，专挟内主之威，皆以党嬖强盛，肆嚣于天下，妖幸侵政，擅椒房之亲。在昔魏冉，富倾嬴国；汉世王凤，同拜五侯。馆第僭于京都，嫱姬丽于宫掖。瑰赂南金，弥玩于王府；缇绣雕文，被饰于土木。高廓洞门，极夏屋之盛；文马朱轩，穷车服之靡。自古擅骄，未有如斯之例。虽三归移于管室，八佾陈于季庭，方之为劣矣。郭况内凭姻宠，外专声厉，远采山丹之穴，积陶朱、程郑之产，未足称其盛欤！曾不恃其戚里，矜其财势，秉温恭之正，守道持盈，而自竞慎，是可谓知几其神乎！ [100]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [100]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 夫后族之盛 - Formal expression meaning 'the prosperity of the maternal family,' introducing the topic.
    • 专挟内主之威 - Means 'exclusively wielding the power of the inner ruler,' referring to the influence of the emperor's mother or consort.
    • 党嬖强盛 - Means 'favored and powerful,' describing the rise of a family through favoritism.
    • 肆嚣于天下 - Means 'to spread arrogance throughout the world,' indicating the family's overbearing behavior.
    • 妖幸侵政 - Means 'evil luck or fortune intrudes into politics,' referring to the interference of a favored family in state affairs.
    • 擅椒房之亲 - Means 'to monopolize the closeness of the imperial harem,' highlighting the family's privileged position.
    • 在昔魏冉，富倾嬴国 - Refers to the historical example of Wei Ran, who was so wealthy that his riches could tip the balance of the Qin state.
    • 汉世王凤，同拜五侯 - Refers to Wang Feng of the Han dynasty, who was granted the title of Duke and had five brothers who were also ennobled.
