    • 魏文帝 - Emperor Wen of Wei, a ruler of the state of Wei during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 公孙龙 - A famous logician and philosopher known for his sharp debating skills.
    • 子游、子夏 - Two famous disciples of Confucius, known for their wisdom and virtue.
    • 仲尼 - A respectful term for Confucius.
    • 秘书丞 - A high-ranking official position in the imperial bureaucracy.
    • 御衣 - A robe worn by the emperor, symbolizing imperial favor.

    Proper Nouns

    • 魏禅晋之岁 - The year of the transition of power from Wei to Jin.
    • 北阙 - The northern gate of the imperial city.
    • 白燕 - A white swallow.

    During the year when the state of Wei ceded power to the state of Jin, a white light appeared near the northern gate of the imperial city, resembling birds, and would occasionally fly around. Officials reported this phenomenon to the emperor. Upon investigation, they found a white swallow, which was considered a divine object. They then made a golden cage for it and placed it in the palace. After ten days, the swallow disappeared. Some scholars commented, "This is an auspicious sign of the metal element, associated with the state of Jin. In ancient times, during the reign of Shikuang, a white swallow came to nest." Checking the Book of Auspicious Signs confirmed this interpretation. The white color is associated with the metal element, and Shikuang was a person from the state of Jin, so the ancient and modern interpretations align.

    Xue Xia, a man from Tianshui, was exceptionally learned. His mother dreamed that someone gave her a box of clothes and said, "This woman will give birth to a wise and virtuous son, honored by emperors." The mother remembered the date of the dream. When Xue Xia was born, he reached adulthood and demonstrated extraordinary talent and eloquence. Emperor Wen of Wei discussed with him all day long, and his responses were fluent and without hesitation. The emperor said, "In the past, Gong Sun Long was known for his sharp debating skills, but he was often considered eccentric and unreliable. Now, what you say is only about the words of the sages. You are as good as Ziyu and Zixia, two famous disciples of Confucius, and even if Confucius were in Wei, he would consider you a top student again." The emperor wrote a letter to Xue Xia, calling him "the top student," and he held the position of Secretary of the Secretariat. Despite his poverty, the emperor gave him a robe, fulfilling the original dream. Xue Xia was the most famous of his time and a great scholar. [113]

    田畴，北平人也。刘虞为公孙瓒所害，畴追慕无已，往虞墓设鸡酒之礼，恸哭之音，动于林野，翔鸟为之凄鸣，走兽为之吟伏。畴卧于草间，忽有人通云：“刘幽州来，欲与田子泰言平生之事。”畴神悟远识，知是刘虞之魂。既近而拜，畴泣不自支，因相与进鸡酒。畴醉，虞曰：“公孙瓒求子甚急，宜窜伏以避害！”畴拜曰：“闻君臣之义，生则尽礼，今见君之灵，愿得同归九地，死且不朽，安可逃乎！”虞曰：“子万古之贞士也，深慎尔仪！”奄然不见，畴亦醉醒。 [114]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [114]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 追慕 - To grieve and yearn for someone after their death.
    • 鸡酒之礼 - A simple offering of chicken and wine, symbolizing respect and mourning.
    • 动于林野 - To move or echo through the forest and fields, suggesting the intensity of the emotions.
    • 翔鸟为之一空 - Birds flying overhead mourn in response, indicating a profound emotional impact.
    • 走兽为之一空 - Wild animals lie down and cry out, further emphasizing the emotional resonance.
    • 神悟远识 - Divine insight and far-sighted wisdom, suggesting a supernatural connection.
    • 同归九地 - To return together to the underworld, implying a desire to share the same fate.
    • 安可逃乎 - How can one escape, indicating a sense of inevitability and acceptance.
    • 奄然不见 - Suddenly disappearing, suggesting a sudden and mysterious departure.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 田畴 - A historical figure known for his loyalty and devotion.
    • 北平 - A region in northern China.
    • 刘虞 - A Han dynasty official who was betrayed and killed by his subordinate, Gongsun Zan.
    • 公孙瓒 - A military general who betrayed and killed Liu Yu.
    • 幽州 - An ancient Chinese province, roughly corresponding to modern-day Beijing and surrounding areas.
    • 子泰 - A respectful title for a son, often used in historical texts.
    • 子万古之贞士也 - A title of honor, meaning 'a gentleman of unwavering integrity throughout the ages.'
    • 深慎尔仪 - A phrase advising caution and propriety in one's actions and words.

    Proper Nouns

    • 田畴 - A historical figure.
    • 北平 - A region in northern China.
    • 刘虞 - A Han dynasty official.
    • 公孙瓒 - A military general.
    • 幽州 - An ancient Chinese province.
    • 子泰 - A respectful title for a son.
    • 九地 - The underworld or afterlife.

    Tian Chou, a native of Beiping, was deeply grieved and yearned for Liu Yu after his death. He went to Liu Yu's tomb and offered a simple meal of chicken and wine. The sound of his sorrowful cries echoed through the forest and fields, causing the birds flying overhead to mourn and the wild animals to lie down and cry out. Tian Chou lay in the grass, when suddenly someone appeared and said, "Liu Yu of Youzhou has come, and he wishes to speak with you about matters of his life." With divine insight and far-sighted wisdom, Tian Chou knew it was the spirit of Liu Yu. As the spirit approached, Tian Chou bowed, unable to control his tears, and they shared the meal of chicken and wine together. Tian Chou became drunk, and Liu Yu said, "Gongsun Zan is searching for you urgently; you should hide to avoid danger!" Tian Chou bowed and replied, "I have heard of the duty of loyalty between a ruler and his subject. In life, one should fulfill all rites. Now that I see your spirit, I wish to return to the underworld with you, where we will both remain immortal. How can I escape?" Liu Yu said, "You are a gentleman of unwavering integrity throughout the ages. Be cautious and maintain your propriety!" Suddenly, the spirit disappeared, and Tian Chou woke up from his drunken stupor. [114]

    曹洪，武帝从弟，家盈产业，骏马成群。武帝讨董卓，夜行失马，洪以其所乘马上帝。其马号曰“白鹄”。此马走时，惟觉耳中风声，足似不践地。至汴水，洪不能渡，帝引洪上马共济，行数百里，瞬息而至。马足毛不湿。时人谓为乘风而行，亦一代神骏也。谚曰：“凭空虚跃，曹家白鹄。”
    　　录曰：王者廓万宇以为邦家，因海岳以为城池，固是安民养德，垂拱而治焉。去乎游历之费，导于敦教之道，无崇宫室，有薄林园。采椽不斫，大唐如斯昭俭；卑宫菲食，伯禹以之戒奢。迄乎三代之王，失斯道矣。伤财弊力，以骄丽相夸，琼室之侈，璧台之富，穷神工之奇妙，人力勤苦。至于春秋，王室凌废，城者作讴，疲于勤劳。晋筑祈褫之宫，为功动于民怨；宋兴泽门之役，劳者以为深嗟。姑苏积费于前，阿房奋竭于后。 [115]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [115]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 為之一空 - Set phrase meaning completely emptied; fields were left barren.

    Proper Nouns

    • 紹興 - Era name of the Southern Song; rendered as Shaoxing.

    Cao Hong, the younger brother of Emperor Wu, was wealthy with extensive property and a herd of fine horses. During Emperor Wu's pursuit of Dong Zhuo, he lost his horse at night, and Cao Hong gave his own horse to the emperor. This horse was named "White Swan." When it ran, one could only hear the wind in the ears, and its hooves seemed not to touch the ground. Upon reaching the Bian River, Cao Hong could not cross, so Emperor Wu pulled him onto the horse and they crossed together, traveling hundreds of miles in an instant. The horse's hooves and hair were not wet. People at the time said it was as if they were riding the wind, and it was also a divine steed of its time. A saying goes: "Leaping into empty space, the White Swan of the Cao family."

    The record states: The ruler extends his domain to form a state, using the sea and mountains as fortifications, which is indeed for the peace and virtue of the people, ruling with ease. Abandoning the expenses of travel, guiding through the path of education, not building grand palaces, but having simple gardens. Using untrimmed logs for beams, the great Tang dynasty exemplified frugality; living in humble dwellings and eating simple food, Yu the Great warned against luxury. By the time of the three dynasties, this principle was lost. Wasting resources and labor, competing in pride and beauty, the extravagance of jade palaces, the wealth of jade platforms, exhausting the wonders of divine craftsmanship, human labor was arduous. By the time of the Spring and Autumn period, the royal house had declined, city builders sang songs, exhausted by their labor. The Jin dynasty built the Qichui Palace, causing public resentment due to the effort; the Song dynasty's construction of the Zemen Gate caused deep sorrow among the laborers. The accumulation of expenses in Gusu (Suzhou) before, and the exhaustion in A'fang Palace later. [115]

    自以业固河山，名超万世，覆灭宗祀，由斯哀哀。窃观明帝，践中区之沃盛，威灵所慑，比强列代，祯祥神宝，史不绝书，殊方珍贡，府无虚月，鼎据三方，称雄四海。而圣教微于尧、禹，历代劣于姬、汉，东鲠闽、吴，西病邛蜀，师旅岁兴，财力日费，不能遵养黎元，远瞻前朴，宫室穷丽，池榭肆其宏广，终取夷灭，数其然哉！任城渊谋神勇，智周祥艺，虽来舟、蓬蒙剑射之好，不能加也。田畴事死如生，守以直节，精诚之至，通于神明。曹洪忠烈为心，爱亲忧国。此穆满之骏，方之“白鹄”，可谓齐足者也。 [116]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [116]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 自以業固河山，名超萬世 - Classical expression meaning 'believing that one's legacy is as solid as rivers and mountains and surpasses all generations in fame.'
    • 覆滅宗祀，由斯哀哀 - Phrase indicating the destruction of ancestral worship and the resulting profound sorrow.
    • 踐中區之沃盛 - Means 'stepping into the fertile and prosperous center of the land.'
    • 威靈所慑，比强列代 - Indicates the power and influence that intimidates even the strongest of past dynasties.
    • 祯祥神宝，史不绝书 - Means 'auspicious signs and divine treasures are frequently recorded in history.'
    • 殊方珍贡，府无虚月 - Refers to exotic and precious tributes that arrive regularly without fail.
    • 鼎据三方，称雄四海 - Describes holding power over three regions and being dominant throughout the world.
    • 圣教微于尧、禹，历代劣于姬、汉 - Suggests that the moral teachings have diminished compared to those of Yao and Yu, and the current dynasty is inferior to the Zhou and Han dynasties.
    • 东鲠闽、吴，西病邛蜀 - Indicates eastern troubles with Min and Wu, and western problems with Qiong and Shu.
    • 师旅岁兴，财力日费 - Means that military campaigns occur annually, and resources are spent daily.
    • 不能遵养黎元，远瞻前朴 - Suggests an inability to nurture the common people and look ahead to simpler times.
    • 宫室穷丽，池榭肆其宏广 - Describes palaces and gardens as being extremely luxurious and expansive.
    • 终取夷灭，数其然哉 - Concludes with the idea that this ultimately leads to destruction, and it is indeed so.
    • 任城渊谋神勇，智周祥艺 - Refers to Ren Cheng's deep strategy and divine courage, as well as his comprehensive wisdom and skills.
    • 田畴事死如生，守以直节 - Means that Tian Chou treats the dead as if they were alive, maintaining his integrity.
    • 精诚之至，通于神明 - Suggests that his sincerity and dedication reach the level of divinity.
    • 曹洪忠烈为心，爱亲忧国 - Describes Cao Hong as having a heart of loyalty and bravery, caring for his family and the country.
    • 此穆满之骏，方之“白鹄”，可谓齐足者也 - Compares these individuals to the swift horses of Mu Man, likening them to the 'White Swan,' suggesting they are equally capable.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 尧、禹 - Refers to Yao and Yu, legendary sage rulers of ancient China.
    • 姬、汉 - Refers to the Zhou and Han dynasties, known for their moral and cultural achievements.
    • 闽、吴 - Refers to the regions of Min and Wu, historically significant areas in southeastern China.
    • 邛蜀 - Refers to the regions of Qiong and Shu, historically significant areas in southwestern China.
    • 任城 - A place name, possibly referring to a specific region or city.
    • 田畴 - A person, likely a historical figure known for his integrity.
    • 曹洪 - A person, likely a historical figure known for his loyalty and bravery.
    • 穆满之骏 - Refers to the swift horses of Mu Man, a legendary ruler of the Zhou dynasty.
    • 白鹄 - A mythical or symbolic bird, often associated with purity and nobility.

    Proper Nouns

    • 尧、禹 - Legendary sage rulers of ancient China.
    • 姬、汉 - The Zhou and Han dynasties.
    • 闽、吴 - Regions in southeastern China.
    • 邛蜀 - Regions in southwestern China.
    • 任城 - A place name, possibly referring to a specific region or city.
    • 田畴 - A historical figure known for his integrity.
    • 曹洪 - A historical figure known for his loyalty and bravery.
    • 穆满 - A legendary ruler of the Zhou dynasty.
    • 白鹄 - A mythical or symbolic bird.

    Believing that his legacy was as solid as rivers and mountains and surpassing all generations in fame, the destruction of ancestral worship brought profound sorrow. Observing Emperor Ming, he stepped into the fertile and prosperous center of the land, where his power and influence intimidated even the strongest of past dynasties. Auspicious signs and divine treasures were frequently recorded in history, and exotic and precious tributes arrived regularly without fail. Holding power over three regions and being dominant throughout the world, yet the moral teachings had diminished compared to those of Yao and Yu, and the current dynasty was inferior to the Zhou and Han dynasties. Eastern troubles with Min and Wu, and western problems with Qiong and Shu, led to annual military campaigns and daily expenditure of resources. He was unable to nurture the common people and look ahead to simpler times, and his palaces and gardens were extremely luxurious and expansive. Ultimately, this led to destruction, and it is indeed so. Ren Cheng possessed deep strategy and divine courage, as well as comprehensive wisdom and skills, which even the archery and swordsmanship of Laizhou and Pengmeng could not surpass. Tian Chou treated the dead as if they were alive, maintaining his integrity, and his sincerity and dedication reached the level of divinity. Cao Hong had a heart of loyalty and bravery, caring for his family and the country. These individuals are like the swift horses of Mu Man, compared to the "White Swan," and can be said to be equally capable. [116]

    拾遗记卷八

    吴

    　　孙坚母妊坚之时，梦肠出绕腰，有一童女负之，绕吴阊门外，又授以芳茅一茎。童女语曰：“此善祥也，必生才雄之子。今赐母以土，王于翼、轸之地，鼎足于天下。百年中应于异宝授于人也。”语毕而觉，日起筮之。筮者曰：“所梦童女负母绕阊门，是太白之精，感化来梦。”夫帝王之兴，必有神迹自表，白气者，金色。及吴灭而践晋祚，梦之征焉。 [117]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [117]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 肠出绕腰 - Vivid and symbolic description of the mother's dream, representing the unusual and auspicious nature of the conception.
    • 太白之精 - Refers to the planet Venus, often associated with brightness and purity, and here symbolizing divine intervention.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 孙坚 - A key figure in the early Three Kingdoms period, known for his military prowess and role in the founding of the Wu kingdom.
    • 吴 - The state of Wu, one of the three major states during the Three Kingdoms period, known for its strategic importance and cultural significance.
    • 阊门 - A famous gate in ancient Suzhou, often associated with prosperity and good fortune.
    • 晋祚 - Refers to the Jin dynasty, which succeeded the Han dynasty and eventually conquered the Wu kingdom.

    Proper Nouns

    • 孙坚 - The historical figure whose mother had the dream.
    • 吴 - The state of Wu, which would later become one of the Three Kingdoms.
    • 阊门 - A specific gate in ancient Suzhou, often associated with prosperity and good fortune.
    • 太白 - The planet Venus, often seen as a symbol of purity and divine favor.
    • 晋祚 - The reign of the Jin dynasty, which succeeded the Han dynasty and eventually conquered the Wu kingdom.

    Lost Records, Volume 8

    Wu

    When Sun Jian's mother was pregnant with him, she dreamed of her intestines emerging and wrapping around her waist, and a young girl carried her, circling around the Wu Gate (阊门). The girl then gave her a single stem of fragrant grass. The girl said, "This is a good omen, and you will surely give birth to a heroic son. Now, you are granted land, and you will rule the region of the Wing and the Altar, standing as one of the three major powers. In a hundred years, a unique treasure will be bestowed upon someone." After the girl finished speaking, the mother woke up and sought divination. The diviner said, "The dream of the young girl carrying the mother around the Wu Gate is the essence of Venus, a divine influence that brought the dream." The rise of emperors is always accompanied by divine signs, and the white light is golden. When Wu eventually fell and the Jin dynasty rose, the dream was proven to be a true omen. [117]

    录曰：按《吴书》云：“孙坚母怀坚之时，梦肠出绕阊门。”与王之说为异。夫西方金位，以叶晋德，兴亡之兆，后而效焉。盖表吴亡而授晋也。夫六梦八征，着明《周易》，授兰怀日，事类而非。及吴氏之兴年，嘉禾之号，芳茅之征信矣。至晋太康元年，孙皓送六金玺云：“时无玉工，故以金为印玺。”夫孙氏擅割江东，包卷百越，吞席汉阳，威惕中夏，富强之业，三雄比盛。时有未宾而兵戈岁起，每梗心于邛蜀，愤慨于燕魏，四方未夷，有事征伐，因之以师旅，遵之以俭素，去其游侈之费，塞兹雕靡之涂，不欲使四方民劳，非无玉工也。固能轻彼池山，贱斯棘实，汉鄙盈车之屑，燕弃璞于衡庑，沉河底谷，义昭攸古，务崇俭约，岂非高欤！及乎吴亡时，以六代金玺归晋，坚母之梦验矣。 [118]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [118]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 錄曰 - Formal introductory phrase meaning 'The record says' or 'It is recorded that.'
    • 按《吳書》云 - Means 'According to Wu Shu it is said,' introducing a reference to the Wu Shu (History of Wu).
    • 與王之說為異 - Means 'differs from Wang's account,' indicating a contrast with another source.
    • 夫西方金位，以葉晉德 - Classical expression meaning 'The West is the position of metal, which aligns with the virtue of Jin,' referring to the Five Elements theory.
    • 興亡之兆，後而效焉 - Means 'the signs of rise and fall, which manifest later,' suggesting a delayed effect.
    • 蓋表吳亡而授晉也 - Means 'This indicates the fall of Wu and the rise of Jin,' summarizing the implication of the earlier statement.
    • 夫六夢八征，著明《周易》 - Means 'The six dreams and eight omens, clearly stated in Zhouyi,' referring to the Book of Changes.
    • 授蘭懷日，事類而非 - Means 'Granting a lily and holding a sun, these are similar but not identical,' referring to a specific divination practice.
    • 及吳氏之興年，嘉禾之號，芳茅之征信矣 - Means 'And the year of Wu's rise, the title 'Jiacheng,' and the verified auspicious reeds,' referring to specific historical and symbolic elements.
    • 至晉太康元年，孫皓送六金璽云 - Means 'By the first year of Tai Kang in Jin, Sun Hao sent six golden seals, saying,' introducing a historical event.
    • 時無玉工，故以金為印璽 - Means 'There were no jade craftsmen, so gold was used for the seals,' explaining a practical reason.
    • 夫孫氏擅割江東，包卷百越 - Means 'The Sun family ruled Jiangdong, encompassing the Baiyue,' describing the territorial control of the Sun family.
    • 吞席漢陽，威惕中夏 - Means 'Swallowing Hanyang, instilling fear in the Central Plains,' describing the military and political power of the Sun family.
    • 富強之業，三雄比盛 - Means 'The industry of wealth and strength, comparable to the three great powers,' highlighting the Sun family's prosperity.
    • 時有未賓而兵戈歲起 - Means 'At times there were those not yet subjugated, and wars broke out annually,' describing ongoing conflicts.
    • 每梗心於邛蜀，愤慨於燕魏 - Means 'Frequently troubled by Qiong and Shu, and filled with resentment towards Yan and Wei,' indicating regional tensions.
    • 四方未夷，有事征伐 - Means 'The four directions were not pacified, and there were frequent military campaigns,' describing the state of affairs.
    • 因之以師旅，遵之以儉素 - Means 'Following this with military campaigns, adhering to frugality and simplicity,' describing the approach to governance.
    • 去其游侈之費，塞兹雕靡之涂 - Means 'Eliminating extravagant spending, blocking the path of luxury and decay,' describing economic policies.
    • 不欲使四方民勞 - Means 'Not wanting to burden the people of the four directions,' expressing a concern for the populace.
    • 非無玉工也 - Means 'It was not that there were no jade craftsmen,' clarifying a previous statement.
    • 固能輕彼池山，賤斯棘實 - Means 'Indeed, they could consider the hills and pools as light, and the thorns as worthless,' describing a dismissive attitude.
    • 漢鄙盈車之屑，燕棄璞于衡庑 - Means 'Han discarded the dust that filled the carriage, and Yan abandoned the unpolished jade on the hall,' using metaphors to describe wastefulness.
    • 沉河底谷，義昭攸古 - Means 'Sinking to the bottom of the river, the meaning is clear from ancient times,' suggesting a historical precedent.
    • 務崇儉約，豈非高欤 - Means 'Striving for frugality and simplicity, is this not noble?' expressing a moral judgment.
    • 及乎吳亡時，以六代金璽歸晉 - Means 'When Wu fell, the six generations of golden seals were returned to Jin,' describing the final act.
    • 堅母之夢驗矣 - Means 'The dream of Sun Jian's mother was verified,' concluding the narrative.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 《吳書》 - The History of Wu, a historical text detailing the state of Wu during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 王之說 - Refers to another historical or literary source, likely a different account of the same events.
    • 五方五色 - Refers to the Five Elements theory, which associates directions with elements and colors.
    • 晉德 - Refers to the virtue of Jin, one of the Five Elements.
    • 六夢八征 - Refers to specific divinations and omens mentioned in the Zhouyi (Book of Changes).
    • 授蘭懷日 - A specific divination practice involving lilies and the sun.
    • 吳氏之興年，嘉禾之號 - Refers to the year of Wu's rise and the title 'Jiacheng,' a symbol of good fortune.
    • 芳茅之征信 - Refers to the verified auspicious reeds, a symbol of good fortune.
    • 晉太康元年 - The first year of Tai Kang in the Jin dynasty, a specific historical period.
    • 孫皓 - The last ruler of the state of Wu, known for his submission to Jin.
    • 六金璽 - Six golden seals, a significant historical artifact.
    • 孫氏 - The Sun family, rulers of the state of Wu.
    • 江東 - The eastern part of the Yangtze River region, a key area of control for the Sun family.
    • 百越 - The various tribes and states in southern China, often in conflict with the central states.
    • 漢陽 - A region in the Han dynasty, often a site of conflict.
    • 中夏 - The Central Plains, the heartland of Chinese civilization.
    • 三雄 - The three great powers of the Three Kingdoms period: Wei, Shu, and Wu.
    • 邛蜀 - Regions in southwestern China, often in conflict with the central states.
    • 燕魏 - Regions in northern China, often in conflict with the central states.
    • 四方 - The four directions, a common metaphor for the entire world.
    • 師旅 - Military campaigns and warfare.
    • 儉素 - Frugality and simplicity, a virtue in Confucian thought.
    • 游侈之費 - Extravagant spending, often criticized in classical texts.
    • 雕靡之涂 - The path of luxury and decay, a negative concept.
    • 池山 - Hills and pools, often used metaphorically in classical texts.
    • 棘實 - Thorns, often used metaphorically to represent something worthless.
    • 漢鄙盈車之屑 - A metaphor for wastefulness, using the image of dust filling a carriage.
    • 燕棄璞于衡庑 - A metaphor for wastefulness, using the image of unpolished jade being discarded.
    • 沉河底谷 - Sinking to the bottom of the river, a metaphor for failure or downfall.
    • 義昭攸古 - The meaning is clear from ancient times, suggesting a historical precedent.
    • 務崇儉約 - Striving for frugality and simplicity, a moral virtue.
    • 堅母之夢 - The dream of Sun Jian's mother, a specific historical or literary event.

    Proper Nouns

    • 孫堅 - Sun Jian, a historical figure.
    • 孫皓 - Sun Hao, the last ruler of the state of Wu.
    • 六金璽 - Six golden seals, a significant historical artifact.
    • 孫氏 - The Sun family, rulers of the state of Wu.
    • 江東 - The eastern part of the Yangtze River region, a key area of control for the Sun family.
    • 百越 - The various tribes and states in southern China, often in conflict with the central states.
    • 漢陽 - A region in the Han dynasty, often a site of conflict.
    • 中夏 - The Central Plains, the heartland of Chinese civilization.
    • 三雄 - The three great powers of the Three Kingdoms period: Wei, Shu, and Wu.
    • 邛蜀 - Regions in southwestern China, often in conflict with the central states.
    • 燕魏 - Regions in northern China, often in conflict with the central states.
    • 四方 - The four directions, a common metaphor for the entire world.
    • 師旅 - Military campaigns and warfare.
    • 儉素 - Frugality and simplicity, a virtue in Confucian thought.
    • 游侈之費 - Extravagant spending, often criticized in classical texts.
    • 雕靡之涂 - The path of luxury and decay, a negative concept.
    • 池山 - Hills and pools, often used metaphorically in classical texts.
    • 棘實 - Thorns, often used metaphorically to represent something worthless.
    • 漢鄙盈車之屑 - A metaphor for wastefulness, using the image of dust filling a carriage.
    • 燕棄璞于衡庑 - A metaphor for wastefulness, using the image of unpolished jade being discarded.
    • 沉河底谷 - Sinking to the bottom of the river, a metaphor for failure or downfall.
    • 義昭攸古 - The meaning is clear from ancient times, suggesting a historical precedent.
    • 務崇儉約 - Striving for frugality and simplicity, a moral virtue.
    • 堅母之夢 - The dream of Sun Jian's mother, a specific historical or literary event.

    The record says: According to Wu Shu, it is recorded that when Sun Jian's mother was pregnant with him, she dreamt of intestines emerging and winding around the city gate of Chengyang. This differs from Wang's account. The West is the position of metal, which aligns with the virtue of Jin; the signs of rise and fall manifest later, indicating the fall of Wu and the rise of Jin. The six dreams and eight omens, clearly stated in Zhouyi, are similar but not identical to the act of granting a lily and holding a sun. And the year of Wu's rise, the title "Jiacheng," and the verified auspicious reeds are all significant. By the first year of Tai Kang in Jin, Sun Hao sent six golden seals, saying, "There were no jade craftsmen, so gold was used for the seals." The Sun family ruled江东, encompassing the Baiyue, swallowing Hanyang, and instilling fear in the Central Plains, with an industry of wealth and strength comparable to the three great powers. At times there were those not yet subjugated, and wars broke out annually, frequently troubled by Qiong and Shu, and filled with resentment towards Yan and Wei. The four directions were not pacified, and there were frequent military campaigns. Following this with military campaigns, adhering to frugality and simplicity, eliminating extravagant spending, blocking the path of luxury and decay, not wanting to burden the people of the four directions. It was not that there were no jade craftsmen. Indeed, they could consider the hills and pools as light, and the thorns as worthless, Han discarded the dust that filled the carriage, and Yan abandoned the unpolished jade on the hall, sinking to the bottom of the river, the meaning is clear from ancient times, striving for frugality and simplicity, is this not noble? When Wu fell, the six generations of golden seals were returned to Jin, and the dream of Sun Jian's mother was verified. [118]

    吴主赵夫人，丞相达之妹。善画，巧妙无双，能于指间以彩丝织云霞龙蛇之锦，大则盈尺，小则方寸，宫中谓之“机绝”。孙权常叹魏、蜀未夷，军旅之隙，思得善画者使图山川地势军阵之像。达乃进其妹。权使写九州岛方岳之势。夫人曰：“丹青之色，甚易歇灭，不可久宝；妾能刺绣，列国方帛之上，写以五岳河海城邑行阵之形。”既成，乃进于吴主，时人谓之“针绝”。虽棘刺木猴，云梯飞(玄鸟)，无过此丽也。权居昭阳宫，倦暑，乃褰紫绡之帷，夫人曰：“此不足贵也。 [119]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [119]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 善画，巧妙无双 - Skilled in painting, unparalleled in her artistry.
    • 能于指间以彩丝织云霞龙蛇之锦 - Able to weave clouds, dragons, and serpents with colored silk between her fingers.
    • 大则盈尺，小则方寸 - Large ones fill a foot, small ones a square inch.
    • 宫中谓之“机绝” - In the palace, she was called the 'Wondrous Weaver.'
    • 军旅之隙 - In the intervals of military campaigns.
    • 思得善画者使图山川地势军阵之像 - Wanted to find a skilled painter to depict landscapes, terrain, and military formations.
    • 列国方帛之上 - On the square pieces of cloth of various states.
    • 五岳河海城邑行阵之形 - The forms of the Five Mountains, rivers, seas, cities, and military formations.
    • 棘刺木猴，云梯飞(玄鸟) - The intricate needlework of a monkey on a thorn, or a cloud ladder flying with a celestial bird.
    • 褰紫绡之帷 - Unveiled the purple gauze curtain.
    • 此不足贵也 - This is not precious.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 吴主 - Ruler of Wu, referring to the king of the state of Wu during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 赵夫人 - Lady Zhao, a historical figure known for her exceptional needlework.
    • 丞相达 - Chancellor Da, a high-ranking official who recommended his sister to the ruler.
    • 五岳 - The Five Great Mountains, a traditional Chinese concept referring to five sacred mountains.
    • 河海 - Rivers and seas, representing natural features.
    • 城邑 - Cities and towns, urban settlements.
    • 行阵 - Military formations, referring to the arrangement of troops.
    • 棘刺木猴 - A monkey woven on a thorn, a metaphor for extremely intricate needlework.
    • 云梯飞(玄鸟) - A cloud ladder flying with a celestial bird, another metaphor for intricate needlework.

    Proper Nouns

    • 吴主 - Ruler of Wu.
    • 赵夫人 - Lady Zhao.
    • 丞相达 - Chancellor Da.
    • 五岳 - The Five Great Mountains.
    • 河海 - Rivers and seas.
    • 城邑 - Cities and towns.
    • 行阵 - Military formations.
    • 棘刺木猴 - A monkey woven on a thorn.
    • 云梯飞(玄鸟) - A cloud ladder flying with a celestial bird.
    • 昭阳宫 - Zhaoyang Palace.

    Lady Zhao of Wu, the younger sister of Chancellor Da, was skilled in painting, unparalleled in her artistry. She could weave clouds, dragons, and serpents with colored silk between her fingers, creating works that ranged from a foot in size to a square inch. In the palace, she was called the "Wondrous Weaver." Sun Quan often lamented the unrest in Wei and Shu, and during the intervals of military campaigns, he wished to find a skilled painter to depict landscapes, terrain, and military formations. Chancellor Da then recommended his sister. Sun Quan asked her to depict the forms of the Five Great Mountains, rivers, seas, cities, and military formations. Lady Zhao said, "The colors of ink and paint are easily faded and cannot be treasured for long; I can embroider on the square pieces of cloth of various states, depicting the forms of the Five Mountains, rivers, seas, cities, and military formations." After completing her work, she presented it to the ruler of Wu, and people at the time called her the "Wondrous Embroiderer." Even the intricate needlework of a monkey on a thorn or a cloud ladder flying with a celestial bird could not surpass the beauty of her embroidery. Sun Quan was in Zhaoyang Palace, feeling the heat, and unveiled the purple gauze curtain. Lady Zhao said, "This is not precious." [119]

    ”权使夫人指其意思焉。答曰：“妾欲穷虑尽思，能使下绡帷而清风自入，视外无有蔽碍，列侍者飘然自凉，若驭风而行也。”权称善。夫人乃?片发，以神胶续之。神胶出郁夷国，接弓弩之断弦，百断百续也。乃织为?縠，累月而成，裁为幔，内外视之，飘飘如烟气轻动，而房内自凉。时权常在军旅，每以此幔自随，以为征幕。舒之则广纵一丈，卷之则可纳于枕中，时人谓之“丝绝”。故吴有“三绝”，四海无俦其妙。后有贪宠求媚者，言夫人幻耀于人主，因而致退黜。虽见疑坠，犹存录其巧工。吴亡，不知所在。 [120]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [120]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 權使夫人指其意思焉 - Sun Quan, the ruler of Wu, asked his wife to express her intentions.
    • 妾欲窮慮盡思 - The wife wishes to exhaust her thoughts and consider everything thoroughly.
    • 能使下绡帷而清风自入 - The fabric allows cool air to enter naturally.
    • 視外無有蔽礙 - Looking outside, there is no obstruction.
    • 列侍者飄然自涼 - Those in attendance feel cool and light.
    • 若驭風而行也 - As if riding the wind.
    • 權稱善 - The ruler praised it.
    • 夫人乃?片發，以神膠續之 - The wife then used a lock of hair and divine glue to join it.
    • 接弓弩之斷弦，百斷百續也 - Joining broken bowstrings, it can join a hundred times.
    • 乃織為?縠，累月而成 - Then she wove it into a fine fabric, taking many months to complete.
    • 裁為幔，內外視之，飄飄如煙氣輕動 - Cut it into a curtain, when viewed from inside and outside, it moves lightly like smoke.
    • 而房內自涼 - And the room inside becomes cool.
    • 時權常在軍旅，每以此幔自隨，以為征幕 - At that time, the ruler was often on military campaigns, always carrying this curtain with him, using it as a tent during campaigns.
    • 舒之則廣縱一丈，卷之則可納於枕中 - When unfolded, it is one zhang wide; when rolled up, it can fit in a pillow.
    • 時人謂之“絲絕” - People at the time called it 'silk thread.'
    • 故吳有“三絕”，四海無儔其妙 - Thus Wu had the 'Three Wonders,' no one in the world could match its excellence.
    • 後有貪寵求媚者，言夫人幻耀於人主 - Later, there were those who sought favor by flattering, saying the wife was using magic to dazzle the ruler.
    • 因而致退黜 - As a result, she was demoted.
    • 雖見疑墜，猶存錄其巧工 - Even though she was suspected and demoted, her skill was still recorded.
    • 吳亡，不知所在 - When Wu fell, her whereabouts are unknown.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 權 - Sun Quan, the ruler of Wu during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 夫人 - The wife of Sun Quan, known for her exceptional weaving skills.
    • 神膠 - A mythical substance used for joining materials, originating from the country of Yuyi.
    • 郁夷國 - A mythical country mentioned in ancient Chinese texts.
    • 三絕 - The 'Three Wonders' of Wu, which include the weaving skill of the wife of Sun Quan.
    • 吳亡 - The fall of the state of Wu, marking the end of the Three Kingdoms period.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • 權使夫人指其意思焉 - This phrase is somewhat elliptical, but it clearly indicates that Sun Quan asked his wife to express her intentions.
    • 妾欲窮慮盡思，能使下绡帷而清風自入 - This sentence combines the wife's desire to think thoroughly with the effect of her weaving, which allows cool air to enter naturally.
    • 乃織為?縠，累月而成 - This clause describes the process of weaving the fabric, emphasizing the time and effort involved.
    • 故吳有“三絕”，四海無儔其妙 - This sentence highlights the exceptional nature of the wife's skill, placing it among the 'Three Wonders' of Wu.
    • 後有貪寵求媚者，言夫人幻耀於人主 - This clause introduces a negative element, where flatterers accuse the wife of using magic, leading to her demotion.

    Proper Nouns

    • 權 - Sun Quan, the ruler of Wu.
    • 夫人 - The wife of Sun Quan.
    • 神膠 - A mythical substance.
    • 郁夷國 - A mythical country.
    • 吳 - The state of Wu during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 三絕 - The 'Three Wonders' of Wu.

    During the reign of Sun Quan, he asked his wife to point out her intentions. She replied, "I wish to exhaust my thoughts and consider everything thoroughly, so that the cool breeze can enter on its own through the fine silk curtain. Looking outside, there is no obstruction, and those in attendance feel cool and light, as if riding the wind." Sun Quan praised her. The wife then took a lock of her hair and used divine glue to join it. The divine glue comes from the country of Yuyi and can join broken bowstrings, even a hundred times. She then wove it into a fine fabric, which took many months to complete. She cut it into a curtain, and when viewed from inside and outside, it moved lightly like smoke. The room inside became cool. At that time, Sun Quan was often on military campaigns, and he always carried this curtain with him, using it as a tent during campaigns. When unfolded, it was one zhang wide; when rolled up, it could fit in a pillow. People at the time called it "silk thread." Thus, Wu had the "Three Wonders," and no one in the world could match its excellence. Later, there were those who sought favor by flattering, saying the wife was using magic to dazzle the ruler, as a result, she was demoted. Even though she was suspected and demoted, her skill was still recorded. When Wu fell, her whereabouts are unknown. [120]

    吴主潘夫人，父坐法，夫人输入织室，容态少俦，为江东绝色。同幽者百余人，谓夫人为神女，敬而远之。有司闻于吴主，使图其容貌。夫人忧戚不食，减瘦改形。工人写其真状以进，吴主见而喜悦，以虎魄如意抚按即折。嗟曰：“此神女也，愁貌尚能惑人，况在欢乐！”乃命雕轮就织室，纳于后宫，果以姿色见宠。每以夫人游昭宣之台，志意幸惬，既尽酣醉，唾于玉壶中，使侍婢泻于台下，得火齐指环，即挂石榴枝上，因其处起台，名曰环榴台。时有谏者云：“今吴、蜀争雄，‘还刘’之名，将为妖矣！”权乃翻其名曰榴环台。又与夫人游钓台，得大鱼。王大喜，夫人曰：“昔闻泣鱼，今乃为喜，有喜必忧，以为深戒！”至于末年，渐相谮毁，稍见离退。时人谓“夫人知几其神”。吴主于是罢宴，夫人果见弃逐。钓台基今尚存焉。 [121]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [121]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 坐法 - Because of a law, referring to a legal offense or punishment.
    • 织室 - Weaving room, a place where women were often confined as a form of punishment or labor.
    • 容态少俦 - Her appearance was rarely matched, describing her exceptional beauty.
    • 江东绝色 - The most beautiful woman in the east of the river (Yangtze), emphasizing her status as the epitome of beauty in the region.
    • 敬而远之 - They respected her but kept their distance, indicating a mixture of awe and reverence.
    • 虎魄如意 - A jade如意 (a ceremonial object), '虎魄' is another term for jade, and '如意' is a symbolic object often used in rituals.
    • 抚按即折 - When he touched it, it broke, indicating the fragility of the object and perhaps a metaphor for the lady's delicate nature.
    • 雕轮就织室 - He had a carriage sent to fetch her from the weaving room, indicating the ruler's desire to bring her to his palace.
    • 纳于后宫 - He brought her into the harem, indicating her new status as a concubine.
    • 果以姿色见宠 - She was indeed favored for her beauty, confirming the ruler's affection.
    • 每以夫人游昭宣之台 - Whenever he went to the昭宣台 (a scenic place) with the lady, indicating a place of leisure and pleasure.
    • 志意幸惬 - He was very pleased, indicating his satisfaction and happiness.
    • 尽酣醉 - Drunk to the point of intoxication, indicating a state of extreme pleasure.
    • 唾于玉壶中 - He spat into a jade pot, a symbolic act of affection or possession.
    • 使侍婢泻于台下 - He ordered a servant to pour it at the base of the platform, indicating a ritualistic act.
    • 得火齐指环 - He found a fire gem ring, a valuable and symbolic object.
    • 挂石榴枝上 - He hung it on a pomegranate branch, indicating a specific location and a symbolic gesture.
    • 因其处起台，名曰环榴台 - He built a platform there and named it the Ring Pomegranate Platform, indicating the creation of a new landmark.
    • 时有谏者云 - At that time, someone advised him, indicating a critical voice.
    • 今吴、蜀争雄，‘还刘’之名，将为妖矣！ - Now Wu and Shu are competing for dominance, the name 'Return Liu' will bring disaster! This is a political and superstitious warning.
    • 权乃翻其名曰榴环台 - Sun Quan then changed the name to Pomegranate Ring Platform, indicating his decision to alter the name.
    • 又与夫人游钓台 - He also went fishing with the lady on the fishing platform, indicating another place of leisure.
    • 得大鱼 - He caught a big fish, indicating a successful catch.
    • 王大喜 - The king was very happy, indicating his joy.
    • 夫人曰：‘昔闻泣鱼，今乃为喜，有喜必忧，以为深戒！’ - The lady said, 'I have heard that fish cry, now they are happy, but happiness will bring sorrow, so be deeply cautious!' This is a philosophical and cautionary statement.
    • 至于末年，渐相谮毁，稍见离退 - By the end of his reign, he gradually fell out of favor and was eventually dismissed, indicating a decline in her status.
    • 时人谓‘夫人知几其神’ - People at the time said, 'The lady was divinely perceptive,' indicating her ability to foresee and understand complex situations.
    • 吴主于是罢宴，夫人果见弃逐 - The ruler of Wu then disbanded the banquet, and the lady was indeed dismissed and banished, indicating the final outcome.
    • 钓台基今尚存焉 - The base of the fishing platform still exists today, indicating the historical significance of the location.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 吴主 - The ruler of Wu, referring to Sun Quan, the founder of the state of Wu during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 潘夫人 - Lady Pan, a historical figure known for her beauty and tragic fate.
    • 江东 - East of the river (Yangtze), referring to the region east of the Yangtze River, which was the territory of the state of Wu.
    • 昭宣之台 - The昭宣台, a scenic and luxurious platform, likely a place of leisure and entertainment.
    • 火齐指环 - Fire gem ring, a valuable and symbolic object, possibly representing power or wealth.
    • 环榴台 - Ring Pomegranate Platform, a newly built platform named after the fire gem ring and pomegranate branch.
    • 还刘 - Return Liu, a term that could be interpreted as a political or superstitious reference, possibly related to the name Liu Bei, a rival leader.
    • 钓台 - Fishing platform, another scenic and leisurely location.

    Proper Nouns

    • 吴主 - The ruler of Wu, referring to Sun Quan.
    • 潘夫人 - Lady Pan, a historical figure.

    Lady Pan of Wu, whose father had been punished by law, was sent to a weaving room where she was imprisoned. Her beauty was unparalleled, making her the most beautiful woman in the east of the river (Yangtze). More than a hundred other women were imprisoned with her, and they revered her as a goddess, treating her with respect but keeping their distance. The officials reported her to the ruler of Wu, who ordered her portrait to be drawn. Lady Pan was so distressed that she stopped eating, becoming thinner and changing her appearance. The artists drew her true likeness and presented it to the ruler, who was pleased upon seeing it. He touched a jade如意 (a ceremonial object) with his hand, and it broke. He exclaimed, "She is a goddess, even her sad appearance can bewitch people, how much more so when she is happy!" He then ordered a carriage to fetch her from the weaving room and brought her into the harem, where she was indeed favored for her beauty. Whenever he went to the昭宣台 (a scenic place) with her, he was very pleased, and after drinking to intoxication, he spat into a jade pot. He ordered a servant to pour the contents at the base of the platform, where he found a fire gem ring. He hung it on a pomegranate branch and built a platform there, naming it the Ring Pomegranate Platform. At that time, someone advised him, "Now Wu and Shu are competing for dominance, the name 'Return Liu' will bring disaster!" Sun Quan then changed the name to Pomegranate Ring Platform. He also went fishing with her on the fishing platform and caught a big fish. The king was very happy, and Lady Pan said, "I have heard that fish cry, now they are happy, but happiness will bring sorrow, so be deeply cautious!" By the end of his reign, he gradually fell out of favor and was eventually dismissed. People at the time said, "The lady was divinely perceptive." The ruler of Wu then disbanded the banquet, and the lady was indeed dismissed and banished. The base of the fishing platform still exists today. [121]

    录曰：赵、潘二夫人，妍明伎艺，婉娈通神，抑亦汉游洛妃之俦，荆巫云雨之类；而能避妖幸之嬖，睹进退之机。夫盈则有亏，道有崇替，居盛必衰，理固明矣。语乎荣悴，譬诸草木，华落张弛，势之必然。巧言萋斐，前王之所信惑。是以申、褒见列于前周，班、赵载详于往汉。异代同闻，可为叹也！
    　　黄龙元年，始都武昌。时越巂之南，献背明鸟，形如鹤，止不向明，巢常对北，多肉少毛，声音百变，闻钟磬笙竽之声，则奋翅摇头。时人以为吉祥。是岁迁都建业，殊方多贡珍奇。吴人语讹，呼背明为背亡鸟。国中以为大妖，不及百年，当有丧乱背叛灭亡之事，散逸奔逃，墟无烟火。果如斯言。后此鸟不知所在。 [122]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [122]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 錄曰 - Formal introductory phrase used to begin a commentary or annotation.
    • 妍明伎藝 - Refers to beauty and skill, suggesting a combination of physical attractiveness and artistic talent.
    • 婉娈通神 - Describes someone as charming and almost divine in their appeal.
    • 漢游洛妃之俦 - Refers to the beautiful women of the Han dynasty who were said to have accompanied the emperor on his travels, often associated with the legend of the Luo River Goddess.
    • 荆巫云雨之类 - Refers to the romantic and mystical elements of ancient Chinese folklore, particularly the story of the Chu king and the goddess Xiang.
    • 避妖幸之嬖 - Means to avoid the favor of those who are considered untrustworthy or dangerous.
    • 睹进退之机 - Means to recognize the right moments for advancement or retreat.
    • 盈则有亏 - Philosophical concept suggesting that fullness inevitably leads to loss or deficiency.
    • 道有崇替 - Refers to the rise and fall of principles or ways of thinking.
    • 居盛必衰 - Common saying indicating that prosperity is followed by decline.
    • 华落张弛 - Refers to the natural cycle of growth and decay, often used metaphorically to describe the ebb and flow of life.
    • 巧言萋斐 - Describes flattery or insincere praise that is often deceptive.
    • 申、褒见列于前周 - Refers to the historical figures Shen and Bao, who were famous for their virtuous conduct during the Zhou dynasty.
    • 班、赵载详于往汉 - Refers to the historical figures Ban and Zhao, who were notable figures in the Han dynasty.
    • 异代同闻 - Means that different generations share similar experiences or stories.
    • 黄龙元年 - The first year of the Huanglong era, a period in the history of the Eastern Wu kingdom.
    • 始都武昌 - Indicates the beginning of the capital being established in Wuchang.
    • 越巂之南 - Refers to a region south of Yuezhi, a historical area in southwestern China.
    • 背明鸟 - Mythical bird that avoids light and faces north, symbolizing darkness and death.
    • 形如鹤 - Describes the bird as resembling a crane.
    • 止不向明 - Means the bird does not face the light.
    • 巢常对北 - Indicates that the bird's nest is always oriented towards the north.
    • 多肉少毛 - Describes the bird as having more flesh and less feathers.
    • 声音百变 - Means the bird can make many different sounds.
    • 闻钟磬笙竽之声，则奋翅摇头 - Describes the bird's reaction to music, specifically to the sounds of bells, chimes, flutes, and pipes.
    • 吴人语讹 - Refers to the local dialect of Wu, which was prone to mispronunciation.
    • 背明为背亡鸟 - The bird is called 'back to death' due to its mispronunciation.
    • 国中以为大妖 - The bird is considered a great omen of evil.
    • 不及百年，当有丧乱背叛灭亡之事 - Predicts that within a century, there will be significant turmoil, betrayal, and destruction.
    • 散逸奔逃，墟无烟火 - Describes the aftermath of the predicted events, where people flee and cities become desolate.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 汉游洛妃之俦 - The legend of the Luo River Goddess, who was said to have accompanied the Han emperor on his travels.
    • 荆巫云雨之类 - The story of the Chu king and the goddess Xiang, who engaged in a romantic and mystical encounter.
    • 申、褒见列于前周 - Historical figures from the Zhou dynasty known for their virtuous conduct.
    • 班、赵载详于往汉 - Historical figures from the Han dynasty, possibly referring to Ban Gu and Zhao Feiyan.
    • 黄龙元年 - The first year of the Huanglong era, a significant period in the history of the Eastern Wu kingdom.
    • 始都武昌 - The establishment of Wuchang as the capital of the Eastern Wu kingdom.
    • 越巂之南 - A historical region in southwestern China, known for its contributions to Chinese culture.
    • 背明鸟 - A mythical bird with symbolic significance, often associated with darkness and death.
    • 吴人语讹 - The local dialect of Wu, which was known for its mispronunciations and unique characteristics.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • The sentence '录曰：赵、潘二夫人，妍明伎艺，婉娈通神，抑亦汉游洛妃之俦，荆巫云雨之类' introduces two women, Zhao and Pan, and compares them to legendary figures from the Han and Chu dynasties.
    • The clause '而能避妖幸之嬖，睹进退之机' describes the women's ability to recognize and avoid danger and make wise decisions.
    • The philosophical statement '夫盈则有亏，道有崇替，居盛必衰，理固明矣' discusses the cyclical nature of life and the inevitability of decline after prosperity.
    • The metaphorical statement '语乎荣悴，譬诸草木，华落张弛，势之必然' compares the rise and fall of fortunes to the natural cycles of plants.
    • The clause '巧言萋斐，前王之所信惑' criticizes flattery and insincere praise, which even wise rulers can be deceived by.
    • The historical reference '是以申、褒见列于前周，班、赵载详于往汉' links the virtuous conduct of Shen and Bao in the Zhou dynasty to the notable figures Ban and Zhao in the Han dynasty.
    • The conclusion '异代同闻，可为叹也' emphasizes the universality of certain experiences across different generations.
    • The historical marker '黄龙元年，始都武昌' sets the context for the following narrative.
    • The detailed description '时越巂之南，献背明鸟，形如鹤，止不向明，巢常对北，多肉少毛，声音百变，闻钟磬笙竽之声，则奋翅摇头' describes the mythical bird and its behavior.
    • The initial perception '时人以为吉祥' indicates that the bird was initially seen as a good omen.
    • The relocation '是岁迁都建业，殊方多贡珍奇' describes the move of the capital to Jianye and the arrival of exotic gifts.
    • The mispronunciation '吴人语讹，呼背明为背亡鸟' explains the local dialect's mispronunciation of the bird's name.
    • The prediction '国中以为大妖，不及百年，当有丧乱背叛灭亡之事，散逸奔逃，墟无烟火' foretells future turmoil and destruction based on the bird's symbolism.

    Proper Nouns

    • 赵、潘二夫人 - Two noblewomen named Zhao and Pan.
    • 汉游洛妃之俦 - The beautiful women of the Han dynasty who accompanied the emperor.
    • 荆巫云雨之类 - The romantic and mystical elements of the Chu king and the goddess Xiang.
    • 申、褒 - Historical figures from the Zhou dynasty.
    • 班、赵 - Historical figures from the Han dynasty.
    • 黄龙元年 - The first year of the Huanglong era.
    • 武昌 - The city of Wuchang, which served as the capital of the Eastern Wu kingdom.
    • 越巂之南 - A historical region in southwestern China.
    • 背明鸟 - The mythical bird that avoids light and faces north.
    • 建业 - The city of Jianye, which became the new capital of the Eastern Wu kingdom.
    • 吴人 - People from the Wu region, known for their unique dialect and cultural practices.

    The Record says: The two noblewomen, Zhao and Pan, were renowned for their beauty and skill, charming and almost divine in their grace. They were indeed comparable to the beautiful women of the Han dynasty who accompanied the emperor on his travels, and to the romantic and mystical elements of the Chu king and the goddess Xiang. Yet, they were able to avoid the favor of those who were untrustworthy and recognize the right moments for advancement or retreat. Fullness inevitably leads to loss, principles rise and fall, and prosperity is followed by decline; this is a clear truth. When discussing the rise and fall of fortunes, it is like the natural cycles of plants: the blooming and withering, the stretching and relaxing, are the inevitable trends of nature. Flattery and insincere praise, which even wise rulers can be deceived by, are common. Therefore, the virtuous conduct of Shen and Bao in the Zhou dynasty is recorded, and the notable figures Ban and Zhao in the Han dynasty are detailed. Different generations share similar experiences, and this is indeed a cause for reflection!

    In the first year of the Huanglong era, the capital was established in Wuchang. At that time, from the south of Yuezhi, a mythical bird called the "back to light" bird was presented. It resembled a crane, did not face the light, and always oriented its nest towards the north. It had more flesh and less feathers, and could make many different sounds. When it heard the sounds of bells, chimes, flutes, and pipes, it would flap its wings and shake its head. At the time, people considered it a good omen. That year, the capital was moved to Jianye, and exotic gifts were received from distant lands. The people of Wu, known for their unique dialect, mispronounced "back to light" as "back to death." The bird was considered a great omen of evil, and it was predicted that within a century, there would be significant turmoil, betrayal, and destruction. People would flee, and cities would become desolate, with no signs of life. Indeed, as predicted, the bird disappeared thereafter. [122]

    张承之母孙氏，怀承之时，乘轻舟游于江浦之际，忽有白蛇长三尺，腾入舟中。母祝曰：“若为吉祥，勿毒噬我！”萦而将还，置诸房内，一宿视之，不复见蛇，嗟而惜之。邻中相谓曰：“昨见张家有一白鹤耸翮入云。”以告承母，母使筮之。筮者曰：“此吉祥也。蛇、鹤延年之物；从室入云，自下升高之象也。昔吴王阖闾葬其妹，殉以美女、珍宝、异剑，穷江南之富。未及十年，雕云覆于溪谷，美女游于冢上，白鹄翔于林中，白虎啸于山侧，皆昔时之精灵，今出于世，当使子孙位超臣极，擅名江表。若生子，可以名曰白鹄。”及承生，位至丞相、辅吴将军，年逾九十，蛇、鹄之祥也。 [123]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [123]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 懷承之時 - Refers to the time when Zhang Cheng was conceived.
    • 乘輕舟游于江浦 - Describes traveling by a small boat on a river or stream.
    • 白蛇長三尺 - A white snake three feet long, symbolizing an unusual and significant event.
    • 騰入舟中 - The snake suddenly enters the boat, indicating a sudden and dramatic occurrence.
    • 若為吉祥，勿毒噬我 - A wish that if the snake is a sign of good fortune, it should not harm the person.
    • 繚而將還 - Wrapping the snake and bringing it back.
    • 置諸房内 - Placing the snake inside the room.
    • 一宿視之，不復見蛇 - After one night, the snake was no longer seen.
    • 嗟而惜之 - Expressing regret and sorrow.
    • 鄰中相謂曰 - Neighbors talking to each other.
    • 昨見张家有一白鹤耸翮入云 - Yesterday, someone saw a white crane with raised wings flying into the clouds.
    • 以告承母 - Telling Zhang Cheng's mother about the sighting.
    • 母使筮之 - The mother asks for a divination.
    • 筮者曰 - The diviner says.
    • 此吉祥也 - This is a sign of good fortune.
    • 蛇、鹤延年之物 - Snakes and cranes are symbols of longevity.
    • 从室入云，自下升高之象也 - From the room to the clouds, a symbol of rising from low to high.
    • 昔吴王阖闾葬其妹 - In ancient times, King Helu of Wu buried his sister.
    • 殉以美女、珍宝、异剑 - Accompanied by beautiful women, precious treasures, and unusual swords.
    • 穷江南之富 - Exhausting the wealth of southern Jiang.
    • 未及十年，雕云覆于溪谷 - Before ten years had passed, carved clouds covered the ravines.
    • 美女游于冢上 - Beautiful women roamed above the tomb.
    • 白鹄翔于林中 - White cranes flew in the forest.
    • 白虎啸于山侧 - White tigers roared on the mountainside.
    • 皆昔时之精灵，今出于世 - All were spirits of the past, now appearing in the world.
    • 当使子孙位超臣极，擅名江表 - This will make your descendants surpass the highest ranks of officials and gain fame in the Jiang region.
    • 若生子，可以名曰白鹄 - If you have a son, you can name him Baihu (White Crane).
    • 及承生，位至丞相、辅吴将军，年逾九十 - When Zhang Cheng was born, he became Chancellor and General of Wu, living past ninety years old.
    • 蛇、鹄之祥也 - This is the omen of the snake and the crane.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 张承之母孙氏 - Zhang Cheng's mother, Sun, who experienced the unusual events.
    • 吴王阖闾 - King Helu of Wu, a historical figure known for his military prowess and the burial of his sister.
    • 江南 - Southern Jiang, referring to the southern part of China, particularly the Yangtze River basin.
    • 雕云覆于溪谷 - Carved clouds covering the ravines, a symbolic representation of the supernatural and the afterlife.
    • 白鹄翔于林中 - White cranes flying in the forest, symbolizing longevity and good fortune.
    • 白虎啸于山侧 - White tigers roaring on the mountainside, representing power and protection.
    • 丞相、辅吴将军 - High-ranking positions in ancient Chinese government, indicating significant political and military authority.
    • 江表 - Refers to the region south of the Yangtze River, often used to describe the area under the control of the Wu Kingdom during the Three Kingdoms period.

    Proper Nouns

    • 张承 - Zhang Cheng, the person whose birth is predicted.
    • 吴王阖闾 - King Helu of Wu, a historical figure.
    • 江南 - Southern Jiang, a geographical region.
    • 雕云 - Carved clouds, a symbolic representation.
    • 白鹄 - White crane, a symbol of longevity and good fortune.
    • 白虎 - White tiger, a symbol of power and protection.
    • 丞相 - Chancellor, a high-ranking government position.
    • 辅吴将军 - General of Wu, a high-ranking military position.
    • 江表 - The region south of the Yangtze River, often used to describe the area under the control of the Wu Kingdom during the Three Kingdoms period.

    Zhang Cheng's mother, Sun, was traveling by a small boat on a river when she was pregnant with him. Suddenly, a white snake three feet long leaped into the boat. The mother prayed, "If this is a sign of good fortune, do not harm me!" She wrapped the snake and brought it back, placing it in the room. After one night, the snake was no longer seen, and she lamented its loss. The neighbors talked to each other, saying, "Yesterday, we saw a white crane with raised wings flying into the clouds at Zhang's house." They told this to Zhang Cheng's mother, who asked a diviner to interpret the sign. The diviner said, "This is a sign of good fortune. Snakes and cranes are symbols of longevity. From the room to the clouds, it symbolizes rising from low to high. In ancient times, King Helu of Wu buried his sister, accompanied by beautiful women, precious treasures, and unusual swords, exhausting the wealth of southern Jiang. Before ten years had passed, carved clouds covered the ravines, beautiful women roamed above the tomb, white cranes flew in the forest, and white tigers roared on the mountainside. All were spirits of the past, now appearing in the world. This will make your descendants surpass the highest ranks of officials and gain fame in the Jiang region. If you have a son, you can name him Baihu (White Crane)." When Zhang Cheng was born, he became Chancellor and General of Wu, living past ninety years old, fulfilling the omens of the snake and the crane. [123]

    录曰：国之将亡，其兆先见。《传》曰：“明神见之，观其德也。”及归命面缚来降，斯为效矣。蛇、鹄者，虫禽之最灵，张氏以为嘉瑞。《吴越春秋》、百家杂说云，吴王阖闾，崇饰厚葬，生埋美人，多藏宝物。数百年后，灵鹄翔于林壑，神虎啸于山丘，湛卢之剑，飞入于楚。收魂聚怪，富丽以极，而诡异失中，不如速朽。昔宋桓、盛姬，前史讥其骄惑，嬴博杨孙，君子贵其合礼。观夫远古，指详中代，求诸事迹，俭泰相悬。至如末世，渐相夸矫，生滋淫湎，死则同殉，委积珍宝，埃尘灭身，乖于同穴，可谓叹欤！
    　　吕蒙入吴，吴主劝其学业，蒙乃博览群籍，以《易》为宗。常在孙策座上酣醉，忽卧，于梦中诵《周易》一部，俄而惊起。众人皆问之。蒙曰：“向梦见伏牺、周公、文王，与我论世祚兴亡之事，日月贞明之道，莫不穷精极妙。未该玄旨，故空诵其文耳。”众座皆云：“吕蒙呓语通《周易》。” [124]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [124]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 國之將亡，其兆先見 - Classical expression meaning 'when a state is about to fall, its signs are seen beforehand.'
    • 明神見之，觀其德也 - Means 'the divine sees it, observing its virtue,' referring to the idea that the gods judge a state's fate based on its moral conduct.
    • 歸命面缚来降 - Literally 'surrendering with bound hands,' a gesture of complete submission, often used in historical contexts of defeat or capitulation.
    • 蛇、鹄者，虫禽之最灵 - Refers to snakes and cranes as the most intelligent creatures among animals, often used in Chinese culture to symbolize wisdom and longevity.
    • 嘉瑞 - Refers to auspicious signs or omens, often seen as indicators of good fortune or divine favor.
    • 吳王阖闾，崇饰厚葬 - Refers to King Helu of Wu, known for his elaborate and extravagant burials.
    • 生埋美人，多藏宝物 - Describes the practice of burying live concubines and hoarding treasures with the deceased, a common but controversial practice in ancient China.
    • 灵鹄翔于林壑，神虎啸于山丘 - Metaphorical expressions describing the spirits of a deceased person manifesting in nature, often seen as a sign of the person's continued influence or presence.
    • 湛卢之剑，飞入于楚 - Refers to the famous sword Zhanlu, which is said to have flown to the state of Chu, symbolizing the transfer of power or the loss of a great artifact.
    • 收魂聚怪，富丽以极 - Describes the accumulation of spirits and strange phenomena, often associated with the opulence and excess of a ruler's burial.
    • 诡异失中 - Means 'strange and out of balance,' suggesting that the practices described are excessive and inappropriate.
    • 速朽 - Means 'to decay quickly,' implying that such practices should be avoided to prevent the rapid deterioration of the deceased's legacy.
    • 宋桓、盛姬，前史讥其骄惑 - Refers to Duke Huan of Song and Lady Sheng, who were criticized in history for their arrogance and confusion.
    • 嬴博杨孙，君子贵其合礼 - Refers to the descendants of Ying Bo and Yang Sun, who were praised for adhering to proper rites and customs.
    • 远古，指详中代 - Means 'looking back to ancient times, pointing to detailed accounts of the middle ages,' suggesting a contrast between past and present practices.
    • 求诸事迹，俭泰相悬 - Means 'seeking through historical records, the difference between frugality and extravagance is vast,' highlighting the disparity between different eras.
    • 末世，渐相夸矫 - Refers to the late period of a dynasty, where practices become increasingly showy and artificial.
    • 生滋淫湎，死则同殉 - Describes the excessive indulgence in life and the practice of human sacrifice in death, both of which are condemned.
    • 委积珍宝，埃尘灭身 - Means 'accumulating precious treasures, only to be buried in dust and forgotten,' criticizing the futility of such practices.
    • 乖于同穴，可谓叹欤 - Means 'to be separated in death, how sad it is,' expressing the sorrow of not being buried together.
    • 吕蒙入吴，吴主劝其学业 - Refers to the historical event where Lu Meng, a general of the Wu Kingdom, was encouraged by the ruler to pursue education.
    • 蒙乃博览群籍，以《易》为宗 - Means 'Lu Meng then extensively read many books, taking the *I Ching* (Book of Changes) as his main focus.'
    • 常在孙策座上酣醉，忽卧，于梦中诵《周易》一部 - Describes a scene where Lu Meng, while drunk, falls asleep and dreams of reciting the entire *I Ching*.
    • 俄而惊起 - Means 'suddenly wakes up,' indicating a sudden awakening from the dream.
    • 吕蒙呓语通《周易》 - Means 'Lu Meng's mutterings were understood as the *I Ching*,' suggesting that his dream recitation was so profound that it was perceived as a deep understanding of the *I Ching*.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 國之將亡，其兆先見 - A common theme in Chinese historiography, emphasizing the importance of moral and political conduct in the rise and fall of states.
    • 明神見之，觀其德也 - Reflects the belief in divine judgment based on virtue, a central tenet in Confucian and Taoist thought.
    • 歸命面缚来降 - A gesture of complete submission, often seen in historical accounts of military defeats or political surrenders.
    • 蛇、鹄者，虫禽之最灵 - Snakes and cranes are often used in Chinese culture to symbolize wisdom, longevity, and spiritual purity.
    • 嘉瑞 - Auspicious signs or omens, often seen as indicators of good fortune or divine favor.
    • 吳王阖闾，崇饰厚葬 - King Helu of Wu is known for his extravagant burials, which often included live concubines and valuable treasures.
    • 灵鹄翔于林壑，神虎啸于山丘 - These metaphors describe the spirits of the deceased manifesting in nature, often seen as a sign of the person's continued influence.
    • 湛卢之剑，飞入于楚 - The famous sword Zhanlu, which is said to have flown to the state of Chu, symbolizes the transfer of power or the loss of a great artifact.
    • 收魂聚怪，富丽以极 - The accumulation of spirits and strange phenomena, often associated with the opulence and excess of a ruler's burial.
    • 诡异失中 - Excessive and inappropriate practices, often seen as a sign of moral decay.
    • 速朽 - The idea that such practices should be avoided to prevent the rapid deterioration of the deceased's legacy.
    • 宋桓、盛姬，前史讥其骄惑 - Duke Huan of Song and Lady Sheng were criticized for their arrogance and confusion, reflecting the importance of humility and clarity in leadership.
    • 嬴博杨孙，君子贵其合礼 - The descendants of Ying Bo and Yang Sun were praised for adhering to proper rites and customs, emphasizing the importance of ritual in Chinese culture.
    • 远古，指详中代 - A contrast between ancient and more recent historical periods, often used to highlight changes in social and cultural practices.
    • 求诸事迹，俭泰相悬 - The vast difference between frugality and extravagance, often used to critique the excesses of the late periods of dynasties.
    • 末世，渐相夸矫 - The late period of a dynasty, where practices become increasingly showy and artificial, often leading to the dynasty's decline.
    • 生滋淫湎，死则同殉 - Excessive indulgence in life and the practice of human sacrifice in death, both of which are condemned in Chinese moral and religious thought.
    • 委积珍宝，埃尘灭身 - The futility of accumulating treasures, often seen as a metaphor for the transient nature of material wealth.
    • 乖于同穴，可谓叹欤 - The sorrow of not being buried together, reflecting the importance of family and continuity in Chinese culture.
    • 吕蒙入吴，吴主劝其学业 - A historical event where Lu Meng, a general of the Wu Kingdom, was encouraged by the ruler to pursue education, highlighting the value of learning and self-improvement.
    • 蒙乃博览群籍，以《易》为宗 - Lu Meng's extensive reading and focus on the *I Ching*, reflecting the importance of the *I Ching* in Chinese philosophy and literature.
    • 常在孙策座上酣醉，忽卧，于梦中诵《周易》一部 - A scene where Lu Meng, while drunk, falls asleep and dreams of reciting the entire *I Ching*, suggesting a deep and intuitive understanding of the text.
    • 俄而惊起 - A sudden awakening from the dream, indicating a moment of profound insight.
    • 吕蒙呓语通《周易》 - Lu Meng's mutterings were understood as the *I Ching*, suggesting that his dream recitation was so profound that it was perceived as a deep understanding of the *I Ching*.

    The Record says: When a state is about to fall, its signs are seen beforehand. The Classic states: "The divine sees it, observing its virtue." When one surrenders with bound hands, this is the effect. Snakes and cranes, the most intelligent among animals, are considered auspicious signs by the Zhang clan. According to The Spring and Autumn Annals of Wu and Yue and various sayings, King Helu of Wu, with his elaborate and extravagant burials, buried live concubines and hoarded many treasures. Hundreds of years later, a spirit crane soars in the forest and valleys, and a divine tiger roars on the hills. The sword Zhanlu flew into the state of Chu. The accumulation of spirits and strange phenomena, reaching the height of opulence, yet being strange and out of balance, is not as good as decaying quickly. In the past, Duke Huan of Song and Lady Sheng were criticized by historians for their arrogance and confusion, while the descendants of Ying Bo and Yang Sun were praised by the virtuous for their adherence to proper rites. Observing ancient times, pointing to detailed accounts of the middle ages, seeking through historical records, the difference between frugality and extravagance is vast. As for the late period of a dynasty, practices become increasingly showy and artificial. Excessive indulgence in life and the practice of human sacrifice in death, accumulating precious treasures, only to be buried in dust and forgotten, being separated in death, how sad it is!

    Lu Meng entered Wu, and the ruler of Wu encouraged his studies. Lu Meng then extensively read many books, taking the I Ching as his main focus. He often drank to excess at Sun Ce's table, suddenly fell asleep, and in his dream recited the entire I Ching. He suddenly woke up. Everyone asked him about it. Lu Meng said: "I dreamed of Fu Xi, Duke of Zhou, and King Wen, who discussed with me the rise and fall of the world and the principles of the sun and moon, none of which were not thoroughly profound and exquisite. Not fully grasping the profound meaning, I merely recited the text." Everyone present said: "Lu Meng's mutterings were understood as the I Ching." [124]

    录曰：夫精诚之至，叶于幽冥，与日月均其明，与四时齐其契，故能德会三古，道合神微。若郑君之感先圣，周盘之梦东里，迹同事异，光被遐策，索隐钩深，妙于玄旨。孔门群说，未若吕生之学焉。
    　　孙和悦邓夫人，常置膝上。和于月下舞水精如意，误伤夫人颊，血流污裤，娇奼弥苦。自舐其疮，命太医合药。医曰：“得白獭髓，杂玉与琥珀屑，当灭此痕。”即购致百金，能得白獭髓者，厚赏之。有富春渔人云：“此物知人欲取，则逃入石穴。伺其祭鱼之时，獭有斗死者，穴中应有枯骨，虽无髓，其骨可合玉舂为粉，喷于疮上，其痕则灭。”和乃命合此膏，琥珀太多，及差而有赤点如朱，逼而视之，更益其妍。诸嬖人欲要宠，皆以丹脂点颊而后进幸。妖惑相动，遂成淫俗。 [125]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [125]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 夫精誠之至 - Classical phrase meaning 'when sincerity reaches its utmost,' used to introduce the main argument.
    • 葉於幽冥 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'to harmonize with the unseen or mysterious,' suggesting a deep connection with the spiritual or metaphysical.
    • 與日月均其明，與四時齊其契 - Parallel structure meaning 'to match the brightness of the sun and moon, to align with the cycles of the four seasons,' emphasizing harmony and balance.
    • 德會三古，道合神微 - Means 'virtue converges with the wisdom of the past, the way aligns with divine subtlety,' suggesting a profound understanding of ancient wisdom and the divine.
    • 若鄭君之感先聖，周盤之夢東里 - Refers to historical anecdotes where individuals were inspired by sages or had prophetic dreams.
    • 跡同事異，光被遐策 - Means 'the actions are the same but the circumstances differ, the glory extends far and wide,' suggesting that similar actions can have different outcomes.
    • 索隱钩深，妙于玄旨 - Means 'to explore the obscure and delve into the profound, to grasp the subtle meanings,' indicating a deep and nuanced understanding.
    • 孔门群说，未若吕生之学焉 - Means 'the various teachings of Confucius' school are not as profound as the learning of Master Lu,' suggesting that Master Lu's teachings surpass those of Confucius.
    • 和于月下舞水精如意 - Means 'Sun He dances with a jade如意 (a ceremonial object) under the moonlight,' suggesting a graceful and elegant action.
    • 误伤夫人颊，血流污裤，娇奼弥苦 - Means 'he accidentally injures her cheek, causing blood to flow and stain her clothes, making her more distressed,' describing a tragic and emotional scene.
    • 自舐其疮，命太医合药 - Means 'she licks her wound herself and orders the imperial physician to prepare medicine,' indicating her resilience and the high regard for medical treatment.
    • 得白獭髓，杂玉与琥珀屑，当灭此痕 - Means 'if you can get the marrow of a white otter, mixed with jade and amber powder, it will remove the mark,' suggesting a magical or rare remedy.
    • 即购致百金，能得白獭髓者，厚赏之 - Means 'he immediately offers a hundred gold pieces as a reward for anyone who can obtain the otter's marrow,' indicating a significant reward.
    • 有富春渔人云 - Means 'a fisherman from Fuchun says,' introducing a witness or informant.
    • 此物知人欲取，则逃入石穴 - Means 'this creature knows when someone wants to take it and hides in a stone cave,' suggesting the creature's intelligence.
    • 伺其祭鱼之时，獭有斗死者，穴中应有枯骨，虽无髓，其骨可合玉舂为粉，喷于疮上，其痕则灭 - Means 'wait until the time when the otter is sacrificed, and if one dies in a fight, there should be dry bones in the cave. Even without marrow, the bones can be ground into powder with jade and sprinkled on the wound, which will remove the mark,' suggesting a detailed and practical method.
    • 和乃命合此膏，琥珀太多，及差而有赤点如朱，逼而视之，更益其妍 - Means 'Sun He then ordered the preparation of this ointment, but there was too much amber, and when it was applied, it left red spots like cinnabar. When viewed closely, it became even more beautiful,' suggesting an unexpected but positive outcome.
    • 诸嬖人欲要宠，皆以丹脂点颊而后进幸 - Means 'various concubines wanted to gain favor and all used cinnabar to paint their cheeks before gaining his attention,' indicating a trend or custom.
    • 妖惑相动，遂成淫俗 - Means 'they influenced each other in a seductive way, leading to a decadent custom,' suggesting a moral decline.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 鄭君之感先聖 - Refers to a historical figure named Zheng who was inspired by a sage.
    • 周盤之夢東里 - Refers to a historical figure named Zhou Pan who had a prophetic dream.
    • 孫和 - A historical figure, possibly a prince or nobleman.
    • 鄧夫人 - A historical figure, possibly a concubine or noblewoman.
    • 水精如意 - A ceremonial object made of jade, often used in rituals or as a symbol of status.
    • 白獭髓 - A rare and valuable substance, believed to have medicinal properties.
    • 富春 - A place name, possibly referring to a region known for its natural resources or cultural significance.
    • 丹脂 - A red pigment, often used for makeup or decoration.

    Proper Nouns

    • 鄭君 - A historical figure.
    • 周盤 - A historical figure.
    • 孫和 - A historical figure.
    • 鄧夫人 - A historical figure.
    • 水精如意 - A ceremonial object.
    • 白獭 - A type of otter.
    • 富春 - A place name.
    • 丹脂 - A red pigment.

    The Record states: When sincerity reaches its utmost, it harmonizes with the unseen, matching the brightness of the sun and moon, aligning with the cycles of the four seasons, thus able to converge virtue with the wisdom of the past and align the way with divine subtlety. If Zheng was inspired by a sage, and Zhou Pan had a prophetic dream in Dongli, their actions were the same but the circumstances differed, their glory extending far and wide. They explored the obscure and delved into the profound, grasping the subtle meanings. The various teachings of Confucius' school are not as profound as the learning of Master Lu.

    Sun He was fond of Deng, often placing her on his knees. Under the moonlight, he danced with a jade如意, accidentally injuring her cheek, causing blood to flow and stain her clothes, making her more distressed. She licked her wound herself and ordered the imperial physician to prepare medicine. The physician said, "If you can get the marrow of a white otter, mixed with jade and amber powder, it will remove the mark." He immediately offered a hundred gold pieces as a reward for anyone who could obtain the otter's marrow. A fisherman from Fuchun said, "This creature knows when someone wants to take it and hides in a stone cave. Wait until the time when the otter is sacrificed, and if one dies in a fight, there should be dry bones in the cave. Even without marrow, the bones can be ground into powder with jade and sprinkled on the wound, which will remove the mark." Sun He then ordered the preparation of this ointment, but there was too much amber, and when it was applied, it left red spots like cinnabar. When viewed closely, it became even more beautiful. Various concubines wanted to gain favor and all used cinnabar to paint their cheeks before gaining his attention. They influenced each other in a seductive way, leading to a decadent custom. [125]

    孙亮作琉璃屏风，甚薄而莹澈，每于月下清夜舒之。常与爱姬四人，皆振古绝色：一名朝姝，二名丽居，三名洛珍，四名洁华。使四人坐屏风内，而外望之，如无隔，惟香气不通于外。为四人合四气香，殊方异国所出，凡经践蹑宴息之处，香气沾衣，历年弥盛，百浣不歇，因名曰“百濯香”。或以人名香，故有朝姝香，丽居香，洛珍香，洁华香。亮每游，此四人皆同舆席，来侍皆以香名前后为次，不得乱之。所居室名为“思香媚寝”。 [126]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [126]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 琉璃屏風 - Translucent or transparent screen made of a material similar to glass, used in ancient Chinese architecture for decorative and functional purposes.
    • 振古絕色 - Means 'of unparalleled beauty from ancient times,' describing the extraordinary beauty of the four women.
    • 百濯香 - A type of perfume that retains its fragrance even after many washes, symbolizing enduring beauty and allure.
    • 思香媚寝 - A room designed for romantic or intimate activities, emphasizing the luxurious and sensual nature of the space.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 孫亮 - Emperor Sun Liang of the Eastern Wu state during the Three Kingdoms period, known for his luxurious lifestyle and artistic tastes.
    • 四人合四气香 - Refers to the practice of creating perfumes from exotic and rare ingredients, reflecting the opulence and sophistication of the imperial court.
    • 百濯香 - Highlights the advanced knowledge of perfumery in ancient China, where perfumes were not only used for personal adornment but also for their symbolic and therapeutic properties.
    • 思香媚寝 - The name of the room suggests a space dedicated to romantic and sensual pleasures, which was a common theme in ancient Chinese literature and art.

    Proper Nouns

    • 孙亮 - Emperor Sun Liang of Eastern Wu.
    • 朝姝、丽居、洛珍、洁华 - The names of the four women, each of which is associated with a specific type of perfume.

    Emperor Sun Liang created a translucent screen, which was very thin and clear. He often unfolded it during clear moonlit nights. He had four favorite concubines, all of unparalleled beauty from ancient times: one named Chao Shu, another named Li Ju, the third named Luo Zhen, and the fourth named Jie Hua. He made four perfumes for them, each from exotic and rare ingredients. These perfumes retained their fragrance even after many washes, and thus were called "hundred-wash perfumes." Sometimes, people named the perfumes after the women, so there were Chao Shu Perfume, Li Ju Perfume, Luo Zhen Perfume, and Jie Hua Perfume. Whenever Emperor Sun Liang went out, these four women accompanied him, always in the order of their perfumes' names, without ever changing. The room where they stayed was called "Thoughtful Fragrant Boudoir," a place designed for romantic and intimate activities. [126]

    蜀

    　　先主甘后，沛人也，生于微贱。里中相者云：“此女后贵，位极宫掖。”及后长，而体貌特异，至十八，玉质柔肌，态媚容冶。先主召入绡帐中，于户外望者，如月下聚雪。河南献玉人，高三尺，乃取玉人置后侧，昼则讲说军谋，夕则拥后而玩玉人。常称玉之所贵，德比君子，况为人形，而不可玩乎？后与玉人洁白齐润，观者殆相乱惑。嬖宠者非惟嫉于甘后，亦妒于玉人也。后常欲琢毁坏之，乃诫先主曰：“昔子罕不以玉为宝，《春秋》美之；今吴、魏未灭，安以妖玩经怀。凡淫惑生疑，勿复进焉！”先主乃撤玉人，嬖者皆退。当斯之时，君子议以甘后为神智妇人焉。 [127]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [127]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 生于微贱 - Born in humble circumstances, emphasizing her lowly origins.
    • 昼则讲说军谋 - Discussing military strategies during the day.
    • 夕则拥后而玩玉人 - Embracing her and playing with the jade figure at night.
    • 嬖宠者非惟嫉于甘后，亦妒于玉人也 - The favored concubines not only envied Gansu but also the jade figure.
    • 凡淫惑生疑，勿复进焉 - To indulge in such temptations will breed doubt; do not indulge further.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 蜀 - Historical region of Shu, later the state of Shu Han during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 先主 - Liu Bei, the founder of Shu Han.
    • 甘后 - Gansu, the wife of Liu Bei.
    • 河南 - Henan, a province in China.
    • 子罕 - A historical figure from the Spring and Autumn period known for his integrity.
    • 吴、魏 - Wu and Wei, the other two major states during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 春秋 - The *Spring and Autumn Annals*, a historical text from the Spring and Autumn period.

    Proper Nouns

    • 蜀 - Historical region of Shu.
    • 先主 - Liu Bei.
    • 甘后 - Gansu.
    • 河南 - Henan.
    • 子罕 - A historical figure from the Spring and Autumn period.
    • 吴 - The state of Wu.
    • 魏 - The state of Wei.
    • 春秋 - The *Spring and Autumn Annals*.

    Shu

    Gansu, the wife of Liu Bei, was born in humble circumstances. A village diviner once said, "This girl will be honored, reaching the highest position in the palace." When Gansu grew up, her appearance was particularly unusual, and at the age of eighteen, her skin was as soft and smooth as jade, and her demeanor was charming and beautiful. Liu Bei summoned her into a silk tent, and those who looked at her from outside saw her as if she were a gathering of snow under the moon. A jade figure, three feet tall, was presented from Henan, and Liu Bei placed it beside Gansu. During the day, he would discuss military strategies with her, and at night, he would embrace her and play with the jade figure. He often said that jade is precious because its virtue is like that of a gentleman, and how much more so for a human figure that cannot be played with? Gansu and the jade figure were equally white and smooth, and those who saw them were often confused as to which one was which. The favored concubines not only envied Gansu but also the jade figure. Gansu once intended to destroy the jade figure and warned Liu Bei, saying, "In the past, Zi Han did not value jade as a treasure, and the Spring and Autumn Annals praised him for this. With Wu and Wei still not defeated, how can one indulge in frivolous playthings? To indulge in such temptations will breed doubt; do not indulge further!" Liu Bei then removed the jade figure, and the favored concubines were all dismissed. At that time, the gentlemen discussed that Gansu was a wise and intelligent woman. [127]

    糜竺用陶朱计术，日益亿万之利，货拟王家，有宝库千间。竺性能赈生恤死，家内马厩屋仄有古冢，中有伏尸，夜闻涕泣声。竺乃寻其泣声之处，忽见一妇人袒背而来，诉云：“昔汉末妾为赤眉所害，叩棺见剥，今袒在地，羞昼见人，垂二百年。今就将军乞深埋，并弊衣以掩形体。”竺许之，即命之为棺椁，以青布为衣衫，置于冢中，设祭既毕。历一年，行于路曲，忽见前妇人，所著衣皆是青布，语竺曰：“君财宝可支一世，合遭火厄，今以青芦杖一枚长九尺，报君棺椁衣服之惠。”竺挟杖而归。所住邻中常见竺家有青气如龙蛇之形。或有人谓竺曰：“将非怪也？”竺乃疑此异，问其家僮。云：“时见青芦杖自出门间，疑其神，不敢言也。 [128]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [128]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 日益亿万之利 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'increased daily by billions in profit,' indicating significant wealth growth.
    • 性能赈生恤死 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'had a nature to provide relief to the living and comfort the dead,' indicating charitable nature.
    • 垂二百年 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'for nearly two hundred years,' indicating a long period of time.
    • 青气如龙蛇之形 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'green vapor in the shape of dragons and snakes,' describing a supernatural phenomenon.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 糜竺 - Historical figure, a wealthy and influential person during the late Eastern Han period.
    • 陶朱 - Tao Zhu, a historical figure known for his business acumen and wealth.
    • 赤眉 - A rebel group during the late Eastern Han period.
    • 青芦杖 - A magical object that appears in the story, symbolizing divine intervention or supernatural power.

    Proper Nouns

    • 糜竺 - Mí Zhú, a historical figure.
    • 陶朱 - Tao Zhu, a historical figure.
    • 赤眉 - A rebel group during the late Eastern Han period.
    • 青芦杖 - A magical object.

    Mí Zhú used the strategies of Tao Zhu, and his wealth increased daily by billions, comparable to that of a royal family, with a thousand treasure houses. Mí Zhú had a nature to provide relief to the living and comfort the dead. In his courtyard, there was an ancient tomb within the stable, containing a corpse. At night, he heard the sound of weeping. Mí Zhú then sought out the source of the weeping and suddenly saw a woman with her back exposed, approaching him. She said, "In the late Han dynasty, I was killed by the Red Eyebrows, and my coffin was stripped. Now I lie exposed on the ground, ashamed to be seen by others during the day, for nearly two hundred years. Now I beg you, General, to bury me deeply and provide me with old clothes to cover my body." Mí Zhú agreed, immediately ordering a coffin to be made and old clothes to be prepared, placing her in the tomb and setting up a sacrifice. After a year, while walking along a winding path, he suddenly saw the same woman again, dressed entirely in blue cloth.
