She said to Mí Zhú, "Your wealth can support you for a lifetime, but you are destined to suffer from a fire disaster. Now I give you a nine-foot-long piece of green reed as a token of gratitude for your kindness in burying me and providing me with clothes." Mí Zhú took the staff and returned home. In the neighborhood where he lived, people often saw green vapor in the shape of dragons and snakes around his house. Someone asked Mí Zhú, "Is this not a supernatural occurrence?" Mí Zhú then began to suspect this strangeness and asked his servants. They replied, "We often see the green reed staff appearing and disappearing outside the gate, and we suspect it to be a divine object, but we dare not speak of it." [128]

    ”竺为性多忌，信厌术之事，有言中忤，即加刑戮，故家僮不敢言。笁货财如山，不可算计，内以方诸盆瓶，设大珠如卵，散满于庭，谓之“宝庭”，而外人不得窥。数日，忽青衣童子数十人来云：“糜竺家当有火厄，万不遗一，赖君能恤敛枯骨，天道不辜君德，故来禳却此火，当使财物不尽。自今以后，亦宜防卫！”竺乃掘沟渠周绕其库。旬日，火从库内起，烧其珠玉十分之一，皆是阳燧旱燥自能烧物。火盛之时，见数十青衣童子来扑火，有青气如云，覆于火上，即灭。童子又云：“多聚鹳鸟之类，以禳火灾；鹳能聚水于巢上也。”家人乃收鵁鶄数千头养于池渠中，以厌火。竺叹曰：“人生财运有限，不得盈溢，惧为身之患害。”时三国交锋，军用万倍，乃输其宝物车服，以助先主：黄金一亿斤，锦绣毡罽积如丘垄，骏马万匹。及蜀破后，无复所有，饮恨而终。 [129]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [129]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 為性多忌 - Means 'of a nature prone to suspicion or jealousy,' indicating a tendency to be wary or mistrustful.
    • 信厭術之事 - Means 'believes in occult or superstitious practices,' referring to the belief in magic or fortune-telling.
    • 有言中忤，即加刑戮 - Means 'if anyone speaks against him, he will punish them severely,' indicating a harsh response to criticism.
    • 家僮不敢言 - Means 'servants dare not speak,' reflecting the fear and silence among servants due to the master's harshness.
    • 笁貨財如山，不可算計 - Means 'his wealth is like a mountain, impossible to count,' emphasizing the vastness of his fortune.
    • 內以方諸盆瓶，設大珠如卵，散滿於庭，謂之“寶庭” - Means 'inside, he uses square vessels and bottles to display large pearls as big as eggs, scattered throughout the courtyard, calling it the 'Treasure Courtyard,' describing a luxurious display of wealth.
    • 外人不得窺 - Means 'no one outside can see,' indicating the exclusivity and secrecy of the Treasure Courtyard.
    • 數日，忽青衣童子數十人來云 - Means 'a few days later, dozens of young boys dressed in green suddenly appeared and said,' introducing a supernatural element.
    • 糜竺家當有火厄，萬不遺一 - Means 'there will be a fire disaster at Mi Zhu's house, not leaving a single thing intact,' predicting a catastrophic event.
    • 賴君能恤斂枯骨，天道不辜君德，故來禳卻此火 - Means 'thanks to your compassion and care for the dead, the heavenly way does not wrong your virtue, so we come to ward off this fire,' explaining the reason for the appearance of the supernatural beings.
    • 當使財物不盡 - Means 'to ensure your wealth is not entirely lost,' indicating the purpose of the intervention.
    • 自今以後，亦宜防衛 - Means 'from now on, you should also be vigilant,' advising Mi Zhu to take precautions.
    • 旬日，火從庫內起，燒其珠玉十分之一 - Means 'ten days later, a fire broke out in the warehouse, burning a tenth of the pearls and jade,' describing the actual disaster.
    • 皆是陽燧旱燥自能燒物 - Means 'all of it was caused by the sun's reflection and dryness, which naturally burns things,' explaining the cause of the fire.
    • 火盛之時，見數十青衣童子來撲火 - Means 'when the fire was at its peak, dozens of young boys dressed in green came to put it out,' describing the supernatural intervention.
    • 有青氣如雲，覆於火上，即滅 - Means 'a green mist like a cloud covered the fire, and it immediately went out,' describing the effect of the intervention.
    • 童子又云：‘多聚鵁鶄之類，以禳火災；鵁鶄能聚水於巢上也。’ - Means 'the young boys said, 'Collect many cranes and other birds to ward off fires; cranes can gather water in their nests,' providing advice on how to prevent future disasters.
    • 家人乃收鵁鶄數千頭養於池渠中，以厭火 - Means 'the family then collected thousands of cranes and kept them in ponds and channels to ward off fires,' describing the family's response to the advice.
    • 竺嘆曰：‘人生財運有限，不得盈溢，懼為身之患害。’ - Means 'Mi Zhu sighed, 'Human life and wealth have limits; one should not be overly greedy, as it can become a burden and harm oneself,' expressing his reflection on the disaster.
    • 時三國交鋒，軍用萬倍，乃輸其寶物車服，以助先主 - Means 'at the time when the three kingdoms were in conflict, military expenses were ten times higher, so he donated his treasures and carriages to assist Lord Liu Bei,' describing his contribution to the war effort.
    • 黃金一億斤，錦繡氈罽積如丘壟，駿馬萬匹 - Means 'one billion pounds of gold, silk and brocade piled up like hills, and ten thousand fine horses,' listing the specific items donated.
    • 及蜀破後，無復所有，飲恨而終 - Means 'after the fall of Shu, he had nothing left and died with regret,' describing the aftermath of the war and his death.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 糜竺 - Refers to Mi Zhu, a prominent figure during the Three Kingdoms period, known for his wealth and loyalty to Liu Bei.
    • 三國交鋒 - Refers to the conflicts between the states of Wei, Shu, and Wu during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 先主 - Refers to Liu Bei, the founder of the state of Shu, often called 'Lord Liu' or 'Emperor Liu.'
    • 蜀破後 - Refers to the fall of the state of Shu after the Battle of Yiling in 222 AD, marking the end of the Shu dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 糜竺 (Mi Zhu) - A prominent figure during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 先主 (Lord Liu/Emperor Liu) - Refers to Liu Bei, the founder of the state of Shu.
    • 蜀 (Shu) - One of the three states during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 三國 (Three Kingdoms) - Refers to the states of Wei, Shu, and Wu.

    Mi Zhu was a man of a suspicious nature who believed in occult practices. If anyone spoke against him, he would punish them severely, so his servants dared not speak. His wealth was like a mountain, impossible to count. Inside, he used square vessels and bottles to display large pearls as big as eggs, scattered throughout the courtyard, calling it the "Treasure Courtyard," and no one outside could see it. A few days later, dozens of young boys dressed in green suddenly appeared and said, "There will be a fire disaster at Mi Zhu's house, not leaving a single thing intact. Thanks to your compassion and care for the dead, the heavenly way does not wrong your virtue, so we come to ward off this fire. To ensure your wealth is not entirely lost, from now on, you should also be vigilant!" Mi Zhu then dug ditches and channels around his warehouse. Ten days later, a fire broke out in the warehouse, burning a tenth of the pearls and jade. All of it was caused by the sun's reflection and dryness, which naturally burns things. When the fire was at its peak, dozens of young boys dressed in green came to put it out. A green mist like a cloud covered the fire, and it immediately went out. The young boys said, "Collect many cranes and other birds to ward off fires; cranes can gather water in their nests." The family then collected thousands of cranes and kept them in ponds and channels to ward off fires. Mi Zhu sighed, "Human life and wealth have limits; one should not be overly greedy, as it can become a burden and harm oneself." At the time when the three kingdoms were in conflict, military expenses were ten times higher, so he donated his treasures and carriages to assist Lord Liu Bei: one billion pounds of gold, silk and brocade piled up like hills, and ten thousand fine horses. After the fall of Shu, he had nothing left and died with regret. [129]

    周群妙闲算术谶说。游岷山采药，见一白猿，从绝峰而下，对群而立。群抽所佩书刀投猿，猿化为一老翁，握中有玉版长八寸，以授群。群问曰：“公是何年生？”答曰：“已衰迈也，忘其年月，犹忆轩辕之时，始学历数，风后、容成，皆黄帝之史，就余授历数。至颛顼时，考定日月星辰之运，尤多差异。及春秋时，有子韦、子野、裨灶之徒，权略虽验，未得其门。迩来世代兴亡，不复可记，因以相袭。至大汉时，有洛下闳，颇得其旨。”群服其言，更精勤算术。乃考校年历之运，验于图纬，知蜀应灭。及明年，归命奔吴。皆云：“周群详阴阳之精妙也。”蜀人谓之“后圣”。白猿之异，有似越人所记，而事皆迂诞，似是而非。 [130]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [130]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 妙闲算术谶说 - Excellently skilled in arithmetic and divination.
    • 从绝峰而下 - Descended from the peak.
    • 对群而立 - Faced Zhou Qun and stood.
    • 抽所佩书刀投猿 - Drew out the book knife he wore and threw it at the ape.
    • 握中有玉版长八寸 - Held a jade tablet eight inches long.
    • 轩辕之时 - During the time of the Yellow Emperor.
    • 始学历数 - Began to study the calendar and astronomy.
    • 风后、容成 - Fenghou and Rongcheng, two legendary figures who assisted the Yellow Emperor in developing the calendar.
    • 颛顼时 - During the time of the Emperor Zhuanxu.
    • 春秋时 - During the Spring and Autumn period.
    • 子韦、子野、裨灶之徒 - Zi Wei, Zi Ye, and Bi Zao, among others.
    • 权略虽验 - Their strategies were effective.
    • 未得其门 - Did not fully grasp the essence.
    • 迩来世代兴亡 - Recent generations of rise and fall.
    • 因以相袭 - Thus passed down through generations.
    • 大汉时 - During the Han dynasty.
    • 洛下闳 - Lu Xiaohong, a famous astronomer and calendar reformer of the Han dynasty.
    • 颇得其旨 - Partially grasped the essence.
    • 详阴阳之精妙也 - Excellently skilled in the subtle aspects of yin and yang.
    • 后圣 - Later sage.
    • 白猿之异 - The strange transformation of the white ape.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 周群 - Zhou Qun, a historical figure known for his skills in divination and astronomy.
    • 白猿 - A white ape, often associated with immortals or divine beings in Chinese mythology.
    • 轩辕 - The Yellow Emperor, a legendary figure credited with many cultural and technological advancements.
    • 风后、容成 - Fenghou and Rongcheng, two legendary figures who assisted the Yellow Emperor in developing the calendar.
    • 颛顼 - Emperor Zhuanxu, another legendary ruler known for his contributions to astronomy.
    • 春秋时 - The Spring and Autumn period (770-476 BCE), a significant era in Chinese history characterized by political fragmentation and cultural development.
    • 子韦、子野、裨灶 - Scholars and diviners of the Spring and Autumn period.
    • 大汉时 - The Han dynasty (206 BCE - 220 CE), a period of great cultural and political achievement in China.
    • 洛下闳 - Lu Xiaohong, a famous astronomer and calendar reformer of the Han dynasty.
    • 后圣 - A title given to Zhou Qun, indicating his wisdom and foresight.

    Proper Nouns

    • 周群 - Zhou Qun, a historical figure known for his skills in divination and astronomy.
    • 轩辕 - The Yellow Emperor, a legendary figure credited with many cultural and technological advancements.
    • 风后、容成 - Fenghou and Rongcheng, two legendary figures who assisted the Yellow Emperor in developing the calendar.
    • 颛顼 - Emperor Zhuanxu, another legendary ruler known for his contributions to astronomy.
    • 春秋时 - The Spring and Autumn period (770-476 BCE).
    • 子韦、子野、裨灶 - Scholars and diviners of the Spring and Autumn period.
    • 大汉时 - The Han dynasty (206 BCE - 220 CE).
    • 洛下闳 - Lu Xiaohong, a famous astronomer and calendar reformer of the Han dynasty.
    • 蜀 - The Shu kingdom, one of the Three Kingdoms of China.
    • 吴 - The Wu kingdom, another of the Three Kingdoms of China.

    Zhou Qun was exceptionally skilled in arithmetic and divination. While traveling in Mount Min to gather herbs, he encountered a white ape descending from a peak, facing him and standing there. Zhou Qun drew out the book knife he wore and threw it at the ape. The ape transformed into an old man, who held a jade tablet eight inches long and handed it to Zhou Qun. Zhou Qun asked, "When were you born?" The old man replied, "I am now very old and have forgotten my age and the month, but I still remember the time of the Yellow Emperor, when I began to study the calendar and astronomy. Fenghou and Rongcheng, both historians of the Yellow Emperor, taught me the calendar and astronomy. By the time of Emperor Zhuanxu, the movements of the sun, moon, and stars were more accurately determined, though there were still many differences. During the Spring and Autumn period, there were scholars like Zi Wei, Zi Ye, and Bi Zao, whose strategies were effective but did not fully grasp the essence. In recent generations, the rise and fall of dynasties have been too numerous to remember, and knowledge has been passed down through generations. By the time of the Han dynasty, there was Lu Xiaohong, who partially grasped the essence." Zhou Qun was impressed by his words and became even more diligent in his studies. He then carefully examined the calendar and its cycles, verifying them with the Tiangui (divinatory texts), and predicted that the Shu kingdom would fall. The following year, as predicted, the Shu kingdom surrendered to the Wu kingdom. All said, "Zhou Qun is expert in the subtle aspects of yin and yang." The people of Shu called him a "later sage." The strange transformation of the white ape is similar to what was recorded by the people of Yue, though the events are somewhat fantastical and seem to be a mix of truth and fiction. [130]

    录曰：孙和、孙亮、刘备，并惑于淫宠之玩，忘于军旅之略，犹比强大魏，克伐无功，可为嗟矣！周群之学，通于神明，白猿之祥，有类越人问剑之言，其事迂诞，若是而非也。夫阴阳递生，五行迭用，由水火相生，亦以相灭。《淮南子》云“方诸向月津为水”，以厌火灾浮。糜氏富于珍奇，削方诸为鸟兽之状，犹土龙以祈雨也。鵁鶄之音，与方诸相乱，盖声之讹矣。羽毛之类，非可御烈火，于义则为乖，于事则违类，先《坟》旧《典》，说以其详焉。 [131]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [131]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 錄曰 - Formal way of introducing a commentary or record.
    • 惑于淫宠之玩 - Bewitched by indulgence in sensual pleasures, a common criticism of rulers who lost focus on governance.
    • 忘于军旅之略 - Forgot the strategies of military affairs, indicating neglect of statecraft.
    • 比强大魏 - Compared to the mighty Wei, referring to the powerful state of Wei during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 克伐无功 - Failed to achieve success in conquests, a common critique of rulers who lacked military or political achievement.
    • 可为嗟矣 - It is indeed a cause for lament, expressing regret or sorrow.
    • 周群之学，通于神明 - Zhou Jun's learning reached the level of divine insight, praising his wisdom.
    • 白猿之祥，有类越人问剑之言 - The omen of the white monkey is similar to the tale of the sword told by the man from Yue, referring to a mythological or symbolic story.
    • 其事迂诞，若是而非也 - The story is fanciful and far-fetched, though it may not be entirely false, acknowledging the story's implausibility.
    • 阴阳递生，五行迭用 - Yin and yang alternate in creation, and the five elements cycle in their use, referring to traditional Chinese cosmology.
    • 由水火相生，亦以相灭 - Water and fire both generate and destroy each other, a paradoxical statement about the nature of opposites.
    • 方诸向月津为水 - The sphere of the moon's dew condenses into water, a reference to a Daoist alchemical process.
    • 以厌火灾浮 - To counteract fire with water, referring to the use of water to extinguish fire.
    • 糜氏富于珍奇 - The Mi family was wealthy in rare and precious items, referring to a wealthy family.
    • 削方诸为鸟兽之状 - Carved the sphere into the shapes of birds and beasts, referring to a decorative or ritual object.
    • 犹土龙以祈雨也 - Like the earth dragon used to summon rain, referring to a folk ritual.
    • 鵁鶄之音，与方诸相乱 - The sound of the *jiangji* bird is confused with the sound of the sphere, referring to a confusion of sounds.
    • 盖声之讹矣 - It is likely a distortion of sound, suggesting that the confusion is due to misinterpretation.
    • 羽毛之类，非可御烈火 - Feathers and the like cannot withstand intense fire, referring to the physical properties of feathers.
    • 于义则为乖，于事则违类 - It is contrary to logic and inconsistent with reality, criticizing the story for being illogical and unrealistic.
    • 先《坟》旧《典》 - In earlier classics and ancient texts, referring to traditional sources of knowledge.
    • 说以其详焉 - The story is explained in detail in these texts, indicating that the story is elaborated upon in classical sources.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 孙和、孙亮、刘备 - Refers to historical figures: Sun He and Sun Liang, sons of Sun Quan, and Liu Bei, a ruler of the Shu Han state during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 淫宠之玩 - Refers to indulgence in sensual pleasures, a common critique of rulers who neglected their duties.
    • 军旅之略 - Refers to military strategies, a key aspect of statecraft.
    • 强大魏 - Refers to the powerful state of Wei during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 克伐无功 - Refers to the failure to achieve success in conquests, a common critique of rulers.
    • 周群 - A historical figure known for his wisdom and learning.
    • 白猿之祥 - Refers to an omen involving a white monkey, likely a mythological or symbolic story.
    • 越人问剑之言 - Refers to a tale from the state of Yue involving a sword, likely a mythological or symbolic story.
    • 阴阳递生，五行迭用 - Refers to traditional Chinese cosmology, the belief in the alternation of yin and yang and the cyclical use of the five elements.
    • 水火相生，亦以相灭 - Refers to the paradoxical relationship between water and fire in traditional Chinese thought.
    • 方诸向月津为水 - Refers to a Daoist alchemical process involving the condensation of dew into water.
    • 以厌火灾浮 - Refers to the use of water to counteract fire, a practical application of the water-fire relationship.
    • 糜氏 - Refers to a wealthy family known for their rare and precious items.
    • 削方诸为鸟兽之状 - Refers to a decorative or ritual object carved into the shapes of birds and beasts.
    • 土龙以祈雨 - Refers to a folk ritual involving an earth dragon to summon rain.
    • 鵁鶄 - Refers to a type of bird, likely the *jiangji* bird, whose sound is confused with the sound of the sphere.
    • 羽毛之类 - Refers to feathers and other light materials, which cannot withstand intense fire.
    • 先《坟》旧《典》 - Refers to earlier classics and ancient texts, the traditional sources of knowledge.

    Proper Nouns

    • 孙和 - Sun He, a historical figure.
    • 孙亮 - Sun Liang, a historical figure.
    • 刘备 - Liu Bei, a historical figure.
    • 周群 - Zhou Jun, a historical figure.
    • 白猿 - The white monkey, a mythological or symbolic figure.
    • 越人问剑之言 - The tale of the sword told by the man from Yue, a mythological or symbolic story.
    • 方诸 - A sphere used in Daoist alchemy.
    • 糜氏 - The Mi family, a wealthy family.
    • 鵁鶄 - The *jiangji* bird, a type of bird.
    • 先《坟》旧《典》 - Earlier classics and ancient texts.

    The Record states: Sun He, Sun Liang, and Liu Bei were all bewitched by indulgence in sensual pleasures, forgetting the strategies of military affairs. Compared to the mighty Wei, they failed to achieve success in conquests, and it is indeed a cause for lament! Zhou Jun's learning reached the level of divine insight, and the omen of the white monkey is similar to the tale of the sword told by the man from Yue. The story is fanciful and far-fetched, though it may not be entirely false. Yin and yang alternate in creation, and the five elements cycle in their use. Water and fire both generate and destroy each other. The Huainanzi states, "The sphere of the moon's dew condenses into water," to counteract fire with water. The Mi family was wealthy in rare and precious items, carving the sphere into the shapes of birds and beasts, like the earth dragon used to summon rain. The sound of the jiangji bird is confused with the sound of the sphere, likely a distortion of sound. Feathers and the like cannot withstand intense fire, it is contrary to logic and inconsistent with reality. In earlier classics and ancient texts, the story is explained in detail. [131]

    拾遗记卷九

    晋时事

    　　武帝为抚军时，府内后堂砌下忽生草三株，茎黄叶绿，若总金抽翠，花条苒弱，状似镫。时人未知是何祥草，故隐蔽不听外人窥视。有一羌人，姓姚名馥，字世芬，充厩养马，妙解阴阳之术，云：“此草以应金德之瑞。”馥年九十八，姚襄则其祖也。馥好读书，嗜酒，每醉时好言帝王兴亡之事。善戏笑，滑稽无穷，常叹云：“九河之水不足以渍曲糵，八薮之木不足以作薪蒸，七泽之麋不足以充庖俎。凡人禀天地之精灵，不知饮酒者，动肉含气耳，何必木偶于心识乎？”好啜浊糟，常言渴于醇酒。群辈常弄狎之，呼为“渴羌”。及晋武践位，忽思见馥立于阶下，帝奇其倜傥，擢为朝歌邑宰。 [132]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [132]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 若總金抽翠 - Metaphorical description of the grass, likening its appearance to gold and emerald.
    • 花條苒弱，狀似燈 - Describes the delicate and weak appearance of the flowers, resembling a lamp.
    • 此草以應金德之瑞 - The statement that the grass is a sign of the 'Golden Virtue' (金德), a concept related to the Five Elements (五行) in Chinese philosophy.
    • 善戲笑，滑稽無窮 - Describes Yao Fu's skill in humor and wit, suggesting he was very clever and entertaining.
    • 九河之水不足以澆曲糵，八藪之木不足以作薪蒸，七澤之麋不足以充庖俎 - A metaphorical statement comparing the vastness of rivers, forests, and marshes to the insatiable nature of Yao Fu's love for alcohol and food.
    • 凡人稟天地之精靈，不知飲酒者，動肉含氣耳，何必木偶於心識乎 - A philosophical statement suggesting that humans, endowed with divine essence, should not be bound by conventional norms and should enjoy life fully.
    • 好啜濁糟，常言渴於醇酒 - Describes Yao Fu's preference for strong, undiluted wine.
    • 群輩常弄狎之，呼為“渴羌” - Indicates that his peers often teased him and called him 'Thirsty Qiang' due to his love for alcohol.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 武帝 - Emperor Wu of Jin (259–290), a significant ruler of the Western Jin Dynasty.
    • 撫軍 - A high-ranking military and administrative position held by Emperor Wu before he became emperor.
    • 金德之瑞 - A concept in Chinese philosophy where 'Golden Virtue' (金德) is one of the Five Elements (五行) and is associated with the metal element and the color white.
    • 姚馥，字世芬 - Yao Fu, a person known for his wisdom and longevity.
    • 姚襄 - Yao Xiang, the grandson of Yao Fu.
    • 朝歌邑宰 - The governor of Chao Ge, a city in Henan Province.
    • 九河 - Refers to the nine major rivers in ancient China.
    • 八藪 - Refers to the eight marshes or wetlands in ancient China.
    • 七澤 - Refers to the seven lakes or marshes in ancient China.
    • 曲糵 - A term for fermented grains used in brewing alcohol.
    • 薪蒸 - Firewood.
    • 庖俎 - Kitchen utensils or the act of cooking.
    • 晉武 - Emperor Wu of Jin, also known as Sima Yan.
    • 儻儻 - Describes someone who is unconventional and talented.
    • 擢 - To promote or appoint someone to a higher position.

    Proper Nouns

    • 武帝 - Emperor Wu of Jin.
    • 撫軍 - A high-ranking official position.
    • 金德之瑞 - A sign of the 'Golden Virtue' (金德).
    • 姚馥，字世芬 - Yao Fu, a person known for his wisdom and longevity.
    • 姚襄 - Yao Xiang, the grandson of Yao Fu.
    • 朝歌邑宰 - The governor of Chao Ge.
    • 九河 - Nine major rivers in ancient China.
    • 八藪 - Eight marshes or wetlands in ancient China.
    • 七澤 - Seven lakes or marshes in ancient China.
    • 曲糵 - Fermented grains used in brewing alcohol.
    • 薪蒸 - Firewood.
    • 庖俎 - Kitchen utensils or the act of cooking.
    • 晉武 - Emperor Wu of Jin, also known as Sima Yan.
    • 儻儻 - Unconventional and talented.
    • 擢 - To promote or appoint someone to a higher position.

    The Shiyiji (拾遺記) Volume Nine

    Jin Dynasty Events

    When Emperor Wu of Jin was serving as a high-ranking official, three grass plants suddenly sprouted in the courtyard of his mansion. The grass had golden stems and green leaves, resembling a blend of gold and jade. The flower stems were delicate and weak, looking like lamps. At the time, no one knew what kind of auspicious grass this was, so it was kept hidden from the outside world. There was a Qiang person named Yao Fu, with the courtesy name Shifen, who worked in the stables and took care of the horses. He was skilled in divination and astronomy and said, "This grass is a sign of the Golden Virtue (金德)."

    Yao Fu lived to be 98 years old, and Yao Xiang was his grandson. Yao Fu loved reading and drinking, and he often spoke about the rise and fall of emperors when he was drunk. He was skilled in humor and wit, and his cleverness was endless. He often said, "The waters of the nine major rivers are not enough to quench the thirst for fermented grains, the wood of the eight marshes is not enough to make firewood, and the deer of the seven lakes are not enough to fill the kitchen." He believed that humans, endowed with divine essence, should not be bound by conventional norms and should enjoy life fully. He preferred strong, undiluted wine.

    His peers often teased him and called him "Thirsty Qiang" due to his love for alcohol. When Emperor Wu of Jin ascended the throne, he suddenly remembered Yao Fu and saw him standing at the foot of the steps. The emperor found him unconventional and talented, and appointed him as the governor of Chao Ge. [132]

    馥辞曰：“老羌异域之人，远隔山川，得游中华，已为殊幸，请辞朝歌之县，长充养马之役，时赐美酒，以乐余年。”帝曰：“朝歌纣之故都，地有美酒，故使老羌不复呼渴。”馥于阶下高声而对曰：“马圉老羌，渐染皇化，溥天夷貊，皆为王臣，今若欢酒池之乐，更为殷纣之民乎？”帝抚玉几大悦，即迁酒泉太守。地有清泉，其味若酒。馥乘醉而拜受之，遂为善政，民为立生祠。后以府地赐张华，犹有草在，故茂先《镫赋》云：“擢九茎于汉庭，美三株于兹馆。贵表祥乎金德，比名类乎相乱。”至惠帝元熙元年，三株草化为三树，枝叶似杨树，高五尺，以应“三杨”擅权之事。时有杨骏、杨瑶、杨济三弟兄，号曰“三杨”。马圉醉羌所说之验。 [133]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [133]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 辭曰 - Means 'said in response' or 'replied,' indicating a formal or polite response.
    • 異域之人 - Refers to someone from a different region or country.
    • 遠隔山川 - Literally 'far apart by mountains and rivers,' used to emphasize geographical distance.
    • 得游中华 - Means 'to be able to wander in China,' indicating a sense of privilege or honor.
    • 殊幸 - Means 'a great fortune' or 'a special blessing.'
    • 朝歌之县 - Refers to the county of Cao Ge, an ancient place name.
    • 长充养马之役 - Means 'to serve long-term as a horse keeper,' indicating a lowly but important role.
    • 时赐美酒，以乐余年 - Means 'to be occasionally given fine wine to enjoy the remaining years,' suggesting a life of leisure and contentment.
    • 抚玉几大悦 - Means 'to pat the jade table and be greatly pleased,' indicating a gesture of approval and satisfaction.
    • 迁酒泉太守 - Means 'to be promoted to the governor of Jiuquan,' indicating a significant career advancement.
    • 地有清泉，其味若酒 - Means 'the place has clear spring water that tastes like wine,' describing the unique quality of the water.
    • 乘醉而拜受之 - Means 'to accept the position while intoxicated,' suggesting a state of joy and acceptance.
    • 遂为善政 - Means 'to govern well,' indicating effective and beneficial rule.
    • 民为立生祠 - Means 'the people built a living temple for him,' indicating high regard and reverence.
    • 后以府地赐张华 - Means 'later, the governor's residence was granted to Zhang Hua,' indicating a significant change in ownership.
    • 犹有草在 - Means 'there still exists the grass,' suggesting a lingering presence or memory.
    • 贵表祥乎金德 - Means 'it signifies auspiciousness in the metal element,' indicating a symbolic or spiritual significance.
    • 比名类乎相乱 - Means 'it is compared to the confusion of names,' suggesting a metaphorical or allegorical meaning.
    • 至惠帝元熙元年 - Refers to the first year of the Yuxi era during the reign of Emperor Huai of Jin.
    • 三株草化为三树 - Means 'the three grasses transformed into three trees,' indicating a miraculous or symbolic transformation.
    • 枝叶似杨树，高五尺 - Means 'the branches and leaves resemble willow trees, five feet tall,' describing the appearance of the transformed trees.
    • 以应“三杨”擅权之事 - Means 'to correspond to the dominance of the 'Three Yangs' in power,' indicating a political reference.
    • 时有杨骏、杨瑶、杨济三弟兄 - Refers to three brothers named Yang Jun, Yang Yao, and Yang Ji, who held significant power.
    • 号曰“三杨” - Means 'they were called the 'Three Yangs',' indicating a collective name or title.
    • 马圉醉羌所说之验 - Means 'the prediction made by the drunk horse keeper proved true,' indicating the fulfillment of a prophecy.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 朝歌 - An ancient place name, the capital of the Shang dynasty, known for its wine.
    • 酒泉 - A place name, the governor's residence, known for its clear spring water that tastes like wine.
    • 张华 - A historical figure, a prominent politician and scholar during the Jin dynasty.
    • 三杨 - Refers to the three powerful Yang brothers during the Jin dynasty, known for their influence in politics.
    • 惠帝元熙元年 - The first year of the Yuxi era during the reign of Emperor Huai of Jin, a significant period in Chinese history.
    • 马圉醉羌 - Refers to the horse keeper who was a Qiang ethnic group member, known for his drunken state and predictions.

    Proper Nouns

    • 馥 - The name of the horse keeper.
    • 老羌 - Refers to the horse keeper, who is a Qiang ethnic group member.
    • 朝歌 - The ancient capital of the Shang dynasty.
    • 酒泉 - The governor's residence.
    • 张华 - A historical figure, a prominent politician and scholar during the Jin dynasty.
    • 三杨 - Refers to the three powerful Yang brothers during the Jin dynasty.
    • 惠帝元熙元年 - The first year of the Yuxi era during the reign of Emperor Huai of Jin.
    • 杨骏、杨瑶、杨济 - The names of the three powerful Yang brothers.

    馥辞曰老羌异域之人远隔山川得游中华已为殊幸请辞朝歌之县长充养马之役时赐美酒以乐余年帝曰朝歌纣之故都地有美酒故使老羌不复呼渴馥于阶下高声而对曰马圉老羌渐染皇化溥天夷貊皆为王臣今若欢酒池之乐更为殷纣之民乎帝抚玉几大悦即迁酒泉太守地有清泉其味若酒馥乘醉而拜受之遂为善政民为立生祠后以府地赐张华犹有草在故茂先镫赋云擢九茎于汉庭美三株于兹馆贵表祥乎金德比名类乎相乱至惠帝元熙元年三株草化为三树枝叶似杨树高五尺以应三杨擅权之事时有杨骏杨瑶杨济三弟兄号曰三杨马圉醉羌所说之验 [133]

    录曰：不得中行，狂狷可也。淳于、优孟之俦，因俳说以进谏。至如姚馥，才性容貌，不与华同，片言窃讽，媚足规范。及其俳谐诡谲，推辞指诫，因物而刺，言之者无罪，抑亦东方曼倩之俦欤！夫心胃之逸朽，故有腐肠烂肠之嗜，是以“五味令人口爽”，老氏以为深诫。未若甘并桂石，美斯松草，含吐烟霞，咀食沆瀣，迅千灵于一朝，方尘劫于俄顷，乎可淫此酣乐，忘彼久视者乎？夫物有事异而名同者，自非穷神达理，莫能遥照。岂可假于诐辞，专求于邪说。天命有兆，历运攸归，何可妄信于谣讹，指怪于纤草？将溺所闻，信诸厥术，可为嗟乎！ [134]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [134]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 不得中行，狂狷可也 - Classical saying meaning 'If one cannot be a person of the middle way, being a wild or strict person is acceptable.' Refers to Confucian ideals of moderation.
    • 淳于、优孟之俦 - Refers to historical figures like Chunyu and You Meng, who were known for using humor and performance to convey serious messages.
    • 因俳说以进谏 - Means 'to use jest and play to offer advice,' referring to a method of indirect persuasion.
    • 片言窃讽 - Means 'to subtly criticize with a few words,' indicating indirect criticism.
    • 媚足规范 - Means 'to be charming enough to set a standard,' suggesting that even playful speech can have a normative effect.
    • 因物而刺 - Means 'to criticize through objects,' referring to indirect criticism via metaphors or allusions.
    • 言之者无罪 - Classical saying meaning 'The speaker is blameless,' emphasizing the freedom of speech.
    • 五味令人口爽 - Classical saying from the *Dao De Jing* meaning 'The five flavors dull the taste buds,' warning against excessive indulgence.
    • 甘并桂石，美斯松草 - Refers to the sweetness of pine and the beauty of grass, likely used metaphorically to contrast worldly pleasures with spiritual or intellectual pursuits.
    • 含吐烟霞，咀食沆瀣 - Means 'to breathe in mist and clouds, to chew on the dew of heaven,' suggesting a life of refinement and detachment.
    • 迅千灵于一朝，方尘劫于俄顷 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'to swiftly transcend a thousand spirits in a moment, to compare dust to an instant,' suggesting the fleeting nature of worldly pleasures.
    • 乎可淫此酣乐，忘彼久视者乎 - Means 'How can one indulge in such pleasures and forget the long view?' suggesting a warning against short-sighted indulgence.
    • 物有事异而名同者 - Means 'There are things that differ in their nature but share the same name,' referring to the complexity of language and meaning.
    • 穷神达理 - Means 'to fathom the divine and understand the principles,' suggesting deep insight.
    • 假于诐辞，专求于邪说 - Means 'to rely on biased words and seek only heretical ideas,' warning against misguided thinking.
    • 天命有兆，历运攸归 - Means 'The mandate of heaven has signs, and the course of history is destined,' referring to the inevitability of fate.
    • 妄信于谣讹，指怪于纤草 - Means 'to blindly believe rumors and point to strange phenomena in small plants,' warning against superstition.
    • 将溺所闻，信诸厥术 - Means 'To be submerged by what one hears and believe in its methods,' suggesting a warning against credulity.
    • 可为嗟乎 - Means 'This is indeed a matter of regret,' expressing sorrow or lament.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 中行 - Refers to the Confucian ideal of moderation, a person who follows the middle way.
    • 狂狷 - Refers to two types of people in Confucian thought: the 'wild' (狂) and the 'strict' (狷), who are acceptable if one cannot be a person of moderation.
    • 淳于 - A historical figure known for using humor and performance to convey serious messages.
    • 优孟 - A historical figure known for using humor and performance to convey serious messages.
    • 姚馥 - A historical figure mentioned in the text, likely a scholar or official.
    • 东方曼倩 - A historical figure known for his wit and humor, often used as an example of someone who used indirect methods to convey serious messages.
    • 老氏 - Refers to Laozi, the author of the *Dao De Jing*.
    • 谣讹 - Refers to rumors or false information.
    • 纤草 - Refers to small plants, possibly used metaphorically.

    Proper Nouns

    • 中行 - A Confucian ideal of moderation.
    • 狂狷 - Two types of people in Confucian thought: the 'wild' (狂) and the 'strict' (狷).
    • 淳于 - A historical figure known for using humor and performance to convey serious messages.
    • 优孟 - A historical figure known for using humor and performance to convey serious messages.
    • 姚馥 - A historical figure mentioned in the text, likely a scholar or official.
    • 东方曼倩 - A historical figure known for his wit and humor, often used as an example of someone who used indirect methods to convey serious messages.
    • 老氏 - Refers to Laozi, the author of the *Dao De Jing*.
    • 谣讹 - Refers to rumors or false information.
    • 纤草 - Refers to small plants, possibly used metaphorically.

    The Record states: "If one cannot be a person of the middle way, being a wild or strict person is acceptable." Figures like Chunyu and You Meng, who used jest and play to offer advice, exemplify this. As for Yao Fu, his talent, character, and appearance were unlike those of others; with a few words, he subtly criticized, yet his charm was enough to set a standard. In his playful and ingenious manner, he used words to advise and pointed out flaws through objects, speaking without blame, and indeed he was of the same ilk as Eastern Manqian! The corruption of the heart and mind leads to a taste for harmful indulgences, which is why the Dao De Jing warns, "The five flavors dull the taste buds." It is better to find sweetness in pine and beauty in grass, to breathe in mist and clouds, to chew on the dew of heaven, to swiftly transcend a thousand spirits in a moment, to compare dust to an instant. How can one indulge in such pleasures and forget the long view? There are things that differ in their nature but share the same name; only those who fathom the divine and understand the principles can see this clearly. How can one rely on biased words and seek only heretical ideas? The mandate of heaven has signs, and the course of history is destined; how can one blindly believe rumors and point to strange phenomena in small plants? To be submerged by what one hears and believe in its methods, this is indeed a matter of regret. [134]

    咸宁四年，立芳蔬园于金墉城东，多种异菜。有菜名曰“芸薇”，类有三种，紫色者最繁，味辛，其根烂熳，春夏叶密，秋蕊冬馥，其实若珠，五色，随时而盛，一名“芸芝”。其色紫者为上蔬，其味辛；色黄者为中蔬，其味甘；色青者为下蔬，其味咸。常以三蔬充御膳。其叶可以藉饮食，以供宗庙祭祀，亦止人渴饥。宫人采带其茎叶，香气历日不歇。
    　　录曰：《大雅》云：“言采其薇。”此之类也。《草木疏》云：其实如豆。”昔孤竹二子避世，不食周粟，于首阳山采薇而食，疑似卉。或云神类非一，弥相惑乱。可以疗饥，其色必紫，百家杂说，音旨相符。论其形品，详斯香色，虽移植芳圃，芬美莫俦。故熏兰有质，物性无改，产乖本地，逾见芬烈，譬诸姜桂，岂因地而辛矣！当此一代，是谓仙蔬，实为神异。 [135]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [135]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 立芳蔬園 - Literal phrase meaning 'to establish a fragrant vegetable garden,' used to describe the creation of a garden with aromatic vegetables.
    • 多種異菜 - Means 'to cultivate many exotic vegetables,' indicating a variety of unusual or rare vegetables.
    • 芸薇 - A type of vegetable with three varieties, each with distinct characteristics.
    • 紫色者最繁 - Means 'the purple variety is the most abundant,' indicating the prevalence of the purple type.
    • 味辛 - Means 'tastes spicy,' describing the flavor of the purple variety.
    • 根烂熳 - Means 'roots spread widely,' describing the root system of the plant.
    • 春夏叶密 - Means 'leaves are dense in spring and summer,' describing the growth pattern of the plant.
    • 秋蕊冬馥 - Means 'flowers in autumn and fragrance in winter,' describing the seasonal characteristics of the plant.
    • 其实若珠 - Means 'its fruit resembles pearls,' describing the appearance of the fruit.
    • 五色 - Means 'five colors,' indicating the variety of colors in the fruit.
    • 随时而盛 - Means 'prosper at different times,' indicating that the fruit is abundant at different seasons.
    • 芸芝 - Another name for the vegetable, possibly indicating its medicinal or sacred properties.
    • 色紫者为上蔬 - Means 'the purple variety is the best vegetable,' indicating its superior quality.
    • 色黄者为中蔬 - Means 'the yellow variety is a medium-quality vegetable,' indicating its moderate quality.
    • 色青者为下蔬 - Means 'the green variety is the lowest-quality vegetable,' indicating its inferior quality.
    • 常以三蔬充御膳 - Means 'often used the three varieties to fill the imperial diet,' indicating their importance in the imperial kitchen.
    • 其叶可以藉饮食 - Means 'its leaves can be used to wrap food,' indicating a practical use of the plant.
    • 以供宗庙祭祀 - Means 'to provide for ancestral temple sacrifices,' indicating its religious significance.
    • 亦止人渴饥 - Means 'also satisfies hunger and thirst,' indicating its practical utility.
    • 宫人采带其茎叶 - Means 'palace women collected and carried its stems and leaves,' indicating its use by palace staff.
    • 香气历日不歇 - Means 'the fragrance lasted for days,' indicating the plant's long-lasting aroma.
    • 大雅 - Refers to the *Da Ya* section of the *Shijing* (Book of Songs), a classic of Chinese literature.
    • 言采其薇 - A phrase from the *Da Ya* section, meaning 'to gather the wild vegetables,' indicating the cultural significance of the plant.
    • 草木疏 - Refers to a classical text on botany, possibly providing additional information about the plant.
    • 其实如豆 - Means 'its fruit is like beans,' indicating the shape of the fruit.
    • 孤竹二子避世 - Refers to the story of two sons of the Duke of Guzhu who fled the world and lived in seclusion.
    • 不食周粟 - Means 'did not eat the grain of Zhou,' indicating their refusal to accept the food of the ruling dynasty.
    • 首阳山采薇而食 - Means 'gathered wild vegetables from Mount Shouyang to eat,' indicating their lifestyle in seclusion.
    • 疑似卉 - Means 'possibly a plant,' indicating uncertainty about the plant's identity.
    • 或云神类非一 - Means 'some say there are multiple divine types,' indicating the plant's mystical nature.
    • 弥相惑乱 - Means 'more and more confused,' indicating the complexity of the plant's identity.
    • 可以疗饥 - Means 'can relieve hunger,' indicating its practical utility.
    • 其色必紫 - Means 'its color must be purple,' indicating a specific characteristic.
    • 百家杂说 - Refers to various scholarly opinions, indicating the diversity of interpretations.
    • 音旨相符 - Means 'sound and meaning are consistent,' indicating the harmony of the plant's characteristics.
    • 论其形品 - Means 'discuss its form and quality,' indicating an analysis of the plant's characteristics.
    • 详斯香色 - Means 'detailed in its fragrance and color,' indicating the plant's sensory qualities.
    • 虽移植芳圃 - Means 'even when transplanted to a fragrant garden,' indicating the plant's adaptability.
    • 芬美莫俦 - Means 'no equal in fragrance and beauty,' indicating the plant's superior qualities.
    • 熏兰有质 - Means 'the scent of orchids has substance,' indicating the plant's lasting fragrance.
    • 物性无改 - Means 'the nature of the object remains unchanged,' indicating the plant's inherent qualities.
    • 产乖本地 - Means 'produced in a place far from its native land,' indicating the plant's adaptability to different environments.
    • 逾见芬烈 - Means 'even more fragrant and intense,' indicating the plant's enhanced qualities in a new environment.
    • 譬诸姜桂 - Means 'like ginger and cinnamon,' indicating the plant's strong and persistent fragrance.
    • 岂因地而辛矣 - Means 'is not due to the place being spicy,' indicating that the plant's qualities are inherent.
    • 当此一代 - Means 'in this era,' indicating the time period.
    • 是谓仙蔬 - Means 'is called a divine vegetable,' indicating the plant's sacred status.
    • 实为神异 - Means 'truly a divine and mysterious plant,' indicating its extraordinary nature.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 咸寧四年 - Refers to the fourth year of the Xining era (278 AD) during the Western Jin dynasty.
    • 金墉城 - Refers to the Jin city, a significant location in the Western Jin dynasty.
    • 大雅 - Refers to the *Da Ya* section of the *Shijing* (Book of Songs), a classic of Chinese literature.
    • 草木疏 - Refers to a classical text on botany, possibly providing additional information about the plant.
    • 孤竹二子避世 - Refers to the story of two sons of the Duke of Guzhu who fled the world and lived in seclusion, a well-known tale in Chinese history.
    • 不食周粟 - Refers to the act of refusing to eat the grain of the ruling dynasty, a symbol of moral integrity and resistance.

    In the fourth year of Xining (278 AD), a fragrant vegetable garden was established east of Jin City, where many exotic vegetables were cultivated. Among these was a vegetable known as "yunwei," which had three varieties: the purple variety was the most abundant, with a spicy flavor, its roots spreading widely, its leaves dense in spring and summer, its flowers blooming in autumn and its fragrance lingering in winter. Its fruit resembled pearls, coming in five colors, and was most abundant at different times of the year. It was also known by the name "yunzhi." The purple variety was considered the best vegetable, with a spicy flavor; the yellow variety was a medium-quality vegetable, with a sweet flavor; and the green variety was the lowest-quality vegetable, with a salty flavor. These three varieties were often used to fill the imperial diet. Their leaves could be used to wrap food, to provide for ancestral temple sacrifices, and to satisfy hunger and thirst. Palace women collected and carried its stems and leaves, whose fragrance lasted for days.

    It is recorded that in the Da Ya section of the Shijing (Book of Songs) it says, "Speak of gathering the wild vegetables," which refers to this type of plant. In the Grass and Wood Treatise it is said, "Its fruit is like beans." In ancient times, the two sons of the Duke of Guzhu fled the world, refusing to eat the grain of Zhou, and gathered wild vegetables from Mount Shouyang to eat. Some say it is a plant, possibly a divine type, and the more one learns about it, the more one becomes confused. It can relieve hunger, and its color must be purple. Various scholarly opinions agree on its sound and meaning. When discussing its form and quality, its fragrance and color are detailed. Even when transplanted to a fragrant garden, its fragrance and beauty are unmatched. Therefore, the scent of orchids has substance, and the nature of the object remains unchanged, even when produced in a place far from its native land, it becomes even more fragrant and intense. Like ginger and cinnamon, its spiciness is not due to the place but to its inherent qualities. In this era, it is called a divine vegetable, truly a divine and mysterious plant. [135]

    张华为九酝酒，以三薇渍曲糵，糵出西羌，曲出北胡。胡中有指星麦，四月火星出，麦熟而获之。糵用水渍麦三夕而萌芽，平旦鸡鸣而用之，俗人呼为“鸡鸣麦”。以之酿酒，醇美，久含令人齿动。若大醉，不叫笑摇荡，令人肝肠消烂，俗人谓为“消肠酒”。或云醇酒可为长宵之乐，两说声同而事异也。闾里歌曰：“宁得醇酒消肠，不与日月齐光。”言耽此美酒，以悦一时，何用保守灵而取长久。至怀帝末，民间园圃皆生蒿棘，狐兔游聚。至元熙元年，太史令高堂忠奏荧惑犯紫微，若不早避，当无洛阳。及诏内外四方及京邑诸宫观林卫之内，及民间园囿，皆植紫薇，以为厌胜。至刘、石、姚、苻之末，此蒿棘不除自绝也。 [136]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [136]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 九酝酒 - A type of wine made through a nine-step fermentation process.
    • 三薇渍曲糵 - A method of brewing wine using three types of herbs (薇) to soak the yeast and malt (曲糵).
    • 糵出西羌，曲出北胡 - Indicates that the yeast (糵) comes from the Western Qiang and the yeast and malt (曲) come from the Northern Hu.
    • 指星麦 - A type of wheat that is harvested when the star (火星) appears in the sky, typically in April.
    • 鸡鸣麦 - A colloquial term for the wheat that is harvested at dawn, when the roosters crow.
    • 消肠酒 - A type of strong wine that can cause severe digestive issues if consumed excessively.
    • 醇酒可为长宵之乐 - A saying that suggests that good wine can provide long-lasting pleasure.
    • 宁得醇酒消肠，不与日月齐光 - A proverb that expresses a preference for the immediate pleasure of drinking good wine over the pursuit of eternal fame or glory.
    • 厌胜 - A form of magic or charm used to ward off evil or misfortune.
    • 荧惑犯紫微 - An astronomical phenomenon where the planet Mars (荧惑) enters the region of the North Star (紫微), often interpreted as an omen of bad luck or disaster.
    • 植紫薇 - Planting the purple wisteria (紫薇) as a protective measure.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 张华 - A prominent figure in Chinese history, known for his contributions to literature and astronomy.
    • 西羌 - A region in the west, inhabited by the Qiang people.
    • 北胡 - A region in the north, inhabited by the Hu people.
    • 荧惑 - The planet Mars, often associated with war and conflict in Chinese astrology.
    • 紫微 - The North Star, symbolizing the emperor and imperial power.
    • 刘、石、姚、苻 - Leaders of various states during the Sixteen Kingdoms period, a time of political fragmentation and warfare.
    • 太史令高堂忠 - A high-ranking official responsible for astronomical observations and predictions.

    Proper Nouns

    • 张华 - Zhang Hua, a historical figure.
    • 西羌 - Western Qiang, a region.
    • 北胡 - Northern Hu, a region.
    • 荧惑 - Mars, a planet.
    • 紫微 - North Star, a celestial body.
    • 刘、石、姚、苻 - Liu, Shi, Yao, and Fu, historical rulers.
    • 太史令高堂忠 - Gao Tang Zhong, a historical official.

    Zhang Hua brewed a nine-fermentation wine, using three types of herbs to soak the yeast and malt. The yeast came from the Western Qiang, and the yeast and malt came from the Northern Hu. Among the Hu people, there was a type of wheat called "pointing-to-the-star wheat," which was harvested in April when the star Mars appeared in the sky. The yeast was soaked in water for three nights until it sprouted, and it was used at dawn when the roosters crowed. People commonly called this wheat "rooster-crowing wheat." Using this wheat to brew wine resulted in a pure and delicious beverage that, when held in the mouth for a long time, could make one's teeth tingle. If one drank too much, it could cause severe digestive issues, leading to the liver and intestines becoming inflamed and deteriorating, and people referred to this as "wine that destroys the intestines." Some say that good wine can provide long-lasting pleasure, but these two sayings, though sounding similar, refer to different things. The people of the neighborhood sang, "Would you rather have a pure wine that destroys the intestines than seek eternal fame like the sun and moon?" This proverb expresses a preference for the immediate pleasure of drinking good wine over the pursuit of lasting glory. It suggests that one should enjoy this fine wine for the moment, rather than seeking to preserve one's spirit for long-term goals. By the end of Emperor Huai's reign, the fields and gardens of the common people were overrun with weeds and thorns, and foxes and rabbits roamed freely. In the first year of the Yuanxi era, the Grand Historian Gao Tang Zhong reported that Mars had entered the region of the North Star, predicting disaster if not avoided early. The emperor then ordered that purple wisteria be planted throughout the inner and outer regions, as well as in the capital's palaces, temples, and gardens, and in the common people's fields, as a protective measure. By the end of the reigns of Liu, Shi, Yao, and Fu, these weeds and thorns had naturally disappeared on their own. [136]

    晋太康元年，白云起于灞水，三日而灭。有司奏云：“天下应太平。”帝问其故，曰：“昔舜时黄云兴于郊野，夏代白云蔽于都邑，殷代玄云覆于林薮，斯皆应世之休征，殊乡绝域应有贡其方物也。”果有羽山之民献火浣布万匹。其国人称：“羽山之上，有文石，生火，烟色以随四时而见，名为‘净火’。有不洁之衣，投于火石之上，虽滞污渍涅，皆如新浣。”当虞舜时，其国献黄布；汉末献赤布，梁冀制为衣，谓之“丹衣”。史家云：“单衣今缝掖也。”字异声同，未知孰是。 [137]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [137]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 天下应太平 - Sign of peace and prosperity for the world.
    • 斯皆应世之休征 - These are signs of peace and prosperity for the world.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋太康元年 - The first year of the Taikang era of the Jin Dynasty, marking the end of the Three Kingdoms period and the beginning of the Jin Dynasty.
    • 灞水 - The Ba River, a river in Shaanxi Province.
    • 舜时黄云兴于郊野 - Historical reference to the Yellow Cloud appearing in the fields during the time of Emperor Shun, symbolizing good fortune.
    • 夏代白云蔽于都邑 - Historical reference to the White Cloud covering the capital cities during the Xia Dynasty, symbolizing peace.
    • 殷代玄云覆于林薮 - Historical reference to the Black Cloud covering the forests and swamps during the Shang Dynasty, symbolizing prosperity.
    • 羽山 - Mount Yu, a mythical mountain.
    • 火浣布 - Fire-washed cloth, a type of fabric that can be cleaned by fire.
    • 文石 - Ornamented stones.
    • 净火 - Pure Fire, a term for the fire produced by the stones.
    • 虞舜 - Emperor Shun, a legendary ruler of ancient China.
    • 汉末 - The end of the Han Dynasty.
    • 梁冀 - Liang Ji, a powerful official during the Eastern Han Dynasty.
    • 丹衣 - Red Robe, a type of clothing.
    • 单衣 - Single robe, a type of clothing.
    • 缝掖 - Sewing the sleeves, a term for the single robe.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋太康元年 - The first year of the Taikang era of the Jin Dynasty.
    • 灞水 - The Ba River.
    • 有司 - Officials.
    • 舜 - Emperor Shun.
    • 夏 - Xia Dynasty.
    • 殷 - Shang Dynasty.
    • 羽山 - Mount Yu.
    • 火浣布 - Fire-washed cloth.
    • 文石 - Ornamented stones.
    • 净火 - Pure Fire.
    • 虞舜 - Emperor Shun.
    • 汉末 - The end of the Han Dynasty.
    • 梁冀 - Liang Ji.
    • 丹衣 - Red Robe.
    • 单衣 - Single robe.
    • 缝掖 - Sewing the sleeves.

    In the first year of the Taikang era of the Jin Dynasty, a white cloud rose over the Ba River and disappeared after three days. Officials reported: "This is a sign that the world will be peaceful." The emperor asked the reason, and they replied: "In the time of Emperor Shun, a yellow cloud appeared in the fields; during the Xia Dynasty, a white cloud covered the capital cities; during the Shang Dynasty, a black cloud covered the forests and swamps. These are all signs of peace and prosperity for the world, and distant lands should offer their unique goods as tribute." Indeed, people from Mount Yu offered 10,000 bolts of fire-washed cloth. The people of the country said: "On Mount Yu, there are ornamented stones that produce fire, with the color of the smoke changing with the seasons, and are called 'Pure Fire.' Unclean clothes, when thrown onto the fire-stones, become clean as if freshly washed." During the time of Emperor Shun, the country offered yellow cloth; at the end of the Han Dynasty, it offered red cloth, which was made into clothing by Liang Ji and called "Red Robe." Historians say: "The single robe is now known as 'sewing the sleeves.'" The characters are different but the pronunciation is the same, and it is unclear which is correct. [137]

    录曰：帝王之兴，叶休祥之应，天无隐祥，地无蓄宝，是以因神物以表运，见星云以观德。按《周官》有冯相氏，以观祥录之数。晋以金德，故白云起于灞水。《山海经》及《异物志》云：“燃洲之兽，生于火中，以毛织为布，虽有垢腻，投火则洁净也。”两说不同，故偕录焉。
    　　因墀国献五足兽，状如狮子；玉钱千缗，其形如环，环重十两，上有“天寿永吉”之字。问其使者五足兽是何变化，对曰：“东方有解形之民，使头飞于南海，左手飞于东山，右手飞于西泽，自脐以下，两足孤立。至暮，头还肩上，两手遇疾风飘于海外，落玄洲之上，化为五足兽，则一指为一足也。其人既失两手，使傍人割里肉以为两臂，宛然如旧也。”因墀国在西域之北，送使者以铁为车轮，十年方至晋。及还，轮皆绝锐，莫知其远近也。 [138]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [138]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 帝王之兴 - Refers to the rise of a ruler or dynasty.
    • 叶休祥之应 - Means 'the response of auspicious signs,' indicating divine approval or prophecy.
    • 天无隐祥，地无蓄宝 - A poetic expression meaning 'heaven does not hide auspicious signs, and earth does not hoard treasures,' emphasizing the idea that divine favor and natural resources are openly available.
    • 因神物以表运，见星云以观德 - A formal statement meaning 'by using divine objects to indicate the course of destiny, and observing stars and clouds to discern virtue,' highlighting the connection between celestial phenomena and moral qualities.
    • 按《周官》有冯相氏，以观祥录之数 - Refers to the *Zhouguan* (Rites of Zhou) and the office of Fengxiangshi, who observed auspicious signs and recorded their meanings.
    • 晋以金德，故白云起于灞水 - A historical reference to the Jin dynasty, which was associated with the metal element, and the appearance of white clouds over the Ba River as a sign of its legitimacy.
    • 《山海经》及《异物志》 - Refers to two classical texts: the *Shanhaijing* (Classic of Mountains and Seas) and the *Yiwuzhi* (Record of Strange Things).
    • 燃洲之兽，生于火中，以毛织为布，虽有垢腻，投火则洁净也 - Describes a mythical creature from the *Shanhaijing* and *Yiwuzhi* that lives in fire and whose fur can be woven into fabric that cleanses itself when burned.
    • 两说不同，故偕录焉 - A concluding remark meaning 'since the two accounts differ, both are recorded,' indicating the author's approach to handling conflicting information.
    • 因墀国献五足兽，状如狮子 - Refers to a five-legged beast from the country of Yincheng, described as lion-like.
    • 玉钱千缗，其形如环，环重十两，上有“天寿永吉”之字 - Describes a thousand strings of jade coins, each string containing ten coins, with the characters 'Tian Shou Yong Ji' (Long Life and Eternal Good Fortune) inscribed on them.
    • 问其使者五足兽是何变化 - Asks the envoy about the transformation of the five-legged beast.
    • 东方有解形之民，使头飞于南海，左手飞于东山，右手飞于西泽，自脐以下，两足孤立 - Describes a mythical people from the east who can detach parts of their bodies.
    • 至暮，头还肩上，两手遇疾风飘于海外，落玄洲之上，化为五足兽，则一指为一足也 - Explains the transformation process where the head returns to the shoulder, and the hands are carried away by the wind to become the legs of the five-legged beast.
    • 其人既失两手，使傍人割里肉以为两臂，宛然如旧也 - Describes how the people regrow their arms by cutting flesh from others.
    • 因墀国在西域之北，送使者以铁为车轮，十年方至晋 - Indicates the location of Yincheng and the long journey of the envoy.
    • 及还，轮皆绝锐，莫知其远近也 - Notes the wear and tear on the wheels and the difficulty in determining the distance traveled.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 帝王之兴 - Refers to the establishment of a new dynasty or the rise of a ruler.
    • 叶休祥之应 - Involves the concept of auspicious signs and divine approval.
    • 天无隐祥，地无蓄宝 - Reflects a belief in the transparency and generosity of nature.
    • 因神物以表运，见星云以观德 - Highlights the importance of celestial and natural phenomena in ancient Chinese cosmology.
    • 按《周官》有冯相氏，以观祥录之数 - Refers to the *Zhouguan* and the role of Fengxiangshi in divination.
    • 晋以金德，故白云起于灞水 - Relates to the Five Elements theory and the legitimacy of the Jin dynasty.
    • 《山海经》及《异物志》 - Are classical texts that document mythical creatures and strange phenomena.
    • 燃洲之兽，生于火中，以毛织为布，虽有垢腻，投火则洁净也 - Describes a creature from these texts that has practical uses.
    • 两说不同，故偕录焉 - Reflects the author's method of recording multiple perspectives.
    • 因墀国献五足兽，状如狮子 - Involves a mythical creature from a distant land.
    • 玉钱千缗，其形如环，环重十两，上有“天寿永吉”之字 - Describes a valuable gift with symbolic meaning.
    • 问其使者五足兽是何变化 - Involves a detailed explanation of a transformation.
    • 东方有解形之民，使头飞于南海，左手飞于东山，右手飞于西泽，自脐以下，两足孤立 - Describes a mythical people with extraordinary abilities.
    • 至暮，头还肩上，两手遇疾风飘于海外，落玄洲之上，化为五足兽，则一指为一足也 - Explains the transformation process.
    • 其人既失两手，使傍人割里肉以为两臂，宛然如旧也 - Describes a unique method of regrowing limbs.
    • 因墀国在西域之北，送使者以铁为车轮，十年方至晋 - Indicates the geographical location and the long journey.
    • 及还，轮皆绝锐，莫知其远近也 - Reflects the difficulty in measuring long distances.

    Proper Nouns

    • 帝王 - Refers to a ruler or dynasty.
    • 周官 - Refers to the *Zhouguan* (Rites of Zhou).
    • 冯相氏 - Refers to the office of Fengxiangshi.
    • 晋 - Refers to the Jin dynasty.
    • 山海经 - Refers to the *Shanhaijing* (Classic of Mountains and Seas).
    • 异物志 - Refers to the *Yiwuzhi* (Record of Strange Things).
    • 燃洲 - Refers to a mythical place.
    • 因墀国 - Refers to the country of Yincheng.
    • 玄洲 - Refers to a mythical place.
    • 西域 - Refers to the Western Regions.
    • 晋 - Refers to the Jin dynasty.

    Record states: The rise of a ruler or dynasty is marked by auspicious signs, as heaven does not hide such omens, and earth does not hoard treasures. Therefore, divine objects are used to indicate the course of destiny, and stars and clouds are observed to discern virtue. According to the Zhouguan (Rites of Zhou), there is an office called Fengxiangshi, who observes auspicious signs and records their meanings. The Jin dynasty, associated with the metal element, saw white clouds arise over the Ba River. The Shanhaijing (Classic of Mountains and Seas) and the Yiwuzhi (Record of Strange Things) describe a creature from the land of Ran Zhou that is born in fire, whose fur can be woven into fabric that cleanses itself when burned. Since these two accounts differ, both are recorded here.

    The country of Yincheng presented a five-legged beast, resembling a lion, and a thousand strings of jade coins, each string containing ten coins, with the characters "Tian Shou Yong Ji" (Long Life and Eternal Good Fortune) inscribed on them. When asked the envoy about the transformation of the five-legged beast, he replied: "There are people in the east who can detach parts of their bodies. They send their head flying to the South Sea, their left hand to the Eastern Mountain, and their right hand to the Western Marsh, leaving only their lower body and two legs. By evening, their head returns to their shoulder, and their hands are carried away by the wind to the land of Xuanzhou, where they transform into the legs of the five-legged beast, with each finger becoming a leg. After losing their hands, they cut flesh from others to regrow their arms, making them appear as before." The country of Yincheng is located to the north of the Western Regions, and the envoy traveled there with iron-wheeled carts, taking ten years to reach the Jin dynasty. Upon his return, the wheels were worn out, and the distance could not be measured. [138]

    太始元年，魏帝为陈留王之岁，有频斯国人来朝，以五色玉为衣，如今之铠。其使不食中国滋味，自赍金壶，壶中有浆，凝如脂，尝一滴则寿千岁。其国有大枫木成林，高六七十里，善算者以里计之，雷电常出树之半。其枝交荫于上，蔽不见日月之光。其下平净扫洒，雨雾不能入焉。树东有大石室，可容万人坐。壁上刻为三皇之像：天皇十三头，地皇十一头，人皇九头，皆龙身。亦有膏烛之处。缉石为床，床上有膝痕深三寸。床前有竹简长尺二寸，书大篆之文，皆言开辟以来事，人莫能识。或言是伏羲画卦之时有此书，或言是仓颉造书之处。 [139]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [139]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 太始元年 - The first year of the Tai Shi era, a specific period in Chinese history.
    • 五色玉為衣 - Clothing made of five-colored jade, indicating a rare and precious material.
    • 如今之铠 - Comparison to modern armor, suggesting a resemblance in appearance.
    • 不食中国滋味 - Does not eat Chinese flavors, indicating a preference or dietary restriction.
    • 自赍金壶 - Carries a golden jug, indicating a personal possession.
    • 壶中有浆，凝如脂 - Describes the contents of the jug as a thick, oily liquid.
    • 尝一滴则寿千岁 - Tasting a drop grants a thousand years of life, a superstitious belief.
    • 大枫木成林 - Refers to a vast forest of large fir trees.
    • 高六七十里 - Indicates the height of the trees, approximately 60-70 li (an ancient Chinese unit of distance).
    • 善算者以里计之 - Skilled calculators measure it in li, indicating a method of measurement.
    • 雷电常出树之半 - Describes lightning frequently occurring halfway up the trees.
    • 枝交荫于上，蔽不见日月之光 - The branches intertwine above, blocking the view of the sun and moon.
    • 其下平净扫洒，雨雾不能入焉 - Describes the ground below as clean and dry, unaffected by rain or fog.
    • 大石室 - Refers to a large stone chamber.
    • 可容万人坐 - Can accommodate ten thousand people sitting.
    • 壁上刻为三皇之像 - Describes carvings of the Three Sovereigns on the walls.
    • 天皇十三头，地皇十一头，人皇九头 - Describes the Three Sovereigns with varying numbers of heads.
    • 皆龙身 - All have dragon bodies, indicating a mythical or divine nature.
    • 亦有膏烛之处 - Refers to areas where lamps or candles are lit.
    • 缉石为床 - Stones are woven into beds, indicating a unique construction.
    • 床上有膝痕深三寸 - Describes deep knee marks on the bed.
    • 床前有竹简长尺二寸，书大篆之文 - Refers to bamboo slips with large seal script writing.
    • 皆言开辟以来事 - All record events since the beginning of time.
    • 人莫能识 - No one can understand, indicating the complexity or antiquity of the writing.
    • 或言是伏羲画卦之时有此书 - Suggests that the book might date back to the time of Fuxi drawing the trigrams.
    • 或言是仓颉造书之处 - Suggests that the place might be where Cangjie invented writing.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 太始元年 - The first year of the Tai Shi era, a specific period in Chinese history.
    • 魏帝 - The emperor of the Wei dynasty, a significant figure in the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 陳留王 - A title used during the Three Kingdoms period, indicating a prince or king.
    • 頻斯國 - An ancient kingdom, possibly a reference to a specific region or people.
    • 三皇 - The Three Sovereigns, legendary rulers in Chinese mythology.
    • 伏羲 - A mythical figure credited with inventing writing and drawing the trigrams.
    • 仓颉 - Another mythical figure credited with inventing writing.

    Proper Nouns

    • 太始元年 - The first year of the Tai Shi era.
    • 魏帝 - The emperor of the Wei dynasty.
    • 陳留王 - A title used during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 頻斯國 - An ancient kingdom.
    • 三皇 - The Three Sovereigns.
    • 伏羲 - A mythical figure.
    • 仓颉 - Another mythical figure.

    In the first year of the Tai Shi era, during the reign of the Wei emperor as the Prince of Chenliu, envoys from the kingdom of Pin Si came to pay homage. They wore clothing made of five-colored jade, resembling modern armor. The envoys did not consume Chinese flavors and carried a golden jug containing a thick, oily liquid; tasting a single drop would grant a thousand years of life. The kingdom had a vast forest of large fir trees, reaching heights of sixty to seventy li. Skilled calculators measured these heights in li, and lightning frequently occurred halfway up the trees. The branches intertwined above, blocking the view of the sun and moon. The ground below was clean and dry, unaffected by rain or fog. To the east of the trees stood a large stone chamber that could accommodate ten thousand people sitting. The walls were carved with images of the Three Sovereigns: the Heavenly Sovereign with thirteen heads, the Earthly Sovereign with eleven heads, and the Human Sovereign with nine heads, all with dragon bodies. There were also areas where lamps or candles were lit. Stones were woven into beds, and deep knee marks were visible on the beds. Before the beds lay bamboo slips, each a foot and two inches long, inscribed with large seal script. These writings recorded events since the beginning of time, none of which could be understood. Some said that the book dated back to the time of Fuxi drawing the trigrams, while others believed it was the place where Cangjie invented writing. [139]

    傍有丹石井，非人之所凿，下及漏泉，水常沸涌，诸仙欲饮之时，以长绠引汲也。其国人皆多力，不食五谷，日中无影，饮桂浆云雾。羽毛为衣，发大如?娄，坚韧如筋，伸之几至一丈，置之自缩如蠡。续人发以为绳，汲丹井之水，久久方得升之水。水中有白蛙，两翅，常来去井上，仙者食之。至周，王子晋临井而窥，有青雀衔玉杓以授子晋，子晋取而食之，乃有云起雪飞。子晋以衣袖挥云，则云雪自止。白蛙化为双白鸠入云，望之遂灭。皆频斯国之所记，盖其人年不可测也。使图其国山川地势瑰异之属，以示张华。华云：“此神异之国，难可验信。”以车马珍服送之出关。 [140]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [140]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 傍有丹石井 - Beside is a red stone well, describing a well made of red stone.
    • 非人之所凿 - Not made by humans, indicating that the well is natural or supernatural.
    • 下及漏泉，水常沸涌 - Describes a spring or underground water source that constantly bubbles and surges.
    • 诸仙欲饮之时，以长绠引汲也 - When the immortals wish to drink, they use a long rope to draw the water, indicating a method used by immortals to access the water.
    • 其国人皆多力，不食五谷 - The people of that country are all strong, and they do not eat the five grains, suggesting a unique diet or lifestyle.
    • 日中无影 - There is no shadow at noon, a phenomenon that suggests unusual conditions or magical properties.
    • 饮桂浆云雾 - Drink mist and clouds flavored with osmanthus, indicating a mystical and fragrant beverage.
    • 羽毛为衣 - Clothing made of feathers, describing the attire of the people.
    • 发大如娄，坚韧如筋 - Hair as large as a loofah and as tough as sinew, describing the unusual characteristics of their hair.
    • 伸之几至一丈，置之自缩如蠡 - When stretched, it can reach nearly a zhang (about 3.3 meters), and when placed, it retracts itself like a snail, describing the elasticity and retractability of their hair.
    • 续人发以为绳，汲丹井之水 - Use human hair to make ropes and draw water from the red well, indicating a method used to access the water.
    • 久久方得升之水 - It takes a long time to draw a single measure of water, emphasizing the difficulty of obtaining water.
    • 水中有白蛙，两翅，常来去井上 - Describes a white frog with wings that frequently flies around the well.
    • 仙者食之 - Immortals eat it, indicating the significance of the frog.
    • 至周，王子晋临井而窥 - During the Zhou dynasty, Prince Jin of Zhou approached the well and looked inside, introducing a historical figure.
    • 有青雀衔玉杓以授子晋 - A green bird carried a jade ladle and gave it to Prince Jin, describing a supernatural event.
    • 子晋取而食之，乃有云起雪飞 - Prince Jin took it and ate it, and then clouds and snow rose up, indicating a magical reaction.
    • 子晋以衣袖挥云，则云雪自止 - Prince Jin waved his sleeve, and the clouds and snow stopped, describing a magical action.
    • 白蛙化为双白鸠入云，望之遂灭 - The white frog transformed into two white doves and flew into the clouds, disappearing from sight, indicating a transformation.
    • 皆频斯国之所记，盖其人年不可测也 - All these are recorded by the people of the country, and their age is immeasurable, suggesting the longevity and mystery of the people.
    • 使图其国山川地势瑰异之属，以示张华 - He asked to draw a map of the country's mountains, rivers, and strange landscapes to show Zhang Hua, indicating a request to document the place.
    • 华云：“此神异之国，难可验信。” - Zhang Hua said, 'This is a divine and extraordinary country, difficult to verify,' expressing skepticism.
    • 以车马珍服送之出关 - He sent him out of the pass with carriages, horses, and fine clothing, indicating a respectful farewell.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 周 - Refers to the Zhou dynasty, a significant period in Chinese history.
    • 王子晋 - Refers to Prince Jin of Zhou, a legendary figure known for his wisdom and supernatural abilities.
    • 张华 - Refers to Zhang Hua, a famous scholar and official of the Western Jin dynasty, known for his knowledge of the supernatural.
    • 丹井 - A red well, often associated with immortals and magical properties in Chinese mythology.
    • 青雀 - A green bird, often a messenger in Chinese mythology.
    • 玉杓 - A jade ladle, a symbol of purity and divinity.
    • 云雾 - Clouds and mist, often associated with the realm of immortals.
    • 白蛙 - A white frog, often a symbol of transformation and magic.
    • 白鸠 - A white dove, often a symbol of peace and transformation.

    Proper Nouns

    • 周 - The Zhou dynasty.
    • 王子晋 - Prince Jin of Zhou.
    • 张华 - Zhang Hua.
    • 丹井 - The red well.
    • 青雀 - The green bird.
    • 玉杓 - The jade ladle.
    • 白蛙 - The white frog.
    • 白鸠 - The white dove.

    Beside the red stone well, not made by humans, the underground spring bubbles and surges constantly. When the immortals wish to drink, they use a long rope to draw the water. The people of that country are all strong and do not eat the five grains; there is no shadow at noon, and they drink mist and clouds flavored with osmanthus. Their clothing is made of feathers, and their hair is as large as a loofah and as tough as sinew. When stretched, it can reach nearly a zhang (about 3.3 meters), and when placed, it retracts itself like a snail. They use human hair to make ropes and draw water from the red well, and it takes a long time to draw a single measure of water. There is a white frog with wings that frequently flies around the well, and the immortals eat it. During the Zhou dynasty, Prince Jin of Zhou approached the well and looked inside. A green bird carried a jade ladle and gave it to Prince Jin, who took it and ate it, and then clouds and snow rose up. Prince Jin waved his sleeve, and the clouds and snow stopped. The white frog transformed into two white doves and flew into the clouds, disappearing from sight. All these are recorded by the people of the country, and their age is immeasurable. He asked to draw a map of the country's mountains, rivers, and strange landscapes to show Zhang Hua. Zhang Hua said, "This is a divine and extraordinary country, difficult to verify." He sent him out of the pass with carriages, horses, and fine clothing. [140]

    张华字茂先，挺生聪慧之德，好观秘异图纬之部，捃采天下遗逸，自书契之始，考验神怪，及世间闾里所说，造《博物志》四百卷，奏于武帝。帝诏诘问：“卿才综万代，博识无伦，远冠羲皇，近次夫子。然记事采言，亦多浮妄，宜更删翦，无以冗长成文。昔仲尼删《诗》、《书》，不及鬼神幽昧之事，以言怪力乱神。今卿《博物志》，惊所未闻，异所未见，将恐惑乱于后生，繁芜于耳目，可更芟截浮疑，分为十卷。”即于御前赐青铁砚，此铁是于阗国所出，献而铸为砚也。赐麟角笔，以麟角为笔管，此辽西国所献。侧理纸万番，此南越所献。后人言“陟里”，与“侧理”相乱，南人以海苔为纸，其理纵横邪侧，因以为名。帝常以《博物志》十卷置于函中，暇日览焉。 [141]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [141]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 挺生 - Classical expression meaning 'naturally endowed' or 'innately gifted,' often used to describe someone with extraordinary talent or virtue.
    • 秘异图纬之部 - Refers to esoteric and mysterious texts, including divination and cosmological works.
    • 捃采 - Means 'to gather' or 'to collect,' often used in the context of compiling knowledge or materials.
    • 书契之始 - Refers to the beginning of written records or the origins of writing.
    • 考验 - Means 'to test' or 'to verify,' often used in the context of examining or validating knowledge.
    • 闾里所说 - Refers to stories or tales circulating among the common people in the streets and neighborhoods.
    • 造《博物志》 - Means 'to compile the *Bamboo Annals of Natural History*,' a reference to the author's work.
    • 博识无伦 - Means 'extremely knowledgeable' or 'unparalleled in learning.'
    • 远冠羲皇 - Refers to surpassing the ancient sages Fuxi and Shen Nong, symbolizing the highest level of wisdom.
    • 近次夫子 - Means 'comparable to Confucius,' indicating the author's near-equal status with the great sage.
    • 记事采言 - Means 'to record events and collect words,' referring to the process of compiling historical and anecdotal material.
    • 浮妄 - Means 'superficial' or 'unsubstantiated,' often used to criticize unreliable or exaggerated claims.
    • 删翦 - Means 'to edit' or 'to prune,' referring to the process of refining or condensing a work.
    • 冗长 - Means 'prolix' or 'wordy,' criticizing excessive length.
    • 鬼神幽昧之事 - Refers to matters related to ghosts, spirits, and the mysterious or supernatural.
    • 怪力乱神 - A phrase from the *Analects* meaning 'to believe in strange powers and spirits,' often used to criticize superstition.
    • 异所未见 - Means 'things never seen before,' referring to unusual or extraordinary phenomena.
    • 惑乱于后生 - Means 'to confuse or mislead future generations.'
    • 繁芜于耳目 - Means 'to overwhelm the senses with excessive or irrelevant information.'
    • 芟截浮疑 - Means 'to cut out vague or uncertain parts,' referring to the process of refining a work.
    • 置于函中 - Means 'to place in a box or case,' often used to describe the storage of important documents.
    • 暇日览焉 - Means 'to read leisurely on a spare day,' indicating a relaxed and unhurried approach to reading.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 张华字茂先 - Zhang Hua, a prominent scholar and statesman of the Western Jin dynasty, known for his literary and scientific achievements.
    • 武帝 - Emperor Wu of Jin, the founder of the Western Jin dynasty, known for his patronage of learning and culture.
    • 《博物志》 - Zhang Hua's encyclopedic work, which covers a wide range of topics, including history, geography, mythology, and natural science.
    • 青铁砚 - A type of inkstone made from blue iron, a rare and valuable material.
    • 麟角笔 - A brush made from the horn of a unicorn (a mythical creature), symbolizing rarity and excellence.
    • 侧理纸 - A type of paper made from seaweed or other materials, often used for writing in ancient China.
    • 陟里 - A variant reading of '侧理,' referring to the same type of paper.

    Proper Nouns

    • 张华 - Zhang Hua, the author of the *Bamboo Annals of Natural History*.
    • 武帝 - Emperor Wu of Jin.
    • 《博物志》 - The *Bamboo Annals of Natural History*.
    • 青铁砚 - A blue iron inkstone.
    • 麟角笔 - A brush made from the horn of a unicorn.
    • 侧理纸 - A type of paper made from seaweed or other materials.
    • 于阗国 - The kingdom of Khotan, a source of rare materials.
    • 辽西国 - The kingdom of Liaoxi, another source of rare materials.
    • 南越 - The kingdom of Nanyue, a source of the side-ri paper.

    Zhang Hua, courtesy name Maoxian, was naturally endowed with the virtue of wisdom. He had a keen interest in esoteric and mysterious texts, including divination and cosmological works. He gathered knowledge from all over the world, starting from the origins of writing, and verified the existence of supernatural beings and the stories circulating among the common people. He compiled the Bamboo Annals of Natural History in four hundred volumes and presented it to Emperor Wu of Jin. The emperor issued an edict questioning him: "Your talent encompasses all ages, and your knowledge is unparalleled. You surpass the ancient sages Fuxi and Shen Nong, and are comparable to Confucius. However, your records of events and collected words contain many superficial and unsubstantiated claims. It is advisable to edit and prune them to avoid excessive length. In the past, Confucius edited the Book of Poetry and Book of Documents, but he did not include matters related to ghosts, spirits, and the mysterious, as he believed in strange powers and spirits. Your Bamboo Annals of Natural History contains many things never seen before, and may confuse future generations and overwhelm their senses. It is suggested that you cut out vague and uncertain parts and divide it into ten volumes." Immediately, the emperor bestowed upon him an inkstone made from blue iron, which was sourced from the kingdom of Khotan and presented as a gift. He also granted him a brush made from the horn of a unicorn, with the horn serving as the brush tube, which was a gift from the kingdom of Liaoxi. Additionally, he received ten thousand sheets of side-ri paper, a gift from the kingdom of Nanyue. Later, people referred to this paper as "Zhi Li," which was confused with "Ce Li." The people of the south made paper from seaweed, which had a texture that was irregular and slanted, and thus named it accordingly. The emperor often placed the ten volumes of the Bamboo Annals of Natural History in a box and read them leisurely on his spare days. [141]

    惠帝元熙二年，改为永平元年，常山郡献伤魂鸟，状如鸡，毛色似凤。帝恶其名，弃而不纳，复爱其毛羽。当时博物者云：“黄帝杀蚩尤，有貙、虎误噬一妇人，七日气不绝，黄帝哀之，葬以重棺石椁。有鸟翔其冢上，其声自呼为伤魂，则此妇人之灵也。”后人不得其令终者，此鸟来集其国园林之中。至汉哀、平之末，王莽多杀伐贤良，其鸟亟来哀鸣。时人疾此鸟名，使常山郡国弹射驱之。至晋初，干戈始戢，四海攸归，山野间时见此鸟。憎其名，改“伤魂”为“相弘”。及封孙皓为归命侯，相弘之义，叶于此矣。永平之末，死伤多故，门嗟巷哭，常山有献，遂放逐之。 [142]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [142]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 改为 - Formal way to denote a change in era names.
    • 献 - To present or offer, indicating that the bird was presented as an offering.
    • 恶其名 - Disliked its name, suggesting that the emperor found the name of the bird unpleasant or ominous.
    • 弃而不纳 - Abandoned and did not accept, indicating that the emperor rejected the offering.
    • 复爱 - Again loved, suggesting that despite disliking the name, the emperor still appreciated the bird's appearance.
    • 博物者 - Those knowledgeable in various fields, referring to scholars or experts who had knowledge of natural history and folklore.
    • 七日气不绝 - Her breath did not cease for seven days, indicating that the woman's spirit lingered.
    • 葬以重棺石椁 - Buried her in a double coffin and stone sarcophagus, showing the emperor's respect and care.
    • 翔其冢上 - Flew over her tomb, describing the bird's behavior.
    • 其声自呼为伤魂 - Its voice called itself 'injured soul', describing the bird's name.
    • 不得其令终 - Did not have a good end, suggesting that the bird's presence was a sign of misfortune.
    • 此鸟来集其国园林之中 - This bird would gather in the gardens of the country, indicating the bird's association with misfortune.
    • 时人疾此鸟名 - People at the time disliked the bird's name, showing the people's reaction.
    • 使常山郡国弹射驱之 - Ordered the people of Changshan to shoot and drive it away, showing the action taken.
    • 干戈始戢 - Weapons were put away, indicating a period of stability.
    • 四海攸归 - All under heaven returned to peace, indicating a return to normalcy.
    • 山野间时见此鸟 - The bird was sometimes seen in the mountains and fields, suggesting a return of the bird.
    • 憎其名 - Disliked its name, showing the renaming of the bird.
    • 改‘伤魂’为‘相弘’ - Changed 'injured soul' to 'mutual prosperity', showing the renaming of the bird.
    • 及封孙皓为归命侯 - When Sun Hao was enfeoffed as the Marquis of Surrender, providing a historical context for the renaming.
    • 相弘之义，叶于此矣 - The meaning of mutual prosperity was fulfilled here, providing a historical context for the renaming.
    • 永平之末 - The end of the Yongping era, marking a period of suffering.
    • 死伤多故 - Many deaths and injuries occurred, describing a period of suffering.
    • 门嗟巷哭 - Doors and streets were filled with lamentation, describing a period of suffering.
    • 常山有献，遂放逐之 - Changshan presented it, and it was subsequently expelled, showing the final action taken.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 惠帝元熙二年 - Refers to the second year of the Yuanxi era during the reign of Emperor Huai of Jin (265-289 AD).
    • 永平元年 - Refers to the first year of the Yongping era, also during the reign of Emperor Huai of Jin.
    • 常山郡 - A region in present-day Hebei Province, historically significant for its contributions to the imperial court.
    • 伤魂鸟 - A mythical bird with a tragic origin, often associated with omens and misfortune.
    • 黄帝杀蚩尤 - A legendary battle between the Yellow Emperor and Chiyou, a significant event in Chinese mythology.
    • 貙、虎误噬一妇人 - A tragic event involving a tiger and a leopard mistakenly devouring a woman, leading to a burial ritual.
    • 汉哀、平之末 - The end of the reigns of Emperor Ai and Emperor Ping, marking the decline of the Western Han dynasty.
    • 王莽多杀伐贤良 - Wang Mang, a usurper who established the Xin dynasty, is known for his harsh policies and persecution of virtuous people.
    • 晋初 - The early years of the Jin dynasty, a period of relative stability after the chaos of the Three Kingdoms.
    • 孙皓为归命侯 - Sun Hao, the last ruler of the Eastern Wu, was enfeoffed as the Marquis of Surrender after the fall of his kingdom.

    In the second year of the Yuanxi era of Emperor Huai, the era name was changed to Yongping. The Changshan region presented an injured soul bird, which looked like a chicken and had feathers the color of a phoenix. The emperor disliked the name of the bird and abandoned it without accepting it, but he still loved its plumage. At the time, scholars knowledgeable in various fields said, "The Yellow Emperor killed Chiyou, and a tiger and a leopard mistakenly devoured a woman. Her breath did not cease for seven days, and the Yellow Emperor mourned her, burying her in a double coffin and stone sarcophagus. A bird flew over her tomb, and its voice called itself 'injured soul,' which is the spirit of this woman." Later, those who did not have a good end would see this bird gathering in the gardens of their country. During the end of the reigns of Emperor Ai and Emperor Ping, Wang Mang killed many virtuous people, and the bird frequently came and mourned. People at the time disliked the bird's name and ordered the people of Changshan to shoot and drive it away. By the early years of the Jin dynasty, when weapons were put away and all under heaven returned to peace, the bird was sometimes seen in the mountains and fields. People disliked its name and changed 'injured soul' to 'mutual prosperity.' When Sun Hao was enfeoffed as the Marquis of Surrender, the meaning of mutual prosperity was fulfilled here. By the end of the Yongping era, there were many deaths and injuries, and doors and streets were filled with lamentation. Changshan presented the bird again, and it was subsequently expelled. [142]

    太始十年，有浮支国献望舒草，其色红，叶如荷，近望则如卷荷，远望则如舒荷，团团似盖。亦云，月出则荷舒，月没则叶卷。植于宫中，因穿池广百步，名曰望舒荷池。愍帝之末，移入胡，胡人将种还胡中。至今绝矣，池亦填塞。
    　　祖梁国献蔓金苔，色如黄金，若萤火之聚。大如鸡卵，投于水中，蔓延于波澜之上，光出照日，皆如火生水上也。乃于宫中穿池，广百步，时观此苔，以乐宫人。宫人有幸者，以金苔赐之，置漆盘中，照耀满室，名曰“夜明苔”；着衣襟则如火光。帝虑外人得之，有惑百姓，诏使除苔塞池。及皇家丧乱，犹有此物，皆入胡中。 [143]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [143]

    Idiomatic Expressions
