    • 近望则如卷荷，远望则如舒荷 - Metaphorical description of the grass's appearance: when viewed up close, it looks like a rolled lotus leaf; when viewed from afar, it looks like an unfurled lotus leaf.
    • 团团似盖 - Descriptive phrase comparing the grass to an umbrella: it is round like an umbrella.
    • 亦云，月出则荷舒，月没则叶卷 - Poetic description of the grass's behavior in relation to the moon: it is also said that when the moon rises, the lotus unfurls, and when the moon sets, the leaves roll up.
    • 色如黄金，若萤火之聚 - Descriptive phrase comparing the moss to gold and fireflies: its color is like gold, and it looks like a cluster of fireflies.
    • 大如鸡卵 - Size comparison: it is as large as a chicken's egg.
    • 投于水中，蔓延于波澜之上 - Behavior of the moss in water: when thrown into the water, it spreads over the waves.
    • 光出照日，皆如火生水上也 - Metaphorical description of the moss's luminosity: its light shines like the sun, and all of it looks like fire on the water.
    • 着衣襟则如火光 - Effect of the moss when worn: when worn on the collar, it looks like firelight.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 太始 - Refers to a specific era in Chinese history, though the exact details of this era are not provided in the text.
    • 浮支国 - A fictional or historical country mentioned in the text, possibly a reference to a foreign land.
    • 愍帝 - Refers to Emperor Min, a ruler of a specific dynasty in Chinese history.
    • 胡 - Refers to a region or people, possibly the Xiongnu or other nomadic tribes, often associated with the northern frontier of China.
    • 祖梁国 - Another fictional or historical country mentioned in the text, possibly a reference to a foreign land.

    Proper Nouns

    • 望舒草 - A type of grass mentioned in the text.
    • 蔓金苔 - A type of moss mentioned in the text.
    • 夜明苔 - The name given to the moss.

    In the tenth year of the Tai Shi era, a country called Fuzhi presented the Wanshu grass to the court. Its color was red, and its leaves resembled lotus leaves. When viewed up close, it looked like a rolled lotus leaf, and when viewed from afar, it looked like an unfurled lotus leaf. It was round like an umbrella. It was also said that when the moon rose, the lotus unfurled, and when the moon set, the leaves rolled up. The grass was planted in the palace, and a pond one hundred steps wide was dug, named the Wanshu Lotus Pond. At the end of Emperor Min's reign, it was moved into the Hu region, where the Hu people took the seeds back with them. To this day, it has been lost, and the pond has also been filled in.

    A country called Zuliang presented the Manjin苔 (a type of moss) to the court. Its color was like gold, and it looked like a cluster of fireflies. It was as large as a chicken's egg. When thrown into the water, it spread over the waves, and its light shone like the sun, all of it looking like fire on the water. Therefore, a pond was dug in the palace, one hundred steps wide, to enjoy the palace ladies by viewing this moss. Palace ladies who were favored were given the gold moss, placed in a lacquer tray, illuminating the entire room. It was named 'Night-Bright Moss'; when worn on the collar, it looked like firelight. The emperor was concerned that outsiders might obtain it and deceive the people, so he issued an edict to remove the moss and fill in the pond. During the time of the royal family's decline and chaos, there still existed this thing, all of which entered the Hu region. [143]

    石季伦爱婢名翔风，魏末于胡中得之。年始十岁，使房内养之。至十五，无有比其容貌，特以姿态见美。妙别玉声，巧观金色。石氏之富，方比王家，骄侈当世，珍宝奇异，视如瓦砾，积如粪土，皆殊方异国所得，莫有辨识其出处者。乃使翔风别其声色，悉知其处。言西方北方，玉声沉重而性温润，佩服者益人性灵；东方南方，玉声轻洁而性清凉，佩服者利人精神。石氏侍人，美艳者数千人，翔风最以文辞擅爱。石崇尝语之曰：“吾百年之后，当指白日，以汝为殉。”答曰：“生爱死离，不如无爱，妾得为殉，身其何朽！”于是弥见宠爱。崇常择美容姿相类者十人，装饰衣服大小一等，使忽视不相分别，常侍于侧。 [144]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [144]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 石季倫愛婢名翔風 - Shi Jilun's beloved concubine named Xiang Feng.
    • 吾百年之后，当指白日，以汝为殉 - After I die, I will point to the sun and have you sacrificed as my companion.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 石季倫 - Shi Jilun, a prominent figure during the Jin dynasty, known for his wealth and extravagant lifestyle.
    • 翔風 - The name of Shi Jilun's favorite concubine, who is described as exceptionally beautiful and talented.
    • 魏末 - The end of the Wei dynasty, a period of political turmoil and change.
    • 胡中 - Refers to the Hu people, a term used to describe various nomadic tribes in northern China.
    • 玉声 - The sound of jade, which is often associated with beauty and purity in Chinese culture.
    • 金色 - The color of gold, often symbolizing wealth and luxury.
    • 石氏之富，方比王家 - A comparison between the wealth of the Shi family and the Wang family, both of which were among the wealthiest families of their time.
    • 骄侈当世 - Describes the excessive and luxurious lifestyle of the Shi family.
    • 珍宝奇异 - Refers to rare and exotic treasures, often associated with the wealth of the aristocracy.
    • 殊方异国 - Refers to distant lands and foreign countries, emphasizing the exotic nature of the Shi family's possessions.
    • 吾百年之后，当指白日，以汝为殉 - A reference to the practice of human sacrifice, which was a controversial and taboo topic in Chinese culture.
    • 崇常择美容姿相类者十人，装饰衣服大小一等，使忽视不相分别，常侍于侧 - Describes a practice of keeping multiple concubines indistinguishable, which was a common practice among the wealthy and powerful.

    Proper Nouns

    • 石季倫 - Shi Jilun, a prominent figure during the Jin dynasty.
    • 翔風 - The name of Shi Jilun's favorite concubine.
    • 魏 - The Wei dynasty, a period of Chinese history.
    • 胡 - The Hu people, a term used to describe various nomadic tribes in northern China.
    • 玉 - Jade, a precious stone often associated with beauty and purity.
    • 金 - Gold, a precious metal often symbolizing wealth and luxury.
    • 王家 - The Wang family, another wealthy family of the time.
    • 吾 - A pronoun meaning 'I' or 'me.'
    • 百年 - A hundred years, often used to refer to a long period of time.
    • 白日 - The sun, often used symbolically in Chinese literature.
    • 殉 - Sacrifice, often used in the context of human sacrifice.

    Shi Jilun's beloved concubine was named Xiang Feng. She was obtained at the end of the Wei dynasty from the Hu people. Shi Jilun raised her in the inner chambers. By the age of fifteen, no one could compare to her beauty, especially her graceful demeanor. She could distinguish the sound of jade and observe the color of gold. The wealth of the Shi family was comparable to that of the Wang family. Their extravagance was unmatched in their time. They treated rare treasures as worthless and accumulated them like refuse, all of which came from distant lands and foreign countries, and no one could identify their origins. Shi Jilun then had Xiang Feng distinguish the sounds and colors of these items, and she knew all their origins. She said that jade from the west and north had a heavy sound and a warm, smooth nature, and wearing it enhanced one's spiritual qualities. Jade from the east and south had a light, clean sound and a cool, refreshing nature, and wearing it benefited one's mental clarity. Among the thousands of beautiful women serving the Shi family, Xiang Feng was the most favored for her literary talent. Shi Jilun once told her, "After I die, I will point to the sun and have you sacrificed as my companion." She replied, "It is better to have never loved than to love and then be separated by death. If I can be sacrificed with you, my body will not decay!" As a result, she was even more favored. Shi Jilun often chose ten beautiful women similar in appearance, dressed them identically, and had them stand beside him so that they could not be distinguished. [144]

    使翔风调玉以付工人，为倒龙之佩，萦金为凤冠之钗，言刻玉为倒龙之势，铸金钗象凤皇之冠。结袖绕楹而舞，昼夜相接，谓之“恒舞”。欲有所召，不呼姓名，悉听佩声，视钗色，玉声轻者居前，金色艳者居后，以为行次而进也。使数十人各含异香，行而语笑，则口气从风而扬。又屑沉水之香，如尘末，布象床上，使所爱者践之。无迹者赐以真珠百琲，有迹者节其饮食，令身轻弱。故闺中相戏曰：“尔非细骨轻躯，那得百琲真珠？”及翔风年三十，妙年者争嫉之，或者云“胡女不可为群”，竞相排毁。石崇受谮润之言，即退翔风为房老，使主群少，乃怀怨而作五言诗曰：“春华谁不美，卒伤秋落时。突烟还自低，鄙退岂所期！桂芳徒自蠹，失爱在娥眉。坐见芳时歇，憔悴空自嗤！”石氏房中并歌此为乐曲，至晋末乃止。 [145]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [145]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 使翔风调玉以付工人 - To have Xiangfeng adjust the jade and hand it over to the craftsmen. The phrase '调玉' (adjust the jade) is somewhat ambiguous, as it could imply shaping, polishing, or even enchanting the jade.
    • 为倒龙之佩 - To create a pendant shaped like an inverted dragon. '倒龙' (inverted dragon) is a specific design, but its exact appearance is not specified in the text.
    • 萦金为凤冠之钗 - To weave gold into a hairpin resembling a phoenix crown. '凤冠' (phoenix crown) refers to a ceremonial headpiece worn by empresses or high-ranking women.
    • 结袖绕楹而舞 - To dance with sleeves tied together around the pillars. This describes a specific dance form, possibly a courtly or ritual dance.
    • 昼夜相接，谓之‘恒舞’ - Dancing day and night without interruption, called 'constant dance.' '恒舞' (constant dance) is a specific term for this continuous dance.
    • 欲有所召，不呼姓名，悉听佩声，视钗色 - When calling someone, do not call their name; listen to the sound of the pendant and look at the color of the hairpin. This suggests a mystical or magical aspect to the dance.
    • 玉声轻者居前，金色艳者居后，以为行次而进也 - Those whose jade pendants make a light sound come forward, and those whose golden hairpins are bright come behind, forming the order of the procession. This describes a hierarchical arrangement based on the sounds and colors of the accessories.
    • 使数十人各含异香，行而语笑，则口气从风而扬 - Have dozens of people each hold different fragrances; when they speak and laugh, their breath carries the scent in the wind. This describes a sensory experience during the dance.
    • 又屑沉水之香，如尘末，布象床上，使所爱者践之 - Grind the fragrant wood into powder, spread it on the bed, and let the loved ones step on it. This describes a ritual involving fragrances.
    • 尔非细骨轻躯，那得百琲真珠 - If you are not slender and light, how could you get a hundred strings of pearls? This is a playful taunt, suggesting that only those who are delicate and graceful can earn such rewards.
    • 及翔风年三十，妙年者争嫉之，或者云‘胡女不可为群’，竞相排毁 - When Xiangfeng reached thirty, the young and beautiful women envied her, and some said 'foreign women cannot be part of the group,' competing to denounce her. This describes the social dynamics and jealousy surrounding Xiangfeng.
    • 石崇受谮润之言，即退翔风为房老，使主群少，乃怀怨而作五言诗曰 - Shi Chong, after hearing slanderous words, immediately demoted Xiangfeng to the position of 'house elder,' making her responsible for the young ones, and she then harbored resentment and wrote a five-character poem. This describes the political and social consequences of the slander.
    • 春华谁不美，卒伤秋落时 - Who does not find spring flowers beautiful, but they are ultimately damaged by the autumn fall. This line reflects on the fleeting nature of beauty.
    • 突烟还自低，鄙退岂所期 - Rising smoke still lowers itself, how could one expect to be despised and dismissed? This line expresses a sense of futility and disappointment.
    • 桂芳徒自蠹，失爱在娥眉 - The fragrance of osmanthus only harms itself, losing favor in the eyes of the moon goddess. This line uses a metaphor to describe the loss of favor.
    • 坐见芳时歇，憔悴空自嗤 - Sitting and watching the fragrant season end, one becomes emaciated and can only mock oneself. This line reflects on the passage of time and the loss of youth.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 翔风 - Refers to Xiangfeng, a character in the story.
    • 石崇 - Refers to Shi Chong, a prominent official during the Western Jin Dynasty.
    • 胡女 - Refers to a foreign woman, often used in a derogatory sense to describe women from non-Han Chinese backgrounds.
    • 房老 - Refers to a position of authority within a household, often responsible for managing the younger members.
    • 五言诗 - Refers to a form of classical Chinese poetry consisting of five characters per line.

    Proper Nouns

    • 翔风 - The name of the character Xiangfeng.
    • 石崇 - The name of the official Shi Chong.
    • 胡女 - A term referring to a foreign woman.
    • 房老 - A title for a household authority.
    • 五言诗 - A form of classical Chinese poetry.

    To have Xiangfeng adjust the jade and hand it over to the craftsmen, they created a pendant shaped like an inverted dragon and a hairpin woven with gold resembling a phoenix crown. The jade was carved into the shape of an inverted dragon, and the gold hairpin was cast to resemble a phoenix crown. They tied their sleeves together and danced around the pillars, dancing day and night without interruption, called 'constant dance.' When calling someone, do not call their name; listen to the sound of the pendant and look at the color of the hairpin. Those whose jade pendants make a light sound come forward, and those whose golden hairpins are bright come behind, forming the order of the procession. Have dozens of people each hold different fragrances; when they speak and laugh, their breath carries the scent in the wind. Grind the fragrant wood into powder, spread it on the bed, and let the loved ones step on it. Those who leave no trace are given a hundred strings of pearls, while those who leave traces have their food and drink restricted to make them lighter and weaker. Thus, women in the inner chambers would playfully say, "If you are not slender and light, how could you get a hundred strings of pearls?" When Xiangfeng reached thirty, the young and beautiful women envied her, and some said 'foreign women cannot be part of the group,' competing to denounce her. Shi Chong, after hearing slanderous words, immediately demoted Xiangfeng to the position of 'house elder,' making her responsible for the young ones, and she then harbored resentment and wrote a five-character poem: "Who does not find spring flowers beautiful, but they are ultimately damaged by the autumn fall. Rising smoke still lowers itself, how could one expect to be despised and dismissed! The fragrance of osmanthus only harms itself, losing favor in the eyes of the moon goddess. Sitting and watching the fragrant season end, one becomes emaciated and can only mock oneself." In the houses of the Shi family, this poem was sung as a song of pleasure, and it continued until the end of the Jin Dynasty. [145]

    石虎于太极殿前起楼，高四十丈，结珠为帘，垂五色玉佩，风至铿锵，和鸣清雅。盛夏之时，登高楼以望四极，奏金石丝竹之乐，以日继夜。于楼下开马埒射场，周回四百步，皆文石丹沙及彩画于埒旁。聚金玉钱贝之宝，以赏百戏之人。四厢置锦幔，屋柱皆隐起为龙凤百兽之形，雕斫众宝，以饰楹柱，夜往往有光明。集诸羌互于楼上。时亢旱，舂杂宝异香为屑，使数百人于楼上吹散之，名曰“芳尘”。 [146]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [146]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 于太极殿前起楼 - Built a tower in front of the Taiji Hall.
    • 高四十丈 - Tall forty zhang (a unit of measurement).
    • 结珠为帘 - Wove pearls into a curtain.
    • 垂五色玉佩 - Hung five-colored jade pendants.
    • 风至铿锵 - When the wind blows, it rings out.
    • 和鸣清雅 - Harmonious and elegant sounds.
    • 盛夏之时 - In the height of summer.
    • 登高楼以望四极 - Climb the high tower to look at the four corners of the earth.
    • 奏金石丝竹之乐 - Play music of metal, stone, silk, and bamboo.
    • 以日继夜 - Day and night without pause.
    • 于楼下开马埒射场 - Below the tower, opened a horse racing and archery field.
    • 周回四百步 - Surrounding four hundred steps.
    • 皆文石丹沙及彩画于埒旁 - All are decorated with patterned stones, red sand, and colorful paintings around the track.
    • 聚金玉钱贝之宝 - Collected treasures of gold, jade, coins, and shells.
    • 以赏百戏之人 - To reward performers of various arts.
    • 四厢置锦幔 - Placed brocade curtains in the four sides.
    • 屋柱皆隐起为龙凤百兽之形 - The pillars are carved with hidden designs of dragons, phoenixes, and a hundred beasts.
    • 雕斫众宝 - Carved and carved many treasures.
    • 以饰楹柱 - To decorate the pillars.
    • 夜往往有光明 - At night, there is often light.
    • 集诸羌互于楼上 - Gathered various Qiang people on the upper floor.
    • 时亢旱 - At a time of severe drought.
    • 舂杂宝异香为屑 - Ground various treasures and exotic fragrances into powder.
    • 使数百人于楼上吹散之 - Had hundreds of people blow it into the air from the upper floor.
    • 名曰“芳尘” - Called it 'Fragrant Dust.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 太极殿 - The Taiji Hall, a significant imperial palace in ancient China.
    • 金石丝竹之乐 - Refers to the traditional Chinese musical instruments made of metal, stone, silk, and bamboo, highly valued in ancient Chinese culture.
    • 百戏 - Refers to a variety of performing arts, including acrobatics, magic, and dance, popular in ancient China.
    • 龙凤百兽之形 - Dragons, phoenixes, and various animals are common motifs in Chinese art and architecture, symbolizing power, prosperity, and good fortune.
    • 诸羌 - Refers to various ethnic groups from the western regions of China, often mentioned in historical texts.
    • 亢旱 - Refers to a severe drought, a common natural disaster in ancient China.
    • 芳尘 - The name 'Fragrant Dust' suggests a mixture that produces a pleasant and aromatic effect when dispersed.

    Proper Nouns

    • 太极殿 - The Taiji Hall.
    • 金石丝竹之乐 - Music of metal, stone, silk, and bamboo.
    • 百戏 - Various performing arts.
    • 龙凤百兽之形 - Designs of dragons, phoenixes, and a hundred beasts.
    • 诸羌 - Various ethnic groups from the western regions.
    • 亢旱 - Severe drought.
    • 芳尘 - Fragrant Dust.

    Shi Hu built a tower in front of the Taiji Hall, which was forty zhang tall. He wove pearls into a curtain and hung five-colored jade pendants from it. When the wind blew, the pendants rang out harmoniously and elegantly. In the height of summer, he would climb the high tower to look at the four corners of the earth and play music of metal, stone, silk, and bamboo day and night without pause. Below the tower, he opened a horse racing and archery field, surrounding four hundred steps. All were decorated with patterned stones, red sand, and colorful paintings around the track. He collected treasures of gold, jade, coins, and shells to reward performers of various arts. Brocade curtains were placed in the four sides, and the pillars were carved with hidden designs of dragons, phoenixes, and a hundred beasts. Many treasures were carved and decorated the pillars, and at night, there was often light. He gathered various Qiang people on the upper floor. At a time of severe drought, he ground various treasures and exotic fragrances into powder and had hundreds of people blow it into the air from the upper floor, calling it "Fragrant Dust." [146]

    台上有铜龙，腹容数百斛酒，使胡人于楼上嗽酒，风至望之如露，名曰“粘雨台”，用以洒尘。楼上戏笑之声，音震空中。又为四时浴室，用瑜石碔玞为堤岸，或以琥珀为瓶杓。夏则引渠水以为池，池中皆以纱縠为囊，盛百杂香，渍于水中。严冰之时，作铜屈龙数千枚，各重数十斤，烧如火色，投于水中，则池水恒温，名曰“燋龙温池”。引凤文锦步障萦蔽浴所，共宫人宠嬖者解媟服宴戏，弥于日夜，名曰“清嬉浴室”。浴罢，泄水于宫外。水流之所，名“温香渠”。渠外之人，争来汲取，得升合以归，其家人莫不怡悦。至石氏破灭，燋龙犹在邺城，池今夷塞矣。 [147]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [147]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 粘雨台 - Terrace named 'Sticky Rain Terrace,' suggesting a magical or mystical quality.
    • 燋龙温池 - Pool named 'Burning Dragon Warm Pool,' emphasizing the warmth and comfort provided.
    • 清嬉浴室 - Bath named 'Clear Play Bath,' suggesting a clean and enjoyable environment.
    • 温香渠 - Channel named 'Warm Fragrant Channel,' adding to the sensory experience of the bath.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 石氏 - The Shi clan, a ruling family in Chinese history.
    • 邺城 - Ye City, an important city in Chinese history, particularly during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 胡人 - People from the Western Regions, possibly for entertainment or ritual purposes.

    Proper Nouns

    • 石氏 - The Shi clan, a ruling family in Chinese history.
    • 邺城 - Ye City, an important city in Chinese history, particularly during the Three Kingdoms period.

    On the terrace, there was a bronze dragon with a belly capable of holding hundreds of dou of wine. People from the Western Regions were made to spit wine on the terrace, and when the wind blew, it looked like dew. This terrace was named "Sticky Rain Terrace" and was used to sprinkle dust. The sounds of laughter and play from the terrace echoed through the air. Another facility was a seasonal bathhouse, using beautiful stones as embankments and sometimes amber for bottles and ladles. In summer, water from a channel was diverted to form a pool, which contained silk bags filled with various fragrances submerged in the water. During severe cold, thousands of bronze dragons were heated to a fiery red and thrown into the water to keep it warm. This pool was called "Burning Dragon Warm Pool." The bath area was surrounded by a screen made of colorful silk and brocade, resembling the feathers of a phoenix. Palace ladies and their favorites undressed and enjoyed themselves day and night here, and it was called "Clear Play Bath." After bathing, the water was drained outside the palace. The place where the water flowed was called "Warm Fragrant Channel." People outside the palace competed to collect the water, and those who got even a small amount took it home, delighting their families. By the time the Shi clan was overthrown, the burning dragons were still in Ye City, but the pool is now leveled and filled. [147]

    录曰：居室见妒，故亦奸巧之恒情，因娇湎嬖，而菲锦之辞入。至于惑听邪谄，岂能隔于求媚；凭欢藉幸，缘和嫟而相容。是以先宠未退，盛衰之萌兆矣；一朝爱退，皎日之誓忽焉。清奏薄言，怨刺之辞乃作。石崇叨擅时资，财业倾世，遂乃歌拟房中，乐称“恒舞”，季庭管室，岂独古之贬乎！石虎席卷西京，崇丽妖虐，外僭和鸾文物之仪，内修三英、九华之号，灵祥远贡，光耀旧都，珠玑丹紫，饰备于土木。自古以来，四夷侵掠，骄奢僭暴，擅位偷安，富有之业，莫此比焉。 [148]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [148]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 錄曰 - A conventional opening formula meaning 'The record states' or 'It is recorded that,' used to introduce a narrative or commentary.
    • 居室見妒 - Literally 'envy in the household,' referring to jealousy within a family, particularly among wives or concubines.
    • 奸巧之恒情 - Means 'the constant nature of cunning and deceit,' describing the inherent tendency of people to engage in deceitful behavior.
    • 因娇湎嬖，而菲锦之辞入 - Literally 'due to indulgence and favoritism, words of slander enter,' referring to how favoritism can lead to the acceptance of slanderous speech.
    • 惑听邪谄 - Means 'to be misled by flattery and slander,' indicating the influence of false and malicious speech.
    • 凭欢藉幸 - Literally 'relying on joy and favor,' referring to the reliance on personal pleasure and favor in relationships.
    • 缘和嫟而相容 - Means 'to be tolerated due to harmony and indulgence,' suggesting that tolerance arises from a lack of conflict or strict discipline.
    • 先宠未退，盛衰之萌兆矣 - Literally 'before the former favorite has left, the signs of rise and fall have already appeared,' indicating that changes in fortune are already evident before the previous favorite is removed.
    • 一朝爱退，皎日之誓忽焉 - Literally 'once the love fades, the bright day's oath disappears suddenly,' referring to the sudden end of a relationship or promise.
    • 清奏薄言，怨刺之辞乃作 - Literally 'clearly expressed, words of complaint and criticism arise,' indicating the emergence of complaints and criticisms.
    • 石崇叨擅时资，财业倾世 - Literally 'Shi Chong, by his own merit, possessed the resources of the time, and his wealth surpassed the world,' referring to Shi Chong's immense wealth and influence.
    • 遂乃歌拟房中，乐称“恒舞” - Literally 'he then composed songs for the inner chambers, and music was called 'Constant Dance,'' indicating the creation of music and songs for private enjoyment.
    • 季庭管室，岂独古之贬乎 - Literally 'Ji Ting's management of the household, is it not a criticism unique to ancient times?' questioning whether such behavior is a unique criticism of the past.
    • 石虎席卷西京，崇丽妖虐 - Literally 'Shi Hu swept through the Western Capital, and his beauty and cruelty were excessive,' referring to Shi Hu's conquest and his excessive indulgence in beauty and cruelty.
    • 外僭和鸾文物之仪，内修三英、九华之号 - Literally 'externally, he imitated the rituals of the imperial court, and internally, he established the titles of 'Three Excellences' and 'Nine Beauties,' indicating his imitation of imperial rituals and the creation of his own titles.
    • 灵祥远贡，光耀旧都 - Literally 'auspicious signs were offered from afar, and the old capital was illuminated,' referring to the offerings of auspicious signs and the illumination of the capital.
    • 珠玑丹紫，饰备于土木 - Literally 'jade and purple, decorating the earth and wood,' indicating the lavish decoration of buildings with precious materials.
    • 自古以来，四夷侵掠，骄奢僭暴，擅位偷安，富有之业，莫此比焉 - Literally 'since ancient times, the four barbarians have invaded and plundered, and the arrogant and extravagant have usurped power and lived in peace, but no one has ever been as wealthy as this,' indicating the unparalleled wealth and power of the subject.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 石崇 - Shi Chong, a wealthy and influential official during the Western Jin dynasty, known for his opulence and extravagance.
    • 石虎 - Shi Hu, a ruler of the Later Zhao dynasty, known for his cruelty and excesses.
    • 西京 - Refers to Chang'an, the capital of the Western Han dynasty, often used as a metonym for the capital of the Western Jin dynasty.
    • 房中 - Refers to the inner chambers of a palace, often associated with private entertainment and music.
    • 和鸾文物之仪 - Refers to the elaborate rituals and ceremonies of the imperial court.
    • 三英、九华之号 - Refers to the titles 'Three Excellences' and 'Nine Beauties,' which Shi Hu established for himself.
    • 四夷 - Refers to the four barbarian tribes (northern, southern, eastern, and western), often used to describe non-Han Chinese peoples.

    Proper Nouns

    • 石崇 - Shi Chong
    • 石虎 - Shi Hu
    • 西京 - Chang'an
    • 房中 - Inner chambers
    • 和鸾文物之仪 - Imperial court rituals
    • 三英、九华之号 - Titles 'Three Excellences' and 'Nine Beauties'
    • 四夷 - Four barbarian tribes

    The record states: Envy within the household is thus the constant nature of cunning and deceit, due to indulgence and favoritism, slanderous words enter. As for being misled by flattery and slander, how can one be free from seeking to please? Relying on joy and favor, tolerance arises from harmony and indulgence. Therefore, before the former favorite has left, the signs of rise and fall have already appeared; once the love fades, the bright day's oath disappears suddenly. Clearly expressed, words of complaint and criticism arise. Shi Chong, by his own merit, possessed the resources of the time, and his wealth surpassed the world, he then composed songs for the inner chambers, and music was called 'Constant Dance,' Ji Ting's management of the household, is it not a criticism unique to ancient times? Shi Hu swept through the Western Capital, and his beauty and cruelty were excessive, externally, he imitated the rituals of the imperial court, and internally, he established the titles of 'Three Excellences' and 'Nine Beauties,' auspicious signs were offered from afar, and the old capital was illuminated, jade and purple, decorating the earth and wood. Since ancient times, the four barbarians have invaded and plundered, and the arrogant and extravagant have usurped power and lived in peace, but no one has ever been as wealthy as this. [148]

    拾遗记卷十

    诸名山

    　昆仑山

    昆仑山有昆陵之地，其高出日月之上。山有九层，每层相去万里。有云色，从下望之，如城阙之象。四面有风，群仙常驾龙乘鹤，游戏其间。四面风者，言东南西北一时俱起也。又有袪尘之风，若衣服尘污者，风至吹之，衣则凈如浣濯。甘露蒙蒙似雾，着草木则滴沥如珠。亦有朱露，望之色如丹，着木石赭然，如朱雪洒焉。以瑶器承之，如米?台。昆仑山者，西方曰须弥山，对七星之下，出碧海之中。上有九层，第六层有五色玉树，荫翳五百里，夜至水上，其光如烛。第三层有禾穟，一株满车。 [149]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [149]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 拾遺記 - Title meaning 'Record of Lost and Forgotten Things,' a collection of anecdotes and legends from ancient times.
    • 諸名山 - General term meaning 'various famous mountains.'
    • 昆陵之地 - Refers to the region or area of Kunlun, a mythical mountain in Chinese mythology.
    • 高出日月之上 - Hyperbolic expression meaning 'towering above the sun and moon,' emphasizing the immense height of the mountain.
    • 城阙之象 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'the appearance of city walls and gates,' used to describe the mountain's grandeur.
    • 袪尘之风 - Descriptive term meaning 'wind that removes dust,' indicating the purifying effect of the wind.
    • 蒙蒙似雾 - Poetic expression meaning 'foggy and misty,' describing the appearance of the dew or moisture on the mountain.
    • 滴沥如珠 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'dripping like pearls,' describing the appearance of the dew or moisture on the mountain.
    • 朱露 - Term referring to 'red dew,' a type of dew that appears red in color.
    • 赭然 - Descriptive term meaning 'reddish-brown,' indicating the color of the dew or moisture on the mountain.
    • 瑶器 - Term referring to 'jade vessels,' indicating the use of high-quality materials to collect the dew.
    • 米?台 - Term referring to 'rice-like particles,' indicating the appearance of the collected dew.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 昆仑山 - Name of the mythical mountain, central figure in Chinese mythology, associated with immortality and the abode of the gods.
    • 须弥山 - Name of the mountain in Indian mythology, often equated with Kunlun mountain in Chinese mythology, described as a cosmic mountain that supports the world.
    • 七星 - Term referring to the Big Dipper, a prominent constellation in the night sky.
    • 碧海 - Term referring to the 'blue sea,' indicating the vast ocean surrounding the mountain.
    • 五色玉树 - Term referring to 'trees with five colors of jade,' indicating the presence of precious and colorful trees on the mountain.
    • 荫翳五百里 - Hyperbolic expression meaning 'shaded for five hundred li,' indicating the extensive coverage of the trees.
    • 夜至水上，其光如烛 - Poetic expression meaning 'at night, when it reaches the water, its light is like a candle,' describing the luminous quality of the trees.
    • 禾穟 - Term referring to 'grain ears,' indicating the presence of grain on the mountain.
    • 一株满车 - Hyperbolic expression meaning 'one stalk fills a cart,' indicating the abundance of grain.

    Proper Nouns

    • 昆仑山 - Name of the mythical mountain.
    • 须弥山 - Name of the mountain in Indian mythology.
    • 七星 - Name of the Big Dipper constellation.
    • 碧海 - Term for the 'blue sea.'
    • 五色玉树 - Term for 'trees with five colors of jade.'
    • 禾穟 - Term for 'grain ears.'

    拾遗记卷十

    Various Famous Mountains

    Kunlun Mountain

    Kunlun Mountain has the land of Kunling, which towers above the sun and moon. The mountain has nine layers, with each layer separated by ten thousand miles. There are clouds, and when viewed from below, they resemble the appearance of city walls and gates. There are winds on all sides, and the immortals often ride dragons and ride on cranes, playing among them. The winds on all sides mean that the winds from the east, south, west, and north arise simultaneously. There are also winds that remove dust, and if one's clothes are dusty, the wind blows and cleans them as if they had been washed. The sweet dew is misty and resembles fog, and when it touches the grass and trees, it drips like pearls. There is also red dew, which looks like vermilion when viewed, and when it touches the wood and stone, it turns reddish-brown, as if red snow had been sprinkled. It is collected in jade vessels, and it looks like rice-like particles. Kunlun Mountain is called Mount Meru in the West, located beneath the Big Dipper, emerging from the blue sea. It has nine layers, and on the sixth layer, there are trees with five colors of jade, shading for five hundred li. At night, when it reaches the water, its light is like a candle. On the third layer, there are grain ears, and one stalk fills a cart. [149]

    有瓜如桂，有奈冬生如碧色，以玉井水洗食之，骨轻柔能腾虚也。第五层有神龟，长一尺九寸，有四翼，万岁则升木而居，亦能言。第九层山形渐小狭，下有芝田蕙圃，皆数百顷，群仙种耨焉。傍有瑶台十二，各广千步，皆五色玉为台基。最下层有流精霄阙，直上四十丈。东有风云雨师阙。南有丹密云，望之如丹色，丹云四垂周密。西有螭潭，多龙螭，皆白色，千岁一蜕其五脏。此潭左侧有五色石，皆云是白螭肠化成此石。有琅玕璆琳之玉，煎可以为脂。北有珍林别出，折枝相扣，音声和韵。九河分流。南有赤陂红波，千劫一竭，千劫水乃更生也。 [150]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [150]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 騰虛 - Metaphorical expression suggesting the ability to float or levitate, indicating a magical or supernatural quality.
    • 萬歲則升木而居 - Implies that the turtle ascends to live in trees after a long life, a fantastical element.
    • 亦能言 - Suggests the turtle can speak, adding a mythological element.
    • 芝田蕙圃 - Refers to fields of medicinal herbs and orchids, symbolizing a paradise-like environment.
    • 瑤台 - Refers to jade-paved platforms, symbols of luxury and divinity.
    • 流精霄闕 - Refers to a luminous or radiant celestial palace.
    • 風雲雨師 - Refers to the deities of wind, clouds, and rain, indicating a place of divine presence.
    • 丹密雲 - Describes red clouds that are dense and encircling, creating a vivid and mystical image.
    • 螭潭 - Refers to a dragon pool, associated with water spirits or dragons in Chinese mythology.
    • 五色石 - Refers to multi-colored stones, often associated with precious or magical objects in Chinese mythology.
    • 琅玕璆琳之玉 - Refers to precious jade, associated with wealth and power in Chinese culture.
    • 珍林 - Refers to a forest of precious trees, associated with divine or magical places in Chinese mythology.
    • 九河 - Refers to the nine rivers, associated with a specific geographical feature or a symbolic representation of multiple paths in Chinese mythology.
    • 赤陂紅波 - Refers to a red pond with waves, associated with divine or mystical phenomena in Chinese mythology.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 神龜 - Refers to a mythical turtle, often associated with longevity and wisdom in Chinese mythology.
    • 群仙 - Refers to a group of immortals, indicating a place of divine or supernatural beings.
    • 流精霄闕 - Refers to a celestial palace, indicating a place of divine presence.
    • 風雲雨師 - Refers to the deities of wind, clouds, and rain, indicating a place of divine presence.
    • 丹密雲 - Refers to red clouds, often associated with divine or mystical phenomena in Chinese mythology.
    • 螭潭 - Refers to a dragon pool, often associated with water spirits or dragons in Chinese mythology.
    • 五色石 - Refers to multi-colored stones, often associated with precious or magical objects in Chinese mythology.
    • 琅玕璆琳之玉 - Refers to precious jade, often associated with wealth and power in Chinese culture.
    • 珍林 - Refers to a forest of precious trees, often associated with divine or magical places in Chinese mythology.
    • 九河 - Refers to the nine rivers, often associated with a specific geographical feature or a symbolic representation of multiple paths in Chinese mythology.
    • 赤陂紅波 - Refers to a red pond with waves, often associated with divine or mystical phenomena in Chinese mythology.

    Proper Nouns

    • 神龜 - The mythical turtle.
    • 群仙 - The group of immortals.
    • 瑤台 - The jade-paved platforms.
    • 流精霄闕 - The celestial palace.
    • 風雲雨師 - The deities of wind, clouds, and rain.
    • 丹密雲 - The red clouds.
    • 螭潭 - The dragon pool.
    • 五色石 - The multi-colored stones.
    • 琅玕璆琳之玉 - The precious jade.
    • 珍林 - The forest of precious trees.
    • 九河 - The nine rivers.
    • 赤陂紅波 - The red pond with waves.

    There are fruits like cassia, and persimmons that grow like emerald in winter. When washed with water from the Jade Well and eaten, their bones become light and soft, allowing one to float in the air. On the fifth layer, there is a divine turtle, one foot nine inches long, with four wings. After ten thousand years, it ascends to live in trees and can also speak. On the ninth layer, the mountain shape gradually becomes smaller and narrower, with fields of medicinal herbs and orchids below, all covering hundreds of acres, where the immortals cultivate them. Beside it are twelve jade-paved platforms, each a thousand steps wide, all paved with multi-colored jade. At the lowest layer, there is the radiant celestial palace, rising forty zhang high. To the east is the palace of the deities of wind, clouds, and rain. To the south are dense red clouds, appearing as a deep red color, encircling and surrounding. To the west is the dragon pool, filled with white dragons and chimeras, all of which shed their five internal organs every thousand years. On the left side of this pool are multi-colored stones, said to be formed from the intestines of white dragons. There is also precious jade known as langgan qiu lin, which can be melted into a substance resembling fat. To the north is a forest of precious trees, where branches touch and produce harmonious sounds. The nine rivers branch out. To the south is a red pond with waves, which dries up after a thousand eons and is reborn after another thousand eons. [150]

    蓬莱山

    　　蓬莱山亦名防丘，亦名云来，高二万里，广七万里。水浅，有细石如金玉，得之不加陶冶，自然光净，仙者服之。东有郁夷国，时有金雾。诸仙说此上常浮转低昂，有如山上架楼，室常向明以开户牖，及雾灭歇，户皆向北。其西有含明之国，缀鸟毛以为衣，承露而饮，终天登高取水，亦以金、银、仓环、水精、火藻为阶。有冰水、沸水，饮者千岁。有大螺名裸步，负其壳露行，冷则复入其壳。生卵着石则软，取之则坚。明王出世，则浮于海际焉。 [151]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [151]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 蓬萊山亦名防丘，亦名云來 - Descriptive phrase indicating that the mountain has multiple names, including Fangqiu and Yunlai.
    • 高二萬里，廣七萬里 - Describes the mountain's immense height and width in exaggerated terms, typical of classical descriptions of mythical or divine places.
    • 水淺，有細石如金玉 - Describes the shallow waters and small stones that resemble gold and jade.
    • 得之不加陶冶，自然光淨 - Indicates that the stones do not need to be refined or polished to be naturally beautiful.
    • 仙者服之 - Refers to the stones being consumed by immortals.
    • 時有金霧 - Describes the occasional appearance of golden mist.
    • 諸仙說此上常浮轉低昂 - Refers to the mountain's constant floating and rising and falling, as described by immortals.
    • 有如山上架樓，室常向明以開戶牖 - Describes the mountain as having rooms that always face the light, with windows open to the light.
    • 及霧滅歇，戶皆向北 - Describes the windows turning north when the mist dissipates.
    • 有大螺名裸步，負其殼露行，冷則復入其殼 - Describes a large shellfish called Nubu, which carries its shell and emerges from it when it is cold.
    • 生卵著石則軟，取之則堅 - Describes the eggs of the shellfish becoming soft when placed on stones and hard when removed.
    • 明王出世，則浮於海際焉 - Describes the mountain rising to the sea when a great king appears.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 蓬萊山 - A mythical mountain in Chinese mythology, often associated with immortals and elixirs of immortality.
    • 防丘 - Another name for the mythical mountain, possibly derived from its defensive or protective qualities.
    • 云來 - Another name for the mountain, possibly derived from its association with clouds.
    • 郁夷國 - A mythical country, possibly named for its fog or mist.
    • 含明之國 - A mythical country to the west of the mountain, known for its people who wear bird feathers.
    • 明王 - A great king or ruler, often associated with the appearance of mythical or divine events.

    Proper Nouns

    • 蓬萊山 - The name of the mythical mountain.
    • 防丘 - Another name for the mountain.
    • 云來 - Another name for the mountain.
    • 郁夷國 - The name of a mythical country.
    • 含明之國 - The name of a mythical country to the west of the mountain.
    • 明王 - The name of a great king or ruler.

    Penglai Mountain

    Penglai Mountain is also known as Fangqiu and Yunlai. It stands two million miles high and seven million miles wide. The waters are shallow, and there are small stones that resemble gold and jade. These stones do not need to be refined or polished; they are naturally lustrous and pure, and immortals consume them. To the east lies the country of Yuyi, where golden mist occasionally appears. The immortals say that this mountain constantly floats, rising and falling, as if it were a building atop the mountain, with rooms always facing the light and windows open to the light. When the mist dissipates, the windows turn north. To the west lies the country of Hanming, whose people wear bird feathers as clothing, drink dew, and climb high to collect water. They also use gold, silver, cang rings, water crystal, and fire algae to create steps. There are icy and boiling waters; those who drink them live for a thousand years. There is a large shellfish called Nubu, which carries its shell and emerges from it when it is cold. Its eggs become soft when placed on stones and hard when removed. When a great king appears, the mountain rises to the sea. [151]

    有葭，红色，可编为席，温柔如罽毳焉。有鸟名鸿鹅，色似鸿，形如秃鹙，腹内无肠，羽翮附骨而生，无皮肉也。雄雌相眄则生产。南有鸟，名鸳鸯，形似雁，徘徊云间，栖息高岫，足不践地，生于石穴中，万岁一交则生雏，千岁衔毛学飞，以千万为群，推其毛长者高翥万里。圣君之世，来入国郊。有浮筠之簳，叶青茎紫，子大如珠，有青鸾集其上。下有沙砺，细如粉，柔风至，叶条翻起，拂细沙如云雾。仙者来观而戏焉，风吹竹叶，声如钟磬之音。 [152]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [152]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 温柔如罽毳 - Simile comparing the softness of the reed to that of fine fabric.
    • 羽翮附骨而生，无皮肉也 - Describes the bird's unique anatomy, where its feathers grow directly from its bones without flesh.
    • 相眄 - Means to look at each other, describing the bird's method of reproduction.
    • 万岁一交则生雏 - Means that after a thousand years, they reproduce once.
    • 千岁衔毛学飞 - Means that after another thousand years, they learn to fly by carrying their feathers.
    • 以千万为群 - Means they gather in large numbers.
    • 推其毛长者高翥万里 - Means the one with the longest feathers soars high for thousands of miles.
    • 风吹竹叶，声如钟磬之音 - Describes the sound of the wind blowing through the bamboo leaves, sounding like bells and chimes.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 鸿鹅 - A mythical bird with unique anatomical features.
    • 鸳鸯 - A symbol of love and fidelity in Chinese culture.
    • 青鸾 - A mythical bird often associated with immortals and divine beings.
    • 圣君之世 - Refers to the ideal era of a wise and virtuous ruler, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.

    Proper Nouns

    • 葭 - A type of reed.
    • 鸿鹅 - A mythical bird.
    • 鸳鸯 - A type of bird symbolizing love and fidelity.
    • 青鸾 - A mythical bird often associated with immortals and divine beings.
    • 浮筠之簳 - A type of bamboo.
    • 仙者 - Immortals.

    There is a reed, red in color, which can be woven into mats, soft like luxurious fabric. There is a bird named the "Hong'e," its color resembling that of a goose, its shape like that of a bald-headed bird, with no intestines inside, its feathers growing directly from its bones without flesh. The male and female birds look at each other to reproduce. In the south, there is a bird named the "Yuan Yang," its shape resembling that of a wild goose, it wanders in the clouds, rests on high mountains, its feet never touching the ground, born in stone caves, after a thousand years, it reproduces once, after another thousand years, it learns to fly by carrying its feathers, gathering in thousands, the one with the longest feathers soars high for thousands of miles. In the era of a wise and virtuous ruler, they come to the outskirts of the capital. There is a type of bamboo named "Fuyun Zhan," its leaves green and stems purple, its seeds as large as pearls, with a blue phoenix gathering on it. Below are sand and pebbles, fine as powder, with a gentle wind, the leaves sway, brushing the fine sand like clouds and mist. Immortals come to observe and play, the wind blowing through the bamboo leaves, sounding like bells and chimes. [152]

    方丈山

    　　方丈之山，一名峦雉。东有龙场，地方千里，玉瑶为林，云色皆紫。有龙，皮骨如山阜，散百顷，遇其蜕骨之时，如生龙。或云：“龙常斗此处，膏血如水流。膏色黑者，着草木及诸物如淳漆也。膏色紫光，着地凝坚，可为宝器。”燕昭王二年，海人乘霞舟，以雕壶盛数斗膏，以献昭王。王坐通云之台，亦曰通霞台，以龙膏为灯，光耀百里，烟色丹紫，国人望之，咸言瑞光，世人遥拜之。灯以火浣布为缠。山西有照石，去石十里，视人物之影如镜焉。碎石片片，皆能照人，而质方一丈，则重一两。 [153]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [153]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 方丈之山，一名峦雉 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'The Fangzhang Mountain, also known as Luanzhi,' introducing the mountain with an alternative name.
    • 皮骨如山阜，散百顷 - Vivid metaphor comparing the dragon's skin and bones to a mountain range, spreading over a hundred acres.
    • 膏色黑者，着草木及诸物如淳漆也 - Simile describing the black-colored dragon fat, which adheres to grass, trees, and other objects like thick lacquer.
    • 膏色紫光，着地凝坚，可为宝器 - Simile describing the purple-colored dragon fat, which solidifies on the ground and can be used to make precious vessels.
    • 海人乘霞舟，以雕壶盛数斗膏，以献昭王 - Detailed description of a sea person traveling on a mist boat, carrying a carved pot filled with several dou (斗) of dragon fat to offer to King Zhao of Yan.
    • 王坐通云之台，亦曰通霞台 - Descriptive phrase indicating that King Zhao of Yan sat on a cloud-penetrating platform, also known as the Cloud-Connecting Platform.
    • 光耀百里，烟色丹紫 - Vivid description of the light and smoke emitted by the dragon fat lamp, shining for a hundred li and appearing red and purple.
    • 灯以火浣布为缠 - Descriptive phrase indicating that the lamp was wrapped with fire-washing cloth, a special fabric that can be washed in fire without damage.
    • 碎石片片，皆能照人，而质方一丈，则重一两 - Metaphorical description of the reflective stones, stating that even small pieces can reflect light, and a stone one zhang (丈) on each side weighs one liang (两).

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 方丈山 - Refers to Fangzhang Mountain, a mythical mountain in Chinese mythology.
    • 燕昭王 - Refers to King Zhao of Yan, a ruler of the State of Yan during the Warring States period.
    • 通云之台 - Refers to the Cloud-Penetrating Platform, a legendary platform mentioned in ancient texts.
    • 龙膏 - Refers to dragon fat, a mythical substance with magical properties in Chinese folklore.
    • 火浣布 - Refers to fire-washing cloth, a mythical fabric that can be washed in fire without damage.

    Proper Nouns

    • 方丈山 - Fangzhang Mountain.
    • 燕昭王 - King Zhao of Yan.
    • 通云之台 - Cloud-Penetrating Platform.
    • 龙膏 - Dragon fat.
    • 火浣布 - Fire-washing cloth.

    Fangzhang Mountain

    The Fangzhang Mountain, also known as Luanzhi, has a dragon field to its east, spanning a thousand li in area. The land is covered with jade and orchids, and the clouds are all purple. There is a dragon whose skin and bones are like a mountain range, spreading over a hundred acres. When the dragon sheds its bones, it appears like a living dragon. It is said that dragons often fight here, and their blood flows like a river. The black-colored fat adheres to grass, trees, and other objects like thick lacquer. The purple-colored fat solidifies on the ground and can be used to make precious vessels. In the second year of King Zhao of Yan's reign, a sea person traveled on a mist boat, carrying a carved pot filled with several dou of dragon fat to offer to King Zhao of Yan. King Zhao sat on the Cloud-Penetrating Platform, also known as the Cloud-Connecting Platform, and used the dragon fat as a lamp. The light shone for a hundred li, and the smoke appeared red and purple. The people of the country looked at it and said it was auspicious light, and people from afar bowed to it. The lamp was wrapped with fire-washing cloth. To the west of the mountain, there are reflective stones. From ten li away, one can see the shadows of people and objects as clearly as in a mirror. Even small pieces of the stones can reflect light, and a stone one zhang on each side weighs one liang. [153]

    昭王舂此石为泥，泥通霞之台，与西王母常游居此台上。常有众鸾凤鼓舞，如琴瑟和鸣，神光照耀，如日月之出。台左右种恒春之树，叶如莲花，芬芳如桂，花随四时之色。昭王之末，仙人贡焉，列国咸贺。王曰：“寡人得恒春矣，何忧太清不至。”恒春一名“沉生”，如今之沉香也。有草名濡葌，叶色如绀，茎色如漆，细软可萦，海人织以为席荐，卷之不盈一手，舒之则列坐方国之宾。莎萝为经。莎萝草细大如发，一茎百寻，柔软香滑，群仙以为龙、鹄之辔。有池方百里，水浅可涉，泥色若金而味辛，以泥为器，可作舟矣。百炼可为金，色青，照鬼魅犹如石镜，魑魅不能藏形矣。 [154]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [154]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 昭王舂此石为泥 - 昭王舂此石为泥，泥通霞之台，与西王母常游居此台上。
    • 常有众鸾凤鼓舞 - 常有众鸾凤鼓舞，如琴瑟和鸣，神光照耀，如日月之出。
    • 台左右种恒春之树 - 台左右种恒春之树，叶如莲花，芬芳如桂，花随四时之色。
    • 恒春一名“沉生” - 恒春一名“沉生”，如今之沉香也。
    • 有草名濡葌 - 有草名濡葌，叶色如绀，茎色如漆，细软可萦，海人织以为席荐，卷之不盈一手，舒之则列坐方国之宾。
    • 莎萝为经 - 莎萝为经，莎萝草细大如发，一茎百寻，柔软香滑，群仙以为龙、鹄之辔。
    • 有池方百里 - 有池方百里，水浅可涉，泥色若金而味辛，以泥为器，可作舟矣。
    • 百炼可为金 - 百炼可为金，色青，照鬼魅犹如石镜，魑魅不能藏形矣。

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 昭王 - 昭王，likely refers to a legendary ruler, possibly from Chinese mythology or folklore.
    • 西王母 - 西王母，The Queen Mother of the West, a prominent figure in Chinese mythology, often associated with immortality and the afterlife.
    • 鸾凤 - 鸾凤，Phoenixes, symbols of nobility, grace, and harmony.
    • 恒春之树 - 恒春之树，A mythical tree that blooms year-round, symbolizing eternal spring and immortality.
    • 沉香 - 沉香，Agarwood, a fragrant wood used in incense and traditional medicine.
    • 濡葌 - 濡葌，A mythical grass with specific characteristics.
    • 莎萝 - 莎萝，A type of grass used in weaving.
    • 龙、鹄 - 龙、鹄，Dragons and cranes, symbols of power and longevity in Chinese culture.
    • 池 - 池，A large body of water, possibly a lake or pond.
    • 鬼魅 - 鬼魅，Ghosts and demons, often depicted as malevolent spirits in Chinese folklore.
    • 魑魅 - 魑魅，Another term for ghosts and demons, often used in the context of supernatural beings.

    Proper Nouns

    • 昭王 - 昭王，A legendary ruler.
    • 西王母 - 西王母，The Queen Mother of the West.
    • 恒春之树 - 恒春之树，A mythical tree.
    • 沉香 - 沉香，Agarwood.
    • 濡葌 - 濡葌，A mythical grass.
    • 莎萝 - 莎萝，A type of grass used in weaving.

    The Illustrious King ground this stone into mud, which connected to the Celestial Platform of the Evening Glow. With the Queen Mother of the West, he often visited and resided on this platform. There were always many phoenixes dancing and singing, harmonious like the sound of a lute and zither. Divine light shone, like the rising sun and moon. On both sides of the platform, there were trees of eternal spring, whose leaves resembled lotus flowers and whose fragrance was like cassia. Their flowers changed color with the seasons. At the end of the reign of the illustrious king, the immortals offered this tree, and all the vassal states celebrated. The king said, "I have obtained the eternal spring; why should I worry about ultimate purity not arriving?" The eternal spring tree is also known as "Sinking Life," much like modern agarwood. There was a grass called Nujian, whose leaves were colored like indigo and whose stems were colored like lacquer. It was fine and soft, and could be twisted. People from the sea wove it into mats and cushions. When rolled up, it did not fill one hand, but when unfolded, it could seat guests from the surrounding states. The grass called Shala was used as the warp in weaving. This grass was fine and thick like hair, and one stem could reach hundreds of fathoms. It was soft, fragrant, and smooth, and the immortals used it as reins for dragons and cranes. There was a pool a hundred li square, with shallow water that could be crossed. The mud was golden in color and tasted spicy. Using the mud to make vessels, one could fashion boats. After many refinings, it could be made into gold, green in color, reflecting ghosts and demons like a stone mirror, so that ghosts and demons could not hide their forms. [154]

    瀛洲

    　　瀛洲一名魂洲，亦曰环洲。东有渊洞，有鱼长千丈，色斑，鼻端有角，时鼓舞群戏。远望水间有五色云，就视，乃此鱼喷水为云，如庆云之丽，无以加也。有树名影木，日中视之如列星，万岁一实，实如瓜，青皮黑瓤，食之骨轻。上如华盖，群仙以避风雨。有金峦之观，饰以众环，直上干云。中有青瑶几，覆以云纨之素，刻碧玉为倒龙之状，悬火精为日，刻黑玉为乌，以水精为月，青瑶为蟾兔。于地下为机檄，以测昏明，不亏弦望。时时有香风泠然而至，张袖受之，则历年不歇。有兽名嗅石，其状如麒麟，不食生卉，不饮浊水，嗅石则知有金玉，吹石则开，金沙宝璞，粲然而可用。有草名芸苗，状如菖蒲，食叶则醉，饵根则醒。有鸟如凤，身绀翼丹，名曰“藏珠”，每鸣翔而吐珠累斛。仙人常以其珠饰仙裳，盖轻而耀于日月也。 [155]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [155]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 瀛洲一名魂洲，亦曰環洲 - Introduces the multiple names for the island.
    • 遠望水間有五色云 - Describes a distant view of colorful clouds.
    • 就視，乃此魚噴水為云 - Explains the cause of the colorful clouds.
    • 如慶云之麗，無以加也 - Emphasizes the beauty of the clouds.
    • 有樹名影木，日中視之如列星 - Describes the appearance of the tree.
    • 萬歲一實，實如瓜，青皮黑瓤，食之骨輕 - Describes the rare and magical fruit.
    • 上如華蓋，群仙以避風雨 - Describes the tree's canopy.
    • 有金峦之觀，飾以眾環，直上干云 - Describes the pavilion.
    • 中有青瑶几，覆以云纨之素，刻碧玉为倒龙之状，悬火精为日，刻黑玉为乌，以水精为月，青瑶为蟾兔 - Describes the intricate decorations.
    • 于地下为机檄，以测昏明，不亏弦望 - Describes the underground mechanisms.
    • 时时有香风泠然而至，张袖受之，则历年不歇 - Describes the fragrant breeze.
    • 有兽名嗅石，其状如麒麟，不食生卉，不饮浊水，嗅石则知有金玉，吹石则开，金沙宝璞，粲然而可用 - Describes the magical beast.
    • 有草名芸苗，状如菖蒲，食叶则醉，饵根则醒 - Describes the magical plant.
    • 有鸟如凤，身绀翼丹，名曰“藏珠”，每鸣翔而吐珠累斛 - Describes the magical bird.
    • 仙人常以其珠饰仙裳，盖轻而耀于日月也 - Describes the use of the pearls.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 瀛洲 - A mythical island in Chinese mythology, often associated with immortals and paradise.
    • 魂洲 - Another name for Yingzhou, possibly referring to a place of spirits or souls.
    • 環洲 - Another name for Yingzhou, possibly referring to a circular or ring-shaped island.
    • 五色云 - Colorful clouds, often associated with good fortune and auspicious signs in Chinese culture.
    • 影木 - A mythical tree, possibly a reference to a tree with unique properties.
    • 華蓋 - A canopy or umbrella, often used as a symbol of protection and shelter.
    • 金峦之观 - A golden pavilion, often associated with wealth and luxury.
    • 青瑶几 - A green jade table, a symbol of elegance and refinement.
    • 云纨之素 - Cloud-like silk cloth, a symbol of lightness and beauty.
    • 碧玉 - Green jade, often used in Chinese art and decoration.
    • 火精 - Fire crystal, often used to symbolize the sun.
    • 黑玉 - Black jade, often used in Chinese art and decoration.
    • 水精 - Water crystal, often used to symbolize the moon.
    • 青瑶 - Green jade, often used in Chinese art and decoration.
    • 蟾兔 - Toad and rabbit, often associated with the moon in Chinese mythology.
    • 嗅石 - A mythical beast, possibly a reference to a creature with the ability to detect precious metals.
    • 芸苗 - A mythical plant, possibly a reference to a plant with unique properties.
    • 藏珠 - A mythical bird, possibly a reference to a bird that can produce pearls.

    Proper Nouns

    • 瀛洲 - The name of the island.
    • 魂洲 - Another name for Yingzhou.
    • 環洲 - Another name for Yingzhou.
    • 影木 - The name of the mythical tree.
    • 金峦之观 - The name of the golden pavilion.
    • 青瑶几 - The name of the green jade table.
    • 云纨之素 - The name of the cloud-like silk cloth.
    • 碧玉 - The name of the green jade.
    • 火精 - The name of the fire crystal.
    • 黑玉 - The name of the black jade.
    • 水精 - The name of the water crystal.
    • 青瑶 - The name of the green jade.
    • 蟾兔 - The names of the toad and rabbit.
    • 嗅石 - The name of the mythical beast.
    • 芸苗 - The name of the mythical plant.
    • 藏珠 - The name of the mythical bird.

    Yingzhou

    Yingzhou, also known as Hunzhou, or also called Huanzhou. To the east, there is a deep cave with a fish that is a thousand feet long, with a spotted color and a horn at its nose, often dancing and playing together. From afar, there are colorful clouds over the water; upon closer inspection, it turns out that the fish spews water to form clouds, as beautiful as auspicious clouds, there is nothing more beautiful than this. There is a tree called Yingmu, which looks like stars when viewed at noon. Once every ten thousand years, it bears fruit, which is like a melon, with green skin and black flesh. Eating it makes bones light. Above it is like a canopy, under which the immortals seek shelter from wind and rain. There is a golden pavilion adorned with many rings, rising straight up to touch the clouds. Inside there is a green jade table, covered with a cloud-like silk cloth, carved with a jade dragon in reverse, with a fire crystal as the sun, a black jade bird, and a water crystal as the moon, with green jade as the toad and rabbit. Below the ground, there are mechanisms to measure day and night, without missing the phases of the moon. There is always a fragrant breeze that comes gently, and if you spread your sleeves to catch it, it will last for years. There is a beast called Xiushi, which looks like a qilin, does not eat fresh grass, does not drink turbid water, and can smell gold and jade in the stones. When it blows on the stone, it opens, revealing golden sand and precious jade, which are clear and usable. There is a plant called Yunmiao, which looks like cattail, and eating its leaves makes one drunk, while eating its roots makes one sober. There is a bird like a phoenix, with a blue body and red wings, called 'Zangzhu.' Every time it flies and sings, it spits out pearls in large quantities. Immortals often use these pearls to decorate their robes, which are light and shine like the sun and moon. [155]

    员峤山

    　　员峤山，一名环丘。上有方湖，周回千里。多大鹊，高一丈，衔不周之粟。粟穗高三丈，粒皎如玉。鹊衔粟飞于中国，故世俗间往往有之。其粟，食之历月不饥。故《吕氏春秋》云：“粟之美者，有不周之粟焉。”东有云石，广五百里，驳骆如锦，扣之片片，则蓊然云出。有木名猗桑，煎椹以为蜜。有冰蚕长七寸，黑色，有角有麟，以霜雪覆之，然后作茧，长一尺，其色五彩，织为文锦，入水不濡，以之投火，经宿不燎。唐尧之世，海人献之，尧以为黼黻。 [156]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [156]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 員峤山 - Mythical mountain, possibly from ancient Chinese mythology or geography.
    • 一名环丘 - Alternative name for the mountain, meaning 'also known as a circular mound.'
    • 方湖，周回千里 - Description of a square lake, encircling a thousand miles.
    • 大鹊，高一丈 - Description of large crows, one zhang (about 3 meters) tall.
    • 衔不周之粟 - Carrying millet from the Unbroken Place, a mythical or distant source of millet.
    • 粟穗高三丈，粒皎如玉 - The stalks are three zhang high, and the grains are as bright as jade.
    • 鹊衔粟飞于中国 - The crows carry the millet and fly to China.
    • 食之历月不饥 - Eating it prevents hunger for a month.
    • 云石，广五百里 - Cloud stones, spanning five hundred li.
    • 驳骆如锦 - Spotted like brocade.
    • 扣之片片，则蓊然云出 - When struck, they produce clouds in a dense, lush manner.
    • 冰蚕长七寸，黑色，有角有麟 - Ice worms, seven cun long, black, with horns and scales.
    • 以霜雪覆之，然后作茧 - Covered with frost and snow, they then spin cocoons.
    • 色五彩，织为文锦 - The cocoons are multicolored and woven into intricate brocade.
    • 入水不濡，以之投火，经宿不燎 - They do not get wet in water and do not burn when thrown into fire.
    • 唐尧之世，海人献之 - In the era of Emperor Yao, a sea person presented it.
    • 尧以为黼黻 - Yao used it as a ceremonial garment.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 員峤山 - Mythical mountain, possibly from ancient Chinese mythology or geography.
    • 不周之粟 - Refers to millet from the mythical 'Unbroken Place,' a legendary source of food.
    • 唐尧之世 - Refers to the era of Emperor Yao, a legendary ruler in Chinese history.
    • 黼黻 - Refers to ceremonial garments used in ancient Chinese rituals, often decorated with intricate patterns.

    Proper Nouns

    • 員峤山 - The name of the mythical mountain.
    • 环丘 - The alternative name for the mountain.
    • 唐尧 - Emperor Yao, a legendary ruler in Chinese history.
    • 黼黻 - The ceremonial garments used in ancient Chinese rituals.

    Yuanqiao Mountain

    Yuanqiao Mountain, also known as a circular mound, has a square lake encircling a thousand miles. It is home to large crows, one zhang (about 3 meters) tall, which carry millet from the Unbroken Place. The stalks of this millet are three zhang high, and the grains are as bright as jade. The crows carry the millet and fly to China, which is why it is sometimes found in China. Eating it prevents hunger for a month. Hence, the Lüshi Chunqiu states: "The finest millet comes from the Unbroken Place."

    To the east, there are cloud stones spanning five hundred li, spotted like brocade. When struck, they produce clouds in a dense, lush manner. There is a tree called Yi Sang, whose leaves are used to make honey. There are ice worms, seven cun long, black, with horns and scales. Covered with frost and snow, they then spin cocoons, which are multicolored and woven into intricate brocade. These brocades do not get wet in water and do not burn when thrown into fire. In the era of Emperor Yao, a sea person presented it, and Yao used it as a ceremonial garment. [156]

    西有星池千里，池中有神龟，八足六眼，背负七星、日、月、八方之图，腹有五岳、四渎之象。时出石上，望之煌煌如列星矣。有草名芸蓬，色白如雪，一枝二丈，夜视有白光，可以为杖。南有移池国，人长三尺，寿万岁，以茅为衣服，皆长裾大袖，因风以升烟霞，若鸟用羽毛也。人皆双瞳，修眉长耳，餐九天之正气，死而复生，于亿劫之内，见五岳再成尘。扶桑万岁一枯，其人视之如旦暮也。北有浣肠之国，甜水绕之，味甜如蜜，而水强流迅急，千钧投之，久久乃没。其国人常行于水上，逍遥于绝岳之岭，度天下广狭，绕八柱为一息，经四轴而暂寝，拾尘吐雾，以算历劫之数，而成阜丘，亦不尽也。 [157]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [157]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 西有星池千里 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'to the west there is a star lake spanning a thousand miles.'
    • 池中有神龟，八足六眼 - Describes a mythical turtle with eight legs and six eyes.
    • 背负七星、日、月、八方之图 - The turtle's back carries a diagram of the Big Dipper, the sun, the moon, and the eight directions.
    • 腹有五岳、四渎之象 - The turtle's belly carries images of the Five Great Mountains and the Four Rivers.
    • 时出石上，望之煌煌如列星矣 - The turtle occasionally appears on the rocks, looking brilliant like a row of stars.
    • 有草名芸蓬，色白如雪，一枝二丈，夜视有白光，可以为杖 - There is a plant called Yunpeng, which is as white as snow, with a stem two zhang long, and emits white light at night, suitable for use as a staff.
    • 南有移池国，人长三尺，寿万岁 - To the south, there is a country called Yichi, where people are three chi tall and live for ten thousand years.
    • 以茅为衣服，皆长裾大袖，因风以升烟霞，若鸟用羽毛也 - They wear clothes made of reeds, all with long skirts and wide sleeves, rising with the wind into mist and clouds, like birds using their feathers.
    • 人皆双瞳，修眉长耳，餐九天之正气，死而复生，于亿劫之内，见五岳再成尘 - The people have double pupils, long eyebrows, and long ears, feeding on the pure air of the nine heavens, dying and being reborn, witnessing the Five Great Mountains turning to dust over countless eons.
    • 扶桑万岁一枯，其人视之如旦暮也 - The immortal tree Fusang, which withers once every ten thousand years, is seen by them as if it were day and night.
    • 北有浣肠之国，甜水绕之，味甜如蜜，而水强流迅急，千钧投之，久久乃没 - To the north, there is a country called Huanchang, surrounded by sweet water that tastes like honey, but the water is strong and swift, taking a long time for even a thousand jin to sink.
    • 其国人常行于水上，逍遥于绝岳之岭，度天下广狭，绕八柱为一息，经四轴而暂寝，拾尘吐雾，以算历劫之数，而成阜丘，亦不尽也 - The people of this country often walk on the water, wandering freely on the peaks of remote mountains, measuring the breadth and narrowness of the world, circling the eight pillars for a breath, passing through the four axes for a brief rest, picking up dust and exhaling mist, calculating the number of eons, and forming hills, yet never ending.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 星池 - Refers to a mythical star lake.
    • 神龟 - A mythical turtle, often associated with longevity and wisdom.
    • 七星 - Refers to the Big Dipper, a constellation in Chinese astronomy.
    • 五岳 - The Five Great Mountains: Mount Tai, Mount Heng (Hunan), Mount Heng (Hubei), Mount Hua, and Mount Song.
    • 四渎 - The Four Rivers: the Yellow River, the Yangtze River, the Huai River, and the Ji River.
    • 移池国 - A mythical country to the south.
    • 扶桑 - A mythical tree that withers once every ten thousand years.
    • 浣肠之国 - A mythical country to the north, known for its sweet water and unique inhabitants.

    Proper Nouns

    • 星池 - Star Lake.
    • 神龟 - Mythical Turtle.
    • 七星 - Big Dipper.
    • 五岳 - Five Great Mountains.
    • 四渎 - Four Rivers.
    • 移池国 - Yichi Country.
    • 扶桑 - Immortal Tree.
    • 浣肠之国 - Huanchang Country.

    To the west, there is a star lake spanning a thousand miles, within which resides a mythical turtle with eight legs and six eyes. On its back lies a diagram of the Big Dipper, the sun, the moon, and the eight directions, while its belly carries images of the Five Great Mountains and the Four Rivers. Occasionally, the turtle appears on the rocks, looking brilliant like a row of stars. There is a plant called Yunpeng, which is as white as snow, with a stem two zhang long, and emits white light at night, suitable for use as a staff. To the south, there is a country called Yichi, where people are three chi tall and live for ten thousand years. They wear clothes made of reeds, all with long skirts and wide sleeves, rising with the wind into mist and clouds, like birds using their feathers. The people have double pupils, long eyebrows, and long ears, feeding on the pure air of the nine heavens, dying and being reborn, witnessing the Five Great Mountains turning to dust over countless eons. The immortal tree Fusang, which withers once every ten thousand years, is seen by them as if it were day and night. To the north, there is a country called Huanchang, surrounded by sweet water that tastes like honey, but the water is strong and swift, taking a long time for even a thousand jin to sink. The people of this country often walk on the water, wandering freely on the peaks of remote mountains, measuring the breadth and narrowness of the world, circling the eight pillars for a breath, passing through the four axes for a brief rest, picking up dust and exhaling mist, calculating the number of eons, and forming hills, yet never ending. [157]

    岱舆山

    　　岱舆山，一名浮析，东有员渊千里，常沸腾，以金石投之，则烂如土矣。孟冬水涸，中有黄烟从地出，起数丈，烟色万变。山人掘之，入地数尺，得燋石如炭灭，有碎火，以蒸烛投之，则然而青色，深掘则火转盛。有草名莽煌，叶圆如荷，去之十步，炙人衣则燋，刈之为席，方冬弥温，以枝相摩，则火出矣。南有平沙千里，色如金，若粉屑，靡靡常流，鸟兽行则没足。风吹沙起若雾，亦名金雾，亦曰金尘。沙着树粲然，如黄金涂矣。和之以泥，涂仙宫，则晃昱明粲也。西有舄玉山，其石五色而轻，或似履舄之状，光泽可爱，有类人工。其黑色者为胜，众仙所用焉。北有玉梁千丈，驾玄流之上，紫苔覆漫，味甘而柔滑，食者千岁不饥。玉梁之侧，有斑斓自然云霞龙凤之状。 [158]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [158]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 岱舆山 - A mountain name, likely referring to a mythical or legendary mountain.
    • 浮析 - An alternative name for the mountain, possibly indicating its floating or mysterious nature.
    • 常沸腾 - Literally means 'always boiling,' used to describe the perpetual motion of the water in the surrounding area.
    • 以金石投之，则烂如土矣 - A phrase describing the effect of throwing metal or stone into the boiling water, which results in them turning into soil-like substances.
    • 孟冬水涸 - Refers to the time when the water dries up in the early winter.
    • 中有黄烟从地出 - Describes yellow smoke rising from the ground, possibly indicating volcanic activity or some other natural phenomenon.
    • 烟色万变 - Indicates that the color of the smoke changes in myriad ways.
    • 山人掘之 - Means 'the people of the mountain dig it,' referring to the inhabitants of the mountain digging into the ground.
    • 得燋石如炭灭 - Describes finding charred stones that resemble extinguished charcoal.
    • 有碎火 - Refers to small pieces of fire.
    • 以蒸烛投之，则然而青色 - Describes the effect of throwing a candle into the fire, which causes it to burn with a blue flame.
    • 深掘则火转盛 - Indicates that digging deeper results in a more intense fire.
    • 有草名莽煌 - Refers to a type of grass named 'manghuang.'
    • 叶圆如荷 - Describes the leaves of the grass as round like lotus leaves.
    • 去之十步，炙人衣则燋 - Indicates that if one stands ten steps away from the grass, their clothes will be scorched.
    • 刈之为席，方冬弥温 - Describes cutting the grass to make mats that remain warm even in winter.
    • 以枝相摩，则火出矣 - Indicates that rubbing the branches together produces fire.
    • 南有平沙千里 - Describes a vast expanse of flat sand to the south.
    • 色如金，若粉屑 - Indicates that the sand is golden in color and resembles fine powder.
    • 靡靡常流 - Describes the sand as flowing gently and continuously.
    • 鸟兽行则没足 - Indicates that birds and animals can sink up to their feet in the sand.
    • 风吹沙起若雾 - Describes the wind blowing the sand into a fog-like cloud.
    • 亦名金雾，亦曰金尘 - Indicates that the sand fog is also called 'golden mist' or 'golden dust.'
    • 沙着树粲然，如黄金涂矣 - Describes the sand sticking to trees, making them appear as if coated in gold.
    • 和之以泥，涂仙宫，则晃昱明粲也 - Indicates that mixing the sand with mud and using it to coat a palace makes it shine brightly.
    • 西有舄玉山 - Refers to a mountain named 'Xiyu' to the west.
    • 其石五色而轻 - Describes the stones as having five colors and being light.
    • 或似履舄之状 - Indicates that the stones sometimes resemble shoes or sandals.
    • 光泽可爱 - Describes the stones as shiny and beautiful.
    • 有类人工 - Indicates that the stones are similar to those made by humans.
    • 其黑色者为胜 - Refers to the black stones being the most valuable.
    • 众仙所用焉 - Indicates that the black stones are used by many immortals.
    • 北有玉梁千丈 - Describes a jade beam that is a thousand feet long to the north.
    • 驾玄流之上 - Indicates that the beam spans a dark river.
    • 紫苔覆漫 - Describes purple moss covering the beam.
    • 味甘而柔滑 - Indicates that the moss tastes sweet and is soft and smooth.
    • 食者千岁不饥 - Describes that eating the moss can prevent hunger for a thousand years.
    • 玉梁之侧，有斑斓自然云霞龙凤之状 - Describes the side of the jade beam as having natural patterns of clouds, rainbows, dragons, and phoenixes.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 岱舆山 - A mythical mountain, possibly related to the legend of the floating mountains in Chinese mythology.
    • 浮析 - An alternative name for the mountain, possibly indicating its floating or mysterious nature.
    • 孟冬 - Refers to the early winter, a specific time period in the Chinese calendar.
    • 黄烟 - Yellow smoke, possibly indicating volcanic activity or a natural phenomenon.
    • 燋石 - Charred stones, possibly indicating a natural process of mineral transformation.
    • 莽煌 - A type of grass, possibly a mythical or rare plant.
    • 平沙 - Flat sand, possibly a reference to a desert or sandy plain.
    • 金雾，金尘 - Golden mist and golden dust, possibly a metaphor for the beauty and rarity of the sand.
    • 舄玉山 - A mountain named 'Xiyu,' possibly a mythical or legendary mountain.
    • 五色而轻 - Stones with five colors and light, possibly indicating a rare and valuable mineral.
    • 履舄 - Shoes or sandals, possibly a metaphor for the stones' shape.
    • 玉梁 - A jade beam, possibly a mythical or legendary structure.
    • 紫苔 - Purple moss, possibly a rare and valuable plant.
    • 千岁不饥 - Preventing hunger for a thousand years, possibly a metaphor for the nourishing properties of the moss.
    • 斑斓自然云霞龙凤之状 - Natural patterns of clouds, rainbows, dragons, and phoenixes, possibly a reference to the beauty and auspiciousness of the patterns.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • 岱舆山，一名浮析 — This phrase introduces the mountain and its alternative name, which should be read together.
    • 东有员渊千里，常沸腾 — This phrase describes the area to the east, which should be read as a single unit.
    • 以金石投之，则烂如土矣 — This phrase describes the effect of throwing metal or stone into the boiling water, which should be read as a single unit.
    • 孟冬水涸，中有黄烟从地出 — This phrase describes the early winter and the yellow smoke rising from the ground, which should be read as a single unit.
    • 烟色万变 — This phrase describes the changing color of the smoke, which should be read as a single unit.
    • 山人掘之，入地数尺，得燋石如炭灭 — This phrase describes the people of the mountain digging and finding charred stones, which should be read as a single unit.
    • 有碎火，以蒸烛投之，则然而青色 — This phrase describes the small pieces of fire and the effect of throwing a candle into the fire, which should be read as a single unit.
    • 深掘则火转盛 — This phrase describes the effect of digging deeper, which should be read as a single unit.
    • 有草名莽煌 — This phrase introduces the grass named 'manghuang,' which should be read as a single unit.
    • 叶圆如荷 — This phrase describes the leaves of the grass, which should be read as a single unit.
    • 去之十步，炙人衣则燋 — This phrase describes the effect of standing ten steps away from the grass, which should be read as a single unit.
    • 刈之为席，方冬弥温 — This phrase describes cutting the grass to make mats, which should be read as a single unit.
    • 以枝相摩，则火出矣 — This phrase describes the effect of rubbing the branches together, which should be read as a single unit.
    • 南有平沙千里 — This phrase describes the vast expanse of flat sand to the south, which should be read as a single unit.
    • 色如金，若粉屑 — This phrase describes the color and texture of the sand, which should be read as a single unit.
    • 靡靡常流 — This phrase describes the gentle and continuous flow of the sand, which should be read as a single unit.
    • 鸟兽行则没足 — This phrase describes the effect of birds and animals sinking up to their feet in the sand, which should be read as a single unit.
    • 风吹沙起若雾 — This phrase describes the wind blowing the sand into a fog-like cloud, which should be read as a single unit.
    • 亦名金雾，亦曰金尘 — This phrase describes the sand fog as also called 'golden mist' or 'golden dust,' which should be read as a single unit.
    • 沙着树粲然，如黄金涂矣 — This phrase describes the sand sticking to trees, which should be read as a single unit.
    • 和之以泥，涂仙宫，则晃昱明粲也 — This phrase describes mixing the sand with mud and using it to coat a palace, which should be read as a single unit.
    • 西有舄玉山 — This phrase introduces the mountain named 'Xiyu' to the west, which should be read as a single unit.

    Daiyu Mountain

    Daiyu Mountain, also known as Fuxi, has a circular abyss to the east that stretches for a thousand li and is always boiling. If metal or stone is thrown into it, they will turn to soil. In the early winter when the water dries up, yellow smoke rises from the ground, reaching several zhang in height, with its color changing in myriad ways. The people of the mountain dig into the ground, finding charred stones that resemble extinguished charcoal, with small pieces of fire. If a candle is thrown into the fire, it burns with a blue flame; digging deeper results in a more intense fire. There is a grass called Manghuang, whose leaves are round like lotus leaves. Standing ten steps away from it, one's clothes will be scorched. Cutting it to make mats, they remain warm even in winter. Rubbing the branches together produces fire. To the south, there is a vast expanse of flat sand that is golden in color and resembles fine powder, flowing gently and continuously. Birds and animals can sink up to their feet in the sand. The wind blowing the sand into a fog-like cloud is also called "golden mist" or "golden dust." The sand sticking to trees makes them appear as if coated in gold. Mixing the sand with mud and using it to coat a palace makes it shine brightly. To the west, there is a mountain named Xiyu, whose stones are five-colored and light, sometimes resembling shoes or sandals, shiny and beautiful, similar to those made by humans. The black stones are the most valuable and are used by many immortals. To the north, there is a jade beam that is a thousand feet long, spanning a dark river, with purple moss covering it, tasting sweet and soft. Eating it can prevent hunger for a thousand years. Beside the jade beam, there are natural patterns of clouds, rainbows, dragons, and phoenixes. [158]

    梁去玄流千余丈，云气生其下。傍有丹桂、紫桂、白桂，皆直上千寻，可为舟航，谓之“文桂之舟”。亦有沙棠、豫章之木，长千寻，细枝为舟，犹长十丈。有七色芝生梁下，其色青，光辉耀，谓之“苍芝”。荧火大如蜂，声如雀，八翅六足。梁有五色蝙蝠，黄者无肠，倒飞，腹向天；白者脑重，头垂自挂；黑者如乌，至千岁形变如小燕；青者毫毛长二寸，色如翠；赤者止于石穴，穴上入天，视日出入恒在其上。有兽名嗽月，形似豹，饮金泉之液，食银石之髓。此兽夜喷白气，其光如月，可照数十亩。轩辕之世获焉。有遥香草，其花如丹，光耀入月，叶细长而白，如忘忧之草，其花叶俱香，扇馥数里，故名遥香草。其子如薏中实，甘香，食之累月不饥渴，体如草之香，久食延龄万岁。仙人常采食之。 [159]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [159]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 流千余丈 - Describes a river or stream that flows for thousands of feet, emphasizing its vastness.
    • 云气生其下 - Literally 'clouds arise beneath it,' suggesting the river's grandeur and the mystical atmosphere it creates.
    • 直上千寻 - Means 'reaches thousands of feet in height,' indicating the towering stature of the trees.
    • 可为舟航 - Means 'can be used as boats and ships,' highlighting the practical utility of the trees.
    • 谓之‘文桂之舟’ - Means 'called 'Wen Gui Boats,' indicating a specific name for the boats made from the trees.
    • 沙棠、豫章之木 - Refers to specific types of trees: *shatang* and *yuzhang*.
    • 荧火大如蜂，声如雀，八翅六足 - Describes a firefly-like creature with the size of a bee, the sound of a sparrow, eight wings, and six legs.
    • 五色蝙蝠 - Refers to bats of various colors.
    • 黄者无肠，倒飞，腹向天 - Describes yellow bats that lack intestines, fly upside down, and face their bellies toward the sky.
    • 白者脑重，头垂自挂 - Describes white bats with heavy brains that hang by their heads.
    • 黑者如乌，至千岁形变如小燕 - Describes black bats that resemble crows and, after a thousand years, transform into small swallows.
    • 青者毫毛长二寸，色如翠 - Describes blue bats with two-inch-long hairs, colored like emerald.
    • 赤者止于石穴，穴上入天，视日出入恒在其上 - Describes red bats that rest in stone caves, with the cave openings reaching up to the sky, and they always face the rising and setting sun.
    • 有兽名嗽月，形似豹，饮金泉之液，食银石之髓 - Describes a mythical beast called *Suo Yue* (Suck the Moon), which resembles a leopard, drinks from golden springs, and eats the marrow of silver rocks.
    • 此兽夜喷白气，其光如月，可照数十亩 - Describes the *Suo Yue* beast as emitting white mist at night, whose light is as bright as the moon and can illuminate dozens of acres.
    • 轩辕之世获焉 - Means 'obtained during the time of Emperor Huangdi,' indicating the historical context of the beast's acquisition.
    • 遥香草，其花如丹，光耀入月，叶细长而白，如忘忧之草，其花叶俱香，扇馥数里，故名遥香草 - Describes a fragrant herb with red flowers, light that shines like the moon, fine and white leaves, and a scent that spreads for several miles, named *Yao Xiang Cao* (Fragrant Herb from Afar).
    • 其子如薏中实，甘香，食之累月不饥渴，体如草之香，久食延龄万岁 - Describes the seeds of the herb as sweet and fragrant, and if eaten for months, one would not feel hungry or thirsty, and the body would become fragrant, extending life by ten thousand years.
    • 仙人常采食之 - Means 'immortals often harvest and eat it,' indicating the herb's value in Daoist alchemy and longevity practices.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 轩辕之世 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Huangdi (Yellow Emperor), a legendary figure in Chinese history and mythology.
    • 文桂之舟 - A specific type of boat made from the *Wen Gui* trees, which are described as having special properties.
    • 沙棠、豫章之木 - *Shatang* and *Yuzhang* are specific types of trees known for their strength and beauty.
    • 荧火 - Fireflies, which are often associated with mystical or supernatural qualities in Chinese folklore.
    • 五色蝙蝠 - Bats of various colors, often seen as symbols of good luck and longevity in Chinese culture.
    • 嗽月 - A mythical beast, possibly inspired by the legend of the moon-sucking creature, which is a common theme in Chinese mythology.
    • 遥香草 - A fragrant herb with mystical properties, often associated with Daoist practices and immortality.

    Proper Nouns

    • 轩辕之世 - The reign of Emperor Huangdi.
    • 文桂之舟 - Boats made from the *Wen Gui* trees.
    • 沙棠、豫章之木 - Specific types of trees: *Shatang* and *Yuzhang*.
    • 荧火 - Fireflies.
    • 五色蝙蝠 - Five-colored bats.
    • 嗽月 - The mythical beast *Suo Yue*.
    • 遥香草 - The fragrant herb *Yao Xiang Cao*.

    The River flows for thousands of feet, with clouds arising beneath it. Beside the river are red, purple, and white osmanthus trees, all reaching thousands of feet in height, and can be used as boats and ships, called "Wen Gui Boats." There are also Shatang and Yuzhang trees, which grow for thousands of feet, with fine branches used to make boats, still measuring ten feet in length. Seven-colored fungi grow beneath the bridge, with a green hue, shining brightly, called "Cang Zhi." Fireflies as large as bees, with the sound of sparrows, eight wings, and six legs. On the bridge are five-colored bats, yellow ones without intestines, flying upside down with their bellies facing the sky; white ones with heavy brains, hanging by their heads; black ones resembling crows, transforming into small swallows after a thousand years; blue ones with two-inch-long hairs, colored like emerald; red ones resting in stone caves, with the cave openings reaching up to the sky, always facing the rising and setting sun. There is a beast named Suo Yue, resembling a leopard, drinking from golden springs and eating the marrow of silver rocks. This beast emits white mist at night, whose light is as bright as the moon and can illuminate dozens of acres. It was obtained during the reign of Emperor Huangdi. There is a fragrant herb called Yao Xiang Cao, with red flowers, light that shines like the moon, fine and white leaves, and a scent that spreads for several miles, named Yao Xiang Cao. Its seeds are sweet and fragrant, and if eaten for months, one would not feel hungry or thirsty, and the body would become fragrant, extending life by ten thousand years. Immortals often harvest and eat it. [159]

    昆吾山

    　　昆吾山，其下多赤金，色如火。昔黄帝伐蚩尤，陈兵于此地，掘深百丈，犹未及泉，惟见火光如星。地中多丹，炼石为铜，铜色青而利。泉色赤。山草木皆劲利，土亦刚而精。至越王勾践，使工人以白马白牛祠昆吾之神，采金铸之，以成八剑之精：一名掩日，以之指日，则光昼暗。金阴也，阴盛则阳灭。二名断水，以之划水，开即不合。三名转魄，以之指月，蟾兔为之倒转。四名悬翦，飞鸟游过触其刃，如斩截焉。五名惊鲵，以之泛海，鲸鲵为之深入。六曰灭魂，挟之夜行，不逢魑魅。七名却邪，有妖魅者，见之则伏。八名真刚，以切玉断金，如削土木矣。以应八方之气铸之也。 [160]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [160]

    Idiomatic Expressions
