    • 昆吾山 - A mountain name, possibly referring to a legendary or historical site; may carry symbolic or mythological significance.
    • 赤金 - Literally 'red gold,' likely referring to a type of metal, possibly copper or a copper alloy, due to its color.
    • 掘深百丈，猶未及泉 - A hyperbolic expression indicating that digging to a great depth (a hundred fathoms) still did not reach water, emphasizing the depth and difficulty.
    • 煉石為銅 - Refers to the process of smelting or refining stone to produce copper, a common technique in ancient metallurgy.
    • 八劍之精 - Refers to the 'essence' or 'spirit' of the eight swords, suggesting they possess extraordinary qualities.
    • 掩日 - A sword that can obscure the sun, symbolizing its immense power.
    • 斷水 - A sword that can cut water, implying its sharpness and magical properties.
    • 轉魄 - A sword that can reverse the moon, suggesting its ability to manipulate natural forces.
    • 懸翦 - A sword that can sever flying birds, indicating its sharpness and precision.
    • 驚鯨 - A sword that can make whales dive deep, symbolizing its power over aquatic creatures.
    • 滅魂 - A sword that can protect against demons, suggesting its ability to ward off evil spirits.
    • 卻邪 - A sword that can subdue demons, indicating its power to repel evil.
    • 真剛 - A sword that can cut through jade and metal, symbolizing its unparalleled hardness and sharpness.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 黃帝伐蚩尤 - Refers to the legendary battle between the Yellow Emperor (Huangdi) and the mythical warrior-king Chiyou, a foundational myth in Chinese history.
    • 越王勾踐 - Refers to King Goujian of Yue, a historical figure known for his rise to power and eventual defeat of the State of Wu in the late Spring and Autumn period.
    • 昆吾之神 - Refers to the deity or spirits associated with Kunwu Mountain, possibly a local or regional god worshipped for protection or guidance.
    • 八方之氣 - Refers to the eight cardinal directions (north, south, east, west, northeast, northwest, southeast, southwest), often associated with cosmic energy or the elements in Chinese cosmology.

    Proper Nouns

    • 昆吾山 - A mountain name, possibly referring to a legendary or historical site.
    • 黃帝 - The Yellow Emperor, a legendary figure in Chinese history and mythology.
    • 蚩尤 - A mythical warrior-king in Chinese mythology, often associated with the Yellow Emperor.
    • 越王勾踐 - King Goujian of Yue, a historical figure in the late Spring and Autumn period.
    • 昆吾之神 - The deity or spirits associated with Kunwu Mountain.
    • 八方之氣 - The eight cardinal directions, often associated with cosmic energy or the elements in Chinese cosmology.

    Kunwu Mountain

    Kunwu Mountain, beneath which there is much red gold, the color of fire. In ancient times, when the Yellow Emperor fought against Chiyou, he arrayed his troops on this land, digging to a depth of a hundred fathoms, yet still not reaching water, seeing only the light of fire like stars. The earth was rich in cinnabar, and stones were smelted into copper, which was blue and sharp. The spring water was red. The grass and trees on the mountain were all strong and sharp, and the soil was firm and refined. By the time of King Goujian of Yue, he sent craftsmen to offer a white horse and a white ox to the deity of Kunwu Mountain, and they collected gold to cast eight swords of great essence: the first was called Conceal-the-Sun, and when pointed at the sun, it would make the daylight dim. Gold is yin, and when yin is strong, yang is extinguished. The second was called Cut-the-Water, and when used to draw water, it would open but not close. The third was called Reverse-the-Moon, and when pointed at the moon, it would make the rabbit of the moon turn around. The fourth was called Hanging-Scissors, and when birds flew past it, they would be severed as if cut. The fifth was called Startle-the-Whale, and when used to sail across the sea, the whales would dive deep. The sixth was called Annihilate-the-Soul, and when carried at night, it would ward off demons. The seventh was called Repel-the-Evil, and when faced with evil spirits, they would cower. The eighth was called True-Steel, and when used to cut jade and sever metal, it was as if carving wood and earth. These were cast in response to the energies of the eight directions. [160]

    其山有兽，大如兔，毛色如金，食土下之丹石，深穴地以为窟；亦食铜铁，胆肾皆如铁。其雌者色白如银。昔吴国武库之中，兵刃铁器，俱被食尽，而封署依然。王令检其库穴，猎得双兔，一白一黄，杀之，开其腹，而有铁胆肾，方知兵刃之铁为兔所食。王乃召其剑工，令铸其胆肾以为剑，一雌一雄，号“干将”者雄，号“镆邪”者雌。其剑可以切玉断犀，王深宝之，遂霸其国。后以石匝埋藏。及晋之中兴，夜有紫色冲斗牛。张华使雷焕为丰城县令，掘而得之。华与焕各宝其一。拭以华阴之土，光耀射人。后华遇害，失剑所在。焕子佩其一剑，过延平津，剑鸣飞入水。及入水寻之，但见双龙缠屈于潭下，目光如电，遂不敢前取矣。 [161]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [161]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 大如兔 - Describes the size of the creature as being similar to a rabbit.
    • 毛色如金 - Describes the fur color as golden.
    • 食土下之丹石 - Describes the creature's diet as including red stones found underground.
    • 深穴地以為窟 - Describes the creature making deep burrows in the ground for its lair.
    • 亦食銅鐵 - Describes the creature also eating copper and iron.
    • 膽腎皆如鐵 - Describes the creature's gallbladder and kidneys as being as hard as iron.
    • 雌者色白如銀 - Describes the female creature's fur as white and silver-like.
    • 封署依然 - Describes the seals and inscriptions on the weapons remaining intact despite being eaten.
    • 開其腹 - Describes opening the creature's abdomen.
    • 寶其一 - Describes the possession and value placed on one of the swords.
    • 拭以華陰之土 - Describes wiping the sword with soil from Huayin.
    • 光耀射人 - Describes the sword emitting a dazzling light that could blind a person.
    • 目光如電 - Describes the dragon's eyes shining like lightning.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 干將 - One of the legendary swords.
    • 莫邪 - The other legendary sword.
    • 吳國武庫 - The armory of the Wu Kingdom, an ancient Chinese state.
    • 晉之中興 - The period of revival during the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 張華 - A prominent official during the Western Jin Dynasty.
    • 雷焕 - An official during the Eastern Jin Dynasty who discovered the swords.
    • 延平津 - A place in ancient China where the swords were lost.
    • 雙龍 - Refers to the mythical creatures often associated with water and power in Chinese mythology.

    Proper Nouns

    • 干將 - One of the legendary swords.
    • 莫邪 - The other legendary sword.
    • 吳國 - The ancient Chinese state of Wu.
    • 武庫 - The armory.
    • 晉 - The ancient Chinese state of Jin.
    • 張華 - A historical figure.
    • 雷焕 - A historical figure.
    • 豐城 - A place name.
    • 延平津 - A place name.
    • 華陰 - A place name.

    In a certain mountain, there lived a creature as large as a rabbit, with fur as golden as gold. It fed on red stones found deep underground, digging deep burrows in the earth for its lair. It also consumed copper and iron, and its gallbladder and kidneys were as hard as iron. The female of this creature had fur that was white and silver-like.

    In ancient times, in the armory of the Wu Kingdom, all the weapons and iron tools were eaten by these creatures, yet the seals and inscriptions on them remained intact. The king then summoned the swordsmiths and ordered them to forge new swords using the gallbladder and kidneys of the creature. Two swords were made, one male and one female, named "Gan Jiang" and "Mo Ye," respectively. These swords could cut through jade and pierce rhinoceros hide. The king treasured them greatly, and they helped him dominate his kingdom. Later, the swords were buried in stone.

    During the revival of the Jin Dynasty, at night, a purple light shot up into the sky, resembling the constellations of the Dipper and Ox. Zhang Hua appointed Lei Huan as the magistrate of Fengcheng County, where he dug and found the swords. Zhang Hua and Lei Huan each possessed one of the swords. When they wiped the swords with soil from Huayin, they emitted a dazzling light that could blind a person. After Zhang Hua was killed, one of the swords was lost. Lei Huan's son wore one of the swords and passed by Yanping Ferry, where the sword began to make a sound and flew into the water. When they tried to retrieve it from the water, they only saw two dragons coiled in the depths, their eyes shining like lightning, and they dared not approach any further. [161]

    洞庭山

    　　洞庭山浮于水上，其下有金堂数百间，玉女居之。四时闻金石丝竹之声，彻于山顶。楚怀王之时，举群才赋诗于水湄，故云潇湘洞庭之乐，听者令人难老，虽《咸池》、《九韶》，不得比焉。每四仲之节，王常绕山以游宴，各举四仲之气以为乐章。仲春律中夹钟，乃作轻风流水之诗，宴于山南；律中蕤宾，乃作皓露秋霜之曲。后怀王好进奸雄，群贤逃越。屈原以忠见斥，隐于沅湘，披蓁茹草，混同禽兽，不交世务，采柏实以全桂膏，用养心神；被王逼逐，乃赴清泠之水。楚人思慕，谓之水仙。 [162]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [162]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 洞庭山浮于水上 - Poetic description suggesting the grandeur and location of Dongting Mountain.
    • 金堂数百间 - Metaphorical expression indicating the opulence and splendor of the place.
    • 玉女居之 - Referring to the inhabitants as beautiful and ethereal beings.
    • 四时闻金石丝竹之声 - Metaphor for the continuous and harmonious music.
    • 彻于山顶 - Indicating the clarity and resonance of the music.
    • 楚怀王之时 - Historical context setting the stage for the events described.
    • 举群才赋诗于水湄 - Literary and social activity of gathering talented individuals to compose poetry by the water's edge.
    • 潇湘洞庭之乐 - Poetic name for the region and its associated pleasures.
    • 听者令人难老 - Metaphor for the rejuvenating effect of the music.
    • 虽《咸池》、《九韶》，不得比焉 - Comparing the music to two famous ancient Chinese musical compositions.
    • 每四仲之节 - Specific times of the year for seasonal festivals.
    • 王常绕山以游宴 - Description of the king's activities of touring around the mountain for entertainment.
    • 各举四仲之气以为乐章 - Metaphor for the music being in tune with nature.
    • 仲春律中夹钟 - Specific time and musical key, reflecting the harmony of nature and music.
    • 轻风流水之诗 - Poetic description of the music, evoking a sense of natural beauty.
    • 宴于山南 - Location of the feast in the south of the mountain.
    • 律中蕤宾 - Specific time and musical key, indicating another specific time and musical key.
    • 皓露秋霜之曲 - Poetic description of the music, evoking a sense of autumnal beauty.
    • 好进奸雄 - King's poor judgment, favoring the wicked and ambitious.
    • 群贤逃越 - Departure of the virtuous, reflecting the consequences of poor leadership.
    • 屈原以忠见斥 - Qu Yuan was dismissed for his loyalty, referring to the historical figure Qu Yuan and his fate.
    • 隐于沅湘 - Qu Yuan hid in the waters of Yuan and Xiang, indicating his retreat.
    • 披蓁茹草 - Metaphor for his hardship, wearing through thorns and eating grass.
    • 混同禽兽 - Living like beasts, indicating his isolation.
    • 不交世务 - Did not engage in worldly affairs, indicating his withdrawal from society.
    • 采柏实以全桂膏 - Metaphor for his self-sustaining lifestyle, gathering pine nuts to preserve cinnamon oil.
    • 用养心神 - Nourishing his spirit, reflecting his spiritual practices.
    • 被王逼逐 - Forced exile, a common theme in historical narratives of political conflict.
    • 乃赴清泠之水 - Leaped into the clear cold water, indicating his suicide.
    • 楚人思慕 - The people of Chu longed for him, indicating the people's admiration.
    • 谓之水仙 - Called him the water deity, indicating his posthumous status.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 洞庭山 - Famous mountain in Hunan Province, known for its scenic beauty and cultural significance.
    • 金堂数百间 - Symbolic of wealth and power.
    • 玉女 - Beautiful and ethereal beings, often associated with Daoist mythology.
    • 四时 - The four seasons, a fundamental concept in Chinese culture and philosophy.
    • 金石丝竹 - Refers to various musical instruments, symbolizing harmony and beauty.
    • 楚怀王 - King Huai of Chu, a historical figure from the Warring States period.
    • 潇湘 - The Xiang River, a significant river in Hunan Province, often associated with poetry and literature.
    • 《咸池》、《九韶》 - Two famous ancient Chinese musical compositions.
    • 四仲之节 - The four seasonal festivals, important cultural events in ancient China.
    • 仲春律中夹钟 - Specific time and musical key, reflecting the harmony of nature and music.
    • 轻风流水之诗 - Poetic description of the music, evoking a sense of natural beauty.
    • 蕤宾 - A musical tone, indicating a specific time and musical key.
    • 皓露秋霜之曲 - Poetic description of the music, evoking a sense of autumnal beauty.
    • 好进奸雄 - Refers to the king's poor judgment, a common theme in historical narratives.
    • 群贤逃越 - Departure of the wise, reflecting the consequences of poor leadership.
    • 屈原 - Famous poet and statesman from the Warring States period, known for his loyalty and tragic fate.
    • 沅湘 - The Yuan and Xiang rivers, significant geographical features in Hunan Province.
    • 披蓁茹草 - Metaphor for hardship and isolation.
    • 混同禽兽 - Metaphor for living in isolation, away from human society.
    • 采柏实以全桂膏 - Metaphor for self-sustaining practices, reflecting Qu Yuan's withdrawal from society.
    • 用养心神 - Spiritual practices, reflecting Qu Yuan's inner life.
    • 被王逼逐 - Forced exile, a common theme in historical narratives of political conflict.
    • 清泠之水 - The clear cold water, a metaphor for his suicide.
    • 水仙 - The water deity, a posthumous title given to Qu Yuan, reflecting his revered status.

    Proper Nouns

    • 洞庭山 - Famous mountain in Hunan Province.
    • 金堂数百间 - Symbolic of wealth and power.
    • 玉女 - Beautiful and ethereal beings.
    • 楚怀王 - King Huai of Chu.
    • 潇湘 - The Xiang River.
    • 《咸池》、《九韶》 - Two famous ancient Chinese musical compositions.

    Dongting Mountain

    Dongting Mountain floats on water, with hundreds of golden halls beneath it, inhabited by jade maidens. Throughout the four seasons, one can hear the sounds of metal, stone, strings, and bamboo, which reach the summit of the mountain. During the reign of King Huai of Chu, talented individuals gathered by the waterside to compose poetry, hence the saying that the joy of Xiang and Dongting is rejuvenating, making listeners difficult to age, even surpassing the Xianchi and Jiuyao. At each of the four seasonal festivals, the king often toured around the mountain for entertainment, creating music in tune with the four seasonal energies. In the middle spring, when the musical tone is Jiazhong, he composed poetry of gentle wind and flowing water, feasting in the south of the mountain; when the musical tone is Ruiben, he composed music of white dew and autumn frost. Later, King Huai favored the wicked and ambitious, causing the virtuous to flee. Qu Yuan, dismissed for his loyalty, hid in the waters of Yuan and Xiang, wearing through thorns and eating grass, living like beasts, and not engaging in worldly affairs. He gathered pine nuts to preserve cinnamon oil to nourish his spirit. Driven away by the king, he leaped into the clear cold water. The people of Chu longed for him and called him the water deity. [162]

    其神游于天河，精灵时降湘浦。楚人为之立祠，汉末犹在。其山又有灵洞，入中常如有烛于前。中有异香芬馥，泉石明朗。采药石之人入中，如行十里，迥然天清霞耀，花芳柳暗，丹楼琼宇，宫观异常。乃见众女，霓裳冰颜，艳质与世人殊别。来邀采药之人，饮以琼浆金液，延入璇室，奏以箫管丝桐。饯令还家，赠之丹醴之诀。虽怀慕恋，且思其子息，却还洞穴，还若灯烛导前，便绝饥渴，而达旧乡。已见邑里人户，各非故乡邻，唯寻得九代孙。问之，云：“远祖入洞庭山采药不还，今经三百年也。”其人说于邻里，亦失所之。 [163]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [163]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 其神游于天河 - Poetic expression indicating a transcendent or otherworldly journey; 'his spirit roams in the Milky Way'.
    • 精灵时降湘浦 - 'Spirit occasionally descends to the Xiang River,' where '精灵' (spirit) refers to a supernatural being or deity.
    • 楚人为之立祠 - 'The people of Chu established a temple for him,' indicating the veneration of a deity or hero.
    • 汉末犹在 - 'Still existed in the late Han period,' marking the historical context.
    • 入中常如有烛于前 - 'Entering often feels as if there is a candle in front,' describing a luminous or guiding presence within the cave.
    • 中有异香芬馥 - 'There is a strange fragrance within,' indicating a unique and pleasant scent.
    • 泉石明朗 - 'The springs and stones are bright and clear,' describing the natural beauty of the cave.
    • 采药石之人 - 'Those who gather herbs and stones,' referring to individuals seeking medicinal or alchemical materials.
    • 迥然天清霞耀 - 'Suddenly, the sky is clear and the clouds are radiant,' describing a sudden shift to a heavenly or ethereal scene.
    • 丹楼琼宇 - 'Red towers and jade palaces,' referring to magnificent and luxurious structures.
    • 宫观异常 - 'Palaces and temples are extraordinary,' emphasizing the grandeur and uniqueness of the architecture.
    • 乃见众女，霓裳冰颜 - 'Then they see many women, dressed in rainbow garments and with icy faces,' describing the appearance of celestial beings.
    • 艳质与世人殊别 - 'Their beauty is unlike that of ordinary people,' emphasizing their otherworldly nature.
    • 来邀采药之人 - 'They come to invite the herb-gatherers,' indicating a welcoming or guiding role.
    • 饮以琼浆金液 - 'They offer them nectar and golden elixirs,' referring to divine or magical beverages.
    • 延入璇室 - 'They invite them into the jade chamber,' describing a luxurious and sacred space.
    • 奏以箫管丝桐 - 'They play flutes, pipes, strings, and zithers,' indicating a musical performance.
    • 饯令还家 - 'They send them back home,' suggesting a farewell or return to the mortal world.
    • 赠之丹醴之诀 - 'They give them the secrets of the elixir of immortality,' referring to alchemical knowledge.
    • 虽怀慕恋 - 'Although they harbor longing,' indicating a sense of regret or desire to stay.
    • 且思其子息 - 'And think of their descendants,' suggesting a concern for their mortal family.
    • 却还洞穴 - 'They return to the cave,' indicating a physical return to the cave.
    • 还若灯烛导前 - 'Again, it feels as if a candle is leading the way,' describing the continued guiding presence.
    • 便绝饥渴 - 'They no longer feel hunger or thirst,' indicating a state of transcendence or fulfillment.
    • 而达旧乡 - 'And reach their old hometown,' suggesting a return to their mortal life.
    • 已见邑里人户 - 'They have already seen the people and houses of their village,' indicating recognition of their surroundings.
    • 各非故乡邻 - 'None of them are from their hometown,' suggesting a sense of disorientation or change.
    • 唯寻得九代孙 - 'They only find the ninth-generation descendant,' indicating a long passage of time.
    • 问之，云 - 'They ask, and the descendant says,' introducing a dialogue.
    • 远祖入洞庭山采药不还，今经三百年也 - 'My distant ancestor entered Mount Dongting to gather herbs and did not return, and it has been three hundred years,' indicating the passage of time and the ancestor's absence.
    • 其人说于邻里 - 'The person tells their neighbors,' suggesting a spread of the story.
    • 亦失所之 - 'And they also lose their way,' indicating a sense of confusion or disappearance.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 天河 - The Milky Way, a celestial feature often associated with gods and immortals in Chinese mythology.
    • 湘浦 - The Xiang River, a significant river in southern China, often associated with the goddess Xiang Jun in Chinese mythology.
    • 楚人 - The people of Chu, an ancient state in China, known for its rich cultural and mythological traditions.
    • 汉末 - The late Han period, a time of political instability and the transition to the Three Kingdoms era.
    • 祠 - A temple or shrine, often dedicated to deities or ancestors.
    • 洞庭山 - Mount Dongting, a mythical mountain associated with immortals and alchemical practices in Chinese folklore.
    • 采药 - The practice of gathering herbs, often associated with alchemy and the pursuit of immortality.
    • 霓裳冰颜 - 'Rainbow garments and icy faces,' a description of celestial beings or immortals.
    • 丹楼琼宇 - 'Red towers and jade palaces,' a description of divine or heavenly architecture.
    • 璇室 - A jade chamber, a sacred or luxurious space in Chinese mythology.
    • 箫管丝桐 - Musical instruments such as flutes, pipes, strings, and zithers, often associated with divine or celestial music.

    Proper Nouns

    • 天河 - The Milky Way.
    • 湘浦 - The Xiang River.
    • 楚人 - The people of Chu.
    • 汉末 - The late Han period.
    • 祠 - A temple or shrine.
    • 洞庭山 - Mount Dongting.
    • 采药 - The practice of gathering herbs.
    • 霓裳冰颜 - 'Rainbow garments and icy faces.'
    • 丹楼琼宇 - 'Red towers and jade palaces.'
    • 璇室 - A jade chamber.
    • 箫管丝桐 - Musical instruments such as flutes, pipes, strings, and zithers.

    The Spirit roamed in the Milky Way, and its spirit occasionally descended to the Xiang River. The people of Chu established a temple for it, and it still existed in the late Han period. The mountain also had a mystical cave; entering it often felt as if there was a candle in front. Within the cave was a strange fragrance, and the springs and stones were bright and clear. Those who gathered herbs and stones entered the cave, feeling as if they had walked ten miles, suddenly finding themselves in a place where the sky was clear and the clouds radiant, with fragrant flowers and dark willows, red towers and jade palaces, and extraordinary palaces and temples. They then saw many women, dressed in rainbow garments and with icy faces, their beauty unlike that of ordinary people. These celestial beings came to invite the herb-gatherers, offering them nectar and golden elixirs, and invited them into the jade chamber, where they played flutes, pipes, strings, and zithers. They sent the herb-gatherers back home, giving them the secrets of the elixir of immortality. Although they harbored longing, and thought of their descendants, they returned to the cave, again feeling as if a candle was leading the way, and no longer felt hunger or thirst, reaching their old hometown. They had already seen the people and houses of their village, but none of them were from their hometown; they only found the ninth-generation descendant. They asked, and the descendant said, "My distant ancestor entered Mount Dongting to gather herbs and did not return, and it has been three hundred years." The person told their neighbors, and they also lost their way. [163]

    录曰：按《禹贡》山海，正史说名山大泽，或不列书图，着于编杂之部。或有乍无，或同乍异，故使览者回惑而疑焉。至如《列子》所说，员峤、岱舆，瑰奇是聚，先《坟》莫记。蓬莱、瀛洲、方丈，各有别名；昆吾神异，张骞亦云焉。睹华戎不同寒暑律人獦禽至其异气，云水草木，怪丽殊形，考之载籍，同其生类。非夫贵远体大，则笑其虚诞。俟诸宏博，验斯灵异焉。 [164]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [164]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 按《禹贡》山海 - Formal expression meaning 'according to the *Yugong* and the *Shanhaijing*.' *Yugong* refers to the *Classic of Mountains and Seas*, and *Shanhaijing* refers to the *Classic of Mountains and Seas*.
    • 正史说名山大泽 - Refers to the official histories' accounts of famous mountains and large lakes.
    • 或不列书图，着于编杂之部 - Means 'some are not listed in books or maps but are recorded in miscellaneous sections.'
    • 或有乍无，或同乍异 - Means 'some appear and disappear, some are the same and then different.'
    • 故使览者回惑而疑焉 - Means 'thus causing readers to be confused and doubtful.'
    • 至如《列子》所说 - Refers to the accounts in the *Liezi*.
    • 员峤、岱舆 - Refers to the mythical mountains *Yuanjiao* and *Daiyu*.
    • 瑰奇是聚 - Means 'gathered in their extraordinary beauty.'
    • 先《坟》莫记 - Means 'not recorded in the earlier classics.'
    • 蓬莱、瀛洲、方丈 - Refers to the mythical islands *Penglai*, *Yingzhou*, and *Fangzhang*.
    • 各有别名 - Means 'each has a different name.'
    • 昆吾神异 - Refers to the mythical *Kunwu* with its supernatural qualities.
    • 张骞亦云焉 - Means 'Zhang Qian also mentioned it.'
    • 睹华戎不同寒暑律人獦禽至其异气 - Means 'observing the different climates and laws of the Huaxia and the Xiongnu, up to their strange atmospheres.'
    • 云水草木，怪丽殊形 - Means 'clouds, water, grass, and trees, with strange and beautiful forms.'
    • 考之载籍，同其生类 - Means 'examining the records, they share the same kind of life.'
    • 非夫贵远体大，则笑其虚诞 - Means 'if one does not value the distant and grand, then one will laugh at their falsehood.'
    • 俟诸宏博，验斯灵异焉 - Means 'awaiting those who are broad and learned, to verify these wonders.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 《禹贡》 - Refers to the *Yugong*, an ancient Chinese text that describes the geography and administration of China during the Xia Dynasty.
    • 山海 - Refers to the *Shanhaijing*, an ancient Chinese text that describes various geographical features and mythical creatures.
    • 正史 - Refers to the official histories of China, such as the *Shiji* (Records of the Grand Historian) and the *Hanshu* (History of the Former Han).
    • 《列子》 - Refers to the *Liezi*, an ancient Chinese text attributed to Lie Yukou, a philosopher of the Warring States period.
    • 员峤、岱舆 - Mythical mountains mentioned in the *Shanhaijing*.
    • 蓬莱、瀛洲、方丈 - Mythical islands mentioned in the *Shanhaijing*.
    • 昆吾 - A mythical place mentioned in the *Shanhaijing*.
    • 张骞 - A famous Chinese explorer and diplomat who traveled to the Western Regions during the Han Dynasty.
    • 华戎 - Refers to the Huaxia (Chinese) and the Xiongnu (a nomadic people).
    • 寒暑律人獦禽 - Refers to the different climates and laws of the Huaxia and the Xiongnu, and the strange animals they encounter.
    • 云水草木 - Refers to the natural elements of clouds, water, grass, and trees.

    Proper Nouns

    • 《禹贡》 - The *Yugong*.
    • 山海 - The *Shanhaijing*.
    • 正史 - Official histories.
    • 《列子》 - The *Liezi*.
    • 员峤、岱舆 - Mythical mountains.
    • 蓬莱、瀛洲、方丈 - Mythical islands.
    • 昆吾 - A mythical place.
    • 张骞 - A famous explorer and diplomat.

    The Record states: According to the Yugong and the Shanhaijing, the official histories recount famous mountains and large lakes, some of which are not listed in books or maps but are recorded in miscellaneous sections. Some appear and disappear, some are the same and then different, thus causing readers to be confused and doubtful. As for the accounts in the Liezi, the mythical mountains Yuanjiao and Daiyu are gathered in their extraordinary beauty, yet they are not recorded in the earlier classics. The mythical islands Penglai, Yingzhou, and Fangzhang each have different names; Kunwu with its supernatural qualities is also mentioned by Zhang Qian. Observing the different climates and laws of the Huaxia and the Xiongnu, up to their strange atmospheres, clouds, water, grass, and trees, with strange and beautiful forms, examining the records, they share the same kind of life. If one does not value the distant and grand, then one will laugh at their falsehood. Awaiting those who are broad and learned, to verify these wonders. [164]

    录曰：按《禹贡》山海，正史说名山大泽，或不列书图，着于编杂之部。或有乍无，或同乍异，故使览者回惑而疑焉。至如《列子》所说，员峤、岱舆，瑰奇是聚，先《坟》莫记。蓬莱、瀛洲、方丈，各有别名；昆吾神异，张骞亦云焉。睹华戎不同寒暑律人獦禽至其异气，云水草木，怪丽殊形，考之载籍，同其生类。非夫贵远体大，则笑其虚诞。俟诸宏博，验斯灵异焉。

    The Record states: According to the Yugong and the Shanhaijing, the official histories recount famous mountains and large lakes, some of which are not listed in books or maps but are recorded in miscellaneous sections. Some appear and disappear, some are the same and then different, thus causing readers to be confused and doubtful. As for the accounts in the Liezi, the mythical mountains Yuanjiao and Daiyu are gathered in their extraordinary beauty, yet they are not recorded in the earlier classics. The mythical islands Penglai, Yingzhou, and Fangzhang each have different names; Kunwu with its supernatural qualities is also mentioned by Zhang Qian. Observing the different climates and laws of the Huaxia and the Xiongnu, up to their strange atmospheres, clouds, water, grass, and trees, with strange and beautiful forms, examining the records, they share the same kind of life. If one does not value the distant and grand, then one will laugh at their falsehood. Awaiting those who are broad and learned, to verify these wonders.
